questions.question: At the hardware level, what physically stores a single bit in DRAM?
options: A transistor across all supported platforms
A capacitor holding or not holding charge
A magnetic platter sector with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: A capacitor holding or not holding charge
questions.explanation: Note: 'RAM is a grid of billions of microscopic capacitors. One capacitor holds charge (1) or doesn't (0) = one bit.'

questions.question: How many bytes does `int score = 0;` reserve on modern platforms?
questions.correctAnswer: 4 bytes
questions.explanation: Note: 'the compiler reserves 4 bytes (32 capacitors) in RAM' for an int.

questions.question: What three properties does every variable have?
questions.correctAnswer: A name, a type, and an address
acceptableAnswers: a name a type and an address
questions.explanation: Note: 'Every variable has three properties: a name, a type (how to interpret the bits), and an address (where in the capacitor grid).'

questions.question: How does the CPU allocate space for local variables on the stack?
options: Writes to a special allocation table
questions.correctAnswer: Subtracts from the RSP register
questions.explanation: Note: 'Allocation = subtract from RSP. Deallocation = add to RSP. One CPU instruction.'

questions.question: The same 32-bit pattern 0x42C80000 can represent different values depending on type. What value as a float?
questions.explanation: The type lens table shows: interpreted as float → 100.0. Same bits, different meaning.

questions.question: Why does `bool` waste 7 bits of a byte?
options: The C++ standard requires it using standard engine approaches
The CPU's smallest addressable unit is one byte — it cannot address individual bits
Compilers are lazy about bool optimization using standard engine approaches in standard
It's reserved for future use through the built-in API with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: The CPU's smallest addressable unit is one byte — it cannot address individual bits
questions.explanation: Note warns: 'CPU can't address bits. 8 bools = 8 bytes, not 1.'

questions.question: How are signed integers encoded in modern C++?
questions.correctAnswer: Two's complement
questions.explanation: Note: 'Negate: flip all bits, add 1.' Enables a single adder circuit for both signed and unsigned.

questions.question: What are the three fields of an IEEE 754 single-precision float?
questions.correctAnswer: Sign, exponent, and mantissa
questions.explanation: 32 bits: sign (1 bit), exponent (8 bits, bias 127), mantissa (23 bits with implicit leading 1).

questions.question: What does `sizeof` return and when is it evaluated?
options: The runtime size of a variable, evaluated at runtime within the current scope
The compile-time size in bytes, evaluated by the compiler before code generation
The number of elements in an array using standard engine approaches
The alignment requirement of a type across all supported platforms with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: The compile-time size in bytes, evaluated by the compiler before code generation
questions.explanation: Note: 'NOT a function call — evaluated at compile time. Zero runtime cost.'

questions.question: Why doesn't `0.1f + 0.2f == 0.3f`?
options: C++ doesn't support decimal arithmetic across all supported platforms
0.1 and 0.2 cannot be exactly represented in binary floating point
The == operator doesn't work with floats in most implementations
It's a compiler bug as defined in the specification
questions.correctAnswer: 0.1 and 0.2 cannot be exactly represented in binary floating point
questions.explanation: Note: 'Floats are approximate. Never use ==.' Binary FP can't exactly represent 0.1 or 0.2.

questions.question: What does RIP hold?
options: Memory addresses for typical use cases
Decoded instructions using standard engine approaches
The address of the next instruction to execute
questions.correctAnswer: The address of the next instruction to execute
questions.explanation: Note: 'RIP holds the address of the next instruction. Control flow is anything that makes RIP jump.'

questions.question: What assembly instructions does an `if` statement compile to?
options: test followed by branch via the core framework with additional processing
cmp followed by a conditional jump (jle, jg, etc.)
if followed by goto in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: cmp followed by a conditional jump (jle, jg, etc.)
questions.explanation: Note: 'The hardware doesn't know if. It knows cmp (subtract, set flags) and jmp variants.'

questions.question: Which CPU flag is set when a comparison produces a result of zero?
questions.correctAnswer: ZF (Zero Flag)
questions.explanation: Note: 'ZF (Zero Flag): result = 0 (values equal).' The je instruction tests this flag.

questions.question: Why is a `switch` with dense case values faster than a long `if-else` chain?
options: The compiler unrolls the switch into parallel comparisons as defined in the specification with additional processing
Switch uses a jump table — O(1) indexed jump instead of O(N) sequential comparisons
Switch statements are compiled to GPU shaders under normal conditions
The CPU has a special SWITCH instruction through the built-in API
questions.correctAnswer: Switch uses a jump table — O(1) indexed jump instead of O(N) sequential comparisons
questions.explanation: Note: 'switch on dense values = jump table. One subtraction, one range check, one indexed jump. O(1).'

questions.question: What is the typical cycle penalty for a branch misprediction?
questions.correctAnswer: ~15-20 cycles
questions.explanation: Note: 'if wrong flushes the pipeline — ~15-20 cycle penalty.'

questions.question: Which loop construct saves one `jmp` instruction compared to `while`?
questions.correctAnswer: do-while
questions.explanation: Note: 'do-while — Saves One Jump. No initial forward conditional jump because the body always executes at least once.'

questions.question: Do `for` and `while` loops produce different assembly code for equivalent logic?
options: It depends on the compiler optimization level
questions.correctAnswer: No — they produce identical assembly
questions.explanation: Note: 'for produces EXACTLY the same machine code as the equivalent while. Syntactic sugar — no performance difference.'

questions.question: What do `break` and `continue` compile to?
options: They are handled entirely by the optimizer with no assembly representation
questions.correctAnswer: Unconditional jmp instructions — single cycle
questions.explanation: Note: 'Both compile to single unconditional jmp instructions — not function calls, zero overhead.'

questions.question: Why does sorting data before a branch-heavy loop sometimes make it faster?
options: Sorted data compresses better in cache under normal conditions with additional processing
The branch predictor thrives on patterns — sorted data creates predictable branches
The compiler optimizes sorted loops differently under normal conditions
It eliminates the need for branching entirely within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: The branch predictor thrives on patterns — sorted data creates predictable branches
questions.explanation: Note: 'Sorting data before processing can make a loop faster because the branch predictor thrives on patterns.'

questions.question: What happens when a `switch` case has no `break`?
options: Execution falls through to the next case
The compiler rejects it with regular usage patte
The case is skipped across common platfo
questions.correctAnswer: Execution falls through to the next case
questions.explanation: Note: 'Without break, execution falls through — deliberate C heritage for shared handlers.'

questions.question: What is the difference between a function declaration and a definition?
options: They are the same thing across all supported platforms
A declaration provides the body; a definition states existence through the built-in API with additional processing
A declaration tells the compiler the function exists; a definition provides the body (instructions)
A declaration is in the .h file; a definition is in the .cpp file in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: A declaration tells the compiler the function exists; a definition provides the body (instructions)
questions.explanation: Note: 'A declaration tells the compiler this function exists somewhere. A definition provides the body — the actual instructions.'

questions.question: What is the difference between parameters and arguments?
options: They are the same thing within the current scope
Parameters are in the declaration; arguments are the values at the call site
Arguments are in the declaration; parameters are at the call site in most practic
Parameters are input; arguments are output under normal conditions with typi
questions.correctAnswer: Parameters are in the declaration; arguments are the values at the call site
questions.explanation: Note: 'Parameters are the variables in the declaration (a, b). Arguments are the values at the call site (Add(3, 4)).'

questions.question: What does the `call` instruction do at the hardware level?
options: Calls malloc to allocate stack space through the built-in API
Saves all registers to memory then jumps using standard engine approaches
Pushes the return address (RSP-=8, [RSP]=RIP_next) and jumps (RIP=&func)
Sends an interrupt to the OS kernel as defined in the specification
questions.correctAnswer: Pushes the return address (RSP-=8, [RSP]=RIP_next) and jumps (RIP=&func)
questions.explanation: Note: 'call: 1. RSP=RSP-8, 2. [RSP]=RIP_next (save return address), 3. RIP=&Add (jump to function).'

questions.question: What does the `ret` instruction do?
options: Returns the value in RAX to the OS through the built-in API
Pops the return address from the stack into RIP and increments RSP
Destroys the stack frame and calls the destructor
Jumps to the instruction before the call as defined in the specification
questions.correctAnswer: Pops the return address from the stack into RIP and increments RSP
questions.explanation: Note: 'ret: 1. RIP=[RSP] (pop return address), 2. RSP=RSP+8 (reclaim stack space).'

questions.question: Which register holds the return value of a function on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: RAX
questions.explanation: Note: 'Integer/pointer returns go in RAX. Float returns in XMM0.'

questions.question: What is the stack frame prologue?
options: The first line of code in main() in most implementations in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
push rbp; mov rbp, rsp; sub rsp, N — saves caller's frame pointer, establishes base, allocates locals
The return statement at the end for typical use cases in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
The call instruction itself by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: push rbp; mov rbp, rsp; sub rsp, N — saves caller's frame pointer, establishes base, allocates locals
questions.explanation: Note shows: 'push rbp (save caller's frame pointer), mov rbp, rsp (new frame base), sub rsp, N (allocate locals).'

questions.question: What causes a stack overflow?
options: Calling too many different functions under normal conditions
Recursion that exhausts the fixed-size stack — RSP crosses unmapped guard pages
Allocating too much memory with new via the core framework
Using too many local variables in a single function under normal conditions with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: Recursion that exhausts the fixed-size stack — RSP crosses unmapped guard pages
questions.explanation: Note: 'When RSP crosses into unmapped guard pages, the MMU raises a page fault → SIGSEGV.'

questions.question: How does Tail Call Optimization transform a recursive call?
options: It inlines the function body for typical use cases
It replaces call with jmp, reusing the same stack frame for constant stack depth
It moves the recursion to the heap through the built-in API with typical engine set
It unrolls the recursion using templates within the current scope across common
questions.correctAnswer: It replaces call with jmp, reusing the same stack frame for constant stack depth
questions.explanation: Note: 'Tail Call Optimization changes call to jmp, reusing the same frame. Constant stack depth.'

questions.question: At the hardware level, how does pass-by-value work?
options: The entire object is copied to the heap across all supported platforms across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
The argument's bits are copied into a register (or stack slot) — the function sees the copy, not the original
A pointer to the original is passed automatically as defined in the specification for common use casesxxxxxxxx
The compiler generates a copy constructor call via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: The argument's bits are copied into a register (or stack slot) — the function sees the copy, not the original
questions.explanation: Note: 'At the hardware level: health's 4 bytes are copied into the EDI register. The function only sees EDI.'

questions.question: What is the maximum stack size on Linux, and roughly how many recursive calls can a simple function make?
options: ~64 KB — about 1,000 calls with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: ~8 MB — roughly 130,000-170,000 recursive calls
questions.explanation: Note: 'With an 8 MB stack (Linux), ~130,000-170,000 recursive calls max. Each frame costs ~48-64 bytes.'

questions.question: Why is accessing consecutive array elements fast?
options: The compiler precomputes all values across all supported platforms for com
Spatial locality — a 64-byte cache line loads 16 consecutive ints at once
Arrays use special fast RAM banks as defined in the specification within the s
The CPU predicts the next index under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: Spatial locality — a 64-byte cache line loads 16 consecutive ints at once
questions.explanation: Note: 'Accessing arr[0] pulls a 64-byte cache line — arr[0] through arr[15] arrive in L1 cache in one go.'

questions.question: What happens when you access `arr[-1]` or `arr[100]` on a C-style array of size 10?
options: The program throws an exception using standard engine approaches
The compiler refuses to compile this as defined in the specification
Undefined behavior — might crash, might silently corrupt memory
The OS catches the error and prints a warning
questions.correctAnswer: Undefined behavior — might crash, might silently corrupt memory
questions.explanation: Note warns: 'Nothing stops arr[5] or arr[-1]. Undefined behavior — might crash, might silently corrupt.'

questions.question: What happens to a C-style array when passed to a function?
options: A copy of the entire array is made under normal conditions
It decays to a pointer — sizeof becomes pointer size (8), array size is lost
It is passed by reference automatically in most implementations
The function receives the array's capacity for typical use cases with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: It decays to a pointer — sizeof becomes pointer size (8), array size is lost
questions.explanation: Note: 'C arrays decay to pointers when passed to functions — sizeof(arr) becomes pointer size, not array size.'

questions.question: What advantage does std::array have over a C-style array?
options: It's stored on the heap for safety under normal conditions
It always bounds-checks every access as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxx
Same memory layout as C array but adds .size(), .at() bounds-checking, and never decays to pointer
It uses SIMD instructions internally by default behavior with additional processing for common use c
questions.correctAnswer: Same memory layout as C array but adds .size(), .at() bounds-checking, and never decays to pointer
questions.explanation: Note: 'Zero-overhead: same memory layout as C array, no heap allocation, adds safety through .at() and size through .size(). Never decays.'

questions.question: What happens internally when std::vector runs out of capacity on push_back?
options: It throws an exception by default behavior
It allocates a new larger buffer, moves/copies all elements, and deallocates the old buffer
It links to a new memory block, keeping the old one via the core framework across common platfo
It compresses existing elements for typical use cases with additional processing within the standard
questions.correctAnswer: It allocates a new larger buffer, moves/copies all elements, and deallocates the old buffer
questions.explanation: Note describes reallocation: allocate bigger, move everything, free the old buffer.

questions.question: How can you prevent std::vector reallocations?
options: Use a smaller type across all supported platforms
Call .reserve(n) before pushing to pre-allocate capacity
Use .shrink_to_fit() regularly across all supported platforms
Reallocations can't be prevented with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: Call .reserve(n) before pushing to pre-allocate capacity
questions.explanation: Note mentions reserve() as the way to prevent reallocations by allocating capacity upfront.

questions.question: What does std::vector::size() return vs std::vector::capacity()?
options: Same thing in most implementations with additional processing within the standard f
size() = elements stored; capacity() = allocated slots before next reallocation
size() = allocated slots; capacity() = elements stored
size() = bytes; capacity() = elements within the current scope with the default
questions.correctAnswer: size() = elements stored; capacity() = allocated slots before next reallocation
questions.explanation: Note: 'size() is how many elements are stored. capacity() is how many fit before the next reallocation.'

questions.question: Which loop form prevents accidental copies of elements?
questions.correctAnswer: for (auto& x : vec)
questions.explanation: Note: 'for (auto x : vec) copies every element. Use for (auto& x : vec) instead.' Auto strips references.

questions.question: What does `int* p = &x;` do?
options: Creates a copy of x named p
Stores the memory address of x in p
Creates an alias p that IS x
Initializes p to the same value as x
questions.correctAnswer: Stores the memory address of x in p
questions.explanation: Note: 'p stores the ADDRESS of x. & = address of.' The diagram shows p contains 0x7FFF1000.

questions.question: How many memory accesses does `*p = 100` require at the assembly level?
options: One via the core framework with additional processing with regular usage pa
Two — one to load the address from p, one to write 100 to that address
Zero — the compiler optimizes it away by default behavior under normal operat
questions.correctAnswer: Two — one to load the address from p, one to write 100 to that address
questions.explanation: Note assembly: 'mov rax, [p]' (load address from p) then 'mov [rax], 100' (write) — two memory accesses.

questions.question: What does pointer arithmetic `p + n` actually compute?
options: p + n words with regular usage pa
questions.correctAnswer: p + n × sizeof(*p) bytes
questions.explanation: Note: 'p + n advances by n × sizeof(element) bytes.' For int* p, p+2 advances 8 bytes (2 × 4).

questions.question: Which of these are equivalent ways to access the 3rd element of an array?
options: arr[2] and *(p + 2) only in most practical scenar
questions.correctAnswer: arr[2], *(p + 2), *(arr + 2), and 2[arr]
questions.explanation: Note: 'arr[2] ≡ *(p + 2) ≡ *(arr + 2) ≡ 2[arr] — all identical.' arr[i] = *(arr + i).

questions.question: What happens when you dereference a nullptr?
options: Throws std::nullptr_exception via the core framework with regular usage patt
Undefined behavior — typically a crash (segfault/access violation)
The compiler prevents this at compile time across common platforms
questions.correctAnswer: Undefined behavior — typically a crash (segfault/access violation)
questions.explanation: Note: 'Dereferencing nullptr → undefined behavior (typically crash).'

questions.question: Why do smart pointers prevent memory leaks that raw pointers can't?
options: They use a garbage collector in most implementations
Their destructor automatically deletes the managed object when the pointer goes out of scope
They store data on the stack instead of the heap within the current scope
They use reference counting for everything through the built-in API with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: Their destructor automatically deletes the managed object when the pointer goes out of scope
questions.explanation: Note: 'Smart pointer destructor automatically deletes the managed object. Raw pointers never auto-delete.'

questions.question: What does `void*` represent?
options: A pointer to nothing by default behavior
A generic pointer that can hold any address — must be cast before dereferencing
A pointer to a void function across all supported platforms within the standard framework
An invalid pointer for typical use cases with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A generic pointer that can hold any address — must be cast before dereferencing
questions.explanation: Note: void* is a type-erased pointer. You can't dereference it without casting to a concrete type first.

questions.question: What is the difference between `int* p` and `int *p`?
options: int* p is preferred in C++ using standard engine approaches
int *p is preferred in C through the built-in API across common platf
There is no difference — the compiler treats them identically
int* p declares a pointer; int *p dereferences
questions.correctAnswer: There is no difference — the compiler treats them identically
questions.explanation: Note: Both are equivalent. The * binds to the variable name, not the type — 'int *p, x' declares one pointer and one int.

questions.question: What are the four stages of the C++ compilation pipeline, in order?
questions.correctAnswer: Preprocessor, Compiler, Assembler, Linker
questions.explanation: Note pipeline: .cpp → Preprocessor (.i) → Compiler (.s) → Assembler (.o) → Linker (.exe).

questions.question: What does the preprocessor do with `#include <iostream>`?
options: Imports only the declarations actually used across all supported platforms
Copy-pastes the entire iostream file (~25,000 lines) into your source
Links to the iostream shared library at runtime
Generates a forward declaration for every symbol
questions.correctAnswer: Copy-pastes the entire iostream file (~25,000 lines) into your source
questions.explanation: Note: '#include — copy-paste entire file. Preprocessed: ~25,000 lines (entire iostream pasted).'

questions.question: At which stage do syntax errors and type errors get caught?
questions.correctAnswer: Compiler
questions.explanation: Note: 'Compiler errors occur here — syntax, type errors.' The compiler is the stage that understands C++.

questions.question: What does the linker do?
options: Converts assembly to machine code under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxx
Expands all #include directives through the built-in API
Resolves symbols — connects function calls to their definitions across object files
Optimizes the generated machine code for typical use cases with typical engine sett
questions.correctAnswer: Resolves symbols — connects function calls to their definitions across object files
questions.explanation: Note: 'The linker resolves symbols. Connects declarations in one file to definitions in another.'

questions.question: What does an 'undefined reference' linker error mean?
options: You have a syntax error by default behavior
You declared a function but never defined it anywhere the linker can find
Your preprocessor macros are broken using standard engine approaches
The compiler ran out of memory for typical use cases within the standard f
questions.correctAnswer: You declared a function but never defined it anywhere the linker can find
questions.explanation: Note: 'Undefined reference: you declared a function but didn't define it, or forgot to link the .cpp file.'

questions.question: What file extension does the assembler produce?
questions.correctAnswer: .o / .obj
questions.explanation: Note: Assembler converts .s (assembly) to .o (object file with machine code + symbol table).

questions.question: What is the difference between a .o file and an executable?
options: No difference via the core framework
A .o file contains machine code with unresolved symbols; the executable has all symbols resolved and a main() entry point
A .o file is text; an executable is binary across all supported platforms with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A .o file is for debugging only through the built-in API in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A .o file contains machine code with unresolved symbols; the executable has all symbols resolved and a main() entry point
questions.explanation: Note: Object files have machine code + symbol table with unresolved references. The linker resolves all symbols into the final executable.

questions.question: Which g++ flag shows the preprocessor output?
questions.correctAnswer: -E
questions.explanation: Note: 'View preprocessor output: g++ -E hello.cpp -o hello.i'. -S generates assembly, -c compiles to object, -o sets output filename.

questions.question: What is a reference in C++?
options: A copy of a variable on the heap for typical use cases
An alias — another name for an existing variable, not a copy and not a pointer
A smart pointer that auto-deallocates across all supported platforms with additional processing
A var pointer that can never be null within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: An alias — another name for an existing variable, not a copy and not a pointer
questions.explanation: Note: 'A reference is an alias — another name for an existing variable. It's not a copy and not a pointer.'

questions.question: What happens when you assign to a reference?
options: The reference changes to point to a new variable
The original variable the reference aliases is modified
A copy is made, original unchanged via the core framework
Program crashes — references are immutable across all supported platforms
questions.correctAnswer: The original variable the reference aliases is modified
questions.explanation: Note: 'ref = 100; // Changes original to 100. They're the SAME variable.'

questions.question: Key difference between references and pointers?
options: References can be null across all supported platforms with additional processing across common plat
References must be initialized, cannot be null, cannot be reassigned to alias something else
References are on heap using standard engine approaches in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
References are slower within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: References must be initialized, cannot be null, cannot be reassigned to alias something else
questions.explanation: Note: 'References must be bound at declaration, cannot be null, and cannot be reassigned.'

questions.question: What is pass-by-reference used for?
options: Copying arguments through the built-in API under normal operat
Avoiding copies of large objects — zero-copy parameter passing
Making functions faster via registers in most practical scenariosxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Avoiding copies of large objects — zero-copy parameter passing
questions.explanation: Note: 'References enable zero-copy parameter passing.' The function works directly on the caller's data.

questions.question: How are references implemented at the hardware level?
options: Special CPU register for typical use cases
As a pointer under the hood — the compiler stores an address
Hash table lookup as defined in the specification
Symbol table alias only using standard engine approaches with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: As a pointer under the hood — the compiler stores an address
questions.explanation: Note: 'References are typically implemented as pointers under the hood — the compiler stores an address.'

questions.question: Why can't you have a reference to a reference?
options: The standard forbids it — references collapse: T& & becomes T&
It would cause infinite recursion through the built-in API
The compiler can't optimize it through the built-in API in standard con
It's allowed in C++20 by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: The standard forbids it — references collapse: T& & becomes T&
questions.explanation: Note: Reference collapsing rules mean T& & collapses to T&. You can't have a reference-to-reference.

questions.question: What does `var int&` guarantee?
options: The reference itself can't be changed to alias something else
You cannot modify the referenced value through this reference
Both the reference and value are var
The value is stored in ROM
questions.correctAnswer: You cannot modify the referenced value through this reference
questions.explanation: Note: 'var int& means the referenced value cannot be modified through this reference.'

questions.question: When should you use a reference vs a pointer for function parameters?
options: Always use references across all supported platforms in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Reference for required parameters that must exist; pointer for optional parameters (can be nullptr)
Always use pointers via the core framework
References for output; pointers for input via the core framework in most practical scenariosxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Reference for required parameters that must exist; pointer for optional parameters (can be nullptr)
questions.explanation: Note: References express 'this must exist.' Pointers can be null for optional parameters.

questions.question: How many distinct meanings does `static` have in C++?
questions.explanation: Note: 'One Keyword, Four Meanings': member variable, member function, local variable, global variable/function.

questions.question: What does a static member variable do?
options: Separate copy per instance across all supported platforms
One variable shared across ALL instances of the class
Prevents modification via the core framework
File-scope visibility for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: One variable shared across ALL instances of the class
questions.explanation: Note: 'Static Member Variables — One Copy, Shared By All.'

questions.question: What is the Static Initialization Order Fiasco?
options: Double initialization bug within the current scope across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Undefined construction order of global objects across .cpp files — crash before main()
Wrong function call order in most implementations
Inline static conflicts as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Undefined construction order of global objects across .cpp files — crash before main()
questions.explanation: Note: 'Global static objects across multiple .cpp files have unspecified construction order. One reads another unconstructed — crash.'

questions.question: How does Meyer's Singleton (function-local static) solve the init order fiasco?
options: Mutex serialization as defined in the specification under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Defers initialization to the first function call, eliminating cross-TU ordering dependencies
Moves to heap for typical use cases
Uses compiler attributes in most implementations with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Defers initialization to the first function call, eliminating cross-TU ordering dependencies
questions.explanation: Note: 'Function-local static defers to first call, eliminating ordering dependency between translation units.'

questions.question: What does C++17 `inline static` allow?
options: Always inlined by optimizer under normal conditions within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Define a static member variable directly in the class definition — no out-of-class .cpp definition needed
Share across DLLs by default behavior
Thread-local storage across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Define a static member variable directly in the class definition — no out-of-class .cpp definition needed
questions.explanation: Note: 'inline static — Definition AND declaration in one place! No .cpp definition required.'

questions.question: What does `static` at file/namespace scope do?
options: Share across all .cpp files as defined in the specification
Internal linkage — visible only within this translation unit (.cpp file)
Initialize before main() as defined in the specification
Prevent modification through the built-in API with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: Internal linkage — visible only within this translation unit (.cpp file)
questions.explanation: Note: 'Static Global Variables/Functions — Internal Linkage. Private to this translation unit.'

questions.question: Since which C++ standard is static local initialization thread-safe?
questions.explanation: Note: 'Thread safety (C++11+): Static local initialization is guaranteed thread-safe via compiler-generated guard variable.'

questions.question: What C++20 keyword enforces compile-time initialization for static variables?
questions.correctAnswer: constinit
questions.explanation: Note: 'C++20 constinit: compile error if initialization requires runtime code — preventing the fiasco for compile-time-constructible values.'

questions.question: What types can structured bindings work with?
options: Only std::pair across all supported platforms within the standard
Only std::tuple as defined in the specification within the stand
Pairs, tuples, arrays, and structs with all public members
Any type with a default constructor
questions.correctAnswer: Pairs, tuples, arrays, and structs with all public members
questions.explanation: Note: 'Structured bindings work with: pairs, tuples, arrays, and structs with all public members.'

questions.question: What three specializations enable custom types for structured bindings?
questions.correctAnswer: std::tuple_size, std::tuple_element, and get<I>()
questions.explanation: Note: specialize std::tuple_size (count), std::tuple_element<I,T> (type per index), provide get<I>(T) via ADL.

questions.question: What does the compiler generate for `auto [x, y] = GetPair();`?
options: Two separate variables using standard engine approaches
A hidden copy with x and y as references into it
x and y moved from the pair
Nothing special through the built-in API
questions.correctAnswer: A hidden copy with x and y as references into it
questions.explanation: Note: 'auto __hidden = GetPair(); then references: x = get<0>(__hidden), y = get<1>(__hidden).'

questions.question: How does `var auto& [x, y]` differ from `auto [x, y]`?
options: No difference using standard engine approaches

questions.question: What does RAII stand for?

questions.question: What's the difference between std::move and std::forward?

forward slower via the core framework across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
move for unique_ptr in most implementations with additional processing across common platforms
questions.correctAnswer: move unconditionally casts to rvalue; forward conditionally casts preserving value category
questions.explanation: move = always rvalue. forward = lvalue stays lvalue, rvalue stays rvalue (perfect forwarding).

questions.question: What is an rvalue reference (T&&)?
options: Reference to var for typical use cases for common use casesxxx
Reference that binds to temporaries — enables moving from them
Same as T& within the current scope
Pointer to T across all supported platforms in standard configur
questions.correctAnswer: Reference that binds to temporaries — enables moving from them
questions.explanation: T&& binds to rvalues (temporaries, std::move results). Key to move semantics: steal resources, don't copy.

questions.question: What does a moved-from object contain?
options: Original data by default behavior with additional processing
Valid but unspecified state — typically empty/null/zero
nullptr always within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: Valid but unspecified state — typically empty/null/zero
questions.explanation: Moved-from objects are in a valid-but-unspecified state. Can be destroyed or assigned to. Don't rely on specific values.

questions.question: What is guaranteed about a moved-from std::unique_ptr?
options: It still owns the object through the built-in API
It is nullptr — the moved-to pointer now owns the object
It's in an unspecified state for typical use cases with additional processing
It throws on access by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: It is nullptr — the moved-to pointer now owns the object
questions.explanation: unique_ptr guarantees moved-from state is nullptr. Other types are in valid-but-unspecified state.

questions.question: What's the difference between var and constexpr?
options: var = runtime promise (won't modify); constexpr = compile-time evaluation guaranteed
var faster within the current scope with additional processing for common use casesxxx
constexpr deprecated in most implementations in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: var = runtime promise (won't modify); constexpr = compile-time evaluation guaranteed
questions.explanation: 'var promises won't write. constexpr guarantees value known at compile time.'

questions.question: What does consteval enforce?
options: Compile or runtime under normal conditions
MUST be compile-time — error if called with runtime arguments
Runtime only via the core framework with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: MUST be compile-time — error if called with runtime arguments
questions.explanation: 'consteval = immediate function. Every call MUST produce a compile-time constant.'

questions.question: What does constinit (C++20) do?
options: Same as constexpr under normal conditions under normal operationxxxxxxx
Compile-time init, runtime mutable — solves static init order fiasco
Prevents mutation for typical use cases within the standard frameworkxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Compile-time init, runtime mutable — solves static init order fiasco
questions.explanation: constinit: initialized at compile time but mutable at runtime. No guard variable overhead.

questions.question: Can a constexpr function be called with runtime arguments?
options: Yes — it behaves like a normal function at runtime
Only in C++20 across all supported platforms
Only with consteval using standard engine approaches
questions.correctAnswer: Yes — it behaves like a normal function at runtime
questions.explanation: constexpr functions CAN be evaluated at compile time. With runtime args, they're normal functions.

questions.question: Where does a constexpr variable's value live at runtime?
options: On the heap as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxx
In .rodata (read-only data segment) or as immediate value in instructions
On the stack through the built-in API
In a register only as defined in the specification with typical engine setting
questions.correctAnswer: In .rodata (read-only data segment) or as immediate value in instructions
questions.explanation: constexpr variables are compile-time constants. Compiler can embed them as immediates or place in .rodata.

questions.question: Why does noexcept move constructor improve vector performance?
options: vector uses move_if_noexcept: noexcept→move(pointer swap); non-noexcept→copy for exception safety
Enables SIMD across all supported platforms for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Binary size as defined in the specification in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: vector uses move_if_noexcept: noexcept→move(pointer swap); non-noexcept→copy for exception safety
questions.explanation: 'vector checks noexcept. Yes → move (~3 cycles). No → copy (thousands of cycles).'

questions.question: What happens if a noexcept function throws?
options: Caught by caller by default behavior under normal
Compiler error by default behavior across common platfo
questions.correctAnswer: std::terminate() — instant death, no recovery
questions.explanation: noexcept is a promise you cannot break. Violation = std::terminate(), no stack unwinding.

questions.question: When should a function be marked noexcept?
options: Always through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Move constructors, destructors, swap — operations that should never fail
Only in templates as defined in the specification in standard configurationsxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Move constructors, destructors, swap — operations that should never fail
questions.explanation: Move ctors and dtors should always be noexcept. Swaps should be. There's no reason for 'stealing guts' to throw.

questions.question: What does the noexcept operator do?
options: Marks a function noexcept as defined in the specification
Compile-time check: noexcept(expr) returns true if expr is noexcept
Executes expr for typical use cases
Catches exceptions within the current scope with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: Compile-time check: noexcept(expr) returns true if expr is noexcept
questions.explanation: noexcept(expression) is a compile-time operator. Returns bool. Used in templates for conditional noexcept.

questions.question: Why are destructors implicitly noexcept?
options: Performance only across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Throwing from destructor during stack unwinding = std::terminate() — double exception
Required by standard in most implementations under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Throwing from destructor during stack unwinding = std::terminate() — double exception
questions.explanation: If a destructor throws while another exception is in flight, the program terminates. noexcept prevents this.

questions.question: Key difference between variant and C union?
options: None across all supported platforms with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
variant has discriminator tracking active alternative — type-safe; C unions don't
On heap under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: variant has discriminator tracking active alternative — type-safe; C unions don't
questions.explanation: variant stores max(sizeof(alternatives)) + discriminator. Always knows which type is active.

questions.question: Does std::optional allocate on heap?
options: No — inline storage, sizeof ≈ sizeof(T) + alignment padding
Only for large T for typical use cases across common platformsx
Depends for typical use cases with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: No — inline storage, sizeof ≈ sizeof(T) + alignment padding
questions.explanation: 'optional is NOT a pointer. No heap allocation. Stores value inline.' sizeof(optional<int>) ≈ 8.

questions.question: What is std::visit used for?
options: Heap allocation as defined in the specification with typical engine
Compile-time exhaustive dispatch over all variant alternatives
Serialization through the built-in API in most practical scenariosxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Compile-time exhaustive dispatch over all variant alternatives
questions.explanation: visit generates dispatch table. If you add a new alternative, visit won't compile until handled. Exhaustive.

questions.question: When should you use std::any?
options: When types aren't enumerable at compile time — scripting, plugin APIs
Never as defined in the specification for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
For small types only as defined in the specification with the default setupxxx
questions.correctAnswer: When types aren't enumerable at compile time — scripting, plugin APIs
questions.explanation: any uses heap + RTTI. Prefer variant for closed type sets, optional for nullable values.

questions.question: What does std::optional::value_or(default) do?
options: Always returns the contained value for typical use cases in most
Returns contained value if present, otherwise returns the default
Throws if empty within the current scope
Modifies the optional across all supported platforms in most practical
questions.correctAnswer: Returns contained value if present, otherwise returns the default
questions.explanation: value_or() is the safe accessor: get the value or a fallback without checking or throwing.

questions.question: What three keywords identify C++20 coroutines?
questions.correctAnswer: co_await, co_yield, co_return
questions.explanation: co_await suspends/resumes, co_yield produces value and suspends, co_return completes the coroutine.

questions.question: What is a coroutine?
options: A thread through the built-in API with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A function that can suspend and resume — like a function with multiple entry/exit points
A lambda as defined in the specification for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A template for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: A function that can suspend and resume — like a function with multiple entry/exit points
questions.explanation: Coroutines can pause mid-execution and resume later. Enables cooperative multitasking without threads.

questions.question: What does co_yield do?
options: Ends the coroutine for typical use cases in most
Produces a value and suspends — for generators
Throws using standard engine approaches across
questions.correctAnswer: Produces a value and suspends — for generators
questions.explanation: co_yield returns a value to the caller and suspends. The coroutine can be resumed to yield more values.

questions.question: What does co_await do?
options: Suspends execution until the awaited operation completes
Starts a thread for typical use cases in standard configuration
Throws using standard engine approaches with regular usage patter
questions.correctAnswer: Suspends execution until the awaited operation completes
questions.explanation: co_await is the primary coroutine keyword: suspend until the awaitable produces a result.

questions.question: Can C++ coroutines run on multiple threads?
options: No, single-threaded only across all supported platforms in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxx
They can — the coroutine itself is stackless; resumption can happen on any thread
Always multithreaded for typical use cases across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Only with co_await via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: They can — the coroutine itself is stackless; resumption can happen on any thread
questions.explanation: C++20 coroutines are stackless. The coroutine frame is heap-allocated. Resumption can happen from any thread.

questions.question: Key advantage of ranges over iterator pairs?
options: Composable pipelines with lazy evaluation — filter|transform|take, zero intermediate allocations
Smaller binary through the built-in API under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Better errors using standard engine approaches across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Composable pipelines with lazy evaluation — filter|transform|take, zero intermediate allocations
questions.explanation: 'Ranges compose with |. filter | transform | take — lazy, no allocations. Zero-cost abstraction.'

questions.question: What is a view in ranges?
options: A copy of data by default behavior with additional processing
A lazy, non-owning range adaptor — computes on-demand, no storage
An iterator by default behavior with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A lazy, non-owning range adaptor — computes on-demand, no storage
questions.explanation: Views are lightweight objects storing reference to underlying range + parameters. Pull model.

questions.question: What does rv::filter(pred) return?
options: A new vector for typical use cases with additional processing
A view — lazy, no allocation, filters on-demand
questions.correctAnswer: A view — lazy, no allocation, filters on-demand
questions.explanation: filter returns a view. No work happens until iteration. Elements are filtered one at a time on-demand.

questions.question: What does rv::transform(fn) do?
options: Modifies in place in most implementations with typical engin
Returns a view applying fn to each element on-demand
Copies everything by default behavior with regular usage patte
questions.correctAnswer: Returns a view applying fn to each element on-demand
questions.explanation: transform is lazy. The function is applied as elements are pulled through the pipeline.

questions.question: What is a projection in std::ranges algorithms?
options: A 3D transform using standard engine approaches for common use casesxxxx
A unary function applied to each element before comparison/operation
A sorting criteria via the core framework for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxx
A view via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: A unary function applied to each element before comparison/operation
questions.explanation: Projections: sort(players, {}, &Player::score) applies &Player::score before comparing. Eliminates 90% of custom comparator lambdas.

questions.question: Why does UE disable C++ exceptions?
options: Too slow for typical use cases
Performance predictability, code size, and console platform constraints
Windows incompatibility by default behavior with typical engine settingsxxxx
Deprecated in C++20 through the built-in API across common platformsxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Performance predictability, code size, and console platform constraints
questions.explanation: Exceptions add 5-15% binary size and make control flow unpredictable. UE uses macros instead.

questions.question: What does check(expr) do?
options: Logs a warning for typical use cases
If false → crashes immediately with message + callstack — in dev AND shipping builds
Throws exception within the current scope with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Returns false as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: If false → crashes immediately with message + callstack — in dev AND shipping builds
questions.explanation: check() is fatal in all builds. ensure() logs and continues (dev only). verify() like check but expr runs in shipping too.

questions.question: How does UE's Cast<T>() work without RTTI?
options: It doesn't — UE uses dynamic_cast across all supported platforms in most
Uses compile-time static class flags generated by UHT — O(1) type check
Falls back to reinterpret_cast as defined in the specification with regu
Uses virtual functions via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: Uses compile-time static class flags generated by UHT — O(1) type check
questions.explanation: UHT generates StaticClass() and TCastFlags. Cast<T>() compares flags — no vtable walk, no RTTI overhead.

questions.question: What UE type replaces std::string?
questions.correctAnswer: FString
questions.explanation: FString = mutable heap string (like std::string). FName = interned string (O(1) comparison). FText = localizable.

questions.question: What is the difference between FName and FString?
options: No difference via the core framework
FName is an interned, immutable string — O(1) comparison via index; FString is a regular mutable string
FName is shorter through the built-in API with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
FName is deprecated using standard engine approaches with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: FName is an interned, immutable string — O(1) comparison via index; FString is a regular mutable string
questions.explanation: FName stores strings in a global table. Comparing two FNames is comparing integers (table indices).

questions.question: Why can't you use raw AActor* to keep a UObject alive in UE?
options: It works fine within the current scope
Only UPROPERTY() members and TObjectPtr prevent GC — raw pointers don't count as references
UE doesn't use pointers within the current scope in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
AActor is abstract for typical use cases in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Only UPROPERTY() members and TObjectPtr prevent GC — raw pointers don't count as references
questions.explanation: Note: 'A raw AActor* does NOT prevent garbage collection. Only UPROPERTY() and TObjectPtr<T> keep UObjects alive.'

questions.question: What UE type replaces std::shared_ptr for non-UObject types?
questions.correctAnswer: TSharedPtr
questions.explanation: TSharedPtr is reference-counted like shared_ptr but for non-UObject types. TObjectPtr is for UObject GC tracking.

questions.question: What does ensure() do differently from check()?
options: Logs error + callstack but continues execution — only in development builds, stripped from shipping
Crashes harder for typical use cases across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Only works in debugger in most implementations with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Logs error + callstack but continues execution — only in development builds, stripped from shipping
questions.explanation: ensure() is for non-fatal errors you want to know about during development without crashing. Stripped from shipping.

questions.question: What are the four compilation stages in order?
questions.correctAnswer: Preprocessor, Compiler, Assembler, Linker
questions.explanation: .cpp → Preprocessor (.i) → Compiler (.s) → Assembler (.o) → Linker (.exe).

questions.question: What does the preprocessor do with #include?
options: Copy-pastes the entire file at the directive position
Links to a shared library across all supported platforms
Checks file existence across all supported platforms
questions.correctAnswer: Copy-pastes the entire file at the directive position
questions.explanation: #include = text paste. <iostream> adds ~25,000 lines before the compiler sees it.

questions.question: What does the linker resolve?
options: Syntax errors through the built-in API across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Symbols — connects 'require' in one .o to 'provide' in another .o or library
Template instantiation using standard engine approaches across common platfo
questions.correctAnswer: Symbols — connects 'require' in one .o to 'provide' in another .o or library
questions.explanation: Each .o has a symbol table: what it provides (defines) and what it requires (declared but not defined). Linker matches them.

questions.question: What does the OS loader do before main() runs?
options: Nothing under normal conditions with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Creates process with private virtual address space, maps .text/.data/.bss/stack/heap, loads dynamic libs
Compiles the program within the current scope
Runs the garbage collector for typical use cases under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Creates process with private virtual address space, maps .text/.data/.bss/stack/heap, loads dynamic libs
questions.explanation: Loader: create process → map segments → load dynamic libraries → jump to _start → __libc_start_main → main().

questions.question: What file extension does the assembler output?
questions.correctAnswer: .o / .obj
questions.explanation: Assembler: .s (assembly text) → .o (binary machine code + symbol table + relocation entries).

questions.question: What does the compiler's optimizer do?
options: Just formats code within the current scope with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Inlining, dead code elimination, loop unrolling, vectorization — transforms IR to faster equivalent
Links libraries within the current scope
Handles templates using standard engine approaches across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Inlining, dead code elimination, loop unrolling, vectorization — transforms IR to faster equivalent
questions.explanation: Compiler: C++ → AST → IR (GIMPLE/LLVM IR) → optimized IR → target assembly. Optimization happens at IR level.

questions.question: What is an 'undefined reference' linker error?
options: Syntax error in code for typical use cases across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A symbol was declared but never defined — the linker can't find its .o or library
Wrong compiler version using standard engine approaches in standard configurationsxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A symbol was declared but never defined — the linker can't find its .o or library
questions.explanation: Undefined reference = linker found a 'require' with no matching 'provide'. Forgot to link a .cpp or library.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of modules over #include?
options: Faster runtime via the core framework in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Dramatically faster compilation — modules are pre-compiled once, not text-pasted into every file
Easier syntax across all supported platforms across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Dramatically faster compilation — modules are pre-compiled once, not text-pasted into every file
questions.explanation: import std; replaces pasting the entire standard library. Modules parsed once, used many times.

questions.question: What does 'export' do in a module?
options: Nothing special for typical use cases
Makes a function/type/namespace visible to importers — without export, it's internal
Optimizes the code via the core framework for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Enables templates within the current scope with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Makes a function/type/namespace visible to importers — without export, it's internal
questions.explanation: Only exported entities are accessible to consumers. Non-exported = module-private.

questions.question: What is a module partition?
options: A separate module by default behavior with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A way to split a large module internally — consumers import the primary module and see everything
A deprecated feature through the built-in API with additional processing with regular usage patternsx
A header file by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: A way to split a large module internally — consumers import the primary module and see everything
questions.explanation: Partitions (module mymath:basics) are internal organization. Consumers only import mymath.

questions.question: What is a header unit?
options: A special kind of module under normal conditions in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A pre-compiled header imported as module: import <iostream>; — migration bridge from #include
A C++23 feature only through the built-in API
A deprecated pattern via the core framework with additional processing with typical engine se
questions.correctAnswer: A pre-compiled header imported as module: import <iostream>; — migration bridge from #include
questions.explanation: Header units let you import existing headers as modules. Not as clean as named modules (macro leakage possible).

questions.question: Why doesn't UE use modules yet?
options: They don't work by default behavior
Compiler support stabilized only recently; UBT needs changes; BMI not portable; massive codebase
Modules are too slow for typical use cases with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Epic doesn't like C++20 in most implementations across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Compiler support stabilized only recently; UBT needs changes; BMI not portable; massive codebase
questions.explanation: Four reasons: unstable compiler support until 2022-23, UBT changes needed, BMI format not portable, 20-year codebase.

questions.question: What is stack unwinding?
options: A compiler optimization across all supported platforms within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
When an exception is thrown, destructors of all local objects are called in reverse order as the stack backtracks to find a catch handler
Memory cleanup using standard engine approaches with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: When an exception is thrown, destructors of all local objects are called in reverse order as the stack backtracks to find a catch handler
questions.explanation: Each frame's cleanup runs destructors in reverse construction order. Exception tables map PC addresses to cleanup actions.

questions.question: Why should you always catch by var reference?
options: Avoids slicing — catching by value would copy and potentially slice derived exception types
Required by the standard through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
It's just convention in most implementations with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Avoids slicing — catching by value would copy and potentially slice derived exception types
questions.explanation: catch (var std::exception& e) preserves the actual exception type. catch (std::exception e) slices it.

questions.question: What are the three exception safety guarantee levels?
questions.correctAnswer: Basic (no leaks), Strong (commit-or-rollback), No-throw (cannot fail)
questions.explanation: Basic: no leaks but state may change. Strong: succeeds OR state unchanged. No-throw: never fails (destructors, swap).

questions.question: What happens if a destructor throws during stack unwinding?
options: It's caught normally using standard engine approaches under normal operationxx
std::terminate() — double exception is fatal, program dies immediately
The exception is ignored across all supported platforms within the stan
The program continues by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: std::terminate() — double exception is fatal, program dies immediately
questions.explanation: Throwing from destructor during another exception = two active exceptions → std::terminate(). Destructors must be noexcept.

questions.question: How does the runtime know which destructors to call during unwinding?
options: It guesses as defined in the specification
Exception tables (.eh_frame on Linux, .pdata/.xdata on Windows) map every PC address to cleanup actions
It walks the call stack manually across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
The compiler inserts try/catch everywhere under normal conditions across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Exception tables (.eh_frame on Linux, .pdata/.xdata on Windows) map every PC address to cleanup actions
questions.explanation: The compiler generates unwind tables. At runtime, the exception machinery uses these tables to find and execute cleanup code.

questions.question: What is CMake?
options: A compiler by default behavior in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A meta-build system — generates native build files (Makefiles, VS solutions) from CMakeLists.txt
A package manager using standard engine approaches with additional processing under normal operat
questions.correctAnswer: A meta-build system — generates native build files (Makefiles, VS solutions) from CMakeLists.txt
questions.explanation: CMake reads CMakeLists.txt and outputs platform-specific build files. It doesn't build directly.

questions.question: Why should you build out-of-source?
options: It's faster via the core framework
Keeps generated files separate from source — can delete build/ and regenerate cleanly
Required by CMake for typical use cases within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
For version control by default behavior with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Keeps generated files separate from source — can delete build/ and regenerate cleanly
questions.explanation: mkdir build && cd build && cmake .. — all generated files in build/. Never build in the source directory.

questions.question: What does target_link_libraries do?
options: Specifies that a target depends on another library — linker will resolve symbols from it
Downloads libraries through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Creates a library using standard engine approaches for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Specifies that a target depends on another library — linker will resolve symbols from it
questions.explanation: target_link_libraries(MyGame PRIVATE Engine) tells CMake MyGame needs Engine. PRIVATE = only MyGame needs it.

questions.question: What is the difference between STATIC and SHARED libraries?
options: No difference within the current scope with additional processing for common use cas
STATIC = copied into executable at link time; SHARED = loaded at runtime (.dll/.so)
STATIC is faster as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxx
SHARED is deprecated via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: STATIC = copied into executable at link time; SHARED = loaded at runtime (.dll/.so)
questions.explanation: add_library(Engine STATIC ...) vs SHARED. Static increases binary size; shared reduces it but adds runtime dependency.

questions.question: What does CMake's configure step do?
options: Compiles across all supported platforms with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Generates build files — reads CMakeLists.txt, detects compiler, finds dependencies, creates Makefiles/sln
Runs tests through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Generates build files — reads CMakeLists.txt, detects compiler, finds dependencies, creates Makefiles/sln
questions.explanation: cmake .. is configure. cmake --build . is build. They're separate steps — reconfigure only when CMakeLists.txt changes.

questions.question: What is the difference between a process and a thread?
options: Same thing across all supported platforms
Process has its own memory space; threads share memory within a process
Thread is faster via the core framework with additional processing
Process can't have threads through the built-in API with the default setupxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Process has its own memory space; threads share memory within a process
questions.explanation: Process = independent memory space. Threads within a process share the same address space (heap, globals).

questions.question: What does each thread have that is private?
options: Nothing under normal conditions with additional processing
Its own stack and register state
Its own heap across all supported platforms
questions.correctAnswer: Its own stack and register state
questions.explanation: Each thread has its own stack and CPU registers. Heap and globals are shared across all threads in the process.

questions.question: What does std::thread::join() do?
options: Starts the thread for typical use cases
Blocks the calling thread until the spawned thread finishes
Detaches the thread for typical use cases across common platfor
Kills the thread within the current scope with regular usage
questions.correctAnswer: Blocks the calling thread until the spawned thread finishes
questions.explanation: join() waits for the thread to complete. Must be called before the std::thread object is destroyed.

questions.question: What happens if a std::thread object is destroyed without join() or detach()?
options: Nothing by default behavior with regular usage patterns
std::terminate() — the thread is still joinable
The thread continues running for common use case
questions.correctAnswer: std::terminate() — the thread is still joinable
questions.explanation: A std::thread destructor calls terminate() if the thread is still joinable. Always join() or detach() before destruction.

questions.question: What is thread overhead?
options: Creating a thread allocates a stack (~1-8MB) and requires kernel scheduling — much heavier than a function call
Minimal — a few bytes across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Only CPU cycles by default behavior with additional processing in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Creating a thread allocates a stack (~1-8MB) and requires kernel scheduling — much heavier than a function call
questions.explanation: Thread creation is expensive. Thread pools reuse threads to amortize this cost.

questions.question: What is a process?
options: An executable loaded into memory with CPU time — a blueprint in flight
questions.correctAnswer: An executable loaded into memory with CPU time — a blueprint in flight
questions.explanation: Note: 'A process is that blueprint in flight: the executable loaded into memory, with CPU time allocated to it.'

questions.question: Which PCB field tracks open file descriptors?
questions.correctAnswer: files_struct
questions.explanation: Note PCB: 'files_struct* → pointer to file descriptor table'

questions.question: What mechanism does fork() use to avoid copying all memory?
questions.correctAnswer: Copy-on-Write (COW)
questions.explanation: Note: 'After fork(), both parent and child share the SAME physical pages, marked read-only in both page tables.'

questions.question: List the six steps of the OS loader when launching a process.
questions.correctAnswer: Read executable, create address space, load sections, load dynamic libraries, create main thread, return to caller
acceptableAnswers: read create load load create return
read executable create address space load sections load libraries create thread return to caller
questions.explanation: The note lists precisely six steps.

questions.question: Where does the PCB live in memory?
options: In the process's own virtual address space
In kernel memory, invisible to the process itself
questions.correctAnswer: In kernel memory, invisible to the process itself
questions.explanation: Note: 'lives in kernel memory, invisible to the process itself.'

questions.question: How many threads does a process start with?
questions.correctAnswer: One — the main thread
questions.explanation: Note: 'Every process starts with one thread (the main thread).'

questions.question: Which syscall creates new processes on Unix/Linux?
questions.correctAnswer: fork()
questions.explanation: Note: 'New processes are created with fork() (or vfork()).'

questions.question: What does exec() do after fork()?
options: Replaces the child's entire address space with the new program's image
questions.correctAnswer: Replaces the child's entire address space with the new program's image
questions.explanation: Note: 'exec REPLACES the entire address space with myprogram's image.'

questions.question: Why is std::thread::hardware_concurrency() potentially misleading?
options: Returns logical cores (including SMT), not physical cores
questions.correctAnswer: Returns logical cores (including SMT), not physical cores

questions.question: What does the OS scheduler do?

questions.question: What is a context switch?

Switching memory allocators as defined in the specification with additional processing with the def
Changing thread priority within the current scope

questions.explanation: Context switch: save current thread's registers, load next thread's. Cost: ~1-5 µs. Too many = thrashing.

questions.question: What is CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler)?

Linux's default scheduler — allocates CPU time proportionally based on virtual runtime
A mutex type under normal conditions
A Windows scheduler by default behavior in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Linux's default scheduler — allocates CPU time proportionally based on virtual runtime
questions.explanation: CFS tracks vruntime per thread. The thread with the lowest vruntime runs next. Fair over time.

questions.question: What is priority inversion?
options: A deadlock variant in most implementations with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A high-priority thread waits for a low-priority thread holding a lock — the low-priority thread can't run
A scheduler bug in most implementations in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A type of mutex via the core framework

questions.explanation: Priority inversion: high-prio blocked on lock held by low-prio. Fix: priority inheritance (low-prio inherits high-prio while holding lock).

questions.question: What is the typical context switch cost on modern hardware?

questions.explanation: Context switch: save/restore registers, switch page tables (CR3), potentially flush TLB. ~1-5 µs on modern CPUs.

questions.question: On x86-64, what specifically happens when a context switch occurs at the instruction level?
options: The OS saves all GPRs to the PCB, performs CR3 write (TLB flush), then restores from new PCB
The CPU automatically saves all registers to a kernel stack
The scheduler runs after every single instruction
A timer interrupt fires, saves RIP/RSP/RFLAGS to kernel stack, restores from next thread
questions.correctAnswer: A timer interrupt fires, saves RIP/RSP/RFLAGS to kernel stack, restores from next thread
questions.explanation: Context switch starts with timer interrupt → CPU pushes RIP/RSP/RFLAGS to kernel stack → scheduler runs → selects next thread → switches kernel stacks → performs CR3 write (TLB flush if no PCID) → iretq to user mode in next thread

questions.question: What does PCID (Process Context Identifier) do for context switching?
options: It tags TLB entries with an address-space ID, allowing TLB entries from multiple processes to coexist
It identifies the CPU core that should run a process
It compresses the page table for faster switching
It caches CR3 values to avoid memory writes
questions.correctAnswer: It tags TLB entries with an address-space ID, allowing TLB entries from multiple processes to coexist
questions.explanation: Without PCID, every CR3 write (context switch) flushes the entire TLB. With PCID, TLB entries are tagged with a 12-bit PCID — entries for the old process survive and don't need to be re-fetched when the process resumes.

questions.question: A game has 8 worker threads on a 4-core CPU. Each context switch costs ~5000 cycles. At 3 GHz, how much time per second is lost to switching if all threads are runnable and switch every 2 ms?
questions.explanation: 8 threads, each quantum 2 ms → 500 switches/s/core × 4 cores = 2000 switches/s. 2000 × 5000 cycles = 10M cycles/s. At 3 GHz (3B cycles/s): 10M/3B = 0.33%. Wait — each switch is 5000 cycles, 2000 switches/s = 10M cycles/s = 10M/3B = 0.33%. But this ignores cache warmup which adds 5-50 µs per switch, making actual overhead 5-15%.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between CFS and EEVDF scheduling?
options: CFS picks the thread with lowest vruntime; EEVDF adds deadlines to prevent unbounded scheduling delays
EEVDF is only for real-time threads; CFS is for normal threads
CFS uses priority queues; EEVDF uses a red-black tree
EEVDF replaced CFS because CFS was slower on ARM
questions.correctAnswer: CFS picks the thread with lowest vruntime; EEVDF adds deadlines to prevent unbounded scheduling delays
questions.explanation: CFS's vruntime approach could cause a CPU-bound thread to wait an unbounded time as other threads accumulated more vruntime. EEVDF assigns each thread a deadline — the time by which it must run — preventing starvation and improving latency for interactive workloads.

questions.question: Why does the Windows NT scheduler boost a thread's priority after it completes an I/O operation?

The thread pays a penalty for blocking, so the boost compensates
I/O operations always produce high-priority results
The thread needs to flush its cache after I/O
questions.correctAnswer: I/O completion suggests the thread is interactive and needs low latency for its next operation
questions.explanation: This is MLFQ behavior: a thread that blocks on I/O before its quantum expires is likely interactive (responding to user input, network data, etc.). Boosting its priority ensures the response is immediate when data arrives.
