questions.question: What is a data race in C++?
options: A fast race condition for typical use cases
Two threads accessing the same memory concurrently, at least one writes, no synchronization — undefined behavior
Two threads running simultaneously under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A compiler warning within the current scope with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Two threads accessing the same memory concurrently, at least one writes, no synchronization — undefined behavior
questions.explanation: C++ standard: concurrent access + at least one write + no synchronization = data race = UB.

questions.question: What does std::mutex::lock() do when the mutex is already locked?
options: Returns false through the built-in API
Blocks (sleeps) until the mutex is unlocked
Crashes under normal conditions in most practical
questions.correctAnswer: Blocks (sleeps) until the mutex is unlocked
questions.explanation: lock() blocks the calling thread. On Linux, it uses futex — fast path in userspace, slow path via kernel FUTEX_WAIT.

questions.question: What is a deadlock?
options: A fast lock across all supported platforms across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Thread A holds lock 1, waits for lock 2; Thread B holds lock 2, waits for lock 1 — neither can proceed
A lock that never releases in most implementations with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A race condition under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: Thread A holds lock 1, waits for lock 2; Thread B holds lock 2, waits for lock 1 — neither can proceed
questions.explanation: Deadlock = circular wait. Four Coffman conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait.

questions.question: What does std::scoped_lock do?
options: Locks one mutex by default behavior
Locks multiple mutexes atomically using deadlock-avoidance algorithm
Unlocks a mutex across all supported platforms within the standard framew
Detects deadlocks using standard engine approaches under normal operat
questions.correctAnswer: Locks multiple mutexes atomically using deadlock-avoidance algorithm
questions.explanation: scoped_lock(m1, m2, m3) locks all simultaneously. If any fails, releases all. RAII: auto-unlock on scope exit.

questions.question: How does a futex (Linux) achieve fast uncontended mutex?
options: Always syscall as defined in the specification for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
CAS on futex word in userspace — fast path ~20 cycles, no kernel involvement. Kernel only on contention.
Spinlock only for typical use cases
Uses hardware transactional memory as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: CAS on futex word in userspace — fast path ~20 cycles, no kernel involvement. Kernel only on contention.
questions.explanation: futex: 0=unlocked, 1=locked-no-waiters, 2=locked-with-waiters. Lock = CAS 0→1 (~20 cycles). Contention → FUTEX_WAIT syscall.

questions.question: In the futex two-phase mutex, what happens on the uncontended lock path?
options: A single LOCK CMPXCHG changes the futex word from 0 to 1 (~20 cycles, no syscall)
The thread calls FUTEX_WAIT, which blocks until the mutex is free
The kernel preempts the owning thread
The thread spins in a loop until the lock is free
questions.correctAnswer: A single LOCK CMPXCHG changes the futex word from 0 to 1 (~20 cycles, no syscall)
questions.explanation: The futex word is an atomic 32-bit integer. When 0 = unlocked, 1 = locked (no waiters), 2 = locked (waiters). Lock attempts CAS 0→1 (~20 cycles). On unlock, if the word was 1, atomic_dec_and_test to 0 — no syscall. If 2, call FUTEX_WAKE to wake waiters.

questions.question: What does `timed_mutex::try_lock_for(10ms)` do on a contended mutex?
options: It blocks for up to 10 ms attempting to acquire the lock, returning false if not acquired in time
It tries once and returns immediately if the lock is held
It blocks for exactly 10 ms regardless of when the lock is released
It throws an exception if the lock cannot be acquired in 10 ms
questions.correctAnswer: It blocks for up to 10 ms attempting to acquire the lock, returning false if not acquired in time
questions.explanation: try_lock_for uses a relative timeout: it calls FUTEX_WAIT with a timeout parameter. If the lock is acquired within 10ms, returns true. If the timeout expires first, FUTEX_WAIT returns ETIMEDOUT and try_lock_for returns false.

questions.question: On ARM64, what instruction pair does std::atomic::compare_exchange_strong compile to?
options: ldaxr (load-acquire exclusive) + stlxr (store-release exclusive), retry loop on failure
CAS instruction (hardware CAS available in ARMv8.1+)
questions.correctAnswer: ldaxr (load-acquire exclusive) + stlxr (store-release exclusive), retry loop on failure
questions.explanation: ARM uses LL/SC (Load-Linked/Store-Conditional). ldaxr loads with acquire+exclusive monitor, stlxr stores with release+condition. If another core wrote to the address between ldaxr and stlxr, the store fails (returns non-zero) and the CAS loop retries.

questions.question: Why does try_lock() need a retry strategy in a contended scenario?
options: Without it, the thread may pin the CPU at 100% in a tight CAS loop, wasting power and starving other threads
try_lock() returns false on every second call by design
The kernel preempts threads that call try_lock()
try_lock() always corrupts the mutex state on failure
questions.correctAnswer: Without it, the thread may pin the CPU at 100% in a tight CAS loop, wasting power and starving other threads
questions.explanation: A tight CAS loop (spin-wait) burns a full CPU core at 100% while doing no useful work. The recommended pattern: try_lock() → std::this_thread::yield() → try_lock() → std::this_thread::sleep_for(1ms) → repeat. This gives other threads a chance to run and release the lock.

questions.question: What are the four conditions required for a data race per the C++ standard?
options: Two threads, same location, concurrent, no ordering
Two+ threads, same memory location concurrently, at least one write, accesses not atomic, no happens-before
Two threads, no mutex, shared variable, same function
Multiple cores, shared cache, no LOCK prefix, same variable

questions.explanation: Circular wait: a cycle of threads each waiting for the next.

questions.question: How can you break the Circular Wait condition?

questions.question: How does Ostrich Algorithm handle deadlocks?

questions.correctAnswer: Killing one or more deadlocked threads or preempting resources from them

questions.question: What does std::atomic guarantee?
options: Atomicity — indivisible read-modify-write, no torn reads/writes, well-defined memory ordering
Faster than mutex via the core framework with additional processing with typical engine settingsxxxx
Thread safety for all operations via the core framework for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Atomicity — indivisible read-modify-write, no torn reads/writes, well-defined memory ordering
questions.explanation: atomic is lock-free for fundamental types. Guarantees: no torn reads, indivisible RMW, controlled memory ordering.

questions.question: What does memory_order_relaxed provide?
options: Full ordering within the current scope in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Atomicity only — no ordering guarantees between threads. Counters where order doesn't matter.
Sequential consistency as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Atomicity only — no ordering guarantees between threads. Counters where order doesn't matter.
questions.explanation: relaxed = cheapest. Use for simple counters where only the final value matters, not the ordering relative to other operations.

questions.question: What does memory_order_seq_cst guarantee?
options: Nothing extra via the core framework
Single total order — all threads agree on the order of all seq_cst operations. Strongest, most expensive.
Same as relaxed as defined in the specification in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Release semantics by default behavior within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Single total order — all threads agree on the order of all seq_cst operations. Strongest, most expensive.
questions.explanation: seq_cst = default. MFENCE on x86 (~20-30 cycles). All threads see all seq_cst ops in the same order.

questions.question: How does acquire-release ordering work?
options: Release store synchronizes-with acquire load on same atomic — creates happens-before edge, transitively ordering all prior writes
Same as relaxed for typical use cases within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Only for mutexes using standard engine approaches in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Release store synchronizes-with acquire load on same atomic — creates happens-before edge, transitively ordering all prior writes
questions.explanation: acquire/release pair creates a synchronizes-with relationship. Classic pattern: flag.store(true, release); while(!flag.load(acquire)).

questions.question: Why is x86 'easy mode' for memory ordering?
options: x86 is slower by default behavior
x86 has Total Store Order (TSO) — stores appear in consistent order to all cores. acquire/release are free.
x86 has more cores for typical use cases with additional processing in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
x86 doesn't use atomics across all supported platforms across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: x86 has Total Store Order (TSO) — stores appear in consistent order to all cores. acquire/release are free.
questions.explanation: x86 TSO makes acquire/release semantically free. ARM/RISC-V are weakly ordered — require explicit barrier instructions.

questions.question: On x86-64, what is the difference between a `relaxed` load and an `acquire` load at the assembly level?
options: On x86-64, both compile to the same `mov` instruction — the TSO memory model already provides acquire semantics for all loads
acquire adds an MFENCE after the load; relaxed has no fence
relaxed uses a regular mov; acquire uses a locked instruction
acquire compiles to two mov instructions for redundancy
questions.correctAnswer: On x86-64, both compile to the same `mov` instruction — the TSO memory model already provides acquire semantics for all loads
questions.explanation: x86's Total Store Order (TSO) guarantees that all loads are acquire and all stores are release. A `mov` load from memory is already acquire. On ARM, relaxed = `ldr` (plain load), acquire = `ldar` (load-acquire, ~3 extra cycles).

questions.question: What does a store buffer do, and why does it cause memory ordering issues?
options: A store buffer holds pending writes in a core before they become visible to L1 cache, so a store from Core 0 may not be visible to Core 1 until the buffer drains
The store buffer holds recently read values for faster access
The store buffer merges multiple stores to the same address
The store buffer is a second-level cache for write-combining
questions.correctAnswer: A store buffer holds pending writes in a core before they become visible to L1 cache, so a store from Core 0 may not be visible to Core 1 until the buffer drains
questions.explanation: Each core has a store buffer (4-16 entries) where stores sit before being written to L1. Store-to-load forwarding makes the store visible to the same core instantly, but other cores see L1 only. A release fence drains the store buffer, ensuring prior stores are visible.

questions.question: In C++ atomics, what does `memory_order_seq_cst` guarantee that `memory_order_acquire`/`memory_order_release` does not?
options: seq_cst total orders all sequentially-consistent operations across all threads; acquire/release only guarantees happens-before between paired operations
seq_cst is faster on ARM than acquire/release
seq_cst prevents data races entirely; acquire/release does not
seq_cst works with non-atomic variables; acquire/release only works with atomics
questions.correctAnswer: seq_cst total orders all sequentially-consistent operations across all threads; acquire/release only guarantees happens-before between paired operations
questions.explanation: With acquire/release, thread A's store (release) and thread B's load (acquire) create a happens-before edge only if B observes A's store. Two stores with release semantics have no ordering relative to each other. seq_cst ensures a single total order visible to ALL threads, enforced via MFENCE on x86 or full dmb on ARM.

questions.question: What happens when you use `fetch_add(1, memory_order_relaxed)` on a counter shared between two threads?
options: The counter will eventually reach the correct total, but the order of intermediate values is undefined between threads
The operation is not atomic and may tear
The compiler deletes the operation as undefined behavior
The counter becomes thread-local and each thread sees its own value
questions.correctAnswer: The counter will eventually reach the correct total, but the order of intermediate values is undefined between threads
questions.explanation: fetch_add with relaxed is atomic (LOCK XADD on x86) — no intermediate value will be torn. Each thread will see the full 32-bit value. However, there's no ordering guarantee: thread A's fetch_add before a `data=42` store may be visible after `data=42` to thread B.

questions.question: What does CMPXCHG do?

questions.question: What is a condition variable used for?

questions.correctAnswer: Higher overhead due to tracking reader count and two lock modes
questions.explanation: RW locks have more internal state than simple mutexes.

questions.question: What is a recursive mutex?
options: A mutex that locks recursively infinitely
A mutex that the same thread can lock multiple times without deadlocking, with an ownership count
A mutex that creates child mutexes
questions.correctAnswer: A mutex that the same thread can lock multiple times without deadlocking, with an ownership count
questions.explanation: std::recursive_mutex allows re-locking by the owner.

questions.question: When would you use a recursive mutex?
options: When a function that already holds a lock needs to call another function that locks the same mutex
questions.correctAnswer: When a function that already holds a lock needs to call another function that locks the same mutex
questions.explanation: Recursive mutexes handle nested lock acquisition by the same thread.

questions.question: What is the performance cost of recursive mutexes?
options: They are faster than regular mutexes
Higher than non-recursive — the ownership check and count tracking add overhead
questions.correctAnswer: Higher than non-recursive — the ownership check and count tracking add overhead
questions.explanation: Recursive mutexes must track the owning thread and recursion count.

questions.question: What is a scoped_lock (C++17)?
options: A lock guard for a single mutex
A variadic RAII lock that can lock multiple mutexes simultaneously with deadlock avoidance
questions.correctAnswer: A variadic RAII lock that can lock multiple mutexes simultaneously with deadlock avoidance
questions.explanation: std::scoped_lock(m1, m2, m3) locks all atomically.

questions.question: What is the difference between lock_guard and scoped_lock?
options: lock_guard locks one mutex; scoped_lock (C++17) can lock multiple mutexes
questions.correctAnswer: lock_guard locks one mutex; scoped_lock (C++17) can lock multiple mutexes
questions.explanation: scoped_lock is variadic; lock_guard takes one.

questions.question: What is a barrier (latch or barrier)?
options: A synchronization point where multiple threads wait until all have arrived before proceeding
questions.correctAnswer: A synchronization point where multiple threads wait until all have arrived before proceeding
questions.explanation: C++20 std::barrier and std::latch support thread coordination.

questions.question: What is std::latch?
options: A single-use barrier — threads decrement a counter and block until it reaches zero
questions.correctAnswer: A single-use barrier — threads decrement a counter and block until it reaches zero
questions.explanation: std::latch (C++20) is a single-use synchronization point.

questions.question: What is std::barrier?
options: A reusable barrier — threads arrive and wait; after all arrive, the completion function runs and the cycle resets
questions.correctAnswer: A reusable barrier — threads arrive and wait; after all arrive, the completion function runs and the cycle resets
questions.explanation: std::barrier (C++20) recycles for phased synchronization.

questions.question: What is a lock-free data structure?
options: A structure that guarantees progress without locks, using atomic operations (especially CAS)
questions.correctAnswer: A structure that guarantees progress without locks, using atomic operations (especially CAS)
questions.explanation: Lock-free: at least one thread always makes progress.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of lock-free structures?
options: No kernel involvement — no context switches or syscalls, even under contention
questions.correctAnswer: No kernel involvement — no context switches or syscalls, even under contention
questions.explanation: Lock-free structures avoid kernel-mediated blocking.

questions.question: What is an RCU (Read-Copy-Update)?
options: A synchronization mechanism where readers proceed without locks, and writers create new versions then atomically publish
questions.correctAnswer: A synchronization mechanism where readers proceed without locks, and writers create new versions then atomically publish
questions.explanation: RCU: readers are never blocked; writers copy and update atomically.

questions.question: When is RCU most appropriate?
options: Read-mostly workloads where reads must be fast and wait-free
questions.correctAnswer: Read-mostly workloads where reads must be fast and wait-free
questions.explanation: RCU optimizes for read-dominant scenarios.

questions.question: What is the key challenge with RCU?
options: Memory reclamation — when is it safe to free old versions that readers might still be accessing?
questions.correctAnswer: Memory reclamation — when is it safe to free old versions that readers might still be accessing?
questions.explanation: RCU must wait for all readers to finish before reclaiming.

questions.question: What is a seqlock?
options: A lock that uses a sequence counter and allows readers to detect concurrent writes by retrying
questions.correctAnswer: A lock that uses a sequence counter and allows readers to detect concurrent writes by retrying
questions.explanation: Seqlock: readers read without blocking but check for concurrent writes.

questions.question: When is a seqlock appropriate?
options: When writes are rare and must be fast, and readers can tolerate occasional retries
questions.correctAnswer: When writes are rare and must be fast, and readers can tolerate occasional retries
questions.explanation: Seqlock favors writers; readers may retry.

questions.question: What is a double-checked locking pattern?
options: Checking a condition, then locking, then checking again — commonly used for lazy initialization
questions.correctAnswer: Checking a condition, then locking, then checking again — commonly used for lazy initialization
questions.explanation: Double-checked locking requires atomic/ordered reads.

questions.question: Why is naive double-checked locking broken?
options: Without proper memory ordering, the initialization write can be reordered after the flag write — another thread sees a partially constructed object
questions.correctAnswer: Without proper memory ordering, the initialization write can be reordered after the flag write — another thread sees a partially constructed object
questions.explanation: Classic DCLP issue: requires memory barriers or std::call_once.

questions.question: What C++ feature safely implements one-time initialization?
questions.correctAnswer: std::call_once (or std::once_flag)
questions.explanation: std::call_once and std::once_flag ensure correct one-time initialization.

questions.question: What is a pthread_once equivalent?
questions.correctAnswer: std::call_once
questions.explanation: C++11's std::call_once is the modern equivalent.

questions.question: What is a counting semaphore used for?
options: Controlling access to a pool of N identical resources
questions.correctAnswer: Controlling access to a pool of N identical resources
questions.explanation: A semaphore tracks N available units.

questions.question: What is a bounded queue for producer-consumer?
options: A fixed-size FIFO where producers wait on full and consumers wait on empty
questions.correctAnswer: A fixed-size FIFO where producers wait on full and consumers wait on empty
questions.explanation: Bounded queue uses condition variables or semaphores.

questions.question: What pattern does the bounded queue use?
options: Producer-consumer with condition variables: wait when full/empty, notify on insert/remove
questions.correctAnswer: Producer-consumer with condition variables: wait when full/empty, notify on insert/remove
questions.explanation: Classic producer-consumer synchronization pattern.

questions.question: What is a work queue?
options: A queue for work items processed by worker threads
questions.correctAnswer: A queue for work items processed by worker threads
questions.explanation: Work queue: submitted tasks consumed by pool threads.

questions.question: What synchronization does the work queue typically use?
options: A mutex + condition variable pair, or a lock-free MPMC queue
questions.correctAnswer: A mutex + condition variable pair, or a lock-free MPMC queue
questions.explanation: Work queues need thread-safe push/pop.

questions.question: What is a 'lock-free' vs 'wait-free' structure at the pattern level?
options: Lock-free: CAS retry loops. Wait-free: bounded steps, no loops
questions.correctAnswer: Lock-free: CAS retry loops. Wait-free: bounded steps, no loops
questions.explanation: Lock-free retries on CAS failure; wait-free bounds steps.

questions.question: What is 'double-checked locking'?
options: A pattern for lazy initialization: check without lock, lock, check again, then initialize
Checking a condition with two threads
questions.correctAnswer: A pattern for lazy initialization: check without lock, lock, check again, then initialize
questions.explanation: DCLP: check → lock → check again → init.

questions.question: How do you implement double-checked locking correctly in C++?
options: Using std::atomic with acquire/release ordering, or std::call_once
questions.correctAnswer: Using std::atomic with acquire/release ordering, or std::call_once
questions.explanation: DCLP requires atomics for ordering.

questions.question: What is a 'monitor' pattern?
options: A synchronized collection of procedures with mutual exclusion and condition variables
questions.correctAnswer: A synchronized collection of procedures with mutual exclusion and condition variables
questions.explanation: Monitor: mutex + CV + guarded operations.

questions.question: How does a monitor differ from a mutex?
options: Monitor includes condition variables for thread coordination; mutex provides only exclusion
Monitor is a type of mutex
questions.correctAnswer: Monitor includes condition variables for thread coordination; mutex provides only exclusion
questions.explanation: Monitor = mutex + CV + operations.

questions.question: What is 'stride scheduling'?
options: Each thread gets a stride proportional to its weight; the thread with smallest pass runs next
questions.correctAnswer: Each thread gets a stride proportional to its weight; the thread with smallest pass runs next
questions.explanation: Stride: deterministic proportional-share scheduling.

questions.question: What is a 'read-copy-update' (RCU) pattern?
options: Readers read without locks; writers copy, modify, and atomically update
questions.correctAnswer: Readers read without locks; writers copy, modify, and atomically update
questions.explanation: RCU: wait-free reads, copy-and-swap writes.

questions.question: What is 'hazard pointer' reclamation?
options: Each thread announces which pointers it is accessing; reclaimers check announcements before freeing
questions.correctAnswer: Each thread announces which pointers it is accessing; reclaimers check announcements before freeing
questions.explanation: Hazard pointers: announce in-use pointers.

questions.question: What is the key challenge of lock-free linked list deletion?
options: Ensuring concurrent inserts don't corrupt the list when a node is removed
questions.correctAnswer: Ensuring concurrent inserts don't corrupt the list when a node is removed
questions.explanation: Concurrent deletion: mark node as logically removed first.

questions.question: What is 'logical removal' in lock-free lists?
options: Marking a node as deleted (e.g., via tagged pointer) before physically unlinking it
questions.correctAnswer: Marking a node as deleted (e.g., via tagged pointer) before physically unlinking it
questions.explanation: Logical removal: mark, then physical unlink.

questions.question: What is a 'lock-free skip list'?
options: A probabilistic balanced data structure with multiple layers of linked lists, using CAS for concurrent inserts and deletes
questions.correctAnswer: A probabilistic balanced data structure with multiple layers of linked lists, using CAS for concurrent inserts and deletes
questions.explanation: Skip list: multi-level linked list, lock-free insert/delete via CAS.

questions.question: What is 'sequential lock' (seqlock)?
options: A lock with a sequence counter; readers read without blocking but check if a write occurred and retry if so
questions.correctAnswer: A lock with a sequence counter; readers read without blocking but check if a write occurred and retry if so
questions.explanation: Seqlock: optimistic reading, retry on write conflict.

questions.question: When would you use a seqlock?
options: When data is write-frequently and readers can tolerate retries (e.g., system time)
questions.correctAnswer: When data is write-frequently and readers can tolerate retries (e.g., system time)
questions.explanation: Seqlock: frequent writes, readers retry.

questions.question: What does std::mutex provide?
options: Mutual exclusion — lock() blocks until available, unlock() releases
Atomic operations across all supported platforms with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: Mutual exclusion — lock() blocks until available, unlock() releases
questions.explanation: mutex = basic mutual exclusion. Use lock_guard or unique_lock for RAII. scoped_lock for multiple mutexes.

questions.question: What does std::atomic<T> provide?
options: Mutual exclusion using standard engine approaches in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Lock-free atomic operations — indivisible read-modify-write with configurable memory ordering
Thread creation for typical use cases with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Lock-free atomic operations — indivisible read-modify-write with configurable memory ordering
questions.explanation: atomic is lock-free for int, pointer, bool on all modern platforms. Use for counters, flags, lock-free structures.

questions.question: What does std::jthread (C++20) add over std::thread?
options: Auto-join on destruction and cooperative cancellation via stop_token
Faster creation within the current scope in standard configurationsxx
More priority levels in most implementations within the standard framework
questions.correctAnswer: Auto-join on destruction and cooperative cancellation via stop_token
questions.explanation: jthread joins automatically in destructor (no terminate()). stop_source/stop_token enable cooperative cancellation.

questions.question: What is the recommended way to pass data between threads?
options: Global variables under normal conditions within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
std::promise/std::future for one-shot; queue + mutex + condition_variable for streaming
Raw pointers through the built-in API
Shared memory as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: std::promise/std::future for one-shot; queue + mutex + condition_variable for streaming
questions.explanation: future/promise for single value transfer. Producer-consumer queue for continuous data flow. Avoid raw shared state.

questions.question: How do you prevent data races on shared data?
options: Ignore them within the current scope
Use std::mutex + lock_guard for mutual exclusion, OR std::atomic for simple types, OR lock-free structures
Use volatile across all supported platforms with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Use more threads under normal conditions in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Use std::mutex + lock_guard for mutual exclusion, OR std::atomic for simple types, OR lock-free structures
questions.explanation: Three strategies: mutex (general, blocks), atomic (lock-free, simple types only), lock-free (complex, hard to write correctly).

questions.question: Data race cause:
questions.correctAnswer: Two cores' load-modify-store interleaved
questions.explanation: Use mutex or atomics

questions.question: Deadlock condition:
options: Circular wait: each waits for the other's lock
questions.correctAnswer: Circular wait: each waits for the other's lock
questions.explanation: All 4 Coffman conditions needed

questions.question: Livelock:
questions.correctAnswer: Cores actively executing but yielding
questions.explanation: Burning CPU, no progress

questions.question: Starvation:
options: Thread never gets resource, others make progress
questions.correctAnswer: Thread never gets resource, others make progress
questions.explanation: System makes progress without it

questions.question: False sharing:
options: Two cores write different bytes on same 64B line
questions.correctAnswer: Two cores write different bytes on same 64B line
questions.explanation: MESI ping-pong

questions.question: Spurious wakeup:
questions.correctAnswer: cv.wait returns without notify
questions.explanation: Always use cv.wait(lock, predicate)

questions.question: Short CS solution:
questions.correctAnswer: Spinlock
questions.explanation: For <1µs critical sections

questions.question: Reader-writer solution:
questions.correctAnswer: std::shared_mutex / FRWLock
questions.explanation: Multiple readers allowed

questions.question: Relaxed ordering bug:
options: Consumer sees stale data from store buffer
questions.correctAnswer: Consumer sees stale data from store buffer
questions.explanation: Use acquire/release ordering

questions.question: What are the three mechanical operations that dominate HDD latency?
questions.correctAnswer: Seek (move head to track), rotational latency (wait for sector to spin under head), and transfer (read data)
questions.explanation: Seek ~8-9ms, rotational ~4ms (7200 RPM), transfer ~16µs for 4KB. 99.9% of latency is mechanical.

questions.question: Why is sequential access 100-750x faster than random access on HDD?
options: Different platters as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Sequential: head stays on track, sectors stream by. Random: head must physically move for every 4KB
Sequential uses cache through the built-in API
Random accesses smaller sectors by default behavior in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Sequential: head stays on track, sectors stream by. Random: head must physically move for every 4KB
questions.explanation: Sequential: 250 MB/s. Random 4KB: ~0.33 MB/s (~83 IOPS × 4KB). Ratio ~750:1. Entirely due to mechanical seek+rotation.

questions.question: What does 'Advanced Format' / 4K native sectors mean?
options: Faster drives for typical use cases with additional processing with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Physical sector size is 4096 bytes, not 512. Logical may still be 512 for backward compat — causes read-modify-write
SSD compatibility through the built-in API in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Physical sector size is 4096 bytes, not 512. Logical may still be 512 for backward compat — causes read-modify-write
questions.explanation: 512e drives: logical 512, physical 4096. A 512-byte write triggers RMW of full 4KB sector. Align partitions to 4KB.

questions.question: What is the areal density of modern HDDs?
options: Bytes per platter for typical use cases for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
How tightly bits are packed on the platter surface — higher density = more data per rotation = higher sequential throughput
Cache size through the built-in API with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: How tightly bits are packed on the platter surface — higher density = more data per rotation = higher sequential throughput
questions.explanation: Higher areal density = more bits pass under the head per rotation. Primary driver of HDD throughput improvements.

questions.question: Why is append-only writing fast on HDD?
options: Different platter via the core framework
Head stays at end of file — no seek needed. In-place update requires seeking to middle: 8ms+ vs microseconds
Append uses cache in most implementations in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
It's not faster by default behavior with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Head stays at end of file — no seek needed. In-place update requires seeking to middle: 8ms+ vs microseconds
questions.explanation: Append = sequential write at current position. In-place = seek + rotational latency. Difference: 100-1000x.

questions.question: What physically stores a single bit on a hard disk drive?
options: A capacitor holding or not holding charge
A magnetic region magnetized in one of two directions
A floating gate transistor with trapped electrons
A series of microscopic pits in an aluminum substrate
questions.correctAnswer: A magnetic region magnetized in one of two directions
questions.explanation: HDDs store data as magnetic polarity changes on a thin film of magnetic material (cobalt-platinum-chromium alloy) on the platter surface.

questions.question: What is the typical RPM range of consumer hard disk drives?
questions.explanation: Consumer HDDs operate at 5,400-7,200 RPM. Enterprise drives use 10,000-15,000 RPM.

questions.question: How far does the read/write head fly above the platter surface in a modern HDD?
questions.correctAnswer: Approximately 3 nanometers
questions.explanation: The head flies on a cushion of air at roughly 3 nanometers. A human hair is ~80,000 nanometers thick.

questions.question: What are the three sequential operations that make up every HDD access?
questions.correctAnswer: Seek, rotational latency, and transfer
questions.explanation: Every HDD access requires: 1) Seek — moving the arm to the right track, 2) Rotational latency — waiting for the right sector to spin under the head, 3) Transfer — actually reading the data.

questions.question: What is the average seek time of a consumer 7,200 RPM hard drive?
questions.correctAnswer: 8-9 milliseconds
questions.explanation: Average seek time for a consumer 7,200 RPM drive is 8-9 ms, which is 40-45 million CPU cycles at 5 GHz.

questions.question: Why is the average rotational latency of a 7,200 RPM drive ~4.17 ms?
options: It's the time for a full rotation divided by 2
It's the time for one quarter rotation
It's derived from the seek time calculation
It's the time for one full rotation divided by 4
questions.correctAnswer: It's the time for a full rotation divided by 2
questions.explanation: A 7,200 RPM drive rotates once every 8.33 ms. On average, you wait half a rotation (4.17 ms) for the correct sector to spin under the head.

questions.question: What happens during a head crash?
options: The drive safely parks the heads and powers down
The read/write head touches the platter, gouging it and destroying data
The voice coil motor burns out from excessive heat
The platters stop spinning and data becomes temporarily unreadable
questions.correctAnswer: The read/write head touches the platter, gouging it and destroying data
questions.explanation: If the head touches the platter (head crash), the platter is gouged, data destroyed, and the drive is dead. The head flies at just 3 nm above the surface.

questions.question: At 7,200 RPM, what is the time for one full rotation of the platter?
questions.correctAnswer: 8.33 milliseconds
questions.explanation: 7,200 RPM = 7,200 ÷ 60 = 120 rotations per second. One rotation = 1/120 = 8.33 ms.

questions.question: What is the approximate sequential read speed of a modern consumer HDD?
questions.explanation: Modern consumer HDDs achieve 150-280 MB/s sequential read speeds. SSDs are much faster at 3,500-7,000 MB/s.

questions.question: How much faster is sequential access compared to random access on an HDD?
questions.correctAnswer: About 750x faster
questions.explanation: The note shows sequential 4K reads at 250 MB/s vs random 4K reads at ~0.33 MB/s - a ratio of approximately 750:1.

questions.question: What is the IOPS (I/O operations per second) for random 4K reads on a typical HDD?
questions.correctAnswer: Approximately 83 IOPS
questions.explanation: At 12 ms per random I/O, you get 1 ÷ 0.012 = ~83 random reads per second = ~330 KB/s.

questions.question: What is a sector on a hard disk?
options: A single byte of data on the platter
A small arc of a track — the minimum readable unit
A group of tracks on the platter surface
questions.correctAnswer: A small arc of a track — the minimum readable unit
questions.explanation: A sector is a small arc of the track. Modern drives use 4,096-byte (4K) sectors (Advanced Format), while legacy drives use 512-byte sectors.

questions.question: What is the logical sector size typically set to for backward compatibility on modern drives?
questions.correctAnswer: 512 bytes
questions.explanation: The logical sector size is usually 512 bytes for backward compatibility, while the physical sector size is 4,096 bytes on all modern drives.

questions.question: What is the read-modify-write penalty on an HDD?
options: The drive spinning down and back up
The extra seek when reading large files
A 512-byte write to a 4K physical sector requiring the drive to read, modify, then write the full physical sector
The latency incurred by switching between read and write modes
questions.correctAnswer: A 512-byte write to a 4K physical sector requiring the drive to read, modify, then write the full physical sector
questions.explanation: When logical = 512 and physical = 4,096, a 512-byte write requires reading the entire 4,096-byte sector, modifying the relevant portion, and writing it back.

questions.question: Why does partition alignment matter for modern HDDs?
options: Misaligned partitions reduce the drive's RPM
Misaligned partitions can cause read-modify-write operations to span two physical sectors
Misaligned partitions cannot be mounted by modern OSes
Alignment only matters for SSDs, not HDDs
questions.correctAnswer: Misaligned partitions can cause read-modify-write operations to span two physical sectors
questions.explanation: If partitions start on a boundary not a multiple of the physical sector size, every read-modify-write spans TWO physical sectors, doubling the overhead.

questions.question: What type of I/O pattern is most efficient for HDDs?
questions.correctAnswer: Sequential I/O
questions.explanation: Sequential access is ~750x faster because the head stays on the same track and sectors flow under it continuously. Random access requires a physical seek each time.

questions.question: Why do game engines pack assets into .pak, .resources, or .vpk archives?
options: To convert many small random reads into one large sequential read
To reduce the total amount of data stored on disk
questions.correctAnswer: To convert many small random reads into one large sequential read
questions.explanation: One big sequential read beats thousands of random reads by orders of magnitude. Loading 500,000 individual files could take 11.7 hours on HDD, vs ~40 seconds from an archive.

questions.question: Why is append-only writing fast on an HDD?
options: The write head is already hot from previous writes
The head is already positioned at the end of the file — no seek needed
Append-only writes bypass the platter and write to a buffer
The OS prioritizes append operations in the I/O scheduler
questions.correctAnswer: The head is already positioned at the end of the file — no seek needed
questions.explanation: Writing 4 KB at the end takes ~16 us transfer time only. An in-place update requires 12 ms seek + 16 us transfer = 750x slower.

questions.question: What does a free-fall sensor do in an HDD?
options: It spins the platters faster during a fall
It parks the heads when acceleration is detected
It locks the actuator arm in place
It increases the air bearing height during shock
questions.correctAnswer: It parks the heads when acceleration is detected
questions.explanation: A free-fall sensor parks the heads on acceleration to prevent a head crash when the device is dropped.

questions.question: What is the approximate transfer time for a single 4 KB sector at 250 MB/s?
questions.correctAnswer: Approximately 16 microseconds
questions.explanation: At 250 MB/s, a 4 KB sector takes 4,096 / 250,000,000 = ~16 us to transfer. This is negligible compared to the 12 ms mechanical latency.

questions.question: What technology fills modern high-capacity HDDs instead of air?
questions.correctAnswer: Helium
questions.explanation: Modern high-capacity HDDs are filled with inert helium instead of air. Helium reduces drag, allowing more platters and lower power consumption.

questions.question: What mechanism positions the actuator arm with nanometer precision?
questions.correctAnswer: A voice coil motor
questions.explanation: A voice coil motor (same electromagnetic principle as a speaker driver) positions the arm with nanometer precision.

questions.question: What does SMART stand for in the context of HDDs?
questions.correctAnswer: Self-Monitoring Analysis and Reporting Technology
questions.explanation: SMART predicts HDD failures by monitoring various drive attributes like reallocated sectors, temperature, and spin-up time.

questions.question: What is the total latency for one random 4 KB read on a 7,200 RPM HDD?
questions.correctAnswer: ~12 ms
questions.explanation: 8 ms (seek) + 4 ms (rotational) + 0.016 ms (transfer) ≈ 12 ms. 99.9% of the latency is mechanical.

questions.question: During that 12 ms random HDD read, how many instructions could a 5 GHz CPU execute?
questions.correctAnswer: Approximately 60 million instructions
questions.explanation: 5 GHz CPU executes 5 billion instructions per second. In 12 ms: 5,000,000,000 × 0.012 = 60 million instructions.

questions.question: What is the platter coating made of in modern HDDs?
questions.correctAnswer: A cobalt-platinum-chromium alloy
questions.explanation: The magnetic coating is a cobalt-platinum-chromium alloy just a few nanometers thick.

questions.question: What happens to the read/write head if it touches the platter?
questions.correctAnswer: It gouges the platter, destroying data and killing the drive (head crash)
questions.explanation: If the head touches the platter — a head crash — the platter is gouged, data destroyed, and the drive is dead.

questions.question: How does read-modify-write happen on a legacy OS writing 512 bytes to a modern 4K sector HDD?
questions.correctAnswer: The drive reads the 4K sector, modifies 512 bytes within it, and writes the full 4K back
acceptableAnswers: reads physical sector alters bytes writes back full sector
reads the 4K physical sector alters the 512 bytes and writes the 4K sector back
questions.explanation: The OS writes 512 bytes to a logical sector, but the drive must read the physical 4,096-byte sector, modify the relevant bytes, and write 4,096 bytes back.

questions.question: In the warehouse analogy, what does the mechanical arm represent?
options: The read/write head on the actuator arm
questions.correctAnswer: The read/write head on the actuator arm
questions.explanation: The warehouse analogy compares an HDD to a filing system: the mechanical arm (actuator) moves to the correct carousel row (seek), then waits for the folder to rotate (rotational latency).

questions.question: How many CPU cycles does a random HDD I/O cost at 5 GHz?
questions.correctAnswer: ~60 million cycles
questions.explanation: 12 ms × 5 GHz = 60 million CPU cycles wasted waiting for a single 4 KB random read on an HDD.

questions.question: True or False: Sequential reads on an HDD require a seek for every 4 KB chunk.
questions.correctAnswer: False
questions.explanation: Reading sequentially means the head stays on the same track, and sectors flow under the head one after another — you read 500+ sectors per rotation at zero additional seek cost.

questions.question: What is the defining characteristic of HDD performance?
questions.correctAnswer: A massive gap (~750:1) between sequential and random I/O throughput
acceptableAnswers: massive gap between sequential and random I/O
questions.explanation: The sequential:random ratio on HDD is ~750:1, making it the defining characteristic of HDD performance.

questions.question: What does ZBR (Zone Bit Recording) do?
options: Stores more sectors on outer tracks than inner tracks
Records data in a spiral pattern like a vinyl record
Uses bit-level recording instead of byte-level
Compresses data before writing to the platter
questions.correctAnswer: Stores more sectors on outer tracks than inner tracks
questions.explanation: Zone Bit Recording places more sectors on outer tracks (constant angular density), so sequential read throughput is higher on outer tracks.

questions.question: What is the average rotational latency at 15,000 RPM (enterprise)?
questions.correctAnswer: 2 milliseconds
questions.explanation: At 15,000 RPM, one rotation = 4 ms. Half rotation average = 2 ms.

questions.question: How many random 4KB reads per second can a 7,200 RPM HDD achieve?
questions.explanation: At 12 ms per random I/O: 1 ÷ 0.012 = ~83 IOPS.

questions.question: What benefit do game replays get from the append-only pattern?
options: They can be compressed more efficiently
Every frame's input data is appended to a log file — writes are essentially free
They can be played back at any speed
questions.correctAnswer: Every frame's input data is appended to a log file — writes are essentially free
questions.explanation: Replay systems append every frame's player inputs to a binary log file. The file grows sequentially — writes have essentially no seek overhead.

questions.question: Why is `fopen`+`fread` for 500,000 small texture files terrible for HDD performance?
options: File headers take too much disk space
Each file open requires a directory walk that becomes a random read
The OS runs out of file descriptors
Textures are too large for HDD buffers
questions.correctAnswer: Each file open requires a directory walk that becomes a random read
questions.explanation: 500,000 separate fopen + fread calls = 500,000 random disk accesses. On HDD that's 500,000 × ~84 ms = 42,000 seconds = 11.7 hours.

questions.question: True or False: The transfer portion of an HDD random 4 KB read accounts for ~50% of the total latency.
questions.correctAnswer: False
questions.explanation: Transfer time is ~0.016 ms out of ~12 ms total — only ~0.13% of the latency. 99.9% is mechanical (seek + rotational).

questions.question: What are the three platter materials mentioned?
questions.correctAnswer: Aluminum or glass coated with magnetic material
questions.explanation: Platters are circular discs of aluminum or glass, coated on both sides with a thin film of magnetic material.

questions.question: What does the actuator arm do in an HDD?
questions.correctAnswer: It swings the read/write heads across the platter surfaces in an arc to position them over the correct track
acceptableAnswers: positions the read/write heads over tracks
moves the heads to the correct track
questions.explanation: All heads are mounted on a common arm assembly that pivots like a record player tonearm, swinging the heads across the platter surfaces.

questions.question: Why can't you read or write less than one sector on an HDD?
questions.correctAnswer: Because the drive presents itself as a block device — the minimum unit of I/O is a sector
acceptableAnswers: minimum unit of I/O is a sector
sector is the minimum addressable unit
questions.explanation: A storage device presents itself as a block device with numbered sectors. You cannot read or write less than one sector.

questions.question: What is the page cache's role when reading 1 byte from an HDD?
questions.correctAnswer: The OS reads the entire 4 KB sector, returns the requested byte, and caches the other 4,095 bytes for future reads
acceptableAnswers: reads entire 4K sector returns 1 byte caches 4095 bytes
reads the full sector and caches the rest
questions.explanation: If you read 1 byte, the OS reads the entire 4,096-byte sector, extracts the byte, and caches the other 4,095 bytes. Your next read of adjacent data is free.

questions.question: Why is an append-only log ~3,000x faster than in-place updates on an HDD?
questions.correctAnswer: Append writes have zero seek cost (head at end of file); in-place updates require 12 ms seek + 16 us transfer
acceptableAnswers: in-place updates need a full seek cycle
questions.explanation: Writing at the end takes ~16 us (no seek). In-place update = 12 ms seek + 16 us = 750× slower for the same 4KB write.

questions.question: How do game engines like Unreal use archive files to optimize HDD loading?
questions.correctAnswer: The cooker packs thousands of individual asset files into .pak archives; the engine memory-maps the archive and accesses assets through its index
acceptableAnswers: packs files into .pak archives and memory-maps them
uses .pak archives with an internal index
cooks assets into a single sequential archive
questions.explanation: The cooker packs assets into .pak archives. At runtime, the engine memory-maps the .pak and accesses assets through the archive's internal index.

questions.question: Can you hear the actuator arm moving during HDD seeks?
questions.correctAnswer: Yes — the clicking/chattering sound is the actuator seeking
acceptableAnswers: yes the clicking sound is the actuator
questions.explanation: The arm literally moves and you can hear it — that clicking/chattering sound is the actuator seeking between tracks.

questions.question: What is a sector's relationship to a track on an HDD?
questions.correctAnswer: A track is a concentric circle on the platter; a sector is a small arc of that track
acceptableAnswers: sector is an arc of a track
track is a concentric circle sector is an arc of it
questions.explanation: Data is laid out in concentric circles (tracks). Sectors are small arcs of each track.

questions.question: What material are HDD platters typically made of?
questions.correctAnswer: Aluminum or glass
questions.explanation: Platters are circular discs of aluminum or glass, coated with magnetic material.

questions.question: How many bytes per second can a 5 GHz CPU process during one HDD random seek?
options: ~60 million instructions but nearly 0 I/O bytes
questions.correctAnswer: ~60 million instructions but nearly 0 I/O bytes
questions.explanation: During a 12 ms HDD seek, the CPU could execute ~60 million instructions, but it's blocked waiting for 4 KB of data.

questions.question: What keeps the HDD read/write head from touching the platter?
questions.correctAnswer: A cushion of air (air bearing)
questions.explanation: The head flies on a cushion of air at ~3 nm. If it touches — head crash.

questions.question: On a 7,200 RPM drive with 8 ms average seek, what is the total random read latency if transfer is 0.016 ms?
questions.correctAnswer: ~12 ms
questions.explanation: 8 ms seek + 4 ms rotational latency + 0.016 ms transfer = ~12 ms.

questions.question: True or False: On an HDD, a single random 4KB read takes longer than 60 million CPU cycles at 5 GHz.
questions.correctAnswer: True
questions.explanation: 12 ms × 5 GHz = 60 million cycles. True statement.

questions.question: What type of HDD failure involves the head physically contacting the platter?
questions.correctAnswer: Head crash
questions.explanation: A head crash occurs when the read/write head touches the platter, gouging it and destroying data.

questions.question: What is the sequential vs random IOPS ratio for a consumer HDD?
questions.explanation: The defining characteristic: ~250 MB/s sequential vs ~0.33 MB/s random = ~750:1 ratio.

questions.question: On an HDD, what percentage of a random 4KB read's total latency is spent on the transfer itself?
questions.explanation: Transfer = 0.016 ms out of 12 ms = ~0.13%. 99.9% is mechanical.

questions.question: What is a typical full-stroke seek time on a modern HDD?
questions.explanation: Full-stroke seek (arm moves from innermost to outermost track) can take 10-15 ms, compared to 8-9 ms average.

questions.question: How many tracks would you need to cross for a worst-case HDD seek?
questions.correctAnswer: The full platter radius — typically tens of thousands of tracks
acceptableAnswers: all tracks from inner to outer
questions.explanation: Worst case: seek from the innermost track to the outermost track, crossing all tracks on the platter surface.

questions.question: How does a save file system benefit from append-only patterns?
questions.correctAnswer: Serialize game state, compress, write atomically to a temp file, fsync, then rename over the old save file — a single sequential write
acceptableAnswers: serialize compress write temp fsync rename
questions.explanation: Rather than patching fields, games serialize full state to a buffer, compress it, and write as one atomic blob. This is a single sequential write.

questions.question: What is the distinguishing characteristic of HDD random I/O performance?
questions.correctAnswer: The massive ratio (~750:1) between sequential and random throughput
questions.explanation: Sequential 250 MB/s vs random ~0.33 MB/s — a 750:1 ratio that defines HDD behavior.

questions.question: What is the fundamental asymmetry of NAND flash?
questions.correctAnswer: Read/program per page (~4-16KB), erase per block (hundreds of pages). Cannot overwrite — must erase first.
acceptableAnswers: read program per page erase per block
read and write per page, erase per block
questions.explanation: Page = read/write unit (~25-100µs read, ~200-1500µs write). Block = erase unit (512-2048 pages, ~1.5-10ms).

questions.question: What is write amplification?
options: Writing data twice across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A 4KB user write can trigger much larger internal writes due to garbage collection — e.g., 4KB → 2MB
Data compression in most implementations in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Faster writes within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: A 4KB user write can trigger much larger internal writes due to garbage collection — e.g., 4KB → 2MB
questions.explanation: Overwrite → old page stale, write to new page. GC: copy valid pages, erase block. User 4KB may cause 2MB+ of NAND writes.

questions.question: What does the Flash Translation Layer (FTL) do?
options: Converts voltages via the core framework under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Maps logical block addresses (LBAs) to physical NAND pages — handles wear leveling, garbage collection, address indirection
Manages power through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Maps logical block addresses (LBAs) to physical NAND pages — handles wear leveling, garbage collection, address indirection
questions.explanation: FTL = SSD's firmware brain. LBA→physical mapping, wear leveling, GC, bad block management. OS sees a block device.

questions.question: Why does SLC endure 50-100x more writes than QLC?
options: Different materials within the current scope
SLC: 2 voltage levels (charged/not). QLC: 16 levels. Tiny voltage windows degrade faster with oxide wear
SLC is slower in most implementations with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
QLC uses error correction for typical use cases for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: SLC: 2 voltage levels (charged/not). QLC: 16 levels. Tiny voltage windows degrade faster with oxide wear
questions.explanation: SLC: 50K-100K P/E cycles. QLC: ~500-1000. More bits per cell = tighter voltage discrimination = faster wear-out.

questions.question: Why do SSDs slow down when nearly full?
options: Thermal throttling as defined in the specification under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Few free blocks → every write triggers GC → write amplification spikes → throughput drops from GB/s to 100s MB/s
Controller bug by default behavior under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Few free blocks → every write triggers GC → write amplification spikes → throughput drops from GB/s to 100s MB/s
questions.explanation: 90% full SSD: few pre-erased blocks. GC must reclaim before writes. Over-provisioning (7-28% hidden spare) mitigates.

questions.question: What physical component stores a single bit in NAND flash memory?
options: A magnetic domain in a cobalt alloy
A floating gate MOSFET with trapped electrons
A phase-change material that switches between crystalline and amorphous states
questions.correctAnswer: A floating gate MOSFET with trapped electrons
questions.explanation: Each bit is stored as the presence or absence of electrons trapped in a floating gate — a conductive layer surrounded by insulating oxide inside a MOSFET transistor.

questions.question: How are electrons made to enter the floating gate during a write (program) operation?
options: By applying heat to the NAND die
By shining ultraviolet light through the chip
Through direct electrical contact with the control gate
questions.correctAnswer: Through Fowler-Nordheim quantum tunneling
questions.explanation: A high voltage applied to the control gate causes electrons to quantum-tunnel through the thin tunnel oxide, where they become trapped in the floating gate.

questions.question: What is the minimum unit for read and write operations in NAND flash?
questions.correctAnswer: A page (4 KB to 16 KB)
questions.explanation: The read/write unit is a page (4 KB to 16 KB). The erase unit is a block (hundreds of pages).

questions.question: What is the minimum unit for erase operations in NAND flash?
questions.correctAnswer: A block (hundreds of pages)
questions.explanation: Erase must be done on an entire block at once — typically 512 to 2,048 pages. You cannot erase individual pages.

questions.question: What is write amplification in the context of SSDs?
questions.correctAnswer: The ratio of actual NAND writes to user-requested writes
acceptableAnswers: ratio of actual writes to requested writes
actual writes divided by user writes
questions.explanation: Write amplification is the ratio of actual NAND writes to user writes. A 4 KB user write can trigger 2 MB of internal writes.

questions.question: Why can't you overwrite a NAND page directly?
options: The controller lacks the necessary voltage levels
The page must be erased first, and erasing destroys the entire block containing that page
The floating gate degrades too quickly with direct overwrites
The NAND chip's write buffer is too small
questions.correctAnswer: The page must be erased first, and erasing destroys the entire block containing that page
questions.explanation: Once written, you cannot change even a single byte. The page must be erased first — and erasing destroys the entire block. Any modification requires copying all valid pages to a new block.

questions.question: What are the three main jobs of the Flash Translation Layer (FTL)?
questions.correctAnswer: Address mapping, garbage collection, and wear leveling
acceptableAnswers: address mapping garbage collection wear leveling
address translation garbage collection wear leveling
questions.explanation: The FTL has three jobs: 1) Address mapping (LBA→PBA), 2) Garbage collection (reclaiming stale pages), 3) Wear leveling (distributing writes evenly).

questions.question: What is the typical endurance (P/E cycles) of TLC NAND?
questions.explanation: TLC (Triple-Level Cell) stores 3 bits per cell with 8 voltage levels, rated for 1,000-3,000 P/E cycles. SLC has 50,000-100,000.

questions.question: What is the typical endurance (P/E cycles) of SLC NAND?
questions.explanation: SLC stores 1 bit per cell with just 2 voltage levels, tolerating 50,000-100,000 P/E cycles because the threshold is much fuzzier.

questions.question: Why does more bits per cell mean lower endurance?
options: More bits requires higher program voltages that damage the oxide faster
The voltage windows between levels are tiny — each P/E cycle degrades the oxide, making level distinction harder
Multi-level cells physically wear out faster due to more electron movement
TLC cells are physically smaller and have less oxide
questions.correctAnswer: The voltage windows between levels are tiny — each P/E cycle degrades the oxide, making level distinction harder
questions.explanation: To distinguish 8 levels (TLC), the voltage windows between levels are measured in millivolts. Each P/E cycle damages the tunnel oxide slightly, making precise charge levels harder to maintain.

questions.question: What is over-provisioning in an SSD?
questions.correctAnswer: Hidden NAND capacity reserved for garbage collection and wear leveling, not exposed to the OS
acceptableAnswers: extra NAND not exposed to OS for GC
questions.explanation: A '1 TB' SSD has ~1,024 GB raw NAND but exposes only 1,000 GB. The hidden ~24 GB is over-provisioning — a dedicated pool for garbage collection.

questions.question: What happens to SSD performance when the drive is 90% full?
options: Performance increases due to higher data density
Throughput drops significantly because every write triggers garbage collection
Write speed increases but read speed drops
questions.correctAnswer: Throughput drops significantly because every write triggers garbage collection
questions.explanation: When 90% full, very few pre-erased blocks exist. Every write triggers GC, potentially dropping throughput from 7,000 MB/s to ~500-1,500 MB/s.

questions.question: What does DMA (Direct Memory Access) do for storage I/O?
options: It deduplicates data before writing to disk
It allows devices to read/write RAM without the CPU copying every byte
It manages the queue of disk commands
It encrypts data in transit between the SSD and RAM
questions.correctAnswer: It allows devices to read/write RAM without the CPU copying every byte
questions.explanation: DMA lets the controller transfer data directly to/from RAM while the CPU runs other threads. The CPU is only involved at start (issuing command) and end (handling interrupt).

questions.question: What does the TRIM command do?
questions.correctAnswer: It tells the SSD which logical blocks are no longer in use so the controller can pre-erase them
acceptableAnswers: notifies SSD which blocks are free
informs SSD of deleted data for erase
questions.explanation: TRIM tells the SSD which blocks are actually free before garbage collection runs, reducing write amplification.

questions.question: What are the erase block sizes in typical NAND flash?
questions.correctAnswer: Typically 512 to 2,048 pages per block
questions.explanation: A block is the erase unit, typically containing 512 to 2,048 pages. A 2 MB block with 4 KB pages = 512 pages.

questions.question: How does the FTL handle write amplification during garbage collection?
options: It reads valid pages from a victim block, writes them to a new block, then erases the old block
It compacts data before writing to reduce amplification
It uses dedicated fast-erase blocks for GC
questions.correctAnswer: It reads valid pages from a victim block, writes them to a new block, then erases the old block
questions.explanation: GC selects a victim block with many stale pages, copies still-valid pages to a new block, then erases the victim block. The valid-page copies are 'extra' writes.

questions.question: What levels of NAND flash store 2 bits per cell?
questions.correctAnswer: MLC
questions.explanation: MLC stores 2 bits per cell with 4 voltage levels. TLC = 3 bits, QLC = 4 bits, SLC = 1 bit.

questions.question: How many voltage levels must QLC distinguish?
questions.correctAnswer: 16 voltage levels
questions.explanation: QLC stores 4 bits per cell = 2^4 = 16 distinct voltage levels. The tiny voltage windows make it difficult.

questions.question: What is the typical read latency for TLC NAND?
questions.explanation: NAND read latency is ~25-100 µs. Compare to DRAM (~100 ns) : NAND is ~250-1000× slower per access.

questions.question: What is the typical program (write) latency for TLC NAND?
questions.explanation: Writes take ~200-1,500 µs — much slower than reads (25-100 µs). Erase is the slowest at 1,500-10,000 µs.

questions.question: What is the typical erase latency for NAND flash?
questions.explanation: Erase is the slowest NAND operation at 1,500-10,000 µs (1.5-10 ms), operating on entire blocks.

questions.question: True or False: Wear leveling causes every NAND block to wear evenly.
questions.correctAnswer: True
questions.explanation: The FTL's wear leveling ensures all blocks wear evenly by mapping repeated writes to the same logical address to different physical blocks.

questions.question: How does the FTL handle a repeated write to the same logical block address (LBA)?
options: It overwrites the same physical page
It erases the block and rewrites
It writes to a new physical page and updates the mapping table, marking the old page as stale
It caches the write in a buffer until the page is erased
questions.correctAnswer: It writes to a new physical page and updates the mapping table, marking the old page as stale
questions.explanation: The FTL never modifies old pages in place. It writes new data to a fresh page, updates the mapping table, and marks the old page as stale.

questions.question: What enterprise over-provisioning range achieves optimal SSD performance?
questions.correctAnswer: 20-30% over-provisioning
questions.explanation: Enterprise SSDs often over-provision by 20-30%, while consumer drives typically have ~2.4% hidden over-provisioning.

questions.question: Can you achieve the same effect as over-provisioning on a consumer SSD?
questions.correctAnswer: Yes — simply leave 10-20% of the SSD unpartitioned or as unallocated space
questions.explanation: Leaving 10-20% unpartitioned gives the controller extra NAND blocks to use as spare area for garbage collection.

questions.question: What is a NAND die?
options: An independent semiconductor piece — an SSD has multiple dies working in parallel
A single block within a NAND chip
The firmware running on the SSD controller
questions.correctAnswer: An independent semiconductor piece — an SSD has multiple dies working in parallel
questions.explanation: A NAND chip contains multiple dies, each is an independent semiconductor piece. An SSD has multiple dies that can operate in parallel.

questions.question: What causes the 'write amplification nightmare' when an SSD writes 512 bytes to a logical 512-byte sector on a 4K physical sector?
options: The SSD must erase the entire block
The SSD must read the entire 4K block, modify 512 bytes, erase the block, and write the full 4K back
The SSD simply overwrites the 512 bytes directly
The SSD splits the 512-byte write across two NAND pages
questions.correctAnswer: The SSD must read the entire 4K block, modify 512 bytes, erase the block, and write the full 4K back
questions.explanation: For SSDs, a misaligned write requires reading the entire physical block, modifying, erasing, and rewriting — massive write amplification.

questions.question: What is the hierarchy of NAND flash organization from largest to smallest?
questions.correctAnswer: NAND Chip → Die → Plane → Block → Page
acceptableAnswers: NAND Chip, Die, Plane, Block, Page
chip die plane block and page
questions.explanation: The hierarchy: NAND Chip → Die → Plane → Block (erase unit) → Page (read/write unit).

questions.question: Why does an SSD slow down when you write to it while it's nearly full?
questions.correctAnswer: There are very few free, pre-erased blocks, so every write triggers garbage collection
acceptableAnswers: no free blocks so GC needed for every write
few free erased blocks triggering GC
questions.explanation: When the drive is 90% full, few free pre-erased blocks exist. Every write triggers garbage collection, potentially dropping throughput from 7,000 MB/s to ~500-1,500 MB/s.

questions.question: How does the FTL's address mapping work?
questions.correctAnswer: It maintains an indirection table mapping Logical Block Addresses (LBAs) to physical NAND locations (die, plane, block, page)
acceptableAnswers: maps LBA to physical NAND location
logical to physical address translation table
LBA to PBA mapping via indirection table
questions.explanation: The FTL maps LBA 42 → 'physical NAND die 2, plane 0, block 15, page 23.' When you overwrite, it writes to a fresh page and updates the mapping.

questions.question: What is the read latency of an SSD's NAND compared to DRAM?
options: NAND is faster than DRAM at reads
questions.correctAnswer: NAND is ~250-1000x slower
questions.explanation: NAND read: ~25-100 µs. DRAM read: ~100 ns. NAND is 250-1000x slower per access.

questions.question: What is the 'spare area' in a NAND page used for?
questions.correctAnswer: ECC bytes, bad block markers, and metadata
acceptableAnswers: ECC bytes and bad block markers
questions.explanation: Each NAND page has a spare area containing ECC (Error Correction Code) bytes, bad block markers, and metadata.

questions.question: Why doesn't `read()` burn CPU cycles while waiting for SSD data?
options: The CPU directly reads from the SSD's memory
DMA transfers data in the background while the scheduler switches to another thread
The SSD has its own CPU that handles all reads
The hardware prefetcher handles all I/O
questions.correctAnswer: DMA transfers data in the background while the scheduler switches to another thread
questions.explanation: The scheduler switches to another thread while DMA transfers data in the background. The CPU is only involved at start (command) and end (interrupt).

questions.question: How many NAND dies are typically inside an SSD?
questions.correctAnswer: Multiple — an SSD has several NAND dies that can operate in parallel for higher throughput
questions.explanation: An SSD has multiple NAND dies that can operate independently. With sequential reads, the controller pipelines reads across all dies simultaneously.

questions.question: What happens to the LBA-to-PBA mapping when you overwrite the same LBA 100 times?
questions.correctAnswer: The FTL maps each write to a different physical page, updating the table each time. The old pages become stale
acceptableAnswers: each write maps to different physical page
100 different physical pages used table updated each time
new page each time old ones go stale
questions.explanation: Each overwrite goes to a fresh physical page. The FTL updates the mapping table and marks old pages as stale. This is how wear leveling works.

questions.question: What does 'NAND' stand for in NAND flash?
questions.correctAnswer: Not AND (logic gate type)
questions.explanation: NAND flash gets its name from the NAND logic gate because its memory cell array resembles a NAND gate structure.

questions.question: After the FTL marks a page as stale, when is it actually erased?
options: During the next write to that LBA
During garbage collection when the entire block is reclaimed
Never — stale pages are permanently wasted
questions.correctAnswer: During garbage collection when the entire block is reclaimed
questions.explanation: Stale pages accumulate in blocks. GC selects victim blocks, copies valid pages, and erases the entire block.

questions.question: What happens if a NAND block has 20% valid pages and 80% stale during GC?
questions.correctAnswer: The 20% valid pages must be copied to a new block before the old block can be erased — causing write amplification
acceptableAnswers: write amplification from valid page copy
questions.explanation: GC copies the 20% valid pages to a new block, then erases the victim block. The copied pages are 'extra' writes the user didn't request.

questions.question: What would be the write amplification factor if every GC operation needs to copy 30% valid pages?
questions.explanation: For every 1 GB of user data written, the GC copies 0.3 GB of valid data. WA factor = 1 + 0.3 = 1.3×.

questions.question: Why do enterprise SSDs have higher write endurance than consumer SSDs?
options: They use SLC or pSLC caching and more over-provisioning
questions.correctAnswer: They use SLC or pSLC caching and more over-provisioning
questions.explanation: Enterprise SSDs use SLC mode or pSLC (pseudo-SLC) for higher endurance, plus 20-30% over-provisioning vs ~2.4% for consumer.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of NAND dies needed to benefit from parallel reads?
questions.explanation: With 2+ dies, the controller can pipeline reads: die 0 reads page 0, die 1 reads page 1 simultaneously.

questions.question: How does the erase-before-write property differ between HDDs and SSDs?
questions.correctAnswer: HDDs can overwrite sectors in place. SSDs must erase an entire block before any page in it can be rewritten
acceptableAnswers: HDD overwrite in-place SSD erase block first
HDD no erase needed SSD block erase
HDD in-place write SSD block erase first
questions.explanation: An HDD can overwrite any sector directly. An SSD must erase the entire block before rewriting any page in it — a fundamental difference.

questions.question: A 4 KB user write on a 2 MB block with 512 pages causes the FTL to rewrite how much data?
questions.correctAnswer: ~2 MB
questions.explanation: The FTL reads all 511 valid pages, modifies 1 page, writes all 512 pages to a new block = 2 MB written for 4 KB.

questions.question: What does the P/E cycle count determine for a NAND cell?
questions.correctAnswer: How many times it can be programmed and erased before the tunnel oxide degrades too much to reliably distinguish charge levels
acceptableAnswers: number of program/erase cycles before failure
questions.explanation: Each P/E cycle damages the tunnel oxide slightly. After enough cycles, the oxide can't reliably isolate the charge for precise level distinction.

questions.question: What is the voltage level difference for adjacent levels in TLC?
questions.correctAnswer: The 3V range divided by 8 levels = ~375 mV between adjacent levels
questions.explanation: TLC has 8 levels in a ~3V range: 3000 / 8 = 375 mV between levels. Compare to SLC's single ~3V threshold.

questions.question: What would be the voltage gap between adjacent levels in QLC?
questions.explanation: QLC has 16 levels in ~3V: 3000 / 16 = ~187.5 mV between adjacent levels. Much tighter than TLC's 375 mV.

questions.question: What is the FTL's address mapping table when an SSD has 4 KB pages and 1 TB capacity?
questions.correctAnswer: ~250 million entries
questions.explanation: 1 TB ÷ 4 KB = 268,435,456 ≈ 250 million entries. At 4 bytes each, the table is ~1 GB.

questions.question: What prevents the FTL's 250 million-entry LBA table from requiring a linear scan?
questions.correctAnswer: The LBA is used as a direct index (or hash) into the table — no linear scan needed
acceptableAnswers: LBA maps to table entry directly
questions.explanation: The FTL uses the LBA itself to index into the mapping table. LBA 42 maps to table slot 42 — O(1) lookup.

questions.question: NAND flash stores bits in:
questions.correctAnswer: Floating gate transistor
questions.explanation: Electrons trapped in oxide layer

questions.question: NAND erase unit:
questions.correctAnswer: Block (hundreds of pages)
questions.explanation: Erase-before-write constraint

questions.question: NAND read/write unit:
questions.correctAnswer: Page (4-16KB)
questions.explanation: Page granularity

questions.question: Write amplification:
options: SSD writes more internally than user requested
questions.correctAnswer: SSD writes more internally than user requested
questions.explanation: Critical performance factor

questions.question: FTL:
questions.correctAnswer: Flash Translation Layer (LBA→physical mapping)
questions.explanation: SSD controller firmware

questions.question: Wear leveling:
options: Distributes writes across all blocks evenly
questions.correctAnswer: Distributes writes across all blocks evenly
questions.explanation: Block rotation

questions.question: Garbage collection:
options: Copy valid pages, erase stale block
questions.correctAnswer: Copy valid pages, erase stale block
questions.explanation: Free block replenishment

questions.question: SLC P/E cycles:
questions.explanation: Single-level cell, most durable

questions.question: QLC P/E cycles:
questions.explanation: 16 voltage levels to distinguish

questions.question: Over-provisioning:
options: Hidden spare NAND for GC buffer
questions.correctAnswer: Hidden spare NAND for GC buffer
questions.explanation: 7-28% typical

questions.question: TLC P/E cycles:
questions.explanation: Mainstream consumer

questions.question: SSD slows when full because:
questions.correctAnswer: GC triggered on every write
questions.explanation: Free block scarcity

questions.question: Fowler-Nordheim tunneling:
options: Electrons tunnel through oxide into floating gate
questions.correctAnswer: Electrons tunnel through oxide into floating gate
questions.explanation: Program/erase mechanism

questions.question: 3D NAND:
questions.correctAnswer: Layers of cells stacked vertically
questions.explanation: Increases density without shrinking cell

questions.question: Why is NVMe faster than SATA for SSDs?
options: NVMe: 65,536 queues × 65,536 commands, direct PCIe connection. SATA: 1 queue × 32 commands, AHCI overhead
NVMe uses better flash through the built-in API with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
NVMe has cache under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: NVMe: 65,536 queues × 65,536 commands, direct PCIe connection. SATA: 1 queue × 32 commands, AHCI overhead
