questions.explanation: SATA designed for single-actuator HDDs (one thing at a time). NVMe designed for SSD parallelism (many NAND dies in parallel).

questions.question: What is the practical bandwidth of PCIe 4.0 ×4?
options: ~2 GB/s under normal conditions with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: ~7-8 GB/s (16 GT/s × 4 lanes × 128b/130b encoding)
questions.explanation: PCIe 4.0: 16 GT/s per lane, 128b/130b → ~1.97 GB/s per lane → ~7.88 GB/s for ×4. Real NVMe ~7 GB/s after overhead.

questions.question: What does the M.2 key tell you?
options: Storage capacity by default behavior under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
M-key = PCIe/NVMe (up to 4 lanes). B-key = SATA. B+M = either. The notch position encodes the protocol.
Speed rating as defined in the specification within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: M-key = PCIe/NVMe (up to 4 lanes). B-key = SATA. B+M = either. The notch position encodes the protocol.
questions.explanation: M.2 is just the connector form factor. Always check: NVMe M.2 (PCIe) vs SATA M.2 (600 MB/s bottleneck).

questions.question: How many PCIe lanes does a typical consumer CPU have?
options: ~20-28 (GPU takes 16, leaving 4-12 for NVMe SSDs and peripherals)
64 using standard engine approaches within the standard frameworkxxxxx
Unlimited using standard engine approaches within the standard frameworkxxx
questions.correctAnswer: ~20-28 (GPU takes 16, leaving 4-12 for NVMe SSDs and peripherals)
questions.explanation: Lane budget is fixed in silicon. Populating multiple M.2 slots may disable SATA ports or bifurcate GPU ×16 to ×8.

questions.question: What is the NVMe command set advantage over AHCI?
options: No difference as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
~50% fewer CPU cycles to set up an I/O operation. No legacy ATA command translation.
More commands via the core framework
Better error handling within the current scope with additional processing with the default
questions.correctAnswer: ~50% fewer CPU cycles to set up an I/O operation. No legacy ATA command translation.
questions.explanation: NVMe command set is streamlined. AHCI carries ATA legacy overhead. NVMe ≈ half the CPU cost per I/O submission.

questions.question: What is the maximum practical bandwidth of SATA III?
questions.correctAnswer: 600 MB/s
questions.explanation: SATA III has 6 Gbps raw bandwidth, but 8b/10b encoding reduces this to 600 MB/s practical maximum.

questions.question: How many command queues does SATA AHCI support, and what is the depth?
questions.correctAnswer: 1 queue with 32 entries
questions.explanation: AHCI was designed for HDDs (one thing at a time), supporting only 1 command queue × 32 entries max.

questions.question: How many queues does NVMe support?
questions.correctAnswer: 65,536 queues × 65,536 entries each
questions.explanation: NVMe was designed for SSDs with massive parallelism — 65,536 queues, each with up to 65,536 commands.

questions.question: What is the sequential read speed of a PCIe 4.0 ×4 NVMe SSD?
questions.explanation: NVMe PCIe 4.0 ×4 achieves ~7,000 MB/s sequential reads. SATA III tops out at 560 MB/s for comparison.

questions.question: What is the sequential read speed of a PCIe 5.0 ×4 NVMe SSD?
questions.explanation: PCIe 5.0 ×4: 32 GT/s per lane × 4 lanes × 128b/130b encoding ≈ 15.75 GB/s theoretical, ~14,000 MB/s real-world.

questions.question: What is the approximate random 4K IOPS of an NVMe PCIe 4.0 ×4 SSD?
questions.correctAnswer: ~1M-1.5M IOPS
questions.explanation: NVMe Gen4 random 4K IOPS: ~1M-1.5M. Compare to SATA SSD: ~100K and HDD: ~83.

questions.question: What does M.2 refer to?
questions.correctAnswer: A form factor / connector — the physical slot, not the protocol
acceptableAnswers: a form factor not a protocol
questions.explanation: M.2 is just the connector form factor. It can carry either SATA (~600 MB/s) or NVMe (up to 7,000 MB/s) — the slot looks identical.

questions.question: How can you tell if an M.2 drive is SATA or NVMe by looking at the connector?
options: SATA M.2 is longer than NVMe M.2
The key notch: M-key = NVMe, B-key = SATA, B+M-key = either
NVMe drives have a heatsink, SATA don't
You can't tell by looking at the connector
questions.correctAnswer: The key notch: M-key = NVMe, B-key = SATA, B+M-key = either
questions.explanation: The notch (key) on the connector tells you: M-key = PCIe/NVMe (up to 4 lanes), B-key = SATA, B+M-key = either.

questions.question: What is the PCIe lane math for PCIe 3.0?
questions.correctAnswer: 8 GT/s per lane with 128b/130b encoding → ~985 MB/s per lane → ~3.94 GB/s for 4 lanes
acceptableAnswers: 8 GT/s ~985 MB/s per lane ~3.94 GB/s for x4
8 GT/s per lane ~985 MB/s per lane
questions.explanation: PCIe 3.0: 8 GT/s with 128b/130b encoding → ~985 MB/s per lane → ~3.94 GB/s for ×4.

questions.question: What is the PCIe lane math for PCIe 4.0?
questions.correctAnswer: 16 GT/s per lane → ~1,969 MB/s per lane → ~7.88 GB/s for 4 lanes
acceptableAnswers: 16 GT/s ~1,969 MB/s per lane ~7.88 GB/s x4
16 GT/s per lane ~1.97 GB/s per lane
questions.explanation: PCIe 4.0: 16 GT/s → ~1,969 MB/s per lane → ~7.88 GB/s for ×4.

questions.question: How many PCIe lanes does a typical consumer CPU provide?
questions.correctAnswer: 20-28 lanes
questions.explanation: Consumer CPUs typically have 20-28 PCIe lanes. A GPU consumes 16, leaving 4-12 for SSDs and other peripherals.

questions.question: How does the NVMe command submission work without a syscall?
questions.correctAnswer: Via memory-mapped doorbell registers — writing to the BAR address triggers the NVMe controller via PCIe TLP
acceptableAnswers: doorbell register write via PCIe TLP
writing to BAR address triggers controller
questions.explanation: The submission queue tail doorbell is mapped into the driver's address space. Writing to it is a normal memory write (~50 ns) — no syscall or ring transition.

questions.question: What allows NVMe to scale to any number of CPU cores without lock contention?
options: A single shared lock optimized for concurrent access
Each core has its own submission queue — no shared state means no locks needed
NVMe uses hardware locks in the PCIe controller
The OS serializes all NVMe commands via a single kernel thread
questions.correctAnswer: Each core has its own submission queue — no shared state means no locks needed
questions.explanation: NVMe assigns one submission queue per CPU core. Each core writes to its own queue — no lock contention, perfect scalability.

questions.question: What is the per-I/O CPU involvement time on NVMe?
questions.correctAnswer: ~1 µs per I/O
questions.explanation: The CPU is involved for ~1 µs per I/O (submission + completion). The rest (~50-100 µs) is free for other work while the SSD handles the operation.

questions.question: What does CXL (Compute Express Link) enable?
questions.correctAnswer: Cache-coherent memory on the PCIe bus — CPU can use load/store instructions directly to CXL-attached memory
acceptableAnswers: cache coherence over PCIe for memory
questions.explanation: CXL adds cache coherence over PCIe. A CXL SSD isn't an I/O device anymore — it's NUMA memory. `*ptr = value` writes directly to the SSD.

questions.question: What is the random 4K read latency difference between SATA SSD and NVMe Gen4?
questions.correctAnswer: SATA SSD: ~120 µs, NVMe Gen4: ~60 µs
questions.explanation: SATA SSD latency ≈ 120 µs for 4K random reads. NVMe Gen4 ≈ 60 µs — half the latency.

questions.question: What is the AHCI protocol limitation that NVMe fixes?
questions.correctAnswer: AHCI has one command queue with 32 entries, creating a bottleneck for multi-core systems
questions.explanation: AHCI's single queue creates lock contention as cores compete. NVMe's 65,536 queues eliminate this entirely.

questions.question: Why does populating multiple M.2 slots sometimes disable SATA ports?
questions.correctAnswer: PCIe lanes are shared — the CPU's limited lanes must be distributed among all connected devices
acceptableAnswers: limited PCIe lanes must be shared
CPU has fixed lanes to allocate
questions.explanation: The CPU has a fixed number of PCIe lanes. Populating multiple M.2 slots requires repurposing lanes, potentially disabling SATA ports or reducing GPU lanes.

questions.question: How does an NVMe DMA transfer complete?
questions.correctAnswer: The NVMe controller signals an MSI-X interrupt; the kernel's ISR reads the Completion Queue entry
questions.explanation: The NVMe controller writes a Completion Queue Entry (CQE), then signals an MSI-X interrupt. The kernel ISR reads the CQ head doorbell to learn the I/O is done.

questions.question: What is the throughput difference between SATA SSD (AHCI) and NVMe PCIe 4.0 ×4?
options: They are identical under sequential loads
SATA is faster for random I/O
questions.correctAnswer: NVMe is ~12.5x faster
questions.explanation: SATA SSD max: 560 MB/s. NVMe Gen4 max: 7,000 MB/s. That's ~12.5× faster.

questions.question: What is the real-world throughput of PCIe as a percentage of theoretical maximum?
questions.correctAnswer: ~85-90% of theoretical maximum
questions.explanation: Real-world throughput is ~85-90% of theoretical maxima due to NVMe protocol overhead and NAND latency.

questions.question: What is the command lifecycle step order when NVMe reads from disk?
questions.correctAnswer: Allocate SQE → Fill SQE with opcode/LBA/PRP → Ring doorbell → Controller DMAs SQE → Controller translates LBA → NAND reads → Controller DMAs data to host → CQE → MSI-X interrupt
acceptableAnswers: SQE doorbell DMA translate NAND read DMA data CQE interrupt
allocate SQE ring doorbell controller DMAs SQE translate LBA NAND reads DMA data CQE interrupt
questions.explanation: The full lifecycle: allocate SQE, fill it, ring doorbell, controller DMAs SQE, translates LBA, reads NAND, DMAs data to host buffer, writes CQE, signals interrupt.

questions.question: What does Optane (3D XPoint) offer compared to NAND flash?
options: ~10 µs latency, ~100M P/E cycles — no erase-before-write
questions.correctAnswer: ~10 µs latency, ~100M P/E cycles — no erase-before-write
questions.explanation: Optane is phase-change memory with ~10 µs latency and ~100M P/E cycles. Unlike NAND, there's no erase-before-write penalty.

questions.question: What is ZNS (Zoned Namespaces) in NVMe 2.0?
questions.correctAnswer: SSD exposes erase zones to the host — eliminates FTL write amplification by letting the host manage data placement
questions.explanation: ZNS lets the host manage data placement in erase zones, eliminating the FTL's write amplification overhead.

questions.question: How does the NVMe driver ring the doorbell register?
questions.correctAnswer: By writing the queue tail index to a memory-mapped register in the device's BAR space — a simple memory write
acceptableAnswers: write tail to memory-mapped BAR register
questions.explanation: The driver writes the tail index to the doorbell register (e.g., SQ0TDBL). This is a memory write (~50 ns) detected by the PCIe root complex as a TLP to the NVMe device.

questions.question: Why is NVMe better for Unreal Engine asset loading?
questions.correctAnswer: NVMe's multi-queue architecture allows I/O from different systems (texture streaming, audio, mesh LOD) to have their own queues, eliminating lock contention
acceptableAnswers: separate queues for texture audio mesh
questions.explanation: UE5's FIoDispatcher leverages NVMe's multi-queue: I/O from texture streaming, audio, mesh LOD each gets its own queue, eliminating lock contention between threads.

questions.question: What encoding does SATA use and what is its overhead?
questions.correctAnswer: 8b/10b encoding with 20% overhead
questions.explanation: SATA uses 8b/10b encoding where each byte is encoded as 10 bits — 20% overhead. PCIe 3.0+ uses 128b/130b with only 1.5% overhead.

questions.question: What encoding does PCIe 3.0+ use and what is its overhead?
questions.correctAnswer: 128b/130b encoding with ~1.5% overhead
questions.explanation: PCIe 3.0+ uses 128b/130b encoding — only ~1.5% overhead compared to SATA's 20%.

questions.question: What PCIe generation provides ~2 GB/s per lane?
questions.correctAnswer: PCIe 4.0
questions.explanation: PCIe 4.0: 16 GT/s → ~1,969 MB/s (~2 GB/s) per lane. PCIe 3.0 is ~1 GB/s, PCIe 5.0 is ~4 GB/s per lane.

questions.question: What PCIe generation provides ~4 GB/s per lane?
questions.correctAnswer: PCIe 5.0
questions.explanation: PCIe 5.0: 32 GT/s → ~3,938 MB/s (~4 GB/s) per lane.

questions.question: What does NVMe's multi-queue design eliminate compared to AHCI?
questions.correctAnswer: Lock contention
questions.explanation: AHCI's single queue causes lock contention as multiple cores compete. NVMe assigns one queue per core — no shared state, no locks.

questions.question: How many PCIe lanes does a GPU typically consume?
questions.explanation: A GPU typically uses 16 PCIe lanes. Consumer CPUs have 20-28 lanes total.

questions.question: What happens when you plug a SATA M.2 drive into an NVMe-only M.2 slot?
options: It doesn't work — the slot is wired for PCIe, not SATA
questions.correctAnswer: It doesn't work — the slot is wired for PCIe, not SATA
questions.explanation: NVMe-only slots have PCIe wiring and M-key notches. SATA M.2 drives need SATA wiring and B-key notches. They're incompatible.

questions.question: What is the peak bandwidth of a single PCIe 5.0 lane?
questions.correctAnswer: ~4 GB/s (32 GT/s with 128b/130b encoding)
questions.explanation: PCIe 5.0: 32 GT/s × 128/130 encoding = ~3.94 GB/s per lane.

questions.question: What is the peak bandwidth of a single PCIe 4.0 lane?
questions.correctAnswer: ~2 GB/s (16 GT/s with 128b/130b encoding)
questions.explanation: PCIe 4.0: 16 GT/s × 128/130 = ~1,969 MB/s ≈ 2 GB/s per lane.

questions.question: What does BAR (Base Address Register) space allow in NVMe?
questions.correctAnswer: Memory-mapped access to the NVMe controller's registers, including doorbell registers — one memory write triggers PCIe TLP
questions.explanation: BAR space maps the NVMe controller's registers into the CPU's address space. Writing to the doorbell register triggers a PCIe TLP — no syscall needed.

questions.question: How does NVMe achieve millions of IOPS with minimal CPU involvement?
questions.correctAnswer: CPU involved ~1 µs per I/O (submission + completion). The NVMe controller DMAs SQE, translates address, issues NAND command, DMAs data, and signals completion — all in hardware
acceptableAnswers: ~1 us CPU per I/O rest hardware
questions.explanation: CPU: allocate SQE, fill it, ring doorbell (~1 µs). Controller: DMAs SQE, looks up LBA, reads NAND, DMAs data, writes CQE, signals interrupt. CPU free for 50-100 µs.

questions.question: What is the form factor and physical connector for NVMe SSDs?
questions.correctAnswer: M.2 or U.2
questions.explanation: NVMe SSDs use M.2 (gumstick) or U.2 (2.5-inch) form factors. M.2 is most common for consumers.

questions.question: What is Optane's latency compared to NAND and DRAM?
options: Faster than DRAM, slower than NAND
Slower than NAND, faster than HDD
~10 µs — between DRAM (~100 ns) and NAND (~80 µs)
questions.correctAnswer: ~10 µs — between DRAM (~100 ns) and NAND (~80 µs)
questions.explanation: Optane: ~10 µs latency. NAND: ~80 µs. DRAM: ~100 ns. Optane sits in the latency gap between DRAM and NAND.

questions.question: SATA III max bandwidth:
questions.correctAnswer: 600 MB/s
questions.explanation: 6 Gbps with 8b/10b encoding

questions.question: NVMe queue count:
questions.correctAnswer: 65,536 queues × 65,536 cmds
questions.explanation: Massive parallelism

questions.question: SATA AHCI queue:
questions.correctAnswer: 1 queue × 32 entries
questions.explanation: Designed for HDDs

questions.question: PCIe 4.0 x4 bandwidth:
questions.explanation: 16 GT/s × 4 lanes

questions.question: M.2 B-key:
questions.correctAnswer: SATA only
questions.explanation: B-key = SATA, M-key = NVMe

questions.question: M.2 M-key:
questions.correctAnswer: NVMe (up to 4 PCIe lanes)
questions.explanation: M-key = PCIe

questions.question: PCIe lanes per GPU:
questions.explanation: ×16 slot typical

questions.question: Consumer PCIe lane count:
questions.explanation: CPU-limited

questions.question: NVMe vs SATA latency:
questions.correctAnswer: NVMe lower (~60µs vs ~120µs)
questions.explanation: NVMe bypasses SATA controller

questions.question: PCIe 5.0 x4:
questions.explanation: 32 GT/s × 4 lanes

questions.question: 128b/130b encoding:
questions.correctAnswer: PCIe 3.0+ encoding, 1.56% overhead
questions.explanation: More efficient

questions.question: NVMe command CPU overhead:
questions.correctAnswer: ~half of AHCI
questions.explanation: Simplified command set

questions.question: What is an inode in Unix filesystems?
options: A filename as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A fixed-size data structure identified by number — stores file metadata (size, permissions, timestamps, block pointers)
A directory within the current scope
A type of file as defined in the specification in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A fixed-size data structure identified by number — stores file metadata (size, permissions, timestamps, block pointers)
questions.explanation: Every file/directory has one inode. Filename lives in directory entry, not the inode. Hard links = multiple directory entries → same inode.

questions.question: What does the page cache do?
options: Caches CPU pages as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Caches disk data in RAM — first read hits disk, subsequent reads hit cache (~100ns vs ~10ms HDD)
Manages virtual memory via the core framework in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Caches disk data in RAM — first read hits disk, subsequent reads hit cache (~100ns vs ~10ms HDD)
questions.explanation: Page cache = kernel-managed DRAM caching disk blocks. Read: check cache first. Write: write to cache, mark dirty, flush async.

questions.question: What does fsync() guarantee that write() alone does not?
options: Better performance within the current scope under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
write() goes to page cache only (volatile). fsync() forces dirty pages to durable storage before returning.
Error checking using standard engine approaches with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: write() goes to page cache only (volatile). fsync() forces dirty pages to durable storage before returning.
questions.explanation: write() is fast (microseconds) but data is only in RAM. Power loss = data loss. fsync() blocks until disk confirms write.

questions.question: How does journaling prevent filesystem corruption after a crash?
options: Backups via the core framework with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Write-ahead log: operations are journaled BEFORE modifying structures. On reboot, committed entries are replayed.
RAID across all supported platforms with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Write-ahead log: operations are journaled BEFORE modifying structures. On reboot, committed entries are replayed.
questions.explanation: Journal: write intent → commit → do work → mark done. Crash: replay committed entries. Inconsistent states become consistent.

questions.question: Why is 'free RAM' misleading on Linux?
options: It's not through the built-in API with additional processing with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Much of 'used' RAM is page cache — instantly evictable if an application needs it. Unused RAM IS wasted RAM.
Linux lies using standard engine approaches under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
It's a kernel bug via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: Much of 'used' RAM is page cache — instantly evictable if an application needs it. Unused RAM IS wasted RAM.
questions.explanation: Page cache = 'used' but available. Kernel will evict clean pages instantly. High free RAM = you have more RAM than your working set.

questions.question: What four things must a filesystem do?
questions.correctAnswer: Track which sectors belong to files, store metadata (size/permissions/timestamps), organize the directory hierarchy, and recover from crashes without corruption
acceptableAnswers: track sectors store metadata organize directories recover from crashes
sector tracking metadata storage directory organization crash recovery
questions.explanation: A filesystem is a specialized database that maps paths to sectors, tracks allocation, stores metadata, organizes directories, and handles crash recovery via journaling.

questions.question: What is an inode on Linux?
questions.correctAnswer: A fixed-size data structure identified by a unique inode number that stores file metadata and pointers to data blocks
acceptableAnswers: fixed-size structure storing file metadata and block pointers
data structure with file info and block pointers
questions.explanation: Every file/directory on Linux has one inode — a fixed-size structure storing type, size, ownership, permissions, timestamps, link count, and data block pointers.

questions.question: What size data do the 12 direct pointers in an ext4 inode cover?
questions.correctAnswer: 48 KB (12 × 4 KB data blocks)
questions.explanation: 12 direct pointers each point to a 4 KB data block, covering the first 48 KB without indirection. Small files are fast because their blocks are directly listed.

questions.question: What does the indirect pointer in an ext4 inode allow?
questions.correctAnswer: It points to a block of 1,024 pointers, each pointing to a 4 KB data block — covering 4 MB
acceptableAnswers: indirect block of pointers for 4 MB
questions.explanation: The indirect pointer points to a block of 1,024 pointers × 4 KB = 4 MB of additional data.

questions.question: What information does an inode NOT store?
questions.correctAnswer: The file's name
questions.explanation: The filename is NOT stored in the inode — it lives only in directory entries. This is why hard links can give multiple names to the same inode.

questions.question: How does NTFS store very small files (< ~700 bytes)?
questions.correctAnswer: Resident data — the data is stored directly inside the MFT record itself, consuming no separate data blocks
questions.explanation: If data fits in the MFT record (< ~700 bytes), NTFS stores it resident — right in the MFT. Reading the file = reading the MFT record, requiring no extra I/O.

questions.question: What is the MFT (Master File Table) in NTFS?
questions.correctAnswer: A file containing one record for every file and directory on the volume — each record is usually 1 KB
acceptableAnswers: file with one record per file/directory
questions.explanation: NTFS's MFT is a file containing one 1 KB record for every file and directory. The MFT is record #0 — it even describes itself.

questions.question: What is the purpose of journaling in a filesystem?
questions.correctAnswer: To prevent filesystem corruption across crashes by writing a write-ahead log of planned changes
acceptableAnswers: prevents corruption by logging changes first
journals planned operations before executing them
questions.explanation: Journaling writes planned operations to a write-ahead log BEFORE modifying the filesystem. On reboot after crash, committed entries are replayed.

questions.question: What does the COMMITTED marker in a journal entry mean?
questions.correctAnswer: The journal entry has been fully written to disk, meaning the described operations are guaranteed to have started and must be replayed on crash recovery
acceptableAnswers: entry fully written to disk must be replayed
journal entry is safe and needs redo
committed means operations must be applied
questions.explanation: COMMITTED means the journal entry was atomically written to disk. On recovery, committed-but-not-checkpointed entries are replayed.

questions.question: What does the CHECKPOINTED marker in a journal entry mean?
questions.correctAnswer: The actual filesystem operations are complete and on disk — the journal entry can be discarded
acceptableAnswers: operations done on disk can discard
questions.explanation: CHECKPOINTED means the real filesystem operations finished successfully. The journal entry is no longer needed and can be discarded.

questions.question: What is the ext4 default journaling mode on most Linux distros?
questions.correctAnswer: data=ordered (metadata + enforce data-before-metadata ordering)
questions.explanation: ext4 defaults to data=ordered on most distributions. It journals metadata but enforces that data blocks are written to disk before the metadata pointing to them.

questions.question: How many random reads does a cold-cache fopen() require on ext4 for a path like /Game/Maps/Level1.umap?
questions.correctAnswer: 7 random reads (3 directory inode reads + 3 directory data reads + 1 target inode read)
questions.explanation: Worst case: 3 directory inode reads + 3 directory data reads + 1 target inode read = 7 random reads. On HDD: 84 ms. On NVMe: 0.35 ms.

questions.question: What is the page cache?
questions.correctAnswer: A region of kernel-managed RAM that caches disk data — all read()/write() traffic passes through it
acceptableAnswers: kernel RAM cache for disk data
kernel-managed RAM that caches file data
questions.explanation: The page cache is kernel-managed RAM that caches disk data. Read data stays in the cache after your read() returns — subsequent reads are ~100 ns instead of disk latency.

questions.question: What is the latency of a page cache hit?
questions.correctAnswer: ~100 ns (RAM speed)
questions.explanation: A page cache hit returns data from RAM in ~100 ns, compared to ~10 ms (HDD) — a 100,000x difference.

questions.question: Why does Task Manager often show high 'in use' RAM even when apps aren't using much?
questions.correctAnswer: The page cache fills free RAM with cached disk data — it shows as 'in use' but is instantly evictable when an application needs it
acceptableAnswers: cached disk data counted as in use
kernel uses free RAM for disk cache
questions.explanation: Much of the 'in use' RAM is the page cache. The kernel instantly evicts clean cache pages if an application needs the memory. Unused RAM is wasted RAM.

questions.question: What is a dirty page in the page cache?
questions.correctAnswer: A page that has been modified in RAM but not yet written to disk
acceptableAnswers: modified but not yet on disk
questions.explanation: When you write to a file, data goes to the page cache first. The page is marked 'dirty' (modified, not yet on disk). write() returns immediately.

questions.question: Why does write() return almost instantly but the data isn't immediately on disk?
questions.correctAnswer: write() copies data to the page cache and marks it dirty — the actual disk write happens later via background writeback
acceptableAnswers: data goes to page cache writeback happens later
write to cache background flush to disk
questions.explanation: Data is copied from userspace to a page cache page frame, marked dirty, and write() returns. Background threads flush dirty pages to disk periodically.

questions.question: What does fsync() guarantee?
questions.correctAnswer: It forces all dirty pages for the given file descriptor to durable storage before returning
acceptableAnswers: forces data to disk before returning
flushes dirty pages to durable storage
questions.explanation: fsync() forces dirty pages to disk and returns ONLY when the disk confirms the write. Without it, a power failure loses data the user thought was saved.

questions.question: What is the default dirty writeback interval on Linux?
questions.correctAnswer: Every ~30 seconds (dirty_writeback_centisecs = 3000)
questions.explanation: Background writeback threads flush dirty pages every ~30 seconds by default, or earlier when dirty_background_ratio (10% of RAM) is exceeded.

questions.question: What happens when dirty pages exceed dirty_ratio (default 20% of RAM)?
questions.correctAnswer: Writes become synchronous — write() blocks until enough pages are flushed to disk
questions.explanation: When dirty pages exceed dirty_ratio (default 20%), write() blocks synchronously until enough dirty pages are flushed.

questions.question: What is read-ahead in the page cache?
questions.correctAnswer: The kernel detects sequential access and pre-loads upcoming pages before the application asks for them
questions.explanation: The kernel detects sequential patterns and asynchronously pre-loads upcoming pages. This turns sequential reads into a pipeline where I/O overlaps with your processing.

questions.question: What are the two levels of buffering between your code and disk?
questions.correctAnswer: The stdio buffer (userspace, per-FILE*) and the page cache (kernelspace)
acceptableAnswers: userspace stdio buffer and kernel page cache
FILE* buffer and kernel page cache
questions.explanation: The stdio buffer (userspace, 4-8 KB per FILE*) is flushed by fflush() or buffer-full. The page cache (kernelspace, dirty pages) is flushed by writeback or fsync().

questions.question: Why doesn't printf('Loading...') immediately appear on screen?
questions.correctAnswer: stdout is line-buffered — text without a newline stays in the stdio buffer until it fills or fflush() is called
acceptableAnswers: buffer doesn't flush until newline or full
questions.explanation: printf('Loading...') puts text in the stdio buffer. Without a newline, it stays there. Use fflush(stdout) or add a newline to flush immediately.

questions.question: What are the three stdio buffering modes?
questions.correctAnswer: _IONBF (unbuffered), _IOLBF (line-buffered), _IOFBF (fully-buffered)
acceptableAnswers: no buffering line buffering full buffering
questions.explanation: _IONBF: every call → immediate write(). _IOLBF: flush on newline or buffer full. _IOFBF: flush only when buffer is full (4-8 KB).

questions.question: Why do databases use direct I/O (O_DIRECT)?
questions.correctAnswer: They have their own buffer pool — the OS page cache would duplicate it, wasting RAM and potentially caching stale data
acceptableAnswers: they manage their own cache avoid duplication
custom buffer pool no page cache needed
questions.explanation: Databases manage a multi-GB application buffer pool with custom caching policies. O_DIRECT bypasses the page cache to avoid duplication and stale data issues.

questions.question: What alignment requirement does O_DIRECT have on Linux?
questions.correctAnswer: The buffer must be aligned to the disk's logical block size (usually 512 bytes)
questions.explanation: O_DIRECT buffers must be aligned to the disk's logical block size (usually 512 bytes). Unaligned buffers cause EINVAL errors.

questions.question: What is an inode number?
questions.correctAnswer: A unique number identifying each file/directory on a filesystem — used internally to locate the inode data structure
questions.explanation: Every file/directory on Linux has a unique inode number. The inode is the metadata structure storing everything about the file except its name.

questions.question: What is a directory in Unix filesystems?
questions.correctAnswer: A special file containing a list of (name, inode_number) pairs — it maps names to inodes
acceptableAnswers: file mapping names to inode numbers
list of name to inode mappings
questions.explanation: A directory is just a special file listing (name, inode_number) pairs. The filename lives only in this entry, not in the inode itself.

questions.question: What is the link count field in an inode?
questions.correctAnswer: How many directory entries (hard links) point to this inode — when it reaches 0, the data can be freed
acceptableAnswers: number of directory entries pointing to inode
number of references to this inode
questions.explanation: The link count tracks how many directory entries point to this inode. Deleting a file removes the entry and decrements the count. Only at 0 is the inode freed.

questions.question: How does ext4 handle files larger than triple-indirect pointer range?
questions.correctAnswer: ext4 uses extents (not individual block pointers) for large files — extents describe contiguous ranges of blocks
acceptableAnswers: uses extent trees instead of indirect blocks
questions.explanation: For large files, ext4 uses extents (block ranges) instead of individual block pointers. An extent can describe a huge contiguous range in a single entry.

questions.question: What happens on ext4 when the triple-indirect pointer is traversed?
questions.correctAnswer: It requires three levels of indirection: first block has pointers to blocks, those have pointers to blocks, those point to data — many extra disk reads
acceptableAnswers: blocks pointing to blocks pointing to blocks pointing to data
questions.explanation: Triple-indirect: a block of pointers to blocks of pointers to blocks of pointers to data blocks. Walking this requires many extra disk reads.

questions.question: What filesystems use Copy-on-Write (COW) instead of journaling?
questions.correctAnswer: btrfs and ZFS
questions.explanation: btrfs and ZFS use Copy-on-Write: never overwrite in-place, always write to new location, atomically update root pointer.

questions.question: How does read-ahead benefit sequential file access?
questions.correctAnswer: It pipelines I/O: the disk transfers data continuously while the application processes previously-read pages, achieving near-full disk bandwidth
acceptableAnswers: overlaps disk transfer with app processing
pre-loads pages while app processes earlier ones
questions.explanation: Read-ahead turns sequential access into a pipeline: the disk transfers data continuously while your application processes already-read pages, achieving near-full disk bandwidth.

questions.question: What type of buffering does stderr typically use?
questions.correctAnswer: Unbuffered (_IONBF) — every write goes directly to the terminal immediately
questions.explanation: stderr is typically unbuffered — every write goes directly to the terminal so error messages appear immediately, even during crashes.

questions.question: If stdout is redirected to a file, which buffering mode applies?
questions.correctAnswer: Fully-buffered (_IOFBF) — output only flushes when the buffer fills (4-8 KB)
questions.explanation: When stdout is directed to a file or pipe, it switches from line-buffered to fully-buffered. Output may not appear for a while — it's sitting in a 4 KB buffer.

questions.question: What does the double-indirect pointer in an ext4 inode cover?
questions.correctAnswer: 1,024² × 4 KB = 4 GB (a block of pointers to blocks of pointers to data)
questions.explanation: Double-indirect: 1,024 pointers to blocks, each block has 1,024 pointers to 4 KB data blocks = 4 GB.

questions.question: What does the triple-indirect pointer in an ext4 inode cover?
questions.explanation: Triple-indirect: 1,024³ × 4 KB = ~4 TB. This is how ext4 handles very large files.

questions.question: What is the I/O count difference for a warm-cache fopen() vs a cold-cache one?
questions.correctAnswer: Cold cache: ~7 I/Os. Warm cache: 0 I/Os (all inodes and directory blocks cached in the page cache)
questions.explanation: With a warm page cache, all inodes and directory blocks are already in RAM. fopen() requires zero disk I/O — it's all served from RAM.

questions.question: What Windows function is equivalent to fsync()?
questions.correctAnswer: FlushFileBuffers()
questions.explanation: On Windows, FlushFileBuffers() is the equivalent of fsync() — it forces dirty pages for a file to durable storage.

questions.question: What happens to page cache data when an application needs more memory?
options: Clean page cache pages are instantly evicted
questions.correctAnswer: Clean page cache pages are instantly evicted
questions.explanation: Clean cache pages are instantly evictable — the kernel drops them to give memory to the application. No writeback needed.

questions.question: What happens when dirty pages reach dirty_background_ratio (10% of RAM)?
options: Background writeback starts flushing dirty pages to disk
questions.correctAnswer: Background writeback starts flushing dirty pages to disk
questions.explanation: At dirty_background_ratio (10%), background writeback threads wake up and start flushing dirty pages. write() still returns immediately.

questions.question: What are the ext4 journaling modes?
questions.correctAnswer: writeback, ordered, and journal
questions.explanation: ext4 modes: data=writeback (metadata only), data=ordered (metadata + enforce data-before-metadata), data=journal (everything journaled).

questions.question: What does a 'data run' in NTFS describe?
questions.correctAnswer: An extent: (starting_cluster, cluster_count) pair describing where a file's data lives on disk
questions.explanation: For non-resident files, NTFS MFT records contain 'data runs' — extents listing (starting_cluster, cluster_count) pairs.

questions.question: In ext4, what type of file data is usually covered by the 12 direct pointers?
questions.correctAnswer: Small files — up to 48 KB
questions.explanation: 12 direct × 4 KB = 48 KB. Most files are small (source code, config), so direct pointers cover the common case quickly.

questions.question: How does the kernel detect sequential access for read-ahead?
questions.correctAnswer: It monitors access patterns — if a process reads pages in increasing order, the kernel assumes more sequential pages will be needed
questions.explanation: The kernel monitors access patterns. Reading page 0, 1, 2 triggers read-ahead: pages 3-6 are pre-loaded asynchronously.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the spare area in an NTFS or ext4 filesystem?
questions.correctAnswer: For different filesystems: ext4 uses reserved space; NTFS uses the MFT itself — different designs for the same goal of organizing data
questions.explanation: The 'spare area' question is more relevant to NAND pages (ECC, bad block markers). For filesystems: ext4 has reserved blocks for the superblock, NTFS uses the MFT.

questions.question: What happens on ext4 when the dirty writeback interval expires?
options: All dirty pages are immediately written
Background threads flush pages that have been dirty for the interval
questions.correctAnswer: Background threads flush pages that have been dirty for the interval
questions.explanation: The pdflush/background writeback threads wake every ~30 seconds and flush pages that have been dirty for a while. Not all dirty pages — only older ones.

questions.question: What is the capacity of a directory entry in terms of maximum filename length?
questions.correctAnswer: ext4: 255 bytes per filename. NTFS: 255 UTF-16 characters
questions.explanation: ext4 limits filenames to 255 bytes. NTFS limits to 255 UTF-16 characters (but full path = 32,767 characters).

questions.question: What hardware guarantee does fsync() rely on at the drive level?
questions.correctAnswer: The drive must flush its internal write cache to non-volatile media and confirm completion — the data is actually on the platter/NAND
acceptableAnswers: drive cache flush to persistent media
questions.explanation: fsync() waits for the drive to confirm that data is on non-volatile media, not just in the drive's volatile write cache.

questions.question: inode stores:
questions.correctAnswer: Metadata + data block pointers
questions.explanation: Excludes filename

questions.question: ext4 direct pointers (12):
options: Point to 4KB data blocks, max 48KB direct
questions.correctAnswer: Point to 4KB data blocks, max 48KB direct
questions.explanation: Small files are fast

questions.question: NTFS MFT:
options: Master File Table: one record per file
questions.correctAnswer: Master File Table: one record per file
questions.explanation: MFT record = 1KB

questions.question: Resident data (NTFS):
options: Small data stored inside MFT record
questions.correctAnswer: Small data stored inside MFT record
questions.explanation: < ~700 bytes

questions.question: Journaling:
questions.correctAnswer: Write-ahead log before metadata changes
questions.explanation: Survives crashes

questions.question: Page cache:
options: OS caches file data in unused RAM
questions.correctAnswer: OS caches file data in unused RAM
questions.explanation: Reads hit page cache first

questions.question: Dirty page:
options: Modified in cache, not written to disk
questions.correctAnswer: Modified in cache, not written to disk
questions.explanation: Written back by pdflush/kswapd

questions.question: fsync:
options: Flushes file data and metadata to disk
questions.correctAnswer: Flushes file data and metadata to disk
questions.explanation: Ensures durability

questions.question: Hard link:
questions.correctAnswer: Multiple directory entries → same inode
questions.explanation: Link count tracks references

questions.question: Symbolic link:
questions.correctAnswer: Separate file pointing to path
questions.explanation: Can cross filesystems

questions.question: VFS:
questions.correctAnswer: Virtual File System (abstracts filesystem types)
questions.explanation: Linux VFS layer

questions.question: fopen → read syscall →
options: Page cache → disk I/O if needed
questions.correctAnswer: Page cache → disk I/O if needed
questions.explanation: Reads check page cache first

questions.question: Block size vs fragment:
options: Block = alloc unit; Fragment = minimum unit
questions.correctAnswer: Block = alloc unit; Fragment = minimum unit
questions.explanation: ext4 can fragment blocks

questions.question: Extents (ext4):
options: Contiguous block ranges replace individual pointers
questions.correctAnswer: Contiguous block ranges replace individual pointers
questions.explanation: Better for large files

questions.question: O_DIRECT:
questions.correctAnswer: Bypasses page cache
questions.explanation: For databases or benchmarks

questions.question: readahead:
options: Speculatively reads more data than requested
questions.correctAnswer: Speculatively reads more data than requested
questions.explanation: Exploits spatial locality

questions.question: ext4 max file size:
questions.correctAnswer: ~16 TB
questions.explanation: Via extent tree

questions.question: NTFS max file size:
questions.correctAnswer: ~16 EB
questions.explanation: 64-bit filesystem

questions.question: btrfs:
questions.correctAnswer: Copy-on-write filesystem with snapshots
questions.explanation: Advanced features

questions.question: What physically stores a bit in DRAM?
options: A transistor within the current scope with additional processing
A capacitor holding or not holding charge (1T1C cell)
A magnetic domain under normal conditions
A flip-flop using standard engine approaches
questions.correctAnswer: A capacitor holding or not holding charge (1T1C cell)
questions.explanation: DRAM = 1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit. Capacitor charged = 1, discharged = 0. Must be refreshed every ~64ms.

questions.question: Why is DRAM called 'dynamic'?
options: It changes speed via the core framework with additional processing in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Capacitor charge leaks out — must be refreshed every ~64ms or data is lost. Unlike SRAM which holds state.
It uses transistors across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Capacitor charge leaks out — must be refreshed every ~64ms or data is lost. Unlike SRAM which holds state.
questions.explanation: Dynamic = decays. Refresh reads and rewrites every row every 64ms. ~4.5% of DRAM time lost to refresh at normal temp.

questions.question: What is the row buffer and why does it matter?
options: When a row is opened, ALL columns are read into sense amplifiers. Subsequent accesses to same row are ~14ns vs ~40ns for closed row.
A register by default behavior across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A type of DRAM under normal conditions in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: When a row is opened, ALL columns are read into sense amplifiers. Subsequent accesses to same row are ~14ns vs ~40ns for closed row.
questions.explanation: Row buffer hit = ~14ns (column access). Row buffer miss = tRCD + CL = ~40ns (precharge + row activate + column). 3x difference.

questions.question: Why is DRAM latency ~80-100ns while L1 cache is ~1ns?
options: DRAM uses slower technology through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Physical distance (15-25cm PCB trace = ~1ns minimum), DRAM array sensing (~50ns), bus transfer overhead
DRAM is larger by default behavior
L1 uses magic across all supported platforms with additional processing with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Physical distance (15-25cm PCB trace = ~1ns minimum), DRAM array sensing (~50ns), bus transfer overhead
questions.explanation: Four reasons: distance (speed of light), DRAM array internal slowness (tRCD + CL + tRP), pin bandwidth bottleneck, bank precharge.

questions.question: What is the memory wall?
questions.correctAnswer: CPU can execute ~400 instructions in the time of one DRAM access (100ns at 4GHz). Every cache level exists to avoid hitting DRAM.
acceptableAnswers: CPU executes 400 instructions per DRAM access
CPU is much faster than RAM
the gap between CPU speed and memory latency
questions.explanation: 4 instructions/ns × 100ns = 400 instructions per DRAM access. All caching, prefetching, data-oriented design exists because of this.

questions.question: What two components make up a single DRAM cell?
questions.correctAnswer: One transistor and one capacitor (1T1C)
questions.explanation: Each DRAM cell stores one bit using one transistor (a switch) and one capacitor (a charge bucket).

questions.question: How many DRAM cells are in a single 16 GB DDR5 DIMM?
questions.correctAnswer: 128 billion
questions.explanation: 16 GB = 16 × 8 = 128 billion bits. Each bit needs one DRAM cell (1T1C).

questions.question: What does the word line do in a DRAM array?
options: Controls the transistor gate, connecting the capacitor to the bit line
Supplies power to the DRAM chip
questions.correctAnswer: Controls the transistor gate, connecting the capacitor to the bit line
questions.explanation: The word line controls the transistor gates for an entire row. When activated, all cells in that row dump their charge onto their bit lines simultaneously.

questions.question: What is a row buffer hit in DRAM?
questions.correctAnswer: When a subsequent memory access targets the same row that's already open in the row buffer — ~10 ns access
acceptableAnswers: access to open row buffer ~10 ns
same row already open fast access
questions.explanation: Once a row is opened, all bits sit in the row buffer. Subsequent accesses to that row take ~10 ns. This is why sequential memory access is fast.

questions.question: How often must every DRAM row be refreshed?
questions.correctAnswer: Every 64 milliseconds at ≤85°C
questions.explanation: At ≤85°C, each row must be refreshed every 64 ms. Above 85°C, the interval drops to 32 ms.

questions.question: How many rows does a typical DDR5 8 Gb die have?
questions.correctAnswer: 8,192 rows
questions.explanation: A typical DDR5 8 Gb die has 8,192 rows. The refresh interval is 64 ms ÷ 8,192 = 7.8125 µs between refreshes.

questions.question: What percentage of DRAM time is spent on refresh at ≤85°C?
questions.explanation: Each refresh takes ~350 ns every 7.8 µs. That's 350 / 7800 ≈ 4.5% of total DRAM time spent on refresh.

questions.question: What is the first access latency (row open + column read) for DDR5-6000?
questions.explanation: DDR5-6000 first access latency is ~80-100 ns, comprising row activation (tRCD), column read (CL), and bus/controller overhead.

questions.question: What is the latency of a subsequent access to the same open row?
questions.explanation: Once a row is open, reading from it takes ~10-15 ns — dramatically faster than the 80-100 ns first access.

questions.question: What is L1 cache latency compared to DRAM?
questions.correctAnswer: L1: ~1 ns, DRAM: ~100 ns
questions.explanation: L1 cache access takes ~1 ns (4-5 cycles at 5 GHz). DRAM access takes ~100 ns — 80-100× slower.

questions.question: Why is DRAM 80-100× slower than L1 cache?
questions.correctAnswer: Physical distance (signals leave the chip), DRAM array internal analog operations, pin bandwidth bottleneck, and precharge penalties
acceptableAnswers: distance DRAM internals pin bandwidth precharge
physical distance array slowness pin bottleneck bank conflicts
off-chip distance analog sensing pin limits row precharge
questions.explanation: Four factors: 1) Physical distance (~20 cm round-trip at 60% speed of light), 2) Analog DRAM operations (tRCD, CL, tRP), 3) Pin bandwidth (~200 memory pins), 4) Bank precharge penalties.

questions.question: How far does a signal travel in one nanosecond on a PCB?
questions.correctAnswer: ~18 cm (at ~60% speed of light)
questions.explanation: Electrical signals in PCB traces travel at ~60% of c. At 60% of c, a signal travels ~18 cm per nanosecond.

questions.question: What is the round-trip signal propagation distance from CPU to RAM and back?
questions.correctAnswer: ~15-25 cm, taking ~0.8-1.4 ns — 5-7 CPU cycles at 5 GHz
questions.explanation: The physical round-trip from CPU socket → RAM slot → back is 15-25 cm, taking ~0.8-1.4 ns. This is the absolute minimum physical lower bound.

questions.question: What does tRCD (Row to Column Command Delay) represent?
questions.correctAnswer: The time from row activation until the column data can be read — the word line is driven and sense amplifiers amplify tiny voltages
acceptableAnswers: row activation to column read delay
time for word line and sense amplifiers
questions.explanation: tRCD is the delay from activating a row until the column can be read. It covers driving the word line, charge flowing from capacitors, and sense amplifiers amplifying millivolt changes. ~14-20 ns.

questions.question: What does CL (CAS Latency) represent?
questions.correctAnswer: The time from column address being presented to data being available on the pins
acceptableAnswers: time from column address to data out
column selection to data output delay
questions.explanation: CL is the time from when the column address is sent to when the data is available on the pins. Typically ~10-15 ns.

questions.question: What does tRP (Row Precharge) represent?
questions.correctAnswer: The time to close the current row and reset the bit lines to a reference voltage before opening a new row
acceptableAnswers: time to close row and reset bitlines
questions.explanation: tRP is the time to precharge (close) the current row's bit lines to a reference voltage. This must happen before a new row can be opened. ~14-20 ns.

questions.question: How many banks does a typical DDR5 chip have?
questions.correctAnswer: 32 banks (DDR5 increased from 16 in DDR4)
questions.explanation: DDR5 has 32 banks (up from 16 in DDR4). Each bank is an independent array that can have one row open at a time.

questions.question: What is the DRAM bandwidth per DDR5-6000 DIMM?
questions.correctAnswer: ~38-51 GB/s depending on the speed grade
questions.explanation: DDR5-4800 to DDR5-6400 provides ~38-51 GB/s per DIMM. Dual-channel doubles this.

questions.question: How much bandwidth does L1 cache deliver internally?
questions.correctAnswer: ~2 TB/s — the internal data paths are thousands of bits wide and run at core clock
questions.explanation: The L1 data cache delivers ~2 TB/s internally. The off-chip DRAM bottleneck is enormous — ~38-51 GB/s is less than 3% of L1 bandwidth.

questions.question: Why do server CPUs need aggressive cooling for RAM?
questions.correctAnswer: Above 85°C, the JEDEC refresh interval halves to 32 ms — wasting ~9% of RAM time instead of ~4.5%
acceptableAnswers: hot RAM refreshes twice as often
high temp doubles refresh rate halves performance
questions.explanation: Above 85°C, capacitors leak faster, requiring 32 ms refresh (double rate). Refresh time goes from ~4.5% to ~9% of total DRAM time.

questions.question: What is Row Hammer?
questions.correctAnswer: Repeated access to one DRAM row causes bit flips in adjacent rows due to capacitive coupling between word lines
acceptableAnswers: repeated row access flips adjacent bits
bit flips from row activation coupling
capacitive coupling between word lines causes bit flips
questions.explanation: Repeatedly activating one row can disturb the charge in adjacent rows through capacitive coupling, causing bit flips. DDR5 includes Row Hammer Refresh (RHR) mitigation.

questions.question: How many CPU instructions can execute during one 100 ns DRAM access at 5 GHz?
questions.correctAnswer: ~400 instructions per nanosecond × 100 ns = 400 instructions
questions.explanation: At 5 GHz, the CPU executes ~4 instructions per nanosecond (superscalar). 4 × 100 ns = 400 instructions lost waiting for one DRAM access.

questions.question: What does DDR stand for in DDR5?
questions.correctAnswer: Double Data Rate — data is transferred on both the rising and falling edges of the clock
acceptableAnswers: Double Data Rate both clock edges
questions.explanation: DDR = Double Data Rate. Data transfers on both the rising and falling clock edges, doubling the effective data rate.

questions.question: How much memory can a single DDR5 DIMM hold?
questions.correctAnswer: Up to 64 GB per stick (128 GB with 3D-stacked DRAM on server)
acceptableAnswers: up to 64 GB (128 GB with 3D stacking)
questions.explanation: Consumer DDR5 DIMMs go up to 64 GB. Server platforms with 3D-stacked DRAM can reach 128 GB per DIMM.

questions.question: What is the bandwidth of the L1 data cache compared to DRAM?
questions.correctAnswer: L1 ~2 TB/s vs DRAM ~50 GB/s
questions.explanation: L1 cache delivers ~2 TB/s internally. DDR5 delivers ~48-51 GB/s. The off-chip bottleneck is enormous.

questions.question: What causes bank conflicts in DRAM?
questions.correctAnswer: Accessing different rows in the same bank requires precharging the current row and activating a new one — paying tRP + tRCD penalty
acceptableAnswers: different rows in same bank need precharge
same bank different row full penalty
bank conflict requires row close and open
questions.explanation: If you need data from a different row in the same bank, you must precharge (tRP) and activate the new row (tRCD) — ~30-40 ns penalty.

questions.question: What does a sense amplifier do in DRAM?
questions.correctAnswer: It senses the tiny voltage difference from a capacitor and amplifies it to a full logic level, determining if the bit is 0 or 1
acceptableAnswers: amplifies tiny capacitor signal to logic level
questions.explanation: Sense amplifiers at the end of each bit line measure the tiny voltage change (millivolts) from the capacitor and determine: is this a 1 or a 0?

questions.question: Why is DRAM read destructive?
questions.correctAnswer: Reading a DRAM cell discharges the capacitor — the data is lost unless the sense amplifier writes it back
questions.explanation: Reading connects the capacitor to the bit line, allowing charge to flow out. The cell must be written back after reading to restore the data.

questions.question: What does SRAM stand for and why doesn't it need refresh?
questions.correctAnswer: Static RAM — uses a 6-transistor flip-flop circuit that holds state indefinitely as long as power is applied (no leaky capacitor)
acceptableAnswers: Static RAM uses flip-flop holds state
questions.explanation: SRAM uses a 6-transistor flip-flop (not a capacitor). It holds state indefinitely with power. No refresh needed, but uses 6 transistors per bit vs DRAM's 1.

questions.question: True or False: A DDR5-6000 module can transfer 6,000 MT/s because of Double Data Rate.
questions.correctAnswer: True
questions.explanation: DDR5-6000 has a 3,000 MHz clock, but transfers data on both edges = 6,000 MT/s (megatransfers per second).

questions.question: What is the 16n prefetch in DDR5?
questions.correctAnswer: DDR5 reads 16 words of data internally per memory access (up from 8n in DDR4 and 4n in DDR3)
questions.explanation: DDR5 uses 16n prefetch — it reads 16 data words internally per memory access, enabling higher bandwidth and efficiency.

questions.question: What is the memory controller part of?
questions.correctAnswer: The CPU die itself — it's no longer on the motherboard chipset (integrated memory controller)
questions.explanation: Modern CPUs have an integrated memory controller (IMC) on the CPU die itself, reducing latency compared to the old northbridge design.

questions.question: What is the typical capacity of a DRAM row in DDR5?
questions.correctAnswer: 8,192 columns = 8 Kb per row (8,192 bits per row in a typical DDR5 8 Gb die)
questions.explanation: A typical DDR5 8 Gb die has rows of 8,192 columns (8 Kb). All 8,192 cells in the row dump their charge when the word line activates.

questions.question: What does the memory wall mean in one sentence?
questions.correctAnswer: The CPU can execute 4 instructions per nanosecond; RAM takes 100 ns to respond — 400 instructions wasted waiting
acceptableAnswers: processor waits hundreds of cycles per memory access
questions.explanation: The memory wall in one sentence: 'The CPU can execute 4 instructions per nanosecond. RAM takes 100 ns to respond. That's 400 instructions wasted.'

questions.question: What is the approximate latency of a DRAM access at DDR5-6000?
questions.explanation: DDR5-6000 first access latency is ~80-100 ns, including row activation, column read, and bus/controller overhead.

questions.question: What is the approximate latency of an L1 cache hit?
questions.correctAnswer: ~1 ns
questions.explanation: L1 cache: ~1 ns (4-5 cycles at 5 GHz). DRAM: ~100 ns. The gap is ~80-100×.

questions.question: How does the DDR5 refresh interval change above 85°C?
questions.correctAnswer: Drops to 32 ms
questions.explanation: Above 85°C, JEDEC requires 32 ms refresh (half the normal 64 ms). Refresh overhead doubles from ~4.5% to ~9%.

questions.question: What is the bandwidth of a DDR5-6400 DIMM?
questions.correctAnswer: ~51 GB/s
questions.explanation: DDR5-6400: 64-bit bus × 6400 MT/s = 51.2 GB/s per DIMM.

questions.question: What component in the CPU manages DRAM timing?
questions.correctAnswer: The integrated memory controller (IMC)
questions.explanation: The IMC on the CPU die manages DRAM: issues refresh commands, schedules reads/writes across banks, and handles timing.

questions.question: What happens during a DRAM row precharge (tRP)?
options: The row is opened for reading
The row buffer is dumped to the CPU
The bit lines are reset to a reference voltage
Data is written back to the capacitor
questions.correctAnswer: The bit lines are reset to a reference voltage
questions.explanation: tRP: before opening a new row, the current row's bit lines must be precharged (reset) to a reference voltage. ~14-20 ns.

questions.question: In the stadium analogy, what does the 'janitor patrolling rows' represent?
questions.correctAnswer: The refresh cycle
questions.explanation: The janitors represent the refresh cycle: they constantly patrol rows, checking cups (capacitors) and refilling wet ones (rewriting data).

questions.question: What is the effective speed ratio between L1 cache and DDR5 DRAM?
questions.correctAnswer: L1: ~1 ns vs DRAM: ~100 ns — DRAM is ~80-100× slower
questions.explanation: L1 cache access takes ~1 ns. DRAM access takes ~80-100 ns. This 80-100× gap is the memory wall.

questions.question: How many DRAM DIMMs does a typical consumer CPU support?
questions.correctAnswer: 2 (dual-channel)
questions.explanation: Consumer CPUs typically support dual-channel DDR4/DDR5 (2 DIMMs). HEDT/server platforms support quad/octa-channel.

questions.question: What is the bandwidth with dual-channel DDR5-6000?
questions.explanation: Dual-channel doubles bandwidth: 2 × 48 GB/s (DDR5-6000) = ~96 GB/s.

questions.question: What does DDR5's on-die ECC do?
questions.correctAnswer: Corrects single-bit errors inside the DRAM chip itself, improving reliability without requiring system-level ECC
questions.explanation: DDR5 includes on-die ECC that corrects single-bit errors within each chip, reducing system-level error rates.

questions.question: What is CAS latency (CL) measuring?
options: Time to start a new row after closing the current one
Time from column address to data available on pins
Time to write data back after a read
questions.correctAnswer: Time from column address to data available on pins
questions.explanation: CL is the column access strobe latency — how many cycles between presenting the column address and getting the data on the output pins.

questions.question: What is virtual memory's fundamental trick?
options: More RAM using standard engine approaches in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Every process thinks it owns ALL memory starting at address 0. The OS silently translates virtual→physical addresses.
Faster memory across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Disk as RAM for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Every process thinks it owns ALL memory starting at address 0. The OS silently translates virtual→physical addresses.
questions.explanation: Virtual address space = private illusion. MMU translates every access. Each process has its own page table → different physical frames.

questions.question: How many levels does x86-64 page table have?
options: 1 using standard engine approaches with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
4 (PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT) — 9 bits each, 12-bit offset = 48-bit virtual address
8 using standard engine approaches with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: 4 (PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT) — 9 bits each, 12-bit offset = 48-bit virtual address
questions.explanation: 4-level radix tree. Each level = 512 entries. 4 sequential memory accesses for one translation. TLB caches recent translations.

questions.question: What is the TLB and why is it critical?
options: A type of DRAM in most implementations under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Translation Lookaside Buffer — a small hardware cache (64 entries L1) for recent virtual→physical translations. ~1 cycle hit.
A page table through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A CPU register by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: Translation Lookaside Buffer — a small hardware cache (64 entries L1) for recent virtual→physical translations. ~1 cycle hit.
questions.explanation: TLB hit = ~1 cycle. TLB miss = 4-level page walk (~50-400ns). Without TLB, the MMU would need 4 memory accesses per translation.

questions.question: How much memory does a 64-entry L1 D-TLB cover with 4KB pages?
options: 4GB by default behavior with additional processing under normal op
256 KB (64 × 4KB) — very small! This is why huge pages matter.
64 MB through the built-in API under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: 256 KB (64 × 4KB) — very small! This is why huge pages matter.
questions.explanation: 64 entries × 4KB = 256KB. If your working set is larger, TLB misses dominate. Solution: 2MB huge pages → 128MB coverage.

questions.question: What are the three key reasons virtual memory exists?
questions.correctAnswer: Isolation (processes can't access each other), simplification (programs start at address 0), overcommit (OS can promise more than physical RAM)
questions.explanation: Isolation = process can't read/write other processes. Simplification = every program thinks it starts at 0. Overcommit = allocate 10GB, use 2GB.

questions.question: What is virtual memory?
questions.correctAnswer: A system where each process has its own private, isolated virtual address space that the OS transparently maps to physical RAM
acceptableAnswers: private virtual address space per process
OS maps virtual to physical addresses transparently
each process thinks it owns all RAM
questions.explanation: Every process thinks it owns all RAM starting from address 0. The OS silently translates every virtual address to a physical address in hardware.

questions.question: What three benefits does virtual memory provide?
questions.correctAnswer: Isolation (processes can't access each other's memory), Simplification (programs assume they own address 0), and Overcommit/Swapping (promise more memory than physically exists)
acceptableAnswers: process isolation simplified programming memory overcommit
questions.explanation: Virtual memory provides: 1) Isolation (processes cannot read/write each other's memory), 2) Simplification (programs start at address 0), 3) Overcommit and swapping.

questions.question: What hardware component translates virtual addresses to physical addresses?
questions.correctAnswer: The MMU (Memory Management Unit) — sits between the CPU core and L1 cache
acceptableAnswers: the MMU between CPU and L1
questions.explanation: The MMU sits between the CPU core and L1 cache. Every memory access — instruction fetch, data load, stack push — passes through it for translation.

questions.question: What is the standard page size on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: 4 KB (4,096 bytes)
questions.explanation: The default page size on x86-64 is 4 KB. Each virtual address belongs to one virtual page. Each physical RAM location belongs to one physical frame.

questions.question: How is a 48-bit virtual address split for the page table walk?
questions.correctAnswer: 9 bits PML4 | 9 bits PDPT | 9 bits PD | 9 bits PT | 12 bits offset (9+9+9+9+12)
questions.explanation: The virtual address is divided: 9 bits PML4 index, 9 bits PDPT index, 9 bits PD index, 9 bits PT index, and 12 bits offset within the 4 KB page.

questions.question: Why is the page table hierarchical (4-level) instead of flat?
questions.correctAnswer: A flat array of 68.7 billion entries × 8 bytes = 550 GB — it obviously doesn't fit. The 4-level radix tree stores only populated entries
acceptableAnswers: flat would be 550 GB too large
radix tree only stores used entries
questions.explanation: A flat mapping of the 256 TB virtual address space would require 550 GB of entries. The 4-level radix tree stores only the pages actually in use.

questions.question: How many levels does the x86-64 page table have?
questions.correctAnswer: 4 levels: PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT → page (plus 5-level paging for Intel's 57-bit extension)
questions.explanation: Standard x86-64 uses 4-level paging: PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT → page. Newer server CPUs add a 5th level for 57-bit virtual addresses.

questions.question: What does a PTE (Page Table Entry) contain?
questions.correctAnswer: The physical frame number, plus flag bits: Present, Read/Write, User/Supervisor, Accessed, Dirty, etc.
acceptableAnswers: frame number with Present RW U/S flags
questions.explanation: A PTE maps a virtual page number to a physical frame number and includes flag bits like Present (valid), Read/Write, User (ring 3), Accessed, and Dirty.

questions.question: What is the TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)?
questions.correctAnswer: A hardware cache inside the CPU that stores recent virtual-to-physical page translations — built from SRAM as content-addressable memory
acceptableAnswers: CPU cache of virtual to physical mappings
questions.explanation: The TLB is a dedicated hardware cache in the CPU storing recent page translations. It's built from SRAM as content-addressable memory that searches all entries in parallel.

questions.question: How many entries does L1 D-TLB typically have?
questions.correctAnswer: 64 entries on Intel, 72 entries on AMD Zen 4+
questions.explanation: L1 D-TLB: 64 entries (Intel) or 72 entries (AMD Zen 4+). Each covers one page. With 4 KB pages: 64 × 4 KB = 256 KB total coverage.

questions.question: How much virtual address space does a 64-entry L1 D-TLB cover with 4 KB pages?
questions.explanation: 64 TLB entries × 4 KB per page = 256 KB total coverage. If your program's working set exceeds 256 KB across different pages, you'll TLB-miss repeatedly.

questions.question: How much latency does a TLB hit add?
questions.correctAnswer: ~1 cycle
questions.explanation: L1 TLB hit: ~1 cycle. L2 STLB hit: ~8-12 cycles. A full page walk (TLB miss): ~50-400 ns depending on cache hits.

questions.question: How much latency does a full page table walk take if all levels miss cache?
questions.correctAnswer: ~320-400 ns (4 DRAM accesses × ~80-100 ns each)
questions.explanation: A full page walk needs 4 sequential memory reads. If all levels miss L1/L2/L3 cache, each hits DRAM at ~80-100 ns = ~320-400 ns total.

questions.question: How much address space does the L1 D-TLB cover with 2 MB huge pages?
questions.explanation: With 2 MB huge pages, the same 64-entry L1 D-TLB covers 128 MB — 512× more than with 4 KB pages.

questions.question: What is the latency of an L2 STLB hit?
questions.correctAnswer: ~8-12 cycles
questions.explanation: L2 STLB hit latency is ~8-12 cycles. The L2 STLB is larger (1,536-3,072 entries) but slower than L1 TLB.

questions.question: How many entries does the L2 STLB have on Intel Skylake+ vs AMD Zen 4?
questions.correctAnswer: Intel: 1,536 entries, AMD Zen 4: 3,072 entries
questions.explanation: L2 STLB: 1,536 entries (Intel Skylake+) or 3,072 entries (AMD Zen 4). It caches combined instruction and data translations.

questions.question: What instruction triggers a page fault when a PTE's Present bit is 0?
questions.correctAnswer: CR2 register stores the faulting address when Page Fault exception #14 is raised
questions.explanation: When the MMU finds Present=0, the CPU raises exception #14 (Page Fault), and the CR2 register contains the faulting virtual address.

questions.question: What register points to the top-level page table (PML4)?
questions.correctAnswer: CR3 register
questions.explanation: CR3 holds the physical address of the PML4 table (the top-level page table). The MMU starts the page walk from CR3.

questions.question: On x86-64, the top 16 bits of a 64-bit virtual address must be sign-extended. What does this mean?
questions.correctAnswer: Bits 48-63 must be copies of bit 47 — addresses are 'canonical'. Non-canonical addresses cause faults
questions.explanation: Virtual addresses must be canonical: bits 48-63 must be copies of bit 47 (sign-extended). Non-canonical addresses cause a general protection fault.

questions.question: How much of the virtual address space is usable with 48-bit addressing?
questions.correctAnswer: 256 TB (128 TB user space + 128 TB kernel space)
questions.explanation: 48-bit virtual addresses give 256 TB. The split is typically 128 TB for user space and 128 TB for kernel space.

questions.question: ASLR randomizes the virtual address space layout. What does ASLR stand for?
questions.correctAnswer: Address Space Layout Randomization
questions.explanation: ASLR randomizes the base addresses of stack, heap, libraries, and executable to make buffer overflow exploitation harder.

questions.question: What happens in the TLB context switch between processes?
questions.correctAnswer: The TLB must be flushed (or tagged with Process Context IDs) because the same virtual address now maps to different physical memory
acceptableAnswers: old translations invalid for new process
questions.explanation: On context switch, the TLB's entries belong to the old process's virtual→physical mappings. They must be flushed or tagged with PCID to avoid using stale translations.

questions.question: What is a TLB miss penalty on a heavily loaded system?
questions.correctAnswer: The CPU pipeline stalls while the MMU walks the page table — at 5 GHz, 320-400 ns = 1,600-2,000 cycles
questions.explanation: During a TLB miss, the CPU pipeline stalls for 320-400 ns while the MMU walks the 4-level page table. At 5 GHz, that's 1,600-2,000 cycles lost.

questions.question: How many PTE entries fit in one 4 KB page?
questions.correctAnswer: 512 entries (4 KB ÷ 8 bytes per PTE)
questions.explanation: Each PTE is 8 bytes. A 4 KB page contains 4,096 / 8 = 512 PTEs — exactly matching the 9-bit index (2^9 = 512) used at each level.

questions.question: Why are page table levels indexed by 9 bits?
questions.correctAnswer: Because 512 PTEs fit in one 4 KB page (2^9 = 512). 9 bits address each entry in the page
questions.explanation: Each page table page holds 512 PTEs (4 KB / 8 bytes). 9 bits are needed to index 512 entries (2^9 = 512).

questions.question: TLB coverage with 4 KB pages is inadequate for what kind of workload?
questions.correctAnswer: Any workload with a working set larger than ~256 KB scattered across different pages (typical game, database, scientific computing)
acceptableAnswers: game engine or database with large random access patterns
questions.explanation: 64 TLB entries × 4 KB = 256 KB coverage. A 2 GB game working set will TLB-miss on virtually every access.

questions.question: What does a 'not present' PTE mean for a valid memory address?
questions.correctAnswer: The page is reserved but not currently in physical RAM — it may be on disk (swap) or never yet allocated (demand paging)
acceptableAnswers: page on disk or not yet allocated
questions.explanation: A 'not present' PTE doesn't mean invalid — it means the page is on disk (swapped out) or not yet allocated (demand paging promises). The PTE stores swap location.

questions.question: How does the MMU know whether a 'not present' PTE means swapped-out or invalid?
questions.correctAnswer: It doesn't — the MMU raises a page fault and the OS handler inspects the VMA tree. If no VMA covers the address, it's invalid (SEGFAULT). If it does, the page is swapped or demand-paged
acceptableAnswers: OS checks VMA tree on page fault
OS page fault handler checks VMA
questions.explanation: The MMU just raises a page fault. The OS handler walks the VMA tree: if a VMA covers the address, it's a valid page to load; if not, it's a SEGFAULT.

questions.question: What is the Accessed bit in a PTE used for?
questions.correctAnswer: Hardware sets it when the page is read or written — the OS uses it for page replacement (LRU approximation) and swapping decisions
acceptableAnswers: set by hardware on access used for LRU
questions.explanation: The Accessed bit is set by hardware on any read or write. The OS periodically clears it and checks later — pages with Accessed=0 are good swap candidates.

questions.question: What is the Dirty bit in a PTE used for?
questions.correctAnswer: Set by hardware when the page is written to — indicates the page has been modified and must be written back to disk before eviction
acceptableAnswers: tracks whether page was written to
dirty bit means needs writeback before eviction
questions.explanation: The Dirty bit is set when the page is written to. Clean pages can be dropped without writeback; dirty pages need to be written to swap/disk first.

questions.question: How many page table pages are accessed during a 4-level x86 page walk?
questions.explanation: 4 memory reads: PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT. Each is a separate memory access depending on the previous result.

questions.question: What is the total TLB miss penalty in cycles at 5 GHz for a full page walk (all cache misses)?
questions.correctAnswer: ~2,000 cycles
questions.explanation: 320-400 ns × 5 GHz = 1,600-2,000 cycles stalled. During this time, the CPU pipeline does nothing useful on that instruction.

questions.question: How much virtual memory does 5-level paging (57-bit) provide?
questions.correctAnswer: 128 PB
questions.explanation: 57-bit virtual address space = 2^57 = 128 PB. Intel's 5-level paging extension adds a 5th page table level.

questions.question: What is a huge page's page table depth?
questions.correctAnswer: 3 levels (skip PT level for 2 MB)
questions.explanation: A 2 MB huge page skips the lowest PTE level: PML4 → PDPT → PD → 2 MB page. 1 GB pages skip two levels: PML4 → PDPT → 1 GB page.

questions.question: What is the minimum page size that can skip the PTE level on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: 2 MB
questions.explanation: 2 MB huge pages skip the lowest page table level (PT). The PD entry directly points to a 2 MB block. 1 GB skips both PT and PD.

questions.question: What is the Accessed bit in a PTE used for by the OS?
questions.correctAnswer: Page replacement and LRU approximation
questions.explanation: The Accessed bit is set by hardware on any access. The OS periodically clears it — pages with Accessed=0 are good candidates for eviction (LRU approximation).

questions.question: How does the OS use the Dirty bit in a PTE?
options: To determine if a page needs writeback before eviction
questions.correctAnswer: To determine if a page needs writeback before eviction
questions.explanation: Dirty = page was modified. Clean pages can be dropped without writeback. Dirty pages must be written to swap/file before the frame is reused.

questions.question: What happens if a process accesses memory outside its VMA regions?
options: The OS allocates new pages automatically
The OS sends SIGSEGV and terminates the process
The OS swaps in from disk
questions.correctAnswer: The OS sends SIGSEGV and terminates the process
questions.explanation: If the page fault handler finds no VMA covering the faulting address, it's an invalid access — SIGSEGV (Linux) or ACCESS_VIOLATION (Windows).

questions.question: On a context switch, how does the kernel handle the TLB?
questions.correctAnswer: It flushes the TLB (or uses Process Context IDs / PCIDs to tag entries by process)
questions.explanation: Without PCID, the entire TLB must be flushed on context switch. With PCID, TLB entries are tagged by process so only non-matching entries need invalidation.

questions.question: How does 5-level paging split the virtual address?
questions.explanation: 5-level paging: 9-bit PML5 + 9-bit PML4 + 9-bit PDPT + 9-bit PD + 9-bit PT + 12-bit offset = 57-bit address.

questions.question: What is PCID (Process Context Identifier)?
questions.correctAnswer: A TLB tagging mechanism that assigns each entry to a specific process, avoiding full TLB flushes on context switches
acceptableAnswers: identifies which process owns TLB entry
questions.explanation: PCID tags TLB entries with the originating process. On context switch, only entries with matching PCID survive — no full flush needed.

questions.question: What is the maximum virtual address space per process with 48-bit addressing?
questions.correctAnswer: 256 TB (128 TB user + 128 TB kernel)
questions.explanation: 48-bit = 256 TB. Split typically: lower 128 TB for user space, upper 128 TB for kernel space.

questions.question: What are the three types of page faults?
questions.correctAnswer: Minor (soft — page in RAM, just update PTE), Major (hard — page on disk, must I/O), Invalid (no mapping — SIGSEGV)
questions.explanation: Minor: ~1-3µs, no I/O. Major: ~50-100µs (NVMe) or 5-10ms (HDD), disk I/O. Invalid: crash (null pointer, use-after-free).

questions.question: What is demand paging?
options: Loading all pages at once through the built-in API under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
