Physical pages allocated on FIRST access (page fault). 100MB allocation = microseconds; real cost amortized per 4KB touched.
Pre-loading pages via the core framework for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Swapping to disk for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Physical pages allocated on FIRST access (page fault). 100MB allocation = microseconds; real cost amortized per 4KB touched.
questions.explanation: new/malloc creates VMA entries but no physical pages. Each 4KB page faulted in on first write. OS is lazy — laziness is performance.

questions.question: What does mmap do that read() doesn't?
options: Same thing within the current scope
Maps a file into virtual address space — file becomes memory. Access triggers page faults that load data on-demand.
Allocates heap across all supported platforms within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Creates a file under normal conditions with additional processing with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Maps a file into virtual address space — file becomes memory. Access triggers page faults that load data on-demand.
questions.explanation: mmap: file pages mapped into process address space. First access = major page fault → DMA from disk. No explicit read() calls.

questions.question: What is thrashing?
options: Fast swapping across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Working set exceeds physical RAM → constant page faults → system spends more time swapping than computing
A type of deadlock for typical use cases in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Working set exceeds physical RAM → constant page faults → system spends more time swapping than computing
questions.explanation: Thrashing = disk I/O saturation from swap. Mouse lags, CPU idle (waiting for disk). Fix: add RAM or reduce working set.

questions.question: What does mlock()/VirtualLock() do?
options: Pins pages in physical RAM — they can never be swapped out. For latency-critical data (audio buffers, game state).
Allocates memory through the built-in API under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Locks a mutex through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Pins pages in physical RAM — they can never be swapped out. For latency-critical data (audio buffers, game state).
questions.explanation: mlock prevents swap for critical pages. Tradeoff: reduces swappable pool. Lock sparingly — only what can't tolerate 10ms stall.

questions.question: What is a page fault?
questions.correctAnswer: An event where the MMU finds the PTE's Present bit = 0 during translation, causing the CPU to trap to the kernel
acceptableAnswers: MMU finds PTE not present traps to kernel
CPU exception when page isn't mapped
Present bit = 0 in PTE causes trap
questions.explanation: A page fault occurs when the MMU discovers the page table entry has Present=0. The CPU raises exception #14 and traps to the OS kernel.

questions.question: What is the latency of a minor page fault?
questions.explanation: A minor fault only requires updating the PTE to point to an existing physical frame — no disk I/O. Takes ~1-3 µs.

questions.question: What is the latency of a major page fault on NVMe?
questions.explanation: A major fault requires reading from disk. On NVMe: ~50-100 µs. On HDD: ~5-10 ms.

questions.question: What is the latency of a major page fault on HDD?
questions.explanation: On HDD, a major page fault takes ~5-10 ms — 50,000-100,000× slower than a RAM access.

questions.question: What is the zero-page trick?
questions.correctAnswer: All newly allocated pages initially point to a single system-wide read-only zero-filled page. On first write, a copy-on-write fault allocates a real page
acceptableAnswers: all new pages share a zero page COW
questions.explanation: When you 'new int[1000]', all pages point to a single read-only zero page. On first write, COW triggers, allocating a real physical frame.

questions.question: What is copy-on-write (COW) in the context of page faults?
questions.correctAnswer: When a page is shared (read-only mapping), the first write triggers a minor fault, causing the kernel to copy the page and remap it as Read+Write private
acceptableAnswers: write triggers copy of shared page
first write copies shared read-only page
COW fault creates private copy on write
questions.explanation: COW: PTE is read-only pointing to shared page. First write → MMU fault → kernel copies page to new frame → updates PTE to Read+Write pointing to private copy.

questions.question: What step happens 5th in the 8-step major page fault sequence?
questions.correctAnswer: The kernel allocates a free physical frame (or evicts a victim page if RAM is full)
questions.explanation: After the kernel walks the VMA tree (step 4), it allocates a free physical frame (step 5). If none free, it must evict a victim page first.

questions.question: What does the CPU do after returning from the page fault handler?
questions.correctAnswer: It retries the faulting instruction — the MMU now finds the PTE is present, loads the TLB, and the access completes normally
questions.explanation: After the kernel updates the PTE and returns to user mode, the CPU retries the faulting instruction. The process sees no evidence anything happened.

questions.question: What does the `mmap` system call do?
questions.correctAnswer: Maps a region of a file (or anonymous memory) into the process's virtual address space so it can be accessed with pointers instead of read()/write()
acceptableAnswers: maps file into virtual address space
questions.explanation: mmap makes a region of a file appear as part of the process's virtual address space. Instead of read()/write(), you just dereference pointers.

questions.question: What does the kernel actually store at mmap time?
questions.correctAnswer: A VMA (Virtual Memory Area) entry recording the virtual address range, file pointer, offset, permissions, and flags — no physical memory is allocated
acceptableAnswers: VMA entry with file info and permissions
just a VMA no physical memory
records mapping but doesn't load file
questions.explanation: mmap only creates a VMA entry. No physical memory is allocated. No file data is read. The first touch triggers a major page fault that loads the data.

questions.question: What is the difference between MAP_PRIVATE and MAP_SHARED in mmap?
questions.correctAnswer: MAP_PRIVATE: writes create a private copy (COW), original file is never modified. MAP_SHARED: writes update the page cache and eventually the file
acceptableAnswers: PRIVATE COW not written back SHARED writes to file
PRIVATE private changes SHARED visible to others
questions.explanation: MAP_PRIVATE uses COW — changes are private. MAP_SHARED modifies the page cache page directly — other processes see changes and they eventually hit disk.

questions.question: Why do games use mmap for loading large levels?
questions.correctAnswer: Only touched pages get loaded, unused pages can be evicted automatically, and the code simplifies to pointer dereferences instead of manual seek+read loops
acceptableAnswers: only load touched pages automatic eviction simpler code
automatic page management and simpler access
questions.explanation: With mmap, only the parts of the level the player sees get loaded. The OS's page cache and read-ahead handle everything. No manual seek+read loops.

questions.question: What is swap?
questions.correctAnswer: A region of storage (dedicated partition or pagefile.sys) used as overflow parking for RAM pages when physical memory is exhausted
acceptableAnswers: storage used when RAM is full
questions.explanation: Swap is storage that acts as overflow space. When RAM is full, unused pages are evicted to swap to make room for active pages.

questions.question: What is the relative cost of a major page fault on NVMe vs RAM access?
questions.correctAnswer: NVMe major fault: ~50-100 µs vs RAM: ~100 ns = 500-1,000× slower
questions.explanation: A major page fault on NVMe takes ~50-100 µs compared to ~100 ns RAM access — 500-1,000× slower.

questions.question: What is the relative cost of a major page fault on HDD vs RAM?
questions.correctAnswer: HDD major fault: ~5-10 ms vs RAM: ~100 ns = 50,000-100,000× slower
questions.explanation: An HDD major fault takes ~5-10 ms. Compared to ~100 ns RAM, that's 50,000-100,000× slower.

questions.question: What does the mlock() system call do?
questions.correctAnswer: It locks pages in RAM — the OS will never swap these pages to disk
acceptableAnswers: locks pages in RAM prevents swap
prevents pages from being paged out
questions.explanation: mlock() tells the OS: 'Never swap these pages.' Critical for latency-sensitive data like audio buffers, game frame data, and network packet rings.

questions.question: What is the danger of locking too much memory with mlock()?
questions.correctAnswer: It reduces the pool of swappable RAM, increasing swap pressure on remaining unlocked pages and potentially causing OOM
acceptableAnswers: can cause OOM by reducing flexible RAM
questions.explanation: Locked pages can't be evicted. The remaining swappable pool is squeezed, making swap pressure worse for unlocked pages. Lock sparingly.

questions.question: What happens when the OS needs to evict a clean file-backed page?
questions.correctAnswer: It simply drops the page — no write needed because the file already contains the data (the page cache copy matches disk)
acceptableAnswers: just drop it no write needed
drop page file is backing store
questions.explanation: Clean file-backed pages match their disk copy. The OS just drops them — the file IS the backing store. No I/O needed.

questions.question: What happens when the OS needs to evict a dirty anonymous page?
questions.correctAnswer: It must write the page to the swap area on disk before freeing the physical frame
acceptableAnswers: dirty anonymous page written to swap
questions.explanation: Dirty anonymous pages (heap, stack) have no backing file. They must be written to the swap area before the physical frame can be reused.

questions.question: What is a VMA (Virtual Memory Area)?
questions.correctAnswer: A kernel structure describing a contiguous range of virtual memory with uniform permissions and backing (file, anonymous, heap, stack)
acceptableAnswers: kernel struct describing virtual memory range
record of a mapped virtual region
VMA defines permissions and backing for a VA range
questions.explanation: A VMA describes a contiguous virtual memory region. It records: start/end addresses, permissions (r/w/x), flags (shared/private), and backing store (file or anonymous).

questions.question: What does the kernel's page fault handler check first?
questions.correctAnswer: The CR2 register to get the faulting address, then walks the VMA tree to check if the address is in a valid mapped region
acceptableAnswers: CR2 for fault address then VMA tree
get faulting address from CR2 check VMA
questions.explanation: The handler reads CR2 (faulting address), then walks the process's VMA tree. In a VMA? Continue. Not in any VMA? Send SIGSEGV.

questions.question: How does Unreal Engine use memory-mapped I/O?
questions.correctAnswer: UE maps .pak archives via mmap, then accesses assets inside them as if they were in memory — the OS handles the loading
acceptableAnswers: uses mmap for asset loading from archives
questions.explanation: Unreal Engine memory-maps .pak archives and accesses assets as if they were in memory. The OS page cache handles loading and eviction transparently.

questions.question: What is a MAP_NORESERVE flag for mmap?
questions.correctAnswer: It tells the kernel not to reserve swap space for the mapping — the virtual address range is reserved but no physical or swap commitment is made
acceptableAnswers: virtual but no physical or swap commitment
questions.explanation: MAP_NORESERVE creates the VMA without reserving swap space. If you actually touch all the pages and RAM is exhausted, the OOM killer may activate.

questions.question: True or False: A minor page fault always requires disk I/O.
questions.correctAnswer: False
questions.explanation: Minor faults require no disk I/O — the physical page is already in RAM (e.g., shared library, zero page, COW). Only the PTE needs updating.

questions.question: What is the difference between major and minor page faults?
questions.correctAnswer: Minor: page in RAM but PTE not set (update PTE only, ~1-3 µs). Major: page on disk (must read from disk, ~50-100 µs NVMe to ~10 ms HDD)
acceptableAnswers: minor RAM only PTE major disk read
minor no I/O major reads from disk
minor update PTE major schedule I/O
questions.explanation: Minor faults just update the PTE (page in RAM, ~1-3 µs). Major faults read from disk (~50 µs - 10 ms depending on storage).

questions.question: What does calloc() do differently from malloc()+zeroing?
questions.correctAnswer: calloc() exploits the zero-page trick — it maps pages to the shared zero page with COW, while malloc()+manual zeroing forces actual allocation
acceptableAnswers: calloc uses zero page COW trick
calloc lazy zero malloc manual forces allocation
questions.explanation: calloc() returns pages pointing to the shared zero page (COW). malloc() + manual zeroing writes to every page, forcing physical allocation for all of them.

questions.question: How does the kernel handle a write to a MAP_PRIVATE page that was never modified before?
questions.correctAnswer: The write triggers a COW fault — kernel copies the page-cache page to a new private frame, remaps it as Read+Write, and the write proceeds
acceptableAnswers: COW fault copies page remaps as private
fault copies shared page to private frame
questions.explanation: First write to MAP_PRIVATE: PTE is read-only → page fault → kernel copies page → updates PTE to Read+Write private → write proceeds. Subsequent writes have zero overhead.

questions.question: In a major page fault on an NVMe SSD, what percentage of the time is actual I/O vs kernel overhead?
options: ~80-95% I/O (50-95 µs NVMe read + ~5-10 µs kernel overhead)
questions.correctAnswer: ~80-95% I/O (50-95 µs NVMe read + ~5-10 µs kernel overhead)
questions.explanation: NVMe read: 50-95 µs. Kernel fault handler: ~5-10 µs (VMA lookup, frame allocation, PTE update). I/O dominates.

questions.question: What is the difference between clean and dirty pages in swap context?
options: Clean = unmodified (can drop), dirty = modified (must writeback)
Clean = user pages, dirty = kernel pages
questions.correctAnswer: Clean = unmodified (can drop), dirty = modified (must writeback)
questions.explanation: Clean: matches disk copy → just drop. Dirty: modified in RAM → must write to swap/file before eviction.

questions.question: On Linux, what state does a process enter during a major page fault?
questions.correctAnswer: TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE
questions.explanation: The process is put in TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE sleep while waiting for disk I/O. It can't be interrupted by signals during this time.

questions.question: What is the VMA tree's role during page fault handling?
options: It provides the physical address mapping
It determines if the faulting address is valid and what type of mapping it has
It stores the process's file descriptors
questions.correctAnswer: It determines if the faulting address is valid and what type of mapping it has
questions.explanation: The kernel walks the VMA tree to check: is this address in a valid VMA? If yes: what type (file-backed, anonymous), what permissions? If no: SEGFAULT.

questions.question: What happens when a process writes to a MAP_PRIVATE mmap'd page for the first time?
options: The write modifies the file directly
A COW fault creates a private copy of the page
The page is written to swap
questions.correctAnswer: A COW fault creates a private copy of the page
questions.explanation: First write to MAP_PRIVATE: PTE is read-only pointing to shared page-cache page. Write triggers COW fault → kernel copies → remaps as Read+Write private.

questions.question: How does `fork()` interact with COW for memory pages?
options: All pages are shared as read-only — first write by either process triggers COW
questions.correctAnswer: All pages are shared as read-only — first write by either process triggers COW
questions.explanation: After fork(), parent and child share all pages as read-only. The first process to write triggers a COW fault, creating a private copy.

questions.question: What is the LRU (Least Recently Used) approximation for page eviction?
questions.correctAnswer: The kernel maintains lists of active and inactive pages. Pages move between lists based on the Accessed bit — inactive pages are evicted first
questions.explanation: The kernel uses a two-list strategy: active and inactive. Pages from the inactive list are evicted first. The Accessed bit determines promotion/demotion.

questions.question: What is the swap size recommendation for Linux?
questions.correctAnswer: varies: equal to RAM for hibernation, or 1-2 GB when no hibernation is needed
questions.explanation: Traditional rule: swap = RAM for hibernation support. Modern recommendation: 1-2 GB if you have enough RAM and no hibernation.

questions.question: Can a minor page fault lead to a process being context-switched out?
options: No — the fault is handled in microseconds within the fault handler
questions.correctAnswer: No — the fault is handled in microseconds within the fault handler
questions.explanation: Minor faults (~1-3 µs) are handled entirely in the page fault handler without sleeping — no context switch needed.

questions.question: What is the Windows equivalent of mlock()?
questions.correctAnswer: VirtualLock()
questions.explanation: VirtualLock() on Windows provides the same guarantee as mlock() on Linux — the specified pages cannot be swapped out.

questions.question: What is OOM score adjustment range?
questions.correctAnswer: -1000 (highly protected) to +1000 (first to die)
questions.explanation: oom_score_adj ranges from -1000 (OOM killer immune) to +1000 (always killed first). Default is 0.

questions.question: Major page fault:
options: Page not in RAM, must read from disk
questions.correctAnswer: Page not in RAM, must read from disk
questions.explanation: 100,000+ cycle penalty

questions.question: Minor page fault:
options: Page in RAM but not in process page table
questions.correctAnswer: Page in RAM but not in process page table
questions.explanation: Zero disk access

questions.question: mmap:
options: Maps file or device into virtual address space
questions.correctAnswer: Maps file or device into virtual address space
questions.explanation: Demand-paged file I/O

questions.question: MAP_SHARED:
options: Changes are visible to other processes and written back to file
questions.correctAnswer: Changes are visible to other processes and written back to file
questions.explanation: Shared mapping

questions.question: MAP_PRIVATE:
questions.correctAnswer: Copy-on-write: changes private to process
questions.explanation: Fork uses this for pages

questions.question: Swap:
options: Page moved to disk when RAM full
questions.correctAnswer: Page moved to disk when RAM full
questions.explanation: Swap out/in

questions.question: Swap file vs partition:
options: File: easier to resize; Partition: faster
questions.correctAnswer: File: easier to resize; Partition: faster
questions.explanation: Windows uses file, Linux uses both

questions.question: Page fault handler:
options: Kernel checks VMA, allocates page (minor) or reads from swap/file (major)
questions.correctAnswer: Kernel checks VMA, allocates page (minor) or reads from swap/file (major)
questions.explanation: MMU raises fault, kernel handles

questions.question: Demand paging:
questions.correctAnswer: Page loaded only when accessed
questions.explanation: Lazy allocation

questions.question: Copy-on-Write:
options: Virtual page shared, physical copied only on write
questions.correctAnswer: Virtual page shared, physical copied only on write
questions.explanation: Efficient fork

questions.question: How much memory does a 64-entry TLB cover with 2MB huge pages?
options: 256 KB across all supported platforms in standa
4 GB as defined in the specification in most
questions.correctAnswer: 128 MB (64 × 2MB) — 512× more than 4KB pages
questions.explanation: 2MB pages = 21 offset bits (vs 12). One TLB entry covers 512× more. 64 entries × 2MB = 128MB. 1GB pages → 64GB coverage.

questions.question: What is NUMA and why does it matter?
options: A CPU brand as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Non-Uniform Memory Access — on multi-socket systems, each CPU has local RAM (~100ns) and remote RAM (~150-250ns)
A type of cache in most implementations within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A page size under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: Non-Uniform Memory Access — on multi-socket systems, each CPU has local RAM (~100ns) and remote RAM (~150-250ns)
questions.explanation: Multi-socket: each CPU socket has its own memory controller. Local access fast, remote access 1.5-2.5× slower via inter-socket link.

questions.question: What is Linux's first-touch memory allocation policy?
options: First thread gets all memory in most implementations with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Physical page allocated from the NUMA node of the thread that FIRST writes to it — placement depends on which thread touches it
Random allocation in most implementations with additional processing for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Physical page allocated from the NUMA node of the thread that FIRST writes to it — placement depends on which thread touches it
questions.explanation: First-touch: if main thread allocates on socket 0, then worker on socket 1 accesses → ALL accesses are remote (slow). Touch from correct thread.

questions.question: What is the OOM Killer?
options: A debugging tool for typical use cases
When kernel can't satisfy allocation (no RAM, no swap), it kills a process based on oom_score heuristics
A type of mutex under normal conditions under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A memory leak detector via the core framework in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: When kernel can't satisfy allocation (no RAM, no swap), it kills a process based on oom_score heuristics
questions.explanation: OOM Killer = last resort. Assigns oom_score (higher = more likely to die). Kills highest-scoring process. No negotiation.

questions.question: What is memory overcommit on Linux?
options: Using too much RAM by default behavior with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
malloc succeeds even when RAM+swap can't cover the request — kernel promises memory, only commits on first write
A memory leak through the built-in API with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A kernel bug through the built-in API
questions.correctAnswer: malloc succeeds even when RAM+swap can't cover the request — kernel promises memory, only commits on first write
questions.explanation: vm.overcommit_memory=0 (heuristic). malloc(10GB) on 512MB machine → non-NULL! OOM Killer handles when you actually use it.

questions.question: What page sizes does x86-64 support besides 4 KB?
questions.correctAnswer: 2 MB (huge pages) and 1 GB (gigantic pages)
questions.explanation: x86-64 supports 4 KB (standard), 2 MB (huge page), and 1 GB (gigantic page) page sizes.

questions.question: How many TLB entries are needed to cover 1 GB with 2 MB pages?
questions.correctAnswer: 512 entries (1 GB ÷ 2 MB)
questions.explanation: To cover 1 GB: 4 KB pages need 262,144 TLB entries. 2 MB pages need only 512 entries. 1 GB pages need 1 entry.

questions.question: How many TLB entries are needed to cover 1 GB with 4 KB pages?
questions.correctAnswer: 262,144 entries (1 GB ÷ 4 KB)
questions.explanation: 1 GB ÷ 4 KB = 262,144 TLB entries needed — far more than the 64-entry L1 D-TLB can hold. This causes massive TLB thrashing.

questions.question: How does Linux Transparent Huge Pages (THP) work?
questions.correctAnswer: The kernel automatically promotes contiguous 4 KB pages to 2 MB huge pages without application changes, if physically contiguous memory is available
questions.explanation: THP automatically tries to promote 512 contiguous 4 KB pages into a single 2 MB huge page. No application changes needed.

questions.question: What is the THP tradeoff regarding memory bloat?
questions.correctAnswer: A 2 MB page is all-or-nothing. If your process uses only 10 KB of it, 2,038 KB is wasted. With 4 KB pages, only 4 KB is wasted per unused page
acceptableAnswers: 2MB all-or-nothing wastes space for small use
2MB page wastes memory if partially used
questions.explanation: A 2 MB huge page used for 10 KB wastes 2,038 KB. With 4 KB pages, you'd only waste 4 KB per unused page. This is 'internal fragmentation.'

questions.question: What is the THP compaction latency problem?
questions.correctAnswer: The kernel's memory compaction daemon physically moves pages to create contiguous 2 MB blocks, causing unpredictable 50-500 ms latency spikes
acceptableAnswers: kernel moves pages causing unpredictable pauses
questions.explanation: The kernel runs memory compaction to create contiguous 2 MB blocks. This involves physically moving pages, causing 50-500 ms pauses. Many latency-sensitive apps disable THP.

questions.question: Why might a game server operator disable THP?
questions.correctAnswer: A random 200 ms compaction pause is worse for gameplay than a consistent 5% TLB miss overhead
acceptableAnswers: unpredictable pause worse than TLB miss overhead
200 ms spike ruins gameplay more than steady 5% TLB misses
compaction latency spikes vs consistent overhead
questions.explanation: A 200 ms pause at 60 FPS drops 12 frames. A 5% TLB miss overhead drops FPS from 60 to 57. The spike is worse for real-time games.

questions.question: What is hugetlbfs (explicit huge pages)?
questions.correctAnswer: A mechanism to pre-allocate huge pages at boot time and use them explicitly via mmap with MAP_HUGETLB — avoids compaction latency
acceptableAnswers: explicit huge page pool via MAP_HUGETLB
questions.explanation: hugetlbfs pre-reserves huge pages at boot. Use mmap with MAP_HUGETLB to allocate from the pool. No compaction latency, but requires pre-planning.

questions.question: How do you reserve 1024 huge pages of 2 MB each?
questions.correctAnswer: echo 1024 > /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages
questions.explanation: Writing to /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages reserves huge pages. `echo 1024 > nr_hugepages` reserves 1024 × 2 MB = 2 GB of huge pages.

questions.question: What does NUMA stand for and when does it matter?
questions.correctAnswer: Non-Uniform Memory Access — matters on multi-socket systems where each CPU has its own local RAM with different access latency than remote RAM
acceptableAnswers: NUMA different latency to local vs remote RAM
questions.explanation: NUMA = Non-Uniform Memory Access. On multi-socket systems, accessing local RAM (~100 ns) is much faster than remote RAM (~150-250 ns) on another socket.

questions.question: What is the latency for local NUMA access vs remote NUMA access?
questions.correctAnswer: Local: ~100 ns. Remote: ~150-250 ns (1.5-2.5× slower, reduced bandwidth)
questions.explanation: Local access: Core reading its socket's RAM → ~100 ns. Remote access: Core reading another socket's RAM → ~150-250 ns, with reduced bandwidth.

questions.question: What is Linux's first-touch NUMA allocation policy?
questions.correctAnswer: When a page is first written to, it's allocated from the NUMA node that the writing thread is running on
acceptableAnswers: page allocated on node of writing thread
first-touch: pages allocated on writer's node
memory allocated from the accessing thread's socket
questions.explanation: First-touch policy: the physical page is allocated from the NUMA node of whatever thread first writes to it. This can cause all memory to land on one socket if the initializing thread isn't careful.

questions.question: What is the first-touch NUMA trap for multi-threaded applications?
questions.correctAnswer: If a single thread allocates and initializes a large array, all pages land on that thread's NUMA node — worker threads on other nodes pay remote access latency
acceptableAnswers: all pages on allocator's node workers remote
single-threaded init causes remote access for workers
questions.explanation: If the main thread on socket 0 allocates and initializes a 32 GB array, all pages land on socket 0's RAM. Workers on socket 1 pay ~2x latency for every access.

questions.question: How do you fix the first-touch NUMA trap?
questions.correctAnswer: Initialize the data in parallel — each worker thread touches its portion of the array, causing pages to be allocated on the worker's local NUMA node
acceptableAnswers: parallel init so each thread touches its pages locally
use OMP parallel for to initialize on target nodes
each worker initializes its own data portion
questions.explanation: Use parallel initialization (e.g., #pragma omp parallel for) where each thread touches the pages it will use — first-touch allocates them locally.

questions.question: What does the OOM Killer do?
questions.correctAnswer: When the kernel truly can't satisfy a memory allocation, it selects a process based on oom_score heuristics and sends SIGKILL
acceptableAnswers: kills a process based on oom_score
selects victim by heuristics sends SIGKILL
arbitrates by killing highest scoring process
questions.explanation: The OOM Killer activates when RAM is exhausted, no clean pages to evict, and swap is full. It assigns every process an oom_score and kills the highest-scoring one.

questions.question: What three factors increase a process's oom_score?
questions.correctAnswer: High memory usage, recently started (less investment), and low nice priority (lower priority = higher score)
acceptableAnswers: high memory usage recent start low priority
memory usage startup time nice value
large RAM recently launched low nice
questions.explanation: Higher oom_score = more likely killed. Factors: large memory usage → higher, recently started → higher, low nice → higher, many children → higher.

questions.question: How do you protect a critical process from the OOM killer?
questions.correctAnswer: echo -1000 > /proc/$pid/oom_score_adj (sets the process as highly protected)
questions.explanation: Writing -1000 to /proc/$pid/oom_score_adj marks the process as highly protected. Writing +1000 makes it the first to die.

questions.question: What does vm.overcommit_memory=2 mean?
questions.correctAnswer: Never overcommit — malloc returns NULL if RAM+swap can't cover the request
acceptableAnswers: no overcommit malloc fails if insufficient
questions.explanation: vm.overcommit_memory=2: strict mode. The kernel refuses allocations that exceed RAM+swap. malloc returns NULL. Programs that don't check malloc return values will SEGFAULT.

questions.question: How does Unreal Engine handle memory management to avoid OS-level OOM?
questions.correctAnswer: It pre-allocates large blocks from the OS at startup and sub-allocates internally — the engine knows exactly how much physical memory is committed
acceptableAnswers: engine controls its own memory allocation pools
questions.explanation: UE uses custom allocators (FMallocBinned) that pre-allocate large OS blocks and sub-allocate internally. It tracks all memory through LLM and can degrade gracefully.

questions.question: What are the four steps of the boot process?
questions.correctAnswer: 1) UEFI/BIOS initializes hardware, 2) Bootloader reads kernel into RAM, 3) Kernel creates page tables and enables paging, 4) init launches user-space
questions.explanation: 1) UEFI/BIOS firmware, 2) Bootloader (GRUB) loads kernel, 3) Kernel enables paging (sets CR3), 4) init/systemd launches user space.

questions.question: At what point in the boot process does virtual memory become active?
questions.correctAnswer: Step 3 — when the kernel creates the real page tables and enables paging by setting the CR3 register
acceptableAnswers: when kernel sets CR3 enables paging
questions.explanation: After the kernel takes over from the bootloader, it creates real page tables and enables paging by setting CR3. From this point, every address is virtual.

questions.question: What is the 1 GB (gigantic) page requirement?
questions.correctAnswer: CPU must support pdpe1gb flag (most server CPUs since ~2012, modern desktop CPUs). Must be reserved at boot — kernel can't create them on the fly
acceptableAnswers: pdpe1gb CPU feature reserved at boot
reserved at boot needs pdpe1gb support
questions.explanation: 1 GB pages require the pdpe1gb CPU feature. They must be reserved at boot — the kernel can't allocate 1 GB of contiguous physical memory on the fly.

questions.question: What is the inter-socket link called on Intel and AMD?
questions.correctAnswer: Intel: UPI (Ultra Path Interconnect), AMD: Infinity Fabric
acceptableAnswers: Intel Ultra Path Interconnect AMD Infinity Fabric
questions.explanation: Intel uses UPI (Ultra Path Interconnect) and AMD uses Infinity Fabric to connect sockets. Remote NUMA access traverses these links.

questions.question: What is the `numa_alloc_onnode()` function used for?
questions.correctAnswer: To explicitly allocate memory from a specific NUMA node rather than relying on first-touch policy
acceptableAnswers: allocate memory on specific NUMA node
questions.explanation: numa_alloc_onnode() bypasses first-touch by explicitly requesting memory from a specific NUMA node. Useful when you know which threads will use which memory.

questions.question: What does UE5's LLM (Low-Level Memory Tracker) do?
questions.correctAnswer: Provides precise visibility into where RAM is going — tracks allocations per system (textures, meshes, physics, audio, etc.)
acceptableAnswers: Low Level Memory tracker for categories
questions.explanation: LLM tracks memory per system (textures, meshes, physics, audio) with minimal overhead, enabling precise memory budgeting and graceful degradation.

questions.question: What happens to unused RAM in the page cache?
questions.correctAnswer: The kernel says 'unused RAM is wasted RAM' and fills it with disk cache — it's instantly evictable when applications need it
acceptableAnswers: used for disk cache instantly evictable
kernel caches disk data in unused RAM
questions.explanation: The kernel fills all free RAM with disk cache. When an application needs memory, clean cache pages are instantly evicted. High 'in use' RAM is often just productive caching.

questions.question: How can you check if your CPU supports 1 GB pages?
questions.correctAnswer: Check /proc/cpuinfo for pdpe1gb flag
questions.explanation: The pdpe1gb CPU flag indicates 1 GB page support. Most server CPUs since ~2012 and modern desktop CPUs have it.

questions.question: What is the maximum page size on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: 1 GB
questions.explanation: x86-64 supports: 4 KB (default), 2 MB (huge page), and 1 GB (gigantic page). 1 GB is the maximum.

questions.question: How does the kernel allocate physical memory for kernel operations?
questions.correctAnswer: Through the page allocator (buddy system) which allocates physical pages in powers of 2
questions.explanation: The kernel's buddy system allocator manages physical memory in powers-of-2 page blocks to minimize fragmentation.

questions.question: What is NUMA node interleaving?
questions.correctAnswer: Memory allocated in a round-robin fashion across all NUMA nodes — no locality benefit but ensures even distribution
questions.explanation: NUMA interleaving allocates pages from each node in turn. Useful for workloads that access the entire array uniformly from all cores.

questions.question: What happens when THP compaction fails to create a 2 MB page?
options: The kernel falls back to 4 KB pages
questions.correctAnswer: The kernel falls back to 4 KB pages
questions.explanation: If memory compaction can't find a contiguous 2 MB block, THP defragmentation falls back to regular 4 KB pages for the allocation.

questions.question: What is a 'memory compaction' daemon?
questions.correctAnswer: A kernel background thread (kswapd could trigger it) that physically moves pages to create larger contiguous free blocks
acceptableAnswers: kernel moves pages for contiguous blocks
questions.explanation: Compaction moves allocated pages to create contiguous free areas. This is what causes the 50-500 ms latency spikes with THP.

questions.question: What is the swap + RAM relationship in memory overcommit?
questions.correctAnswer: Overcommit = 0: kernel checks if RAM + swap can satisfy the request. Types: 0=heuristic, 1=always, 2=never
questions.explanation: vm.overcommit_memory=0 (default): heuristic overcommit estimates if RAM+swap can cover. 1: always yes. 2: never overcommit.

questions.question: What does the OOM killer do if the killed process doesn't free enough memory?
options: It kills the same process again
It kills additional processes until memory is freed
It gives up and returns an error
questions.correctAnswer: It kills additional processes until memory is freed
questions.explanation: After killing a process, if insufficient memory is freed, the OOM killer selects the next highest-scoring process and continues until the allocation can succeed.

questions.question: What is the boot process step between firmware and operating system?
questions.correctAnswer: The bootloader (GRUB/Windows Boot Manager) reads the kernel image from disk into RAM
questions.explanation: After UEFI/BIOS initializes hardware, the bootloader reads the kernel image from disk into RAM and sets up initial page tables.

questions.question: What does init/systemd do during boot?
questions.correctAnswer: Launches user-space: login screen, desktop environment, services, and daemons
questions.explanation: After the kernel activates paging and filesystems, init/systemd is spawned as the first user process to launch the rest of user space.

questions.question: What is the total downstream bandwidth for a 64-player game at 30 bytes/player at 60 Hz?
questions.explanation: 64 players × 30 bytes/update × 60 updates/s = 115,200 B/s ≈ 112 KB/s. With UDP overhead (~28 bytes), total ≈ 64 × 58 × 60 = ~222 KB/s.

questions.question: Huge page sizes (Linux):
questions.correctAnswer: 2MB and 1GB
questions.explanation: Reduces TLB misses

questions.question: TLB miss reduction with huge pages:
options: Fewer entries needed for large data sets
questions.correctAnswer: Fewer entries needed for large data sets
questions.explanation: 1 huge page = 512 regular pages

questions.question: NUMA:
options: Non-Uniform Memory Access (memory local to core is faster)
questions.correctAnswer: Non-Uniform Memory Access (memory local to core is faster)
questions.explanation: Access time depends on distance

questions.question: NUMA node:
questions.correctAnswer: CPU core(s) + local memory controller
questions.explanation: Access local memory is faster

questions.question: numactl:
questions.correctAnswer: Controls NUMA memory policy
questions.explanation: Bind/Interleave memory

questions.question: Transparent Huge Pages:
questions.correctAnswer: Kernel automatically promotes pages to 2MB
questions.explanation: THP: no application changes needed

questions.question: Memory interleaving:
questions.correctAnswer: Spread memory across NUMA nodes
questions.explanation: Balances bandwidth

questions.question: Boot process memory init:
options: Kernel initializes page tables, identity maps, enables long mode
questions.correctAnswer: Kernel initializes page tables, identity maps, enables long mode
questions.explanation: From bootloader kernel entry

questions.question: mm_init:
questions.correctAnswer: Kernel memory management subsystem init
questions.explanation: Phase 3 kernel boot

questions.question: What are the stages of compilation in order?
questions.correctAnswer: Preprocessor, Compiler (front-end, optimizer, back-end), Assembler, Linker
questions.explanation: .cpp → preprocessor (.i) → compiler: front-end (AST), optimizer (IR), back-end (assembly) → assembler (.o) → linker (executable).

questions.question: What does the compiler front-end do?
options: Optimizes through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxx
Lexing, parsing, semantic analysis — produces AST and IR from C++ source
Generates machine code in most implementations in most practical scenarios
questions.correctAnswer: Lexing, parsing, semantic analysis — produces AST and IR from C++ source
questions.explanation: Front-end: lexer → tokens, parser → AST, semantic analysis → typed AST, IR generation. This is where C++ syntax errors are caught.

questions.question: What does the optimizer do?
options: Transforms IR to faster equivalent: inlining, dead code elimination, loop unrolling, constant folding, vectorization
Generates assembly via the core framework for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Links libraries across all supported platforms with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Transforms IR to faster equivalent: inlining, dead code elimination, loop unrolling, constant folding, vectorization
questions.explanation: Optimizer works on IR (GIMPLE/LLVM IR). Most passes are target-independent. -O0 skips most; -O2 is standard; -O3 is aggressive.

questions.question: What does the linker do?
options: Compiles across all supported platforms across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Resolves symbols: connects each 'require' (undefined symbol) to a 'provide' (definition) across .o files and libraries
Generates assembly through the built-in API with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Resolves symbols: connects each 'require' (undefined symbol) to a 'provide' (definition) across .o files and libraries
questions.explanation: Each .o has symbol table: exports (defines) and imports (references). Linker matches them. Static: copies code. Dynamic: PLT/GOT stubs.

questions.question: What is an object file (.o)?
options: Binary machine code + symbol table + relocation entries — not yet executable
Assembly text using standard engine approaches under normal operationxxxxxxxxxx
An executable for typical use cases with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Binary machine code + symbol table + relocation entries — not yet executable
questions.explanation: .o contains compiled code but unresolved symbols. Linker combines .o files, resolves relocations, produces executable.

questions.question: What is GIMPLE and why does GCC use it?
options: GIMPLE is a three-address code IR that breaks complex expressions into simple operations with at most two operands, enabling simpler optimization passes
GIMPLE is the assembly output of the GCC compiler
GIMPLE is GCC's debug output format
GIMPLE is a C++ standard library component
questions.correctAnswer: GIMPLE is a three-address code IR that breaks complex expressions into simple operations with at most two operands, enabling simpler optimization passes
questions.explanation: GIMPLE (GNU IMPLEmentation Language) decomposes every expression into a sequence of simple three-address statements: each operation takes at most two operands and produces one result. This makes data-flow analysis, dead code elimination, and constant propagation much simpler to implement — every optimization pass works on the same canonical IR format.

questions.question: What does the preprocessor do with an `#include` directive?
options: It reads the file and checks for errors
It literally copies the entire contents of the file into the source at the point of inclusion
It compiles the included file separately
It creates a link to the included file at runtime
questions.correctAnswer: It literally copies the entire contents of the file into the source at the point of inclusion
questions.explanation: The preprocessor finds the file (e.g., iostream) in the system include path and copies the entire file into your source. A single `#include <iostream>` can expand to ~30,000+ lines.

questions.question: Why is `#define BAD_SQ(x) x * x` dangerous when called as `BAD_SQ(5 + 1)`?
options: It uses too much memory during preprocessing
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: The preprocessor performs pure text substitution. `BAD_SQ(5+1)` expands to `5 + 1 * 5 + 1`, which is parsed by the compiler as `5 + (1*5) + 1 = 11`. Defensive parentheses like `#define SQUARE(x) ((x)*(x))` fix this.

questions.question: What is the correct order of stages in the compilation pipeline?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: The pipeline is: Preprocessor (text transformation), Compiler (C++ → assembly), Assembler (assembly → object file), Linker (object files → executable).

questions.question: What is the output of the compiler (Phase 2) stage?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: The compiler translates C++ source into assembly text (.s). The assembler then converts assembly into machine code in an object file (.o/.obj).

questions.question: In GCC's middle-end, what is GIMPLE?
options: A high-level Intermediate Representation using three-address code — no more than one operator per statement
A type of object file section
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: GIMPLE (GNU IMPLEmentation Language) breaks complex expressions into simple three-address statements. For example, `x = a + b * c` becomes `t1 = b * c; x = a + t1;`.

questions.question: What does the assembler produce?
options: An object file containing machine code bytes, a symbol table, and relocation entries
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: The assembler translates assembly mnemonics into actual machine code bytes and packages them into an object file containing sections like `.text`, `.data`, `.bss`, `.symtab`, and `.rela.text`.

questions.question: Which optimization level enables function inlining, CSE, loop optimizations, and auto-vectorization?
questions.explanation: -O2 enables inlining, CSE, loop optimizations, auto-vectorization, instruction scheduling, and strict aliasing. It's the standard optimization level for production code.

questions.question: What does Dead Code Elimination (DCE) do?
options: Removes computations whose results are never used
questions.correctAnswer: Removes computations whose results are never used
questions.explanation: DCE removes computations whose results are never used, such as unused variables and unreachable branches. This reduces code size and execution time.

questions.question: Why can `-O3` sometimes be slower than `-O2`?
options: Aggressive loop unrolling and inlining can bloat code size past the L1 instruction cache (32 KB), causing I-cache misses that wipe out performance gains
The compiler adds extra debugging symbols
It uses more memory and triggers swapping
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: At -O3, aggressive unrolling and inlining increase binary size. If the hot code no longer fits in the L1 instruction cache, every iteration triggers an I-cache miss, potentially making it slower than -O2.

questions.question: What is the main Intermediate Representation used by Clang/LLVM?
questions.correctAnswer: C
questions.explanation: Clang/LLVM uses LLVM IR, which is a typed Static Single Assignment (SSA) form. GCC uses GIMPLE → RTL. MSVC uses a proprietary internal backend IR via c2.dll.

questions.question: What does `g++ -c main.cpp -o main.o` do?
options: Stops after assembly, producing an object file
questions.correctAnswer: C
questions.explanation: The `-c` flag tells g++ to stop after the assembler stage, producing an object file (.o). The linker has not yet run, so the object file contains unresolved symbol references.

questions.question: What is Constant Folding?
options: Evaluating constant expressions at compile time, e.g., `60 * 60 * 24` → `86400`
A runtime optimization that caches function results
Reordering basic blocks for better branch prediction
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Constant folding evaluates constant expressions at compile time rather than runtime. The compiler replaces `60 * 60 * 24` with the computed value `86400`.

questions.question: In register allocation, what happens when there are more virtual registers than physical registers (16 GPRs on x86-64)?
options: The compiler uses SIMD registers instead
The program is split into multiple translation units
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Register allocation is NP-complete; compilers use graph-coloring heuristics. When the number of live variables exceeds physical registers, some variables are 'spilled' — stored to stack slots and loaded when needed.

questions.question: Which IR level does GCC use that models CPU registers and memory addresses abstractly?
questions.correctAnswer: RTL (Register Transfer Language)
questions.explanation: RTL (Register Transfer Language) is GCC's lower-level IR that models CPU registers, memory addresses, and machine instructions abstractly. The backend walks RTL to match CPU instruction patterns.

questions.question: What are relocation entries in an object file?
options: Entries that mark the file for relocation to a different folder
Entries that define which functions are exported
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Relocation entries record places in the machine code where placeholder addresses need to be patched once the linker determines the final addresses of symbols from other object files or libraries.

questions.question: Which compiler uses `c2.dll` for its backend?
questions.correctAnswer: C
questions.explanation: MSVC uses a proprietary internal IR handled by its own backend (`c2.dll`). Unlike GCC (GIMPLE/RTL) or Clang (LLVM IR), there is no exposed intermediate format comparable to LLVM IR or GIMPLE.

questions.question: What does the GCC flag `-fopt-info-vec` do?
options: Reports which loops were vectorized and why others were not
questions.correctAnswer: C
questions.explanation: `-fopt-info-vec` tells GCC to report which loops were successfully vectorized, and which were not (with reasons). It's a diagnostic tool for understanding auto-vectorization behavior.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)?
options: Computes identical subexpressions once instead of recomputing them
Eliminates common header files from includes
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: CSE detects subexpressions that appear multiple times with identical operands and computes them once, reusing the result. For example, if `a*b` appears twice, it's computed once and stored.

questions.question: What are the three stages inside the compiler (Phase 2)?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: The compiler has three stages: Front-end (lexing, parsing, semantic analysis), Middle-end (IR creation and optimization), and Back-end (instruction selection, register allocation, code emission).

questions.question: How does Loop-Invariant Code Motion optimize code?
options: It moves the loop counter outside the loop
It changes the order of loop iterations
It converts while loops to for loops
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Loop-invariant code motion hoists calculations whose result doesn't change across iterations (like `sqrt(2.0)`) out of the loop body, executing them once before the loop instead of on every iteration.

questions.question: What does Strength Reduction do in compiler optimization?
options: Replaces expensive operations with cheaper equivalents, e.g., `i*8` → `i << 3`
Reduces the number of variables in a function
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Strength reduction replaces expensive operations with cheaper ones. For example, integer multiplication by a constant can be replaced by bit shifts (`i*8` → `i << 3`), and array index multiplication can be replaced by pointer addition.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT performed during the preprocessor phase?
questions.correctAnswer: C
questions.explanation: Template instantiation happens during the compiler's front-end phase (semantic analysis), not during preprocessing. The preprocessor only handles text transformation — `#include`, `#define`, `#ifdef`, etc.

questions.question: What does the `.bss` section in an object file contain?
options: Uninitialized data — stored as just a size with no bytes in the file
questions.correctAnswer: C
questions.explanation: The `.bss` section holds zero-initialized data. It's stored as just a size requirement (no actual bytes in the file) because all values are known to be zero. At load time, the OS allocates and zeros the memory.

questions.question: What does `-Os` optimize for?
options: Binary size — it applies `-O2` minus size-increasing passes and prefers smaller instruction encodings
questions.correctAnswer: C
questions.explanation: `-Os` optimizes for size. It uses `-O2` level optimizations but disables those that increase code size. It prefers smaller instruction encodings and avoids loop unrolling that would bloat the binary.

questions.question: In LLVM IR, what naming convention is used?
questions.explanation: LLVM IR uses Static Single Assignment (SSA) form, where each variable is assigned exactly once, and new versions of variables (e.g., `%add`, `%sub`, `%result`) are created for each assignment.

questions.question: What tool or command stops the GCC pipeline after the preprocessor stage?
questions.explanation: `g++ -E main.cpp -o main.i` runs only the preprocessor and outputs the expanded source. The compiler, assembler, and linker are skipped.

questions.question: What does the `##` operator do in C preprocessor macros?
questions.explanation: The `##` operator (token pasting) concatenates two tokens during macro expansion. For example, `#define CONCAT(a, b) a ## b` makes `CONCAT(var, 1)` expand to `var1`.

questions.question: Why is C++ parsing considered notoriously hard?
questions.explanation: C++ has a context-sensitive grammar. For example, `A * B` could be multiplication of two variables OR a pointer declaration — the parser must consult the symbol table to decide, requiring recursive descent with backtracking and semantic feedback.

questions.question: What is the role of instruction scheduling in the compiler back-end?
acceptableAnswers: reorder instructions to keep the CPU pipeline full
reorder instructions to avoid pipeline stalls
questions.explanation: Instruction scheduling reorders instructions to keep the CPU's execution pipeline full, accounting for instruction latencies. For example, it avoids using the result of `imul` (3-cycle latency) in the very next instruction.

questions.question: What is the Itanium C++ ABI and which compilers use it?
acceptableAnswers: Itanium C++ ABI is used by GCC and Clang on Linux
GCC and Clang use Itanium ABI
questions.explanation: GCC and Clang use the Itanium C++ ABI on Linux (and macOS for Clang). It defines name mangling, exception handling, and class layout conventions. MSVC uses its own ABI.

questions.question: How does the preprocessor treat excluded code blocks in `#ifdef`/`#endif`?
acceptableAnswers: the excluded code never reaches the compiler
questions.explanation: Code inside an `#ifdef` block whose condition is false is excluded by the preprocessor — it never reaches the compiler. This is how platform-specific code compiles on one target and vanishes on another.

questions.question: What does the `#line` preprocessor directive do?
acceptableAnswers: changes the reported line number in compiler diagnostics
modifies the line number for error reporting
questions.explanation: `#line` changes the reported line number (and optionally filename) that the compiler uses in diagnostics. It's used by code generators (like yacc/bison) so error messages reference the original source file, not the generated one.

questions.question: In the trace of `int x = 5; x = x + 1;` through the pipeline with `-O2`, what does the optimized IR produce?
questions.explanation: With -O2, the compiler constant-folds `5+1=6` and eliminates the variable entirely. The optimized IR becomes just `ret i32 6` — no variable needed.

questions.question: What hex bytes represent `mov eax, 6; ret` on x86-64?
questions.explanation: `B8` = mov eax, imm32; `06 00 00 00` = 6 in little-endian; `C3` = ret (near return).

questions.question: What is Auto-Vectorization?
acceptableAnswers: converting scalar loop operations to SIMD instructions
using SIMD to process multiple elements per instruction
questions.explanation: Auto-vectorization converts scalar loop operations to SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) instructions, processing multiple elements at once — e.g., processing 4 floats at once with SSE instructions.

questions.question: What is the `.symtab` section in an object file?
acceptableAnswers: the symbol table listing functions and variables defined or referenced
questions.explanation: `.symtab` is the symbol table section containing entries for functions and variables that are either defined in this object file or referenced from elsewhere. Each entry has a name, value (offset), size, type, and binding.

questions.question: What is the difference between how GCC and Clang handle Link-Time Optimization?
acceptableAnswers: GCC uses -flto with GIMPLE in .o files, Clang uses -flto=thin with LLVM bitcode
GCC GIMPLE LTO vs Clang ThinLTO
questions.explanation: GCC's `-flto` embeds GIMPLE IR in object files for cross-module optimization. Clang's `-flto=thin` (ThinLTO) uses LLVM bitcode and parallelizes LTO by partitioning the module graph for faster builds.

questions.question: What does UHT (Unreal Header Tool) do in UE's compilation pipeline?
acceptableAnswers: parses UCLASS/UPROPERTY/UFUNCTION macros and generates .generated.h and .gen.cpp files
questions.explanation: UHT runs BEFORE the standard compiler pipeline. It parses `UCLASS()`, `UPROPERTY()`, `UFUNCTION()` macros and generates `.generated.h` and `.gen.cpp` files containing reflection metadata, serialization code, and other boilerplate.

questions.question: What is the recommended modern C++ alternative to `#define MAX_HEALTH 100`?
questions.explanation: `constexpr int MAX_HEALTH = 100;` gives you type checking, namespace scoping, and debugger visibility. Macros provide none of these. Valid remaining macro uses: include guards, platform detection, and certain code-generation patterns.

questions.question: What is the difference between `.text` and `.data` sections in an object file?
acceptableAnswers: .text contains executable machine code, .data contains initialized global/static variables
questions.explanation: `.text` contains the actual CPU instructions (machine code). `.data` contains initialized global and static variables with their initial values. `.bss` contains zero-initialized data.

questions.question: In which component of UE does UBT read `.Build.cs` files?
questions.explanation: UBT reads `.Build.cs` files (defining module dependencies and compiler flags) and `.Target.cs` files (defining build configurations). It constructs a module dependency graph and invokes the platform compiler with correct flags.

questions.question: Why do GCC, Clang, and MSVC produce different code for the same C++ source?
acceptableAnswers: different IR design, optimization pass ordering, register allocator heuristics, and instruction selection models
different compiler architectures and optimization strategies
questions.explanation: While front-ends parse identically, the IR design (GIMPLE/RTL vs LLVM IR vs MSVC internal), optimization pass ordering, register allocator heuristics, and instruction selection models differ. Performance deltas of 5-20% at the same `-O2` are common.

questions.question: What does Function Inlining optimization do?
acceptableAnswers: copies the function body to the call site, eliminating call overhead
replaces a function call with the function's body code
questions.explanation: Function inlining copies the callee's body into the caller at each call site, eliminating the `call`/`ret` overhead and enabling further optimizations across the merged code (constant folding, CSE, etc.).

questions.question: What is Loop Unrolling and what is its primary benefit?
acceptableAnswers: replicating the loop body to reduce branch overhead per element
duplicating loop iterations to reduce the number of branches and loop counter updates
questions.explanation: Loop unrolling replicates the loop body multiple times, reducing the number of iterations and thus the branch/loop-counter overhead. For a 1000-iteration loop unrolled 4×, you get 250 iterations with 4× the work each.

questions.question: In the LLVM IR shown (`%add = add i32 %a, %b`), what does `i32` represent?
acceptableAnswers: a 32-bit integer type in LLVM IR
questions.explanation: In LLVM IR, `i32` denotes a 32-bit integer type. The IR is strongly typed. `%add = add i32 %a, %b` means: add 32-bit integers %a and %b, store result in SSA value %add.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the stringizing operator `#` in C preprocessor macros?
acceptableAnswers: converts a macro parameter to a string literal
questions.explanation: The `#` operator in a macro definition converts the parameter into a quoted string literal. For example, `#define STR(x) #x` makes `STR(hello)` expand to `"hello"`.

questions.question: How does the compiler handle the instruction `lea eax, [rdi + rdi*4]`?
acceptableAnswers: it computes eax = edi * 5 using the Address Generation Unit (AGU), not the integer multiplier
lea avoids the multiplier for small constants
questions.explanation: The LEA instruction uses the CPU's Address Generation Interconnect (AGU) to compute effective addresses. `lea eax, [rdi + rdi*4]` computes `eax = edi * 5` with 1-cycle latency vs `imul`'s 3-4 cycles.

questions.question: What does the `#error` preprocessor directive do?
acceptableAnswers: forces a compile-time error with a custom message
questions.explanation: `#error "message"` forces the preprocessor to emit an error with the given message, halting compilation. It's commonly used in `#else` branches to catch unsupported platform configurations.

questions.question: What is the Debug info format used by GCC on Linux?
questions.explanation: GCC uses DWARF for debug information on Linux. MSVC uses PDB (Program Database) format, and Clang supports both DWARF (Linux) and CodeView (Windows).

questions.question: What are the three categories of LLVM IR? (hint: they correspond to different levels of detail)
questions.explanation: LLVM IR exists in three forms: (1) in-memory during compilation, (2) bitcode (.bc) for serialization, and (3) human-readable text (.ll). They are equivalent representations.

questions.question: What is a translation unit in C++ compilation?
acceptableAnswers: a source file after preprocessing, including all #included headers
questions.explanation: A translation unit is the result of preprocessing a single source file. All `#include` directives have been resolved, macro substitutions applied, and conditional compilation blocks selected. The compiler then parses this single expanded file.

questions.question: Why does GCC use both GIMPLE and RTL IRs?
acceptableAnswers: GIMPLE is high-level for architecture-independent optimizations, RTL is low-level for machine-specific optimizations
two-level IR design for separating platform-independent and platform-dependent passes
questions.explanation: GCC uses GIMPLE as a high-level IR for architecture-independent optimizations (DCE, CSE, inlining) and RTL as a low-level IR that models CPU registers and instructions for machine-specific optimizations (instruction selection, register allocation, scheduling).

questions.question: What command-line flag would you use to get a vectorization report from Clang?
questions.explanation: Clang uses `-Rpass=loop-vectorize` to report successfully vectorized loops and `-Rpass-missed=loop-vectorize` to report why loops were NOT vectorized.

questions.question: What is the `.rela.text` section in an ELF object file?
acceptableAnswers: relocation entries that tell the linker to patch addresses in the .text section
relocation table for the code section
questions.explanation: `.rela.text` contains relocation entries for the `.text` (code) section. Each entry specifies an offset in `.text` where the linker must patch an address once the target symbol's final location is known.

questions.question: What does an AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) represent in the compiler?
acceptableAnswers: the program's grammatical structure as a tree
the parsed representation of source code structure
questions.explanation: The AST captures the program's grammatical structure as a tree. For example, `int x = a + b * 2` becomes a declaration node with a binary operation subtree, capturing the full syntactic structure of the expression.

questions.question: What is the purpose of `#pragma once`?
acceptableAnswers: ensures the header file is included only once per translation unit
questions.explanation: `#pragma once` tells the compiler to include the file only once per translation unit, similar to traditional include guards (`#ifndef HEADER_H` / `#define HEADER_H` / `#endif`). It's simpler but less portable than full include guards.

questions.question: How does the compiler handle register allocation, and why is it described as NP-complete?
acceptableAnswers: maps virtual registers to physical registers using graph-coloring heuristics
graph coloring heuristic for NP-complete register mapping
questions.explanation: Register allocation maps the unlimited IR virtual registers to the 16 physical x86-64 GPRs. It's NP-complete because it's equivalent to graph coloring — finding the minimum number of colors (registers) needed so that interfering variables (adjacent nodes) don't share a color.

questions.question: What happens during semantic analysis in the compiler front-end?
acceptableAnswers: validates meaning — type compatibility, declaration checks, function overload resolution, template instantiation
questions.explanation: Semantic analysis walks the AST and validates meaning: Are types compatible? Is every variable declared? Does the function overload resolve? This is where template instantiation fires, generating concrete code from template definitions.

questions.question: What are the three object file formats for Linux, macOS, and Windows?
questions.explanation: Linux uses ELF (Executable and Linkable Format), macOS/iOS uses Mach-O, and Windows uses COFF inside PE (Portable Executable). Object file extensions are `.o`, `.o`, and `.obj` respectively.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the MSVC `/GL` flag in the context of LTO?
acceptableAnswers: enables Whole Program Optimization by generating instrumented object files for link-time code generation
questions.explanation: MSVC's `/GL` (Whole Program Optimization) generates object files with special IR enabling cross-module optimization at link time. It pairs with `/LTCG` (Link-Time Code Generation) to perform the actual inter-procedural optimizations.

questions.question: What does the command `g++ -S main.cpp -o main.s` do?
acceptableAnswers: stops after the compiler stage, outputting assembly text
questions.explanation: The `-S` flag tells g++ to stop after the compiler stage (Phase 2), outputting assembly text (`.s` file). The assembler and linker are not invoked.

questions.question: What does the `g++ -c` flag do?
options: Stops after the assembler, producing an object file (.o)
questions.correctAnswer: C
questions.explanation: The `-c` flag tells g++ to stop after the assembler stage, producing an object file (.o). The linker has not yet run, so the object file contains unresolved symbol references.

questions.question: What happens during register spilling?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Register allocation uses graph-coloring heuristics. When the number of live variables exceeds physical registers, some are 'spilled' — stored to stack slots and loaded back when needed, adding ~5-10 cycles overhead per spill.

questions.question: What intermediate representation does GCC's backend use that models CPU registers and memory addresses?
questions.correctAnswer: RTL (Register Transfer Language)
questions.explanation: RTL (Register Transfer Language) is GCC's lower-level IR that models CPU registers, memory addresses, and machine instructions abstractly. The backend walks RTL to match CPU instruction patterns.

questions.question: What is Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)?
options: Computes identical subexpressions once instead of recomputing them — e.g., a*b appearing twice is computed once and reused
Eliminates common header files from includes
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: CSE detects subexpressions that appear multiple times with identical operands and computes them once, reusing the result. This reduces both code size and execution time.

questions.question: What is Loop-Invariant Code Motion?
options: It moves the loop counter outside the loop
It changes the order of loop iterations
It converts while loops to for loops
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Loop-invariant code motion hoists calculations whose result doesn't change across iterations out of the loop body, executing them once before the loop instead of on every iteration.

questions.question: What does the `.bss` section hold?
options: Uninitialized data — stored as just a size with no bytes in the file
questions.correctAnswer: C
questions.explanation: The `.bss` section holds zero-initialized data. It's stored as just a size requirement (no actual bytes in the file) because all values are known to be zero. At load time, the OS allocates and zeros the memory.

questions.question: What text representation format does LLVM IR use?
questions.explanation: LLVM IR exists in three equivalent forms: in-memory during compilation, bitcode (.bc) for serialization, and human-readable text (.ll).

questions.question: In the `int x=5; x=x+1;` trace with -O2, what does optimized IR produce?
questions.explanation: With -O2, the compiler constant-folds `5+1=6` and eliminates the variable entirely. The optimized IR becomes just `ret i32 6`.

questions.question: What transformation does Auto-Vectorization perform?
acceptableAnswers: converts scalar loop operations to SIMD instructions
using SIMD to process multiple elements per instruction
questions.explanation: Auto-vectorization converts scalar loop operations to SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) instructions, processing multiple elements at once — e.g., processing 4 floats at once with SSE instructions.

questions.question: What is the modern C++ alternative to `#define MAX_HEALTH 100`?
questions.explanation: `constexpr int MAX_HEALTH = 100;` gives you type checking, namespace scoping, and debugger visibility. Macros provide none of these.

questions.question: What is the difference between `.text` and `.data` sections?
acceptableAnswers: .text contains executable machine code, .data contains initialized global/static variables
questions.explanation: `.text` contains the actual CPU instructions (machine code). `.data` contains initialized global and static variables with their initial values.

questions.question: What is Loop Unrolling's primary benefit?
acceptableAnswers: reducing branch overhead by replicating the loop body multiple times per iteration
questions.explanation: Loop unrolling replicates the loop body multiple times per iteration, reducing the number of branch and loop-counter operations. For a 1000-iteration loop unrolled 4×, you get 250 iterations.

questions.question: What does the `.rela.text` section contain in an ELF object file?
acceptableAnswers: relocation entries for patching addresses in the .text section
questions.explanation: `.rela.text` contains relocation entries for the `.text` (code) section. Each entry specifies an offset where the linker must patch an address once the target symbol's location is known.

questions.question: What is an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)?
acceptableAnswers: a tree representation of the program's grammatical structure built during parsing
questions.explanation: The AST captures the program's grammatical structure as a tree. For example, `int x = a + b * 2` becomes a declaration node with a binary operation subtree capturing the full syntactic structure.

questions.question: What is lexing in the compiler front-end?
acceptableAnswers: splits source text into tokens — keywords, identifiers, literals, operators
questions.explanation: Lexing (or tokenization) splits the source text into a stream of tokens while discarding whitespace and comments. For example, `int x = a + b * 2;` becomes `[Keyword:int] [Identifier:x] [Operator:=]...`.

questions.question: What does `g++ -S main.cpp -o main.s` do?
acceptableAnswers: stops after the compiler stage, outputting assembly text
questions.explanation: The `-S` flag tells g++ to stop after the compiler stage (Phase 2), outputting assembly text (`.s` file). The assembler and linker are not invoked.

questions.question: What does the MSVC `/GL` flag enable?
acceptableAnswers: Whole Program Optimization by generating instrumented object files for link-time code generation
questions.explanation: MSVC's `/GL` (Whole Program Optimization) generates object files with special IR enabling cross-module optimization at link time, paired with `/LTCG`.

questions.question: How does the GCC `-fopt-info-vec` flag help developers?
acceptableAnswers: reports which loops were vectorized and why others were not
questions.explanation: `-fopt-info-vec` tells GCC to report which loops were successfully vectorized and which were not, along with reasons. It's a diagnostic tool for understanding auto-vectorization.

questions.question: What is a translation phase in the C++ standard?
acceptableAnswers: one of the sequential steps (1-9) defined by the C++ standard that transforms source code to the final program
questions.explanation: The C++ standard defines 9 translation phases, from trigraph replacement (phase 1) through preprocessing (phase 4) to template instantiation and linking. The pipeline covers phases 4-9.

questions.question: What does `g++ -E main.cpp -o main.i` produce?
acceptableAnswers: a preprocessed source file with all #includes expanded and all macros substituted
questions.explanation: `-E` stops after preprocessing, outputting the expanded source to `main.i`. A single `#include <iostream>` can expand to ~30,000+ lines of C++ code.

questions.question: At which optimization level does GCC auto-vectorize loops?
questions.explanation: At `-O2` and above, GCC and Clang analyze loops for vectorization potential. Requirements include: contiguous memory access, no pointer aliasing, and simple loop bodies.

questions.question: What does the `#` stringizing operator do in a macro like `#define DBG(x) printf(#x "=%d\n", x)`?
acceptableAnswers: converts the macro parameter x to the string literal "x"
questions.explanation: `#x` in a macro body converts the parameter token to a string literal. `DBG(counter)` expands to `printf("counter" "=%d\n", counter)`.

questions.question: Why do compilers like GCC and Clang produce different code from MSVC for the same C++ source?
acceptableAnswers: different IR design, optimization pass ordering, register allocator heuristics, and instruction selection models
questions.explanation: While front-ends parse identically, the IR design, optimization pass ordering, register allocator heuristics, and instruction selection models differ between compilers, leading to 5-20% performance differences.

questions.question: What is the purpose of parsing in the compiler front-end?
acceptableAnswers: verifies the token sequence follows the language grammar and builds an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
questions.explanation: Parsing takes the token stream from the lexer and checks it against the C++ grammar rules. If valid, it builds an AST representing the program's grammatical structure. If invalid, it reports syntax errors.

questions.question: What is the difference between static and dynamic linking?
options: Static: library code copied into executable at build time. Dynamic: library loaded at runtime, shared across processes
Static is faster through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Dynamic is deprecated within the current scope with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Static: library code copied into executable at build time. Dynamic: library loaded at runtime, shared across processes
questions.explanation: Static: larger binary, no runtime dependency. Dynamic: smaller binary, shared .so/.dll, can update library without recompiling.

questions.question: What are PLT and GOT in dynamic linking?
options: File formats via the core framework under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Procedure Linkage Table and Global Offset Table — lazy binding: first call resolves address via dynamic linker, subsequent calls jump directly
Linker scripts as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Procedure Linkage Table and Global Offset Table — lazy binding: first call resolves address via dynamic linker, subsequent calls jump directly
questions.explanation: PLT stub → GOT entry. First call: GOT points to dynamic linker (resolve). After: GOT updated to actual function address. Lazy binding.

questions.question: What is ASLR?
options: A compiler flag under normal conditions under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Address Space Layout Randomization — OS randomizes base addresses of stack, heap, libraries each execution to prevent exploits
A linker across all supported platforms with additional processing in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Address Space Layout Randomization — OS randomizes base addresses of stack, heap, libraries each execution to prevent exploits
questions.explanation: ASLR = security through unpredictability. Attacker can't hardcode addresses. PIE (Position Independent Executable) required.

questions.question: What is an ELF file?
options: A game format within the current scope
Executable and Linkable Format — the standard binary format on Linux (like PE on Windows, Mach-O on macOS)
A compiler as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A debug format in most implementations with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Executable and Linkable Format — the standard binary format on Linux (like PE on Windows, Mach-O on macOS)
questions.explanation: ELF contains: header, program headers (segments for loader), section headers (.text, .data, .bss, .rodata), symbol tables.

questions.question: What is relocation?
options: Moving code within the current scope
