128 bytes BELOW RSP that the current function can use without adjusting RSP — signal handlers skip it
A kernel region across all supported platforms in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Windows equivalent of shadow space across all supported platforms within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: 128 bytes BELOW RSP that the current function can use without adjusting RSP — signal handlers skip it
questions.explanation: Red zone = System V only. Windows has NO red zone — below RSP is volatile (interrupts may overwrite). Cross-platform code: never assume it.

questions.question: What is the shadow space (Microsoft x64)?
options: Caller MUST allocate 32 bytes on stack before call — callee may spill its 4 register args there
A debug region through the built-in API across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Linux equivalent of red zone in most implementations with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Caller MUST allocate 32 bytes on stack before call — callee may spill its 4 register args there
questions.explanation: Shadow space = 32 bytes reserved by caller. Simplifies variadic functions and debugging. System V has no shadow space.

questions.question: How does System V differ from MS x64 for argument registers?
options: SysV: RDI,RSI,RDX,RCX,R8,R9 (6 regs). MS: RCX,RDX,R8,R9 (4 regs). SysV has red zone; MS has shadow space.
SysV uses stack in most implementations with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx

questions.explanation: Key differences: 6 vs 4 arg registers, RDI/RSI callee-saved on Windows (caller-saved on SysV — #1 porting trap), red zone vs shadow space.

questions.question: Why does Windows x64 use only 4 argument registers vs System V's 6?
options: Windows is slower through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Historical continuity with x86 __fastcall (ECX,EDX). Shadow space handles spill. Different design tradeoffs.

questions.explanation: MS extended x86 __fastcall. AMD defined SysV ABI for 64-bit; Linux/macOS adopted it. Microsoft created their own. Historical accident.

questions.question: What is a stack buffer overflow?
options: Stack allocation failure in most implementations with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Writing past the end of a stack buffer overwrites saved RBP and return address — attacker controls RIP on ret
A type of deadlock in most implementations across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A compiler warning for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Writing past the end of a stack buffer overwrites saved RBP and return address — attacker controls RIP on ret
questions.explanation: Buffer at [RBP-N], saved RBP at [RBP], return address at [RBP+8]. Overflow upward overwrites return address. ret → attacker code.

questions.question: What is a stack canary?
options: A random value placed between locals and saved RBP — checked before ret. If corrupted (overflow) → abort.
A type of cookie via the core framework with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A debug feature within the current scope in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A random value placed between locals and saved RBP — checked before ret. If corrupted (overflow) → abort.
questions.explanation: Canary = compiler-inserted guard value. Overwrite buffer → corrupt canary → detected at epilogue check. Bypassable if canary leaked.

questions.question: What is ROP (Return-Oriented Programming)?
options: A programming paradigm under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Chaining existing code snippets (gadgets ending in ret) to execute arbitrary logic — bypasses NX/DEP
A type of recursion through the built-in API
A compiler optimization in most implementations for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Chaining existing code snippets (gadgets ending in ret) to execute arbitrary logic — bypasses NX/DEP
questions.explanation: NX bit makes stack non-executable. ROP reuses existing .text code instead of injecting new code. Gadget = short instruction sequence + ret.

questions.question: What protects against ROP at the hardware level?
options: Nothing as defined in the specification under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Intel CET Shadow Stack — CPU maintains hardware-protected copy of return addresses; ret compares against it
Stack canary in most implementations within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Intel CET Shadow Stack — CPU maintains hardware-protected copy of return addresses; ret compares against it
questions.explanation: Shadow stack = hardware-enforced. Every call pushes to shadow stack; ret pops and compares. Mismatch → #CP fault. Hard to bypass.

questions.question: What is Tail Call Optimization (TCO)?
options: Converting call to jmp when the call is in tail position — reuses current frame, constant stack depth
A security feature in most implementations in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Loop unrolling for typical use cases in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Converting call to jmp when the call is in tail position — reuses current frame, constant stack depth
questions.explanation: Tail call = last operation before return. Compiler: call → jmp. Same frame reused. Infinite recursion becomes safe (constant stack).

questions.question: Why are 4x4 matrices used for 3D transforms?
options: Style as defined in the specification
Homogeneous coordinates enable translation as a linear operation — without the 4th row/column, translation requires separate vector addition
Faster than 3x3 under normal conditions within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Required by GPUs via the core framework with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Homogeneous coordinates enable translation as a linear operation — without the 4th row/column, translation requires separate vector addition
questions.explanation: 4x4 matrix: (x,y,z,1) × M. The 4th column encodes translation. 3x3 can't represent translation in a single matrix multiply.

questions.question: What is the order of the MVP transformation?
options: Model → World, World → View, View → Projection (M first, then V, then P)
V×P×M across all supported platforms with additional processing for common
Any order across all supported platforms for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Model → World, World → View, View → Projection (M first, then V, then P)
questions.explanation: MVP = Projection × View × Model. Applied right-to-left to vertex: P·V·M·v. Model: local→world. View: world→camera. Projection: camera→clip.

questions.question: Why is matrix multiplication not commutative?
options: It is commutative under normal conditions
Order matters — rotate then translate ≠ translate then rotate. AB ≠ BA for matrices.
It's a bug via the core framework with additional processing with typical engine setting
Only for some matrices as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Order matters — rotate then translate ≠ translate then rotate. AB ≠ BA for matrices.
questions.explanation: Matrix multiplication is composition of transformations. Rotating then translating moves in the rotated direction. Translating then rotating moves first, then rotates around origin.

questions.question: What does the inverse of a rotation matrix equal?
options: The same matrix as defined in the specification
Its transpose — rotation matrices are orthogonal: R⁻¹ = R^T
Zero matrix via the core framework
Identity within the current scope with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: Its transpose — rotation matrices are orthogonal: R⁻¹ = R^T
questions.explanation: Rotation matrices are orthonormal: columns are unit vectors, mutually perpendicular. R^T × R = I. Computing transpose is cheaper than full inverse.

questions.e: A matrix's columns ARE its basis vectors. Applying a matrix to a vector expresses it in that matrix's coordinate system.
questions.explanation: A matrix's columns ARE its basis vectors. The first column is where (1,0,0) goes; the second is where (0,1,0) goes; the third is where (0,0,1) goes. Applying the matrix transforms vectors into the coordinate system defined by these basis vectors.

A unit vector via the core framework with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A normal vector by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: The axes defining a coordinate system — (1,0,0), (0,1,0), (0,0,1) in standard basis. Changing basis = changing coordinate system.

questions.question: What problem do quaternions solve that Euler angles don't?
options: Speed within the current scope with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Gimbal lock — when two rotation axes align, you lose a degree of freedom. Quaternions avoid this entirely.
Memory usage within the current scope with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Gimbal lock — when two rotation axes align, you lose a degree of freedom. Quaternions avoid this entirely.
questions.explanation: Gimbal lock: pitch=90° → yaw and roll become the same rotation. Quaternions use 4D hypersphere — no singularities.

questions.question: How many components does a quaternion have?
options: 3 within the current scope with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
4 (w, x, y, z) — scalar part w + vector part (x,y,z). Unit quaternion constraint: w²+x²+y²+z²=1
6 within the current scope with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: 4 (w, x, y, z) — scalar part w + vector part (x,y,z). Unit quaternion constraint: w²+x²+y²+z²=1
questions.explanation: Quaternion = cos(θ/2) + sin(θ/2)(x·i + y·j + z·k). 4 numbers, 1 constraint = 3 DOF for rotation. Same as rotation matrix but compact.

questions.question: What is SLERP?
options: Linear interpolation under normal conditions for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Spherical Linear intERPolation — interpolates along the great circle on the 4D hypersphere, constant angular velocity
A type of quaternion via the core framework with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A rotation axis by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: Spherical Linear intERPolation — interpolates along the great circle on the 4D hypersphere, constant angular velocity
questions.explanation: slerp(q0,q1,t) = (sin((1-t)Ω)/sin(Ω))·q0 + (sin(tΩ)/sin(Ω))·q1. Stays on unit hypersphere. Constant speed rotation.

questions.question: Why not just lerp quaternions?
options: It's fine for typical use cases
lerp goes through interior of hypersphere — produces non-unit quaternions. Normalizing after (nlerp) fixes magnitude but not speed.
It's slower for typical use cases in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx

questions.correctAnswer: lerp goes through interior of hypersphere — produces non-unit quaternions. Normalizing after (nlerp) fixes magnitude but not speed.
questions.explanation: Lerp cuts chord through sphere. Nlerp normalizes but speeds up in middle. Slerp stays on surface at constant angular velocity.

questions.question: How are quaternions related to rotation matrices?
options: No relation through the built-in API
A unit quaternion q can be converted to a 3x3 rotation matrix. The conversion formula involves q's components in a specific pattern.
Quaternions are faster for typical use cases within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Quaternions replace matrices by default behavior with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A unit quaternion q can be converted to a 3x3 rotation matrix. The conversion formula involves q's components in a specific pattern.
questions.explanation: Quaternion→matrix: standard formula. Quaternion rotation: v' = q·v·q⁻¹ (sandwich product). Two quat multiplies vs 9 multiplies + 6 adds for matrix×vector.

questions.question: What does the dot product a·b compute geometrically?
options: Area using standard engine approaches with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxx
|a||b|cos(θ) — the projection of a onto b, times b's length. Measures how parallel they are.
A perpendicular vector under normal conditions in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: |a||b|cos(θ) — the projection of a onto b, times b's length. Measures how parallel they are.
questions.explanation: a·b > 0 = acute angle. a·b = 0 = perpendicular. a·b < 0 = obtuse. Used everywhere: lighting (N·L), projection, angle measurement.

questions.question: What does the cross product a×b compute?
options: Parallel component within the current scope within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A vector perpendicular to both a and b, magnitude = |a||b|sin(θ) (area of parallelogram). Direction: right-hand rule.
The angle within the current scope
Distance across all supported platforms with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A vector perpendicular to both a and b, magnitude = |a||b|sin(θ) (area of parallelogram). Direction: right-hand rule.
questions.explanation: Cross product: normal vector computation, torque, angular momentum. Only defined in 3D (and 7D). Wedge product generalizes to any dimension.

questions.question: What is the relationship between sin, cos and the unit circle?
options: No relation across all supported platforms for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
(cos θ, sin θ) is a point on the unit circle at angle θ from the x-axis
They are reciprocals using standard engine approaches under normal oper
They are derivatives by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: (cos θ, sin θ) is a point on the unit circle at angle θ from the x-axis
questions.explanation: Unit circle: radius=1. cos = x-coordinate, sin = y-coordinate. cos² + sin² = 1 (Pythagorean identity). Foundation of all trigonometry.

questions.question: What are radians and why are they used?
options: Arbitrary unit within the current scope
Natural angle measure: 1 radian = arc length equals radius. 2π rad = 360°. Makes calculus clean (derivative of sin is cos).
Degrees are better as defined in the specification under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Historical only under normal conditions within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Natural angle measure: 1 radian = arc length equals radius. 2π rad = 360°. Makes calculus clean (derivative of sin is cos).
questions.explanation: In radians: d/dx sin(x) = cos(x). In degrees: d/dx sin(x°) = (π/180)cos(x°). Radians make the math elegant. Game engines use both.

questions.e: v̂ = v/|v|. Used for direction vectors, surface normals, lighting. Normalizing zero vector = undefined (avoid).
questions.explanation: v̂ = v/|v| produces a unit vector (magnitude=1) pointing in the same direction. Used for direction vectors, surface normals, light directions, and any case where only orientation matters, not magnitude. Normalizing the zero vector is undefined (avoid).
questions.question: What is vector normalization?
options: Making it zero across all supported platforms with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Dividing a vector by its magnitude to produce a unit vector (length=1) — preserves direction
Adding components via the core framework
Cross product using standard engine approaches for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Dividing a vector by its magnitude to produce a unit vector (length=1) — preserves direction

questions.question: What defines a cubic Bezier curve?
options: 4 control points — endpoints (P0,P3) the curve passes through, handles (P1,P2) that pull the curve
A single equation in most implementations under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Random points for typical use cases with additional processing for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: 4 control points — endpoints (P0,P3) the curve passes through, handles (P1,P2) that pull the curve
questions.explanation: Cubic Bezier: B(t) = (1-t)³P0 + 3(1-t)²tP1 + 3(1-t)t²P2 + t³P3. Tangent at t=0: 3(P1-P0). Tangent at t=1: 3(P3-P2).

questions.question: What is the advantage of Catmull-Rom over Bezier?
options: More control points within the current scope with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Catmull-Rom passes THROUGH all control points (interpolating). Bezier points are 'magnets' — curve doesn't pass through handles.
Faster computation via the core framework
Fewer points under normal conditions with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Catmull-Rom passes THROUGH all control points (interpolating). Bezier points are 'magnets' — curve doesn't pass through handles.
questions.explanation: Catmull-Rom = interpolating spline. Each segment uses 4 points, passes through middle two. Tangent at P_i = (P_i+1 - P_i-1)/2.

questions.question: What is de Casteljau's algorithm?
options: A sorting algorithm via the core framework with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Recursive linear interpolation to evaluate Bezier curves — numerically stable, avoids computing Bernstein polynomials
A rotation method under normal conditions within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A curve type for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Recursive linear interpolation to evaluate Bezier curves — numerically stable, avoids computing Bernstein polynomials
questions.explanation: de Casteljau: repeatedly lerp between adjacent control points until one remains. O(n²) but numerically stable. Geometric intuition: fold paper.

questions.e: B-splines: don't necessarily pass through control points. Local control = editing one CV changes only local region. NURBS adds weights.
questions.explanation: B-splines use overlapping basis functions defined over a knot vector. Moving one control point only affects nearby curve segments (local control), and the curve is C² continuous at knots — smoother than Catmull-Rom (C¹).
questions.question: What is a B-spline?
options: A Bezier variant in most implementations with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Basis spline — generalization with local control (moving one point affects only nearby segments) and C² continuity
A type of lerp via the core framework across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A rotation within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: Basis spline — generalization with local control (moving one point affects only nearby segments) and C² continuity

questions.e: Speed = |dB/dt|. This is not constant. Arc-length reparameterization fixes this by driving t as a function of desired distance.
questions.explanation: The derivative magnitude |B'(t)| varies across t. Near control points the curve may move slowly; between them it may speed up. Arc-length reparameterization measures cumulative distance and maps it to parameter t.
questions.question: Why is uniform t-stepping not constant speed along a Bezier?
options: It is constant through the built-in API
|B'(t)| varies with t — the derivative magnitude changes, so equal Δt covers different distances
Bug in formula for typical use cases with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxx
Only for cubics across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: |B'(t)| varies with t — the derivative magnitude changes, so equal Δt covers different distances

questions.question: Why does Slerp use sin functions in its formula?
options: It's computing the geodesic on S³ — the weights come from spherical trigonometry: sin((1-t)Ω)/sin(Ω)
Optimization across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Historical reasons through the built-in API in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: It's computing the geodesic on S³ — the weights come from spherical trigonometry: sin((1-t)Ω)/sin(Ω)
questions.explanation: slerp = great circle arc on quaternion hypersphere. Weights ensure constant angular velocity. When Ω→0, sin(Ω)→0 — fall back to nlerp.

questions.question: What is CCD (Cyclic Coordinate Descent) in IK?
options: Iterative IK: rotate each joint from end-effector toward root to point at target. Simple but can produce unnatural poses.
A rotation method across all supported platforms with additional processing for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A curve type under normal conditions within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Iterative IK: rotate each joint from end-effector toward root to point at target. Simple but can produce unnatural poses.
questions.explanation: CCD: for each joint (distal→proximal), rotate to minimize angle between ToEnd and ToTarget. Linear convergence. Can over-rotate proximal joints.

questions.question: What is FABRIK and how does it differ from CCD?
options: Same as CCD via the core framework
Forward And Backward Reaching IK — enforces distance constraints without computing angles. Forward pass: reach to target. Backward: anchor base.
A curve type as defined in the specification for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A rendering technique under normal conditions across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Forward And Backward Reaching IK — enforces distance constraints without computing angles. Forward pass: reach to target. Backward: anchor base.
questions.explanation: FABRIK: no trig functions. Position-based. Forward: pull tip to target, adjust joints. Backward: anchor base, readjust. More natural poses than CCD.

questions.e: Quaternions double-cover SO(3). q and -q = same rotation. Slerp without check might take the LONG way around the hypersphere.
questions.explanation: q and -q represent the same rotation (quaternions double-cover SO(3)). Before slerp, compute dot product: if negative, negate one quaternion. This ensures the shorter arc is taken — without this, slerp might go the long way around the hypersphere.
questions.question: What is the double-cover problem with quaternions?
options: q and -q represent the same rotation. Before slerp, check dot product — if negative, negate one to take shorter arc.
Speed issue as defined in the specification under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Type error as defined in the specification in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: q and -q represent the same rotation. Before slerp, check dot product — if negative, negate one to take shorter arc.

questions.e: FK: angles→position (deterministic). IK: position→angles (underdetermined, needs solver). FK is trivial; IK is the hard problem.
questions.explanation: FK computes end effector position from joint angles — deterministic, one solution. IK computes joint angles from end effector position — underdetermined (multiple solutions), requires iterative solver like CCD or FABRIK. FK is forward; IK is inverse.
questions.question: What is Forward Kinematics (FK)?
options: Same as IK via the core framework
Joint-driven: specify joint angles, compute end effector position. Like controlling a puppet joint-by-joint.
Inverse of IK through the built-in API across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A rendering technique for typical use cases within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Joint-driven: specify joint angles, compute end effector position. Like controlling a puppet joint-by-joint.

questions.question: What is Linear Blend Skinning (LBS)?
options: A rendering technique through the built-in API with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Weighted blend of bone transforms per vertex: v' = Σ w_i·M_i·IB_i·v. Standard GPU skinning.
A rotation method across all supported platforms in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
An IK solver across all supported platforms
questions.correctAnswer: Weighted blend of bone transforms per vertex: v' = Σ w_i·M_i·IB_i·v. Standard GPU skinning.
questions.explanation: Each vertex influenced by 1-4 bones. Bone weights sum to 1. GPU blends 4 matrices per vertex. Fast but causes candy-wrapper effect.

questions.question: What is the candy-wrapper effect in LBS?
options: Performance issue across all supported platforms within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
When a joint twists, linear blend of rotation matrices produces non-rigid transforms — mesh collapses inward at joint
A visual artifact using standard engine approaches with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: When a joint twists, linear blend of rotation matrices produces non-rigid transforms — mesh collapses inward at joint
questions.explanation: Cause: SO(3) not closed under addition. Blending two rotation matrices does NOT produce a rotation matrix. Determinant < 1 = volume collapse.

questions.question: How do dual quaternions fix the candy-wrapper effect?
options: More bones within the current scope with additional processing with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Dual quaternion blend + normalization always produces rigid transform — rotation+translation, determinant=1, no volume loss
Better weights via the core framework
More vertices within the current scope within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Dual quaternion blend + normalization always produces rigid transform — rotation+translation, determinant=1, no volume loss
questions.explanation: DQS: convert bone transforms to dual quaternions, blend, normalize. Result always rigid. ~2× cost of LBS. UE supports both.

questions.question: What is the bind pose?
options: The current animation pose via the core framework within the standard frameworkxxxxxxx
The skeleton pose the mesh was modeled in — the reference configuration for skinning
The T-pose only within the current scope in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Any pose via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: The skeleton pose the mesh was modeled in — the reference configuration for skinning
questions.explanation: Bind pose = rest pose. Inverse bind matrices (IB_i) map vertices to bone-local space IN the bind pose. Computed once at import.

questions.question: How many bones typically influence a single vertex?
options: 1 using standard engine approaches with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
4 (with MAX_BONE_INFLUENCES). Weights sum to 1. Extra influences dropped if > limit.
Unlimited under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: 4 (with MAX_BONE_INFLUENCES). Weights sum to 1. Extra influences dropped if > limit.
questions.explanation: UE default: 4 influences per vertex. 8 possible with higher quality settings. Artists paint weights in Maya/Blender. Red=strong, blue=weak.

questions.question: What does the Fourier Transform convert?
options: Voltage to current as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Time-domain signal to frequency-domain — reveals which frequencies (pitches) are present
Analog to digital by default behavior
Sound to image within the current scope with additional processing in most practical sce
questions.correctAnswer: Time-domain signal to frequency-domain — reveals which frequencies (pitches) are present
questions.explanation: DFT: X[k] = Σx[n]·e^(-i·2π·k·n/N). Maps N time samples to N frequency bins. Magnitude = amplitude, angle = phase.

questions.question: Why is the FFT O(N log N) instead of O(N²)?
options: Cooley-Tukey: decomposes DFT of size N into two DFTs of size N/2 recursively — divide and conquer
Uses GPU as defined in the specification with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Uses lookup tables across all supported platforms within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Cooley-Tukey: decomposes DFT of size N into two DFTs of size N/2 recursively — divide and conquer
questions.explanation: X[k] = E[k] + W_N^k·O[k]; X[k+N/2] = E[k] - W_N^k·O[k]. Butterfly operations. log₂(N) stages. N=1024: DFT=1M ops, FFT=10K ops.

questions.question: How does the convolution theorem enable fast reverb?
options: It doesn't as defined in the specification in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Time convolution = frequency multiplication. FFT both signals, multiply, IFFT result. O(N log N) vs O(N²) direct.
It uses GPU under normal conditions
It compresses audio within the current scope under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Time convolution = frequency multiplication. FFT both signals, multiply, IFFT result. O(N log N) vs O(N²) direct.
questions.explanation: Dry audio ∗ impulse response → FFT(audio)·FFT(IR) → IFFT. 10,000× faster for real-time convolution reverb.

questions.question: What is a spectrogram?
options: A waveform display by default behavior
2D visualization: time on x-axis, frequency on y-axis, color = magnitude. Generated by sliding FFT window.
A type of filter for typical use cases under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A audio format in most implementations with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: 2D visualization: time on x-axis, frequency on y-axis, color = magnitude. Generated by sliding FFT window.
questions.explanation: Spectrogram = STFT (Short-Time Fourier Transform). Window function (Hann) reduces leakage. Hop size controls time resolution. Used in audio editors.

questions.e: Window functions prevent spectral leakage from the discontinuity at frame boundaries. Hann, Hamming, Blackman are common choices.
questions.explanation: The Hann window tapers the signal to zero at frame boundaries (raised cosine shape). Without it, the abrupt cut at frame edges creates spectral leakage — energy from one frequency bin bleeding into neighboring bins.
questions.question: What window function is commonly used before FFT and why?
options: Rectangular (none) in most implementations with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Hann window — tapers signal to zero at edges, reducing spectral leakage from abrupt frame boundaries
Gaussian as defined in the specification under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Hann window — tapers signal to zero at edges, reducing spectral leakage from abrupt frame boundaries

questions.question: How does sound intensity fall off with distance?
options: Inverse-square law: I = P/(4πr²). Every distance doubling = -6 dB.
Not at all for typical use cases with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Exponentially using standard engine approaches across common platformsxx
questions.correctAnswer: Inverse-square law: I = P/(4πr²). Every distance doubling = -6 dB.
questions.explanation: SPL(r) = SPL(1m) - 20·log₁₀(r). At 1m=80dB, 2m=74dB, 4m=68dB. Games clamp with min/max distance and configurable falloff.

questions.question: What cues does the brain use to localize sound?
questions.correctAnswer: ITD (interaural time difference), ILD (interaural level difference), and spectral notches (pinna filtering)
acceptableAnswers: time difference, level difference, pinna filtering
questions.explanation: ITD: ~0.25ms max. ILD: head shadow attenuates far ear (high freqs). Spectral notches: pinna shape → frequency dips that encode elevation.

questions.question: What is an HRTF?
options: A filter type under normal conditions
Head-Related Transfer Function — pair of filters per direction capturing how sound reaches eardrums. Convolution applies spatialization.
A file format across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A speaker setup through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Head-Related Transfer Function — pair of filters per direction capturing how sound reaches eardrums. Convolution applies spatialization.
questions.explanation: HRTF(θ,φ) = FIR filter (128-512 taps) per ear. Measured in anechoic chamber. Applied via convolution (FFT-based). Enables 3D audio on headphones.

questions.question: What causes the Doppler effect for sound?
options: Volume change across all supported platforms with additional processing for common use casesxxxxxxxxxx
Relative motion: f_heard = f_source·c/(c+v_relative). Approaching = higher pitch. Receding = lower.
Temperature for typical use cases with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Relative motion: f_heard = f_source·c/(c+v_relative). Approaching = higher pitch. Receding = lower.
questions.explanation: c = ~343 m/s. Car at 30 m/s approaching: f_heard = f_source·1.096 (~1.6 semitones higher). UE applies per-sound-source.

questions.e: Two speakers at center = +3dB if linear. Constant-power: -3dB each at center = combined 0dB. Total power constant across pan positions.
questions.explanation: Constant-power pan law: L = cos(θ+90°), R = sin(θ+90°). At center (θ=0): L=R=√2/2 ≈ 0.707 = -3dB each, but combined acoustic output = 0dB. Prevents the center phantom image from being louder than hard-panned sounds.
questions.question: What is constant-power pan law?
options: Equal volume for typical use cases
L=cos(θ+90°), R=sin(θ+90°). Prevents center from being +3dB louder than sides.
Equal power by default behavior with additional processing under normal operationxx
Equal amplitude across all supported platforms in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: L=cos(θ+90°), R=sin(θ+90°). Prevents center from being +3dB louder than sides.

questions.question: Why is interpolation needed for networked multiplayer?
options: To save bandwidth through the built-in API across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Server updates arrive at 10-20Hz (50-100ms apart). Client renders at 60Hz — must interpolate between received positions.
To compress data as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Server updates arrive at 10-20Hz (50-100ms apart). Client renders at 60Hz — must interpolate between received positions.
questions.explanation: Without interpolation: characters teleport between received positions. With: smooth motion, ~50ms render-behind latency (acceptable for other players).

questions.question: What is client-side prediction?
options: Predicting server state using standard engine approaches
Client simulates own input locally for instant response, then reconciles with server's authoritative state
Predicting other players in most implementations with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxx
A type of interpolation using standard engine approaches with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Client simulates own input locally for instant response, then reconciles with server's authoritative state
questions.explanation: Own character: can't wait 50ms RTT. Predict locally → send input → server corrects → rewind and replay. UE's CharacterMovementComponent does this.

questions.question: What is dead reckoning?
options: A navigation method as defined in the specification with additional processing in most pr
Extrapolating motion when packets stop arriving — assume constant velocity/acceleration
A type of interpolation in most implementations across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Server authority through the built-in API
questions.correctAnswer: Extrapolating motion when packets stop arriving — assume constant velocity/acceleration
questions.explanation: Dead reckoning: last position + velocity·dt (+ ½·acceleration·dt²). After timeout (1-2s), freeze or disconnect. Used for packet loss.

questions.question: What is a jitter buffer?
options: A network buffer under normal conditions
Queue that delays packet playback to smooth network jitter — trades added latency for smooth motion
A type of interpolation through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A server component under normal conditions in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Queue that delays packet playback to smooth network jitter — trades added latency for smooth motion
questions.explanation: Packets arrive with variable delay. Buffer holds and releases at steady rate. Larger buffer = smoother + more latency. Adaptive buffers resize.

questions.e: Hermite: position + velocity at each endpoint. Produces smooth curves. Linear: constant velocity between updates, jerky direction changes.
questions.explanation: Hermite interpolation uses position AND velocity at both endpoints. This produces C¹-continuous curves — no sudden direction changes when a new server update arrives. Linear interpolation produces constant velocity between updates, causing visible jerks at each new packet.
questions.question: What is Hermite interpolation advantage over linear for networking?
options: Uses endpoint velocities for C¹ continuity — no sudden direction changes at received updates
Simpler using standard engine approaches in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
More bandwidth for typical use cases across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Uses endpoint velocities for C¹ continuity — no sudden direction changes at received updates

questions.question: What is a rotor in Geometric Algebra?
options: A motor within the current scope
The GA version of a quaternion — scalar + bivector. Rotates vectors via sandwich product: v' = RvR̃.
A translation across all supported platforms for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A matrix using standard engine approaches under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: The GA version of a quaternion — scalar + bivector. Rotates vectors via sandwich product: v' = RvR̃.
questions.explanation: Rotor: R = cos(θ/2) - sin(θ/2)B where B is unit bivector. Same 4 components as quaternion. Isomorphic to quaternions.

questions.question: What does the sandwich product RvR̃ do?
options: Nothing special within the current scope with additional processing under normal operationxxxxx
Rotates vector v by twice the angle encoded in rotor R. R̃ is reverse (conjugate) of R.
Translates as defined in the specification in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Rotates vector v by twice the angle encoded in rotor R. R̃ is reverse (conjugate) of R.
questions.explanation: Sandwich = universal GA pattern. Reflection: -nvn. Rotation: RvR̃ (two reflections). Translation: TvT̃. Everything is a sandwich.

questions.question: How are rotors more general than quaternions?
options: They're not within the current scope
Rotors work in ANY dimension — 2D (complex numbers), 3D (quaternions), 4D+. Quaternions only in 3D.
They're faster under normal conditions in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Smaller as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Rotors work in ANY dimension — 2D (complex numbers), 3D (quaternions), 4D+. Quaternions only in 3D.
questions.explanation: Dimension-independence is the rotor's key advantage. 2D rotor = 2 components (complex). 3D rotor = 4 components (quaternion). 4D rotor = 8 components.

questions.e: Wedge product = 0 iff vectors are linearly dependent (parallel). a∧b = |a||b|sin(θ)·B. sin(0)=0, sin(180)=0.
questions.explanation: a∧b = 0 when vectors are linearly dependent — they point in the same or opposite direction. The parallelogram they span has zero area. sin(0)=sin(π)=0, so |a||b|sin(θ)=0.
questions.question: What does a∧b = 0 mean geometrically?
options: They are perpendicular via the core framework within the
a and b are collinear (parallel) — they span zero area
They are equal through the built-in API
They are opposites across all supported platforms
questions.correctAnswer: a and b are collinear (parallel) — they span zero area

questions.e: I = Hodge dual operator. v·I converts vector to bivector. I²=-1 makes it behave like imaginary unit. Basis of complex/quaternion behavior in GA.
questions.explanation: I = e₁₂₃ is the unit volume element. I² = -1 makes it behave like the imaginary unit i. Multiplying a vector by I: v·I converts to bivector; multiplying a bivector by I: B·I converts to vector. This is the Hodge dual operation.
questions.question: What is the pseudoscalar I in 3D GA?
options: A unit vector through the built-in API
I = e₁₂₃ — the volume element. I² = -1 (in Euclidean). Multiplying by I flips grade (vector↔bivector, scalar↔trivector).
A rotor across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A scalar across all supported platforms across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: I = e₁₂₃ — the volume element. I² = -1 (in Euclidean). Multiplying by I flips grade (vector↔bivector, scalar↔trivector).

questions.question: What is Projective Geometric Algebra (PGA)?
options: A 2D-only algebra across all supported platforms
Models 3D Euclidean geometry in a 4D algebra with degenerate metric (e₀²=0). Points=trivectors, lines=bivectors, planes=vectors.
A rendering technique across all supported platforms with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A type of rotor using standard engine approaches within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Models 3D Euclidean geometry in a 4D algebra with degenerate metric (e₀²=0). Points=trivectors, lines=bivectors, planes=vectors.
questions.explanation: PGA: adds projective dimension. Points are intersections of 3 planes. Lines = join of 2 points. Ray-plane intersection = meet(L,Π). One operation.

questions.question: What is a motor in PGA?
options: An engine using standard engine approaches with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Rigid body motion (rotation + translation) as one object. Motor = even-grade element. Acts via sandwich. Generalizes dual quaternions.
A translator under normal conditions in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Rigid body motion (rotation + translation) as one object. Motor = even-grade element. Acts via sandwich. Generalizes dual quaternions.
questions.explanation: Motor slerp: one interpolation for rotation AND translation. No separate lerp(pos) + slerp(quat). Unified rigid body interpolation.

questions.question: How does PGA express ray-plane intersection?
options: Parametric equation under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
L ∨ Π — the meet (regressive product) of a line L and plane Π is their intersection point. One algebraic product.
Matrix solve under normal conditions with additional processing with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: L ∨ Π — the meet (regressive product) of a line L and plane Π is their intersection point. One algebraic product.
questions.explanation: Join (∧) creates higher grade. Meet (∨) = intersection. Ray = join(camera, farPoint). Hit = meet(ray, plane). All algebra, no special cases.

questions.e: PGA = Cl(3,0,1) — degenerate metric. e₀²=0 enables translation via rotor-like sandwich. Same algebra models points, lines, planes, and rigid motions.
questions.explanation: e₀ is a null basis vector (e₀²=0). This degenerate metric is the mathematical trick that encodes translation in PGA. Null vectors represent ideal points at infinity; their exponential generates translations just as bivector exponentials generate rotations.
questions.question: Why is e₀² = 0 in PGA?
options: e₀ is a null vector representing the ideal point at origin — encodes translation. Null vectors are the key to modeling Euclidean geometry projectively.
e₀ doesn't exist within the current scope with additional processing
e₀ is a regular vector under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: e₀ is a null vector representing the ideal point at origin — encodes translation. Null vectors are the key to modeling Euclidean geometry projectively.

questions.e: Motor = even-grade element in PGA. 8 components: 1 scalar, 3 e₀ᵢ bivectors (translation), 3 eᵢⱼ bivectors (rotation), 1 e₀₁₂₃. Represents rigid body motion.
questions.explanation: A PGA motor has 8 components: 1 scalar + 3 e₀ᵢ bivectors (translation generators) + 3 eᵢⱼ bivectors (rotation generators) + 1 e₀₁₂₃ pseudoscalar. It is isomorphic to a dual quaternion and represents any rigid body motion.
questions.question: How many components does a PGA motor have?
options: 4 across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
8 (scalar + 6 bivectors + pseudoscalar) — isomorphic to dual quaternion
16 under normal conditions under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: 8 (scalar + 6 bivectors + pseudoscalar) — isomorphic to dual quaternion

questions.question: What makes A* different from Dijkstra's algorithm?
options: No difference by default behavior in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A* uses a heuristic h(n) estimating distance to goal — guides search toward target. Dijkstra is h(n)=0 (explores all directions).
A* is slower by default behavior
A* is simpler via the core framework with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A* uses a heuristic h(n) estimating distance to goal — guides search toward target. Dijkstra is h(n)=0 (explores all directions).
questions.explanation: f(n) = g(n) + h(n). g = cost from start. h = estimated cost to goal. If h is admissible (never overestimates), A* finds optimal path.

questions.question: What makes a heuristic admissible for A*?
options: Any estimate through the built-in API with additional processing for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
h(n) must never overestimate the true remaining cost — typically Euclidean distance for spatial grids
h(n) must be zero by default behavior
h(n) must be exact using standard engine approaches under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: h(n) must never overestimate the true remaining cost — typically Euclidean distance for spatial grids
questions.explanation: Admissible = optimistic. Euclidean distance ≤ actual path distance. Manhattan distance on grid = admissible (but not on diagonals).

questions.question: What data structure makes A* efficient?
options: Array as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Priority queue (min-heap) — always expand the node with lowest f(n) first
Stack using standard engine approaches in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Priority queue (min-heap) — always expand the node with lowest f(n) first
questions.explanation: Open set = min-heap by f(n). Extract-min gives best candidate. Closed set = visited nodes. O(b^d) worst case; heuristic reduces this dramatically.

questions.e: Navmesh: convex polygons = nodes. Shared edges = connections. Much fewer nodes than grid cells. UE generates navmesh from level geometry.
questions.explanation: Navmesh drastically reduces search space compared to grids — dozens of convex polygons vs thousands of grid cells. A* on navmesh searches polygon connectivity graph. UE generates navmeshes using Recast.
questions.question: What is a navigation mesh (navmesh)?
options: A grid across all supported platforms
A polygon mesh representing walkable areas — A* finds paths along mesh edges/vertices, much fewer nodes than grids
A type of graph in most implementations under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
An obstacle across all supported platforms with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A polygon mesh representing walkable areas — A* finds paths along mesh edges/vertices, much fewer nodes than grids

questions.e: Navmesh drastically reduces search space. UE's Recast/Detour generates navmeshes. Pathfinding on navmesh = string-pulling for smooth final path.
questions.explanation: Dijkstra = A* with h(n)=0. Without heuristic guidance, Dijkstra explores equally in all directions. A* uses Euclidean/Manhattan distance to guide search toward the goal — dramatically fewer expanded nodes.
questions.question: What is the difference between A* on a grid vs a navmesh?
options: Same algorithm using standard engine approaches with additional processing
Grid: thousands of cells, Manhattan/Euclidean heuristic. Navmesh: dozens of polygons, Euclidean heuristic between centroids + edge costs. Navmesh is much faster.
Navmesh uses different algorithm as defined in the specification
Grid is always better for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Grid: thousands of cells, Manhattan/Euclidean heuristic. Navmesh: dozens of polygons, Euclidean heuristic between centroids + edge costs. Navmesh is much faster.

questions.question: What are Craig Reynolds' steering behaviors?
options: Simple forces combining to produce complex motion: seek, flee, arrive, wander, separation, alignment, cohesion
Physics simulations for typical use cases in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Animation techniques through the built-in API in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Simple forces combining to produce complex motion: seek, flee, arrive, wander, separation, alignment, cohesion
questions.explanation: Steering = desired velocity - current velocity. Each behavior computes a force. Combine via weighted sum or priority. Emergent flocking from 3 rules.

questions.question: What three rules produce flocking/boids behavior?
questions.correctAnswer: Separation (avoid neighbors), alignment (match average heading), cohesion (move toward center of neighbors)
questions.explanation: Reynolds 1987. Three simple rules = emergent flock. No leader, no centralized control. Each boid reacts only to local neighbors.

questions.question: What is the arrive behavior?
options: Same as seek in most implementations
Seek with deceleration — slows down as it approaches target, stops at target instead of overshooting
A flocking rule in most implementations with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A pathfinding method by default behavior with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Seek with deceleration — slows down as it approaches target, stops at target instead of overshooting
questions.explanation: Arrive: if within slowing radius, scale speed by distance/radius. Seek would overshoot and orbit. Arrive smoothly decelerates to zero.

questions.e: For each neighbor within radius: compute repulsion vector away from neighbor. Sum and normalize. Key component of flocking.
questions.explanation: Separation steers away from nearby neighbors within a defined radius. Force is inversely proportional to distance — closer neighbors push harder. Sum repulsion vectors, normalize. Prevents agents from crowding or overlapping.
questions.question: What is separation steering?
options: Steer away from nearby neighbors — prevents crowding. Force inversely proportional to distance.
Following under normal conditions in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Wandering via the core framework under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Steer away from nearby neighbors — prevents crowding. Force inversely proportional to distance.

questions.e: Wander: circle projected ahead of agent. Random displacement on circle changes each frame. Produces natural-looking random movement.
questions.explanation: Wander projects a circle ahead of the agent and picks a random point on that circle each frame. The agent steers toward that point, producing smooth random-looking movement. The circle radius and distance control turning rate and randomness.
questions.question: What is wander steering?
options: Random teleport by default behavior with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Produces random, smooth wandering motion — projects a circle ahead, picks random point on circle, steers toward it
Flocking across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Produces random, smooth wandering motion — projects a circle ahead, picks random point on circle, steers toward it

questions.question: What algorithm generates navigation meshes in UE?

Recast — voxelizes geometry, marks walkable surfaces, generates convex polygons, creates graph
Random generation in most implementations for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Recast — voxelizes geometry, marks walkable surfaces, generates convex polygons, creates graph
questions.explanation: Recast pipeline: voxelization → filter walkable → generate distance field → contour → polygon mesh → detail mesh. Detour handles runtime pathfinding.

questions.question: What is 'string pulling' in pathfinding?

Post-processing step that smooths the A* path on a navmesh — removes unnecessary waypoints by pulling the path tight

questions.correctAnswer: Post-processing step that smooths the A* path on a navmesh — removes unnecessary waypoints by pulling the path tight
questions.explanation: A* on navmesh gives polygon corners. String pulling (funnel algorithm) smooths to shortest path within the navmesh corridor.

questions.e: Detour = runtime component. Uses Recast-generated data. Provides pathfinding queries, local avoidance, crowd movement.
questions.explanation: Recast step 1 rasterizes input triangles into a voxel heightfield. Each column stores spans of solid cells. A span is walkable if the surface slope < maxSlope (typically 45°) and the step height to adjacent spans < maxStep (agent can climb).
questions.question: What does Detour do at runtime?

Pathfinding queries on the pre-generated navmesh — A*, string pulling, crowd simulation
Physics for typical use cases with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx

questions.e: Recast: triangle mesh → voxel heightfield. Each column stores walkable spans. Filter by agent height, max slope, max step.
questions.explanation: After A* finds a sequence of navmesh polygons, the funnel algorithm tightens the path to the shortest route within that polygon corridor. It's like pulling a string taut through the polygons — removes unnecessary detours at polygon corners.

questions.e: Recast step 1: rasterize input geometry into heightfield. Compact representation: spans per column. Filter by walkability parameters.
questions.explanation: Detour is the runtime component that uses Recast-generated navmesh data. It provides pathfinding queries (A* on polygon graph), crowd simulation (local avoidance), and dynamic obstacle handling. Recast = offline generation; Detour = runtime queries.

options: Rendering under normal conditions with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Converts triangle mesh to 3D voxel grid. Each column stores spans of solid cells. Walkable if slope < maxSlope and step height < maxStep.

questions.e: A* gives polygon sequence. Funnel algorithm tightens to shortest path staying within the corridor. Produces natural-looking smooth paths.

questions.question: How do you test if two AABBs overlap?
options: Check all vertices within the current scope
Check if intervals overlap on ALL three axes: (a_min ≤ b_max) AND (a_max ≥ b_min) for x, y, z
Check one axis via the core framework with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Check distance across all supported platforms within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Check if intervals overlap on ALL three axes: (a_min ≤ b_max) AND (a_max ≥ b_min) for x, y, z
questions.explanation: AABB-AABB = 6 comparisons (2 per axis). O(1). Separating axis: if any axis has gap, no overlap. All 3 overlap = collision.

questions.question: How do you find the closest point on an AABB to a point?
options: Center of AABB for typical use cases with additional processing
Clamp each coordinate: x = clamp(px, min.x, max.x), same for y, z
Distance formula as defined in the specification under normal opera
questions.correctAnswer: Clamp each coordinate: x = clamp(px, min.x, max.x), same for y, z
questions.explanation: Clamp to each axis-aligned interval. Result is the point ON or INSIDE the AABB closest to the query point. Basis of sphere-AABB test.

questions.e: |c1 - c2| < r1 + r2. Square both sides to avoid sqrt: |c1-c2|² < (r1+r2)². Fastest collision test. Use for broad-phase rough checks.
questions.explanation: AABB-AABB intersection tests if intervals overlap on all three axes: a_min ≤ b_max AND a_max ≥ b_min for X, Y, Z. Only 6 comparisons total. O(1). This is the fastest general collision test.
questions.question: How do you test sphere-sphere collision?
options: Distance between centers < sum of radii. O(1), one distance calculation.
AABB test across all supported platforms in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxx
Ray cast in most implementations within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Distance between centers < sum of radii. O(1), one distance calculation.
Find closest point on AABB to sphere center (clamp each coordinate), then check distance to that point < sphere radius
acceptableAnswers: closest point on AABB then distance check
questions.explanation: 1. Clamp sphere center to AABB min/max → closest point. 2. Distance from closest point to center < radius = collision. O(1).
questions.question: How do you test sphere-AABB collision?

questions.e: AABB = cheap, fast, recompute on rotation. OBB = rotates with object, tighter fit, more expensive. Broad phase uses AABBs.
questions.explanation: Spheres are rotationally invariant — the test doesn't change when the sphere rotates. AABBs must be recomputed when an object rotates (the bounding box gets larger). Spheres are simpler but less tight for elongated shapes.
questions.question: What is the advantage of AABBs over OBBs?
options: More precise through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
AABB: axis-aligned — intersection test is 6 comparisons (O(1)). OBB: oriented — SAT needs 15 axes. AABB much faster, less precise.
OBB is faster for typical use cases
Same performance for typical use cases within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: AABB: axis-aligned — intersection test is 6 comparisons (O(1)). OBB: oriented — SAT needs 15 axes. AABB much faster, less precise.

questions.e: |c1-c2|² < (r1+r2)². No SQRT needed. 3 subtractions + 3 multiplies + 2 additions + 1 compare. ~7 operations total.
questions.explanation: AABBs are always axis-aligned — must be recomputed when objects rotate, and the new AABB may be much larger than the object (a rotated sword gets a huge box). OBBs rotate with the object for a tighter fit but cost more to test.
questions.question: How does sphere-sphere collision work?
options: Complex math as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Distance between centers < sum of radii. Square both sides to avoid sqrt. O(1), fastest collision test.
Matrix multiply within the current scope under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Distance between centers < sum of radii. Square both sides to avoid sqrt. O(1), fastest collision test.

questions.question: What is the slab method for ray-AABB intersection?
options: Check each vertex by default behavior with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Compute ray entry/exit times for each axis pair. Overlap of all intervals = hit. t_entry ≤ t_exit for all 3 axes.
Check distance as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Compute ray entry/exit times for each axis pair. Overlap of all intervals = hit. t_entry ≤ t_exit for all 3 axes.
questions.explanation: For each axis: t_near = (min - origin) / dir, t_far = (max - origin) / dir. t_enter = max of t_nears. t_exit = min of t_fars. Hit if t_enter ≤ t_exit.

questions.question: What is the Moller-Trumbore algorithm?
options: Fast ray-triangle intersection — solves barycentric coordinates via Cramer's rule with precomputed edge vectors. O(1).
Sphere test via the core framework with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
GJK variant by default behavior with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Fast ray-triangle intersection — solves barycentric coordinates via Cramer's rule with precomputed edge vectors. O(1).
questions.explanation: M-T: computes (u,v,t) where u,v are barycentrics and t is ray distance. Hit if u≥0, v≥0, u+v≤1, t>0. Standard method for ray-triangle.

questions.e: For each axis: t_near = (min - origin)/dir, t_far = (max - origin)/dir. Overlap of all 3 intervals = hit. Handle division by zero (parallel rays).
questions.explanation: The slab method computes t_near = (min - origin)/dir and t_far = (max - origin)/dir for each axis. t_enter = max of t_nears, t_exit = min of t_fars. Intersection if t_enter ≤ t_exit and t_exit ≥ 0. Handle parallel rays (dir=0) by checking if origin is between min and max.
questions.question: What is the slab method for ray-AABB intersection?
options: Check corners under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Compute ray entry/exit times for each axis pair. t_enter = max(t_nears), t_exit = min(t_fars). Hit if t_enter ≤ t_exit.
Check distance under normal conditions within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Compute ray entry/exit times for each axis pair. t_enter = max(t_nears), t_exit = min(t_fars). Hit if t_enter ≤ t_exit.

questions.e: M-T: precomputes edge vectors. Solves (u,v,t) where u,v are barycentrics. Hit if u≥0, v≥0, u+v≤1, t>0. Industry standard for ray-triangle.
questions.explanation: Möller-Trumbore solves ray-triangle via Cramer's rule with precomputed edge vectors E1=P1-P0, E2=P2-P0. Computes barycentric coordinates (u,v) and ray distance t. Intersection if u≥0, v≥0, u+v≤1, t≥0. Standard in every ray tracer and GPU.
questions.question: What is the Moller-Trumbore algorithm?
options: AABB test for typical use cases
Fast ray-triangle intersection — solves barycentric coordinates via Cramer's rule. O(1). Standard method.
Sphere test for typical use cases with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
GJK variant via the core framework within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Fast ray-triangle intersection — solves barycentric coordinates via Cramer's rule. O(1). Standard method.

questions.e: Parametric form: one parameter t. Each t gives a point. t < 0 is behind the ray (usually ignored). Direction should be normalized.
questions.explanation: P(t) = Origin + t·Direction with t≥0. At t=0: ray origin. t increases: points further along ray. t<0: behind the ray (usually discarded). Direction should be normalized for meaningful t values (t = distance along ray).
questions.question: What is the parametric ray equation?
options: P = O + D via the core framework with additional processing within the standard f
P(t) = Origin + t·Direction, t≥0. t=0 at origin, t increases along ray.
P = D - O under normal conditions
P = O·D + t across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: P(t) = Origin + t·Direction, t≥0. t=0 at origin, t increases along ray.

questions.question: What is the Separating Axis Theorem (SAT)?
options: A distance test via the core framework
Two convex polyhedra don't overlap iff there exists an axis where their projections don't overlap. Test candidate axes.
A bounding volume as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A ray test by default behavior with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Two convex polyhedra don't overlap iff there exists an axis where their projections don't overlap. Test candidate axes.
questions.explanation: SAT: project both shapes onto axis. If projections don't overlap → separating axis found → NO collision. If ALL candidate axes overlap → collision.

questions.question: Which axes must be tested for OBB-OBB SAT?
questions.correctAnswer: 15 axes: 3 face normals of A + 3 face normals of B + 9 cross products of edges (3×3)
acceptableAnswers: face normals plus edge cross products
questions.explanation: Box A: 3 face normals. Box B: 3 face normals. Cross products: 3 edges of A × 3 edges of B = 9 axes. Total: 15. Test all 15.

questions.e: SAT is exact for convex polyhedra. Number of axes tested depends on shape complexity. OBB-OBB = 15 axes. Triangle-triangle = 11 axes.
questions.explanation: OBB-OBB tests 15 axes: 3 face normals of A + 3 face normals of B + 9 cross products (each edge of A × each edge of B). The cross product axes catch cases where neither face normal separates — two boxes nearly touching at corners.
questions.question: What shape pairs can SAT handle?
options: Only AABBs within the current scope
Any convex polyhedra — works for OBB, convex hulls, capsules. Requires enumerating candidate separating axes.
Only spheres under normal conditions with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Infinite shapes by default behavior in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Any convex polyhedra — works for OBB, convex hulls, capsules. Requires enumerating candidate separating axes.

questions.e: SAT: fixed axis set, O(num_faces²) for OBB. GJK: iterative, ~3-6 iterations for convex shapes. GJK is more general.
questions.explanation: SAT is exact for convex polyhedra — you enumerate a finite set of candidate axes, and if ANY separates, there is no collision. For general convex shapes, SAT works but the axis set depends on the shape complexity.
questions.question: What is the difference between SAT and GJK?
options: Same thing across all supported platforms across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
SAT tests static set of axes (good for boxes). GJK iteratively finds separating axis (works for ANY convex shape, no pre-computed axes).
GJK is faster via the core framework
SAT is newer within the current scope with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: SAT tests static set of axes (good for boxes). GJK iteratively finds separating axis (works for ANY convex shape, no pre-computed axes).

questions.e: Edge-edge cross products catch cases where neither face normal separates. Example: two boxes almost touching at corners, no face normal separates.
questions.explanation: Project both shapes onto the candidate axis: interval = [min(vertices·axis), max(vertices·axis)]. If the two intervals don't overlap, this axis separates the shapes → NO collision. If ALL candidate axes have overlapping intervals → collision.
questions.question: Why test cross products of edges for OBB SAT?
options: Two boxes can have a separating axis that is perpendicular to an edge from each — the cross product direction. 3×3=9 additional axes.
It's faster for typical use cases in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Required by standard in most implementations for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Two boxes can have a separating axis that is perpendicular to an edge from each — the cross product direction. 3×3=9 additional axes.

questions.question: What does GJK compute?
options: Whether two convex shapes overlap — by checking if the origin is inside their Minkowski difference
Contact points across all supported platforms across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Normals in most implementations across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Whether two convex shapes overlap — by checking if the origin is inside their Minkowski difference
questions.explanation: GJK: iteratively builds simplex in Minkowski difference space (A - B). If origin is inside simplex → overlap. Support function finds extreme points.

questions.question: What is the Minkowski difference used in GJK?
options: A - B = {a - b | a∈A, b∈B}. If origin ∈ (A - B), then A and B intersect.
A×B through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
|A-B| via the core framework under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A - B = {a - b | a∈A, b∈B}. If origin ∈ (A - B), then A and B intersect.
questions.explanation: Minkowski difference transforms intersection test into origin-inclusion test. GJK doesn't need to explicitly compute A - B — uses support function.

questions.question: What is the support function in GJK?
options: A helper through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Returns the point in a shape furthest in a given direction — used to build the simplex iteratively
A distance function by default behavior with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Returns the point in a shape furthest in a given direction — used to build the simplex iteratively
questions.explanation: support(shape, direction) = argmax(p·direction for p in shape). GJK calls this to find next simplex vertex. O(1) for simple shapes, O(n) for meshes.

questions.e: GJK detects overlap. EPA computes MTV (Minimum Translation Vector) — how far and in what direction to push shapes apart. GJK+EPA = full collision response.
questions.explanation: EPA (Expanding Polytope Algorithm) runs after GJK detects overlap. It expands the simplex outward until it touches the Minkowski difference boundary, computing the Minimum Translation Vector — the shortest distance and direction to separate the shapes.
questions.question: What is EPA (Expanding Polytope Algorithm)?
options: Same as GJK under normal conditions
Extends GJK — after detecting overlap, EPA expands simplex to find penetration depth and contact normal
A rendering technique via the core framework within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A broad phase across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Extends GJK — after detecting overlap, EPA expands simplex to find penetration depth and contact normal

questions.e: Minkowski difference shrinks A to a point and expands B by A's shape. If that expanded shape contains the origin, they overlap.
questions.explanation: A - B = {a-b | a∈A, b∈B}. This transforms the intersection test into an origin-inclusion test: if the origin is inside A-B, then A and B share at least one point (intersect). GJK tests origin inclusion without explicitly computing A-B.
questions.question: What is the Minkowski difference?
options: A - B = {a-b | a∈A, b∈B}. If origin ∈ (A-B), A and B intersect. Transforms intersection to origin test.
A×B via the core framework with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
|A-B| in most implementations with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A - B = {a-b | a∈A, b∈B}. If origin ∈ (A-B), A and B intersect. Transforms intersection to origin test.

questions.e: support(shape, d) = extreme point in direction d. O(1) for sphere/box. O(n) for convex hull. GJK never needs explicit Minkowski difference.
questions.explanation: The support function returns the point in a shape furthest in a given direction: argmax(p·d for p in shape). For a sphere: center + radius·d̂. For a box: corner with signs matching direction. GJK uses support functions to build the simplex iteratively.
questions.question: What is the support function in GJK?
options: A helper using standard engine approaches with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Returns point in shape furthest in given direction: argmax(p·d for p in shape). GJK calls this to build simplex.
Normal across all supported platforms in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Returns point in shape furthest in given direction: argmax(p·d for p in shape). GJK calls this to build simplex.

questions.question: What does Newton's Second Law (F=ma) govern in physics engines?
options: Rotation only via the core framework
Linear acceleration: a = F/m. Integrate acceleration to get velocity, velocity to get position.
Collision only under normal conditions across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Nothing as defined in the specification with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Linear acceleration: a = F/m. Integrate acceleration to get velocity, velocity to get position.
questions.explanation: Forces cause acceleration. Gravity: F=mg. Friction, springs, constraints all produce forces. Integration accumulates force effects over time.

questions.question: What is the inertia tensor?
options: A scalar for typical use cases
3×3 matrix describing how mass is distributed — determines angular acceleration from torque: τ = I·α
A vector through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A force for typical use cases with additional processing for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: 3×3 matrix describing how mass is distributed — determines angular acceleration from torque: τ = I·α
questions.explanation: Rotational analog of mass. Inertia tensor depends on shape and mass distribution. Diagonalized in principal axes. Harder to rotate around axes with larger inertia.

questions.e: ω is a 3D vector. Direction = axis of rotation (right-hand rule). magnitude = radians per second. L = I·ω (angular momentum).
questions.explanation: The inertia tensor I is a 3×3 matrix describing mass distribution. The same torque applied around different axes produces different angular acceleration depending on how mass is distributed. τ = I·α (rotational Newton's Second Law).
questions.question: What is angular velocity?
options: Same as linear for typical use cases for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
ω — rate of rotation around an axis. Direction = rotation axis, magnitude = speed (rad/s).
A force via the core framework
A position for typical use cases with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: ω — rate of rotation around an axis. Direction = rotation axis, magnitude = speed (rad/s).

questions.e: Rotational mass. Sphere: diagonal (all equal). Box: diagonal (different per axis). Non-diagonal for arbitrary shapes. Diagonalized in body frame.
questions.explanation: 6 DOF: position(3) + orientation(3). Orientation can be Euler angles (3), quaternion (4 with 1 constraint), or rotation matrix (9 with 6 constraints). State vector includes: position, velocity, orientation, angular velocity.
questions.question: What is the inertia tensor?
options: A scalar through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
3×3 matrix describing mass distribution. τ = I·α (torque = inertia tensor × angular acceleration). Depends on shape.
A vector across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A force via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: 3×3 matrix describing mass distribution. τ = I·α (torque = inertia tensor × angular acceleration). Depends on shape.
3 translational (x, y, z) + 3 rotational (pitch, yaw, roll)
acceptableAnswers: x y z pitch yaw roll
questions.explanation: 6 DOF = complete description of position and orientation in 3D space. A rigid body's state = position(3) + velocity(3) + orientation(4) + angularVelocity(3).
questions.question: What are the six degrees of freedom of a rigid body?

questions.e: COM = weighted average of mass distribution. Linear dynamics use COM position and velocity. Rotation is around COM.
questions.explanation: Center of mass is the weighted average of mass distribution. Linear dynamics (F=ma) apply at COM. Forces applied away from COM create torque (τ = r × F) and cause rotation. COM simplifies: translate like a point mass, rotate around COM.
questions.question: What is the center of mass?
options: Geometric center within the current scope for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
The point where all mass is concentrated for linear motion. Forces applied off-center create torque (rotation).
Arbitrary point via the core framework with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Body origin via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: The point where all mass is concentrated for linear motion. Forces applied off-center create torque (rotation).

questions.question: What is Euler integration?
options: Exact solution within the current scope with additional processing for common use casesxxxxx
