options: When property replicates from server to client, OnRep_Func() is called on client — add effects, update UI
Server call across all supported platforms with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Multicast in most implementations within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: When property replicates from server to client, OnRep_Func() is called on client — add effects, update UI

questions.e: Use Replicated for position/velocity (handled by movement component). Use ReplicatedUsing for health (need damage flash), state (need animation trigger).
questions.explanation: OnRep fires only on clients when a replicated value arrives from the server. The server must manually trigger effects when it changes the variable. This is a common bug: server changes health → expects damage flash, but OnRep doesn't fire on the server.
questions.question: What is the difference between Replicated and ReplicatedUsing?
options: Same as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Replicated: silent update (client variable changes, no callback). ReplicatedUsing: update + callback function invoked.
One is faster as defined in the specification with additional processing with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Replicated: silent update (client variable changes, no callback). ReplicatedUsing: update + callback function invoked.

questions.e: OnRep is client-only. Server: change variable → call effect function manually OR rely on the client to trigger effects via OnRep.
questions.explanation: PushModel is an opt-in UE5 optimization: you explicitly call MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME(Class, Property) when a replicated property changes. Without PushModel, UE compares every replicated property every tick to detect changes — expensive for many properties.
questions.question: Does the server call OnRep when it changes a ReplicatedUsing variable?
options: Yes, always as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
No — OnRep only fires on CLIENTS when the replicated value arrives. Server must manually call effects.
Only in Blueprints using standard engine approaches with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: No — OnRep only fires on CLIENTS when the replicated value arrives. Server must manually call effects.

questions.e: PushModel: opt-in. You explicitly mark properties dirty. Without it, UE compares ALL replicated properties every tick (expensive). PushModel reduces comparison cost.
questions.explanation: OnRep_Health typically receives the old value for comparison. void OnRep_Health(float OldHealth) — you can compute damage taken = OldHealth - Health. This is more useful than just knowing 'health changed' — you can scale effects by damage magnitude.
questions.question: What is PushModel (UE5) for replication?
options: A rendering technique in most implementations with additional processing in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxx
Explicit dirty marking: MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME. Only replicates changed properties. Saves bandwidth.
A network protocol within the current scope
Deprecated using standard engine approaches in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Explicit dirty marking: MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME. Only replicates changed properties. Saves bandwidth.

questions.question: What is O(1) and when is it achievable?
options: Any algorithm through the built-in API in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Constant time — independent of input size. Array access, hash table lookup (average). The ideal complexity.
Only in math within the current scope
Never using standard engine approaches under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Constant time — independent of input size. Array access, hash table lookup (average). The ideal complexity.
questions.explanation: O(1) = runtime doesn't grow with input. arr[i], map.find(key) average. The holy grail. Rare for complex operations.

questions.question: What is O(n²) and why is it dangerous?
options: Quadratic — nested loops over input. 1000 elements = 1M operations. 100K elements = 10B. Unusable at scale.
Always fine as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
O(1) using standard engine approaches across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Quadratic — nested loops over input. 1000 elements = 1M operations. 100K elements = 10B. Unusable at scale.
questions.explanation: O(n²): bubble sort, naive collision detection. Double the input = 4x the time. Must be avoided at scale. Use hashing or spatial partitioning.

questions.e: log₂(1M) ≈ 20. Binary search, BST find/insert, heap push/pop. Any 'divide problem size by constant each step' algorithm.
questions.explanation: log₂(1,000,000) ≈ 20. Binary search halves the search space each step. Balanced BST operations (find, insert, delete) all descend at most the tree height. Each level discards half the remaining possibilities.
questions.question: What is O(log n) and what algorithms achieve it?
options: Constant as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Logarithmic — binary search, balanced tree operations. Doubling input = 1 more step. Scales excellently.
Quadratic as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Logarithmic — binary search, balanced tree operations. Doubling input = 1 more step. Scales excellently.

questions.e: Ω(n log n) = lower bound for comparison-based sorting. Merge sort, heap sort, quicksort (average) achieve this. Counting sort beats it by NOT comparing.
questions.explanation: Ω(n log n) is a proven lower bound for comparison-based sorting: there are n! possible orderings, each comparison gives 1 bit of information, so you need at least log₂(n!) ≈ n log n comparisons to distinguish the correct ordering.
questions.question: What is O(n log n) and why is it the lower bound for comparison sorting?
options: Decision tree argument: n! possible orderings, each comparison gives 1 bit → need log₂(n!) ≈ n log n comparisons. Optimal.
It could be faster with quantum computing via the core framework with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
It's not a real bound across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Decision tree argument: n! possible orderings, each comparison gives 1 bit → need log₂(n!) ≈ n log n comparisons. Optimal.

questions.e: Amortized = average over sequence. Push N elements: O(N) total = O(1) amortized per push. Reallocation copies old elements — costs O(old_size).
questions.explanation: Most push_back calls are O(1) (just increment size). Occasionally a push_back triggers O(n) reallocation (allocate new buffer, copy all elements). The occasional expensive operation averages to O(1) per push when amortized over many calls.
questions.question: What is amortized O(1) and how does vector::push_back achieve it?
options: Always O(1) as defined in the specification for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Individual push_back may be O(n) (reallocation), but averaged over N pushes it's O(1) — reallocation happens rarely.
Never O(1) within the current scope
It doesn't achieve it under normal conditions in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Individual push_back may be O(n) (reallocation), but averaged over N pushes it's O(1) — reallocation happens rarely.

questions.question: What physically stores a single bit in DRAM?
questions.correctAnswer: A capacitor
questions.explanation: DRAM stores each bit as charge in a tiny capacitor. The capacitor leaks charge over time, which is why DRAM needs periodic refreshing (~64ms per row).

questions.question: How many unique values can 8 bits represent?
questions.explanation: 8 bits = 2^8 = 256 unique values. Range depends on interpretation: 0-255 (unsigned) or -128 to 127 (two's complement signed).

questions.question: What is the decimal value of binary 1011?
What does the hexadecimal digit F represent in decimal?
questions.explanation: Hexadecimal uses 0-9 and A-F. A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15.

questions.question: How many bytes are in a kilobyte (binary, not decimal)?
questions.explanation: In computing, a kilobyte (KiB) is 2^10 = 1024 bytes. The decimal kilobyte (1000 bytes) is used for storage marketing but not for memory addressing.

questions.question: What is the largest unsigned integer representable in 4 bits?
questions.explanation: 4 bits can represent 2^4 = 16 values (0-15). The largest is 15 (binary 1111).

questions.question: Which numbering system uses digits 0-7?
questions.correctAnswer: Octal
questions.explanation: Octal uses base-8 with digits 0-7. It was historically popular because 3 bits = 1 octal digit (e.g., Unix file permissions use octal notation).

questions.question: How many bytes are in a binary kilobyte?
questions.explanation: A KiB is 2^10 = 1024 bytes.

questions.question: What is the largest unsigned integer in 4 bits?
questions.explanation: 4 bits = 2^4 = 16 values.

What is binary 1101 in decimal?
A bit is physically what?
questions.correctAnswer: A transistor at high/low voltage
questions.explanation: A bit is a transistor at low (~0V) or high (~1.2V) voltage.

questions.question: What voltage distinguishes 1 from 0?
questions.explanation: Above ~0.6V = 1; below = 0.

questions.question: Name the three terminals of a MOSFET.
questions.correctAnswer: Source, drain, gate
questions.explanation: Gate voltage controls current flow between source and drain.

questions.question: When does an NFET conduct?
questions.correctAnswer: Gate high (~1.2V)
questions.explanation: NFET: Gate high -> conducts, pulls to ground.

questions.question: Why is CMOS power-efficient?
options: Transistor gates are capacitors; current flows only when switching
questions.correctAnswer: Transistor gates are capacitors; current flows only when switching
questions.explanation: Static CMOS draws near-zero power. Only switching draws current.

questions.question: Which gate outputs 0 only when both inputs are 1?
questions.correctAnswer: NAND

questions.question: Which gate is universal?
questions.correctAnswer: NAND
questions.explanation: NAND is universal: NOT = NAND(A,A). Entire CPU from NANDs.

questions.question: What does a half adder output?
questions.correctAnswer: Sum and carry
questions.explanation: Half adder: A XOR B = Sum, A AND B = Carry.

questions.question: How does full adder differ from half adder?
questions.correctAnswer: Adds 3 bits (A,B,Cin)
questions.explanation: Full adder: A + B + Cin -> Sum + Cout.

questions.question: What is a ripple-carry adder?
options: Chained full adders, each Cout feeds next Cin
questions.correctAnswer: Chained full adders, each Cout feeds next Cin
questions.explanation: An 8-bit adder chains 8 full adders. Real CPUs use carry-lookahead.

questions.question: How does ALU subtract?
questions.correctAnswer: Inverts B and sets CarryIn=1
questions.explanation: A - B = A + (~B + 1). Same adder, different inputs.

questions.question: What is positional notation?
questions.correctAnswer: Digit weights = power of base
questions.explanation: 42 = 4*10 + 2*1. Binary column weights are powers of 2.

questions.question: What column weight does LSB always have?
questions.explanation: The rightmost bit represents 2^0 = 1, regardless of width.

questions.question: Why does one hex digit = 4 bits?
options: 16 = 2^4 so 4-bit pattern maps to one hex digit
questions.correctAnswer: 16 = 2^4 so 4-bit pattern maps to one hex digit
questions.explanation: 16 = 2^4. Every 4-bit pattern maps to exactly one hex digit.

questions.question: In two's complement, how do you negate a number?
options: Set the MSB to 1 within th
questions.correctAnswer: Flip all bits and add 1
questions.explanation: Two's complement negation: invert all bits (one's complement), then add 1. Example: 5 (00000101) → flip (11111010) → add 1 (11111011) = -5.

questions.question: What is the two's complement representation of -1 in 8 bits?
questions.explanation: -1 in two's complement is all bits set to 1. For 8 bits: 11111111. This works because 11111111 + 1 = 00000000 (overflow discarded), giving 0.

questions.question: What is the range of an 8-bit signed integer?
questions.explanation: 8-bit signed: -2^7 to 2^7-1 = -128 to 127. The asymmetry (-128 exists but +128 doesn't) is a two's complement property: 10000000 = -128.

questions.question: In big-endian byte order, where is the most significant byte stored?
options: It depends on the operating system
questions.correctAnswer: At the lowest memory address
questions.explanation: Big-endian stores the most significant byte at the lowest address (the 'big end' first). Network byte order is big-endian. Little-endian (x86) stores the least significant byte first.

questions.question: What problem does sign extension solve?
options: Adding two negative numbers under normal conditions with additional processing
Converting a smaller signed integer to a larger one
Detecting overflow as defined in the specification
questions.correctAnswer: Converting a smaller signed integer to a larger one
questions.explanation: Sign extension copies the sign bit to all new higher bits when widening a signed integer. Example: int8_t -5 (11111011) → int16_t (11111111 11111011) = still -5.

questions.question: Why does 0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3 in floating-point?
questions.correctAnswer: 0.1 and 0.2 have infinite repeating binary representations that must be truncated to 23 (float) or 52 (double) mantissa bits — the sum of two approximations is inexact
acceptableAnswers: 0.1 is a repeating fraction in binary
0.1 cannot be exactly represented in binary
questions.explanation: 0.1 decimal = 0.0001100110011... binary (infinite repeating). IEEE 754 stores only 23 (float) or 52 (double) mantissa bits. Truncated values are approximations. Sum of two approximations compounds error: 0.1+0.2≈0.3000000119≠0.3.

questions.question: What are subnormal numbers and why are they a performance trap?
questions.correctAnswer: Numbers so small they use implicit leading 0 instead of 1 — operations are 10-100× slower because the FPU traps to microcode
acceptableAnswers: very small floats that are extremely slow
numbers below normal range that trap to microcode
questions.explanation: When exponent=0, formula changes from (1.mantissa) to (0.mantissa). FPU can't handle these in one cycle — traps to microcode. Game physics drifting into subnormals can drop from 60 FPS to 6 FPS. Fix: FTZ/DAZ in MXCSR.

questions.question: What is the default IEEE 754 rounding mode and why 'round to nearest even'?
options: Round to nearest even eliminates statistical bias — .5 rounds up half the time and down half the time, preventing systematic inflation
It's required by the C++ standard
questions.correctAnswer: Round to nearest even eliminates statistical bias — .5 rounds up half the time and down half the time, preventing systematic inflation
questions.explanation: Always rounding .5 up adds small positive bias per tie-break. Over millions of values, bias compounds. Round to even (1.5→2, 2.5→2, 3.5→4, 4.5→4) distributes ties equally. Default on all IEEE 754 hardware.

questions.question: Why does NaN != NaN?
options: NaN means 'not a valid number' — two invalid things aren't necessarily equal. Every comparison with NaN returns false by design
NaN comparison is undefined behavior in C++
NaN equality depends on quiet vs signaling encoding
questions.correctAnswer: NaN means 'not a valid number' — two invalid things aren't necessarily equal. Every comparison with NaN returns false by design
questions.explanation: NaN = 'result is not a number.' No meaningful comparison between two NaN results. IEEE 754 specifies all comparisons with NaN return false — including NaN==NaN. Use std::isnan() to check.

questions.question: What is FMA (Fused Multiply-Add) and why is it more accurate?
questions.correctAnswer: Computes a*b + c with a SINGLE rounding step instead of two — without FMA, (a*b) rounds once then +c rounds again, accumulating twice the error
acceptableAnswers: multiply and add with one rounding
questions.explanation: Without FMA: a*b rounds once (intermediate result fits format), +c rounds again = double error. FMA: multiply produces infinite-precision intermediate, adds c, THEN rounds once. Available on all modern CPUs, fundamental GPU matrix op.

questions.question: Why is signed integer overflow undefined behavior but unsigned overflow is defined?
questions.correctAnswer: Unsigned overflow wraps modulo 2^n (well-defined). Signed overflow is UB so compilers can optimize assuming it never happens — enabling better code generation
acceptableAnswers: unsigned wraps, signed is UB for optimization
compiler optimizations assume no signed overflow
questions.explanation: Unsigned: result wraps (well-defined). Signed: overflow is UB so compiler can assume a+b never overflows. This allows removing overflow checks: 'if (a+b < 0)' deleted because compiler proves a,b positive so a+b 'can't' overflow. Use __builtin_add_overflow().

questions.question: Why is integer division so slow compared to multiplication?
questions.correctAnswer: Division takes 20-80 cycles (iterative algorithm, hard to pipeline) vs 3-5 for multiply. Compilers replace division by constant with multiplication by reciprocal
acceptableAnswers: division is 20-80 cycles vs 3-5 for multiply
questions.explanation: Integer division uses iterative algorithms (non-restoring/SRT) taking many cycles and resisting pipelining. Compilers emit (x*0xCCCCCCCD)>>35 for x/10 — ~5× faster than DIV instruction.

questions.question: What is NaN boxing in JavaScript engines?
questions.correctAnswer: Exploiting 2^51 unused NaN mantissa bits to store pointers alongside doubles in 64 bits — NaN check on top bits distinguishes pointers from numbers
acceptableAnswers: using NaN mantissa bits to store pointers
storing pointers in NaN bit patterns
questions.explanation: 64-bit IEEE 754 value with all-1s exponent and non-zero mantissa is NaN. 51 unused mantissa bits remain. V8/SpiderMonkey store pointers there: if NaN check passes, it's a pointer; otherwise a double. Single 64-bit value holds either.

questions.question: Negate a number in two's complement?
questions.correctAnswer: Flip all bits and add 1
questions.explanation: Two's complement negation: invert all bits, then add 1.

questions.question: 8-bit two's complement of -1?
questions.explanation: -1 is all bits set to 1.

questions.question: 8-bit signed integer range?
Big-endian: where is MSB stored?
questions.correctAnswer: Lowest address
questions.explanation: Big-endian stores MSB at lowest address. Network byte order is big-endian.

questions.question: Why 0.1+0.2 != 0.3?
questions.correctAnswer: 0.1 and 0.2 have infinite repeating binary representations truncated to 23 or 52 mantissa bits
acceptableAnswers: 0.1 is a repeating fraction in binary
questions.explanation: 0.1 decimal = 0.0001100110011... binary. IEEE 754 truncates.

questions.question: What are subnormal numbers?
questions.correctAnswer: Numbers so small they use implicit leading 0 instead of 1. Operations are 10-100x slower.
acceptableAnswers: very small floats that are slow
questions.explanation: When exponent=0, formula changes from (1.mantissa) to (0.mantissa).

questions.question: Why round-to-nearest-even?
questions.correctAnswer: Eliminates statistical bias; .5 rounds up half the time and down half the time
questions.explanation: Always rounding .5 up adds bias. Round-to-even distributes ties equally.

questions.question: Why NaN != NaN?
options: NaN is invalid; comparisons with NaN return false
questions.correctAnswer: NaN is invalid; comparisons with NaN return false
questions.explanation: IEEE 754 specifies all comparisons with NaN return false, including NaN==NaN.

questions.question: What is FMA?
questions.correctAnswer: Computes a*b + c with a SINGLE rounding step instead of two
acceptableAnswers: multiply and add with one rounding
questions.explanation: Without FMA: a*b rounds once, +c rounds again. FMA rounds once.

questions.question: Why is signed overflow UB but unsigned defined?
questions.correctAnswer: Unsigned wraps modulo 2^n. Signed is UB so compiler can optimize assuming it never happens
acceptableAnswers: unsigned wraps, signed is UB for optimization
questions.explanation: Unsigned: wraps. Signed overflow: UB. Compiler removes overflow checks.

questions.question: Why is integer division slower than multiply?
questions.correctAnswer: Division takes 20-80 cycles vs 3-5 for multiply
questions.explanation: Division uses iterative algorithms. Compilers replace with multiply by reciprocal.

questions.question: What is NaN boxing?
questions.correctAnswer: Storing pointers in 2^51 unused NaN mantissa bits alongside doubles in 64 bits
acceptableAnswers: using NaN mantissa bits to store pointers
questions.explanation: All-1s exponent + non-zero mantissa = NaN. 51 unused mantissa bits store pointers.

questions.question: Why no separate subtraction circuit?
options: Would take too much die space
questions.correctAnswer: ALU inverts B and adds 1
questions.explanation: A - B = A + (~B + 1). Same adder, saving transistors.

questions.question: 1111 as 4-bit signed = ?
Asymmetry in two's complement range?
questions.correctAnswer: Negative has one more (-8 to +7)
questions.explanation: 1000 = -8 has no positive counterpart (+8 doesn't fit).

questions.question: MSB weight for signed?
questions.explanation: Unsigned MSB = +2^(n-1). Signed MSB = -2^(n-1).

questions.question: 8-bit two's complement of -13?
Little-endian: 0x12345678 at address 1000?
questions.explanation: Little-endian stores LSB at lowest address.

questions.question: Why did x86 choose little-endian?
questions.correctAnswer: LSB first for extending 8-bit addition
questions.explanation: Same ALU circuit processes first byte regardless of operand width.

questions.question: What does htons() do on little-endian?
questions.correctAnswer: Swaps bytes: 0x1234 -> 0x3412
questions.explanation: Host TO Network Short. On x86, compiles to BSWAP.

questions.question: x86 MUL stores result where?
questions.correctAnswer: RDX:RAX
questions.explanation: Full 128-bit: RDX (high), RAX (low).

questions.question: Detect unsigned overflow?
questions.correctAnswer: Check if result < operand
questions.explanation: If *r = a+b; (*r < a) detects wrap-around.

questions.question: int8_t(127) + 1 = ?
questions.explanation: 01111111+00000001=10000000. Signed=-128. Signed overflow is UB.

questions.question: INT_MIN / -1 is UB because?
questions.correctAnswer: +2147483648 doesn't fit
questions.explanation: Result INT_MAX+1 overflows. Crashes on x86.

questions.question: Compiler optimizes x/10 as?
questions.correctAnswer: (x * 0xCCCCCCCD) >> 35
questions.explanation: Multiply by reciprocal when divisor is compile-time constant.

questions.question: % 16 optimized as?
questions.explanation: For power-of-two divisors, x % n -> x & (n-1).

questions.question: IEEE 754 float format?
questions.correctAnswer: 1 sign, 8 exp, 23 mantissa
questions.explanation: 32-bit: sign=1, exponent=8 (bias 127), mantissa=23 with hidden leading 1.

questions.question: Float representation of 6.5?
Why is 0.1 inexact in binary?
questions.correctAnswer: Infinite binary fraction (0.0001100110011...)
questions.explanation: Each binary fractional position is 1/2, 1/4, 1/8... 1/10 can't be finite.

questions.question: What is ulp?
questions.correctAnswer: Unit in the Last Place — spacing between consecutive floating-point numbers

questions.question: Catastrophic cancellation?
questions.correctAnswer: Subtracting nearly-equal numbers loses precision
questions.explanation: 1.0000001 - 1.0 = 0.0000001. Only 1 digit precision.

questions.question: What does BSWAP do?
questions.correctAnswer: Byte swap for htonl/htons
questions.explanation: Compilers recognize byte-swapping pattern and emit BSWAP.

questions.question: std::bit_cast does what?
questions.correctAnswer: Reinterprets bits safely between types
questions.explanation: Constexpr, avoids UB from strict aliasing violations.

questions.explanation: Little-endian: LSB first

questions.question: Don't use bitfields in protocols because:

questions.explanation: Exponent=0, no implicit 1

questions.correctAnswer: LSB first for width extension
questions.explanation: Extend 8-bit add to wider widths

questions.question: Modern ARM defaults to:
questions.correctAnswer: Little

IEEE 754 value formula:

Why two's complement?
questions.correctAnswer: One adder for both
questions.explanation: Same circuit works for signed/unsigned

questions.question: Double-precision bias:

questions.question: int8_t 0xFF:
Double exponent bits:
MSB weight in 8-bit signed:

questions.question: Int8_t does NOT include:

questions.question: 0xFF unsigned vs signed:
questions.explanation: Same bits, different interpretation

questions.question: Carry flag (CF) indicates:
questions.correctAnswer: Unsigned overflow
questions.explanation: Carry from MSB = unsigned overflow

questions.question: Overflow flag (OF) indicates:
questions.correctAnswer: Signed overflow
questions.explanation: OF for signed overflow detection

questions.question: What does the x86 instruction `mov eax, [ebx]` do?
options: Copy EAX value into EBX across all supported platforms
Load value at memory address in EBX into EAX
Move the address of EBX into EAX
Compare EAX and EBX as defined in the specification
questions.correctAnswer: Load value at memory address in EBX into EAX
questions.explanation: Brackets `[]` denote memory dereference. `mov eax, [ebx]` loads the 32-bit value at the memory address stored in EBX into register EAX.

questions.question: How many general-purpose registers does x86-64 have (excluding stack/base pointer)?
questions.explanation: x86-64 has 16 GPRs: RAX, RBX, RCX, RDX, RSI, RDI, RBP, RSP, and R8-R15. Excluding RSP (stack pointer) and RBP (base pointer), 14 remain for general use.

questions.question: What is the difference between `cmp` and `sub` on x86?
options: cmp modifies the destination, sub doesn't
sub modifies the destination, cmp doesn't
questions.correctAnswer: sub modifies the destination, cmp doesn't
questions.explanation: `cmp a, b` computes a-b and sets flags but discards the result. `sub a, b` computes a-b, sets flags, AND stores the result in a. cmp is used before conditional jumps.

questions.question: Which flag is set when an arithmetic operation results in zero?
questions.correctAnswer: ZF
questions.explanation: The Zero Flag (ZF) is set to 1 when an operation produces a zero result. `je` (jump if equal) checks ZF=1. cmp followed by je is how if/else compiles.

questions.question: What does `jne` (jump if not equal) check?
questions.correctAnswer: ZF=0
questions.explanation: jne checks if ZF (Zero Flag) is 0, meaning the previous comparison was NOT equal. jne and jnz are the same instruction with different mnemonics.

questions.question: What is a 'nop sled' used for in assembly?
options: Padding code to align jump targets
questions.correctAnswer: All of the above
questions.explanation: NOP (0x90) instructions are multi-purpose: aligning jump targets to cache line boundaries (perf), filling unused space, and in exploit development as a 'landing zone' for shellcode.

questions.question: mov eax, [ebx] does what?
options: Load value at address in EBX into EAX
questions.correctAnswer: Load value at address in EBX into EAX
questions.explanation: Brackets [] mean memory dereference.

questions.question: How many GPRs in x86-64 excluding RSP/RBP?
questions.explanation: x86-64 has 16 GPRs. Excluding RSP and RBP, 14 remain.

questions.question: cmp vs sub difference?
questions.correctAnswer: sub modifies dest, cmp doesn't
questions.explanation: cmp sets flags, discards result. sub sets flags AND stores result.

questions.question: Which flag set on zero result?
questions.correctAnswer: ZF
questions.explanation: Zero Flag (ZF) set when operation produces zero result.

questions.question: jne checks what?
questions.correctAnswer: ZF=0
questions.explanation: jne checks ZF=0; previous comparison was NOT equal.

questions.question: NOP sled used for?
questions.correctAnswer: All of the above
questions.explanation: NOP (0x90): alignment, padding, exploit landing zones.

questions.question: Writing to EAX zero-extends to RAX?
questions.correctAnswer: Yes, upper 32 bits cleared
questions.explanation: Writing 32-bit register zero-extends to 64-bit. 16/8-bit writes do NOT.

questions.question: What is RIP-relative addressing?
questions.correctAnswer: Addresses relative to RIP
questions.explanation: Default for global data in x86-64. Enables position-independent code.

questions.question: LEA used for?
questions.correctAnswer: Address arithmetic without memory access
questions.explanation: lea rax,[rax+rax*2] = rax*3. Runs on AGU, parallel to ALU.

questions.question: What does push rax do?
questions.correctAnswer: Decrements RSP by 8, stores RAX at [RSP]
questions.explanation: RSP -= 8, [RSP] = rax. Stack grows downward.

questions.question: What does ret do?
questions.correctAnswer: Pops RIP from stack
questions.explanation: Pops return address (pushed by call) into RIP.

questions.question: xor eax,eax does what?
questions.correctAnswer: Zeroes EAX
questions.explanation: 2 bytes vs mov eax,0 = 5 bytes. CPU recognizes zeroing idiom.

questions.question: What flags does test eax,eax set?
options: ZF and SF based on EAX & EAX
questions.correctAnswer: ZF and SF based on EAX & EAX
questions.explanation: test a,b computes a&b, sets ZF and SF.

questions.question: SysV: register for 1st int arg?
questions.correctAnswer: RDI
questions.explanation: SysV: RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9. Then stack.

questions.question: Windows x64: 1st arg register?
questions.correctAnswer: RCX
questions.explanation: Windows: RCX, RDX, R8, R9. Plus 32 bytes shadow space.

questions.question: After cmp with equal values, which jump taken?
questions.correctAnswer: je (equal)
questions.explanation: ZF=1 so je is taken.

questions.question: ja vs jg difference?
questions.correctAnswer: ja=unsigned, jg=signed
questions.explanation: ja checks CF=0 and ZF=0. jg checks ZF=0 and SF=OF.

questions.question: Callee-saved in SysV?
questions.correctAnswer: RBX, RBP, R12-R15
questions.explanation: Callee must preserve these. Caller-saved: RAX, RCX, RDX, RSI, RDI, R8-R11.

questions.question: Function prologue in x86-64?
options: push rbp; mov rbp,rsp; sub rsp,N
questions.correctAnswer: push rbp; mov rbp,rsp; sub rsp,N
questions.explanation: Save caller RBP, set our frame, allocate locals.

questions.question: What does leave do?
questions.correctAnswer: mov rsp,rbp; pop rbp
questions.explanation: Restores RSP and caller's RBP.

questions.question: What does call do?
questions.correctAnswer: Pushes RIP then jumps
questions.explanation: Pushes next instruction address, then jumps to target.

questions.question: x86 scale factor must be?
questions.explanation: Scale: 1(byte), 2(word), 4(dword), 8(qword).

questions.question: At -O2, int add(int a,int b) compiles as?
questions.correctAnswer: lea eax,[rdi+rsi]; ret
questions.explanation: No stack spills, no prologue (leaf function). Optimized.

questions.question: Strength reduction?
options: Replace expensive with cheap: i*4 -> shl
questions.correctAnswer: Replace expensive with cheap: i*4 -> shl
questions.explanation: i*4 -> shl. x/10 -> multiply by reciprocal. 5x faster.

questions.question: Constant folding?
options: Compute constant expressions at compile time
questions.correctAnswer: Compute constant expressions at compile time
questions.explanation: 3+4 computed at compile time -> mov eax,7.

questions.question: [rbx+rcx*4+8] addressing used for?
questions.correctAnswer: Struct field array[i].field offset 8
questions.explanation: rbx=array base, rcx=index, 4=sizeof(int), 8=field offset.

questions.question: movzx eax, byte [rcx] does what?
questions.correctAnswer: Load byte, zero-extend to 32 bits
questions.explanation: movzx = Move with Zero-eXtend. For signed: movsx.

questions.question: Partial register stall?
questions.correctAnswer: Writing AX/AL/AH without zero-extend can stall when full register is read
questions.explanation: Writing EAX zero-extends. Writing AX does not; CPU may stall merging partial writes.

questions.question: XMM registers used for?
questions.correctAnswer: 128-bit SSE SIMD
questions.explanation: XMM0-15 are 128-bit SSE vector registers (4xfloat, 2xdouble).

questions.question: sar vs shr difference?
options: sar preserves sign bit, shr fills zeros
questions.correctAnswer: sar preserves sign bit, shr fills zeros
questions.explanation: shr = shift right zeros. sar = shift arithmetic right (sign-fill).

questions.question: Function inlining at assembly level?
options: Body inserted at call site, no call instruction
questions.correctAnswer: Body inserted at call site, no call instruction
questions.explanation: After inlining, no call instruction. Code appears directly at call site.

questions.question: Get Intel syntax from GCC?
questions.correctAnswer: g++ -S -masm=intel file.cpp
questions.explanation: -masm=intel switches to Intel syntax (dest, src order).

questions.question: Code size: xor eax,eax vs mov eax,0?
questions.correctAnswer: xor=2 bytes, mov=5 bytes
questions.explanation: xor eax,eax = 31 C0 (2 bytes). mov eax,0 = B8 00 00 00 00 (5 bytes).

questions.question: RAX holds:
questions.correctAnswer: Return value
questions.explanation: Accumulator/return

questions.question: Intel syntax: mov eax,[rbp-8] means:
questions.correctAnswer: EAX stores [rbp-8]
questions.explanation: Intel: dest=eax, src=[rbp-8]

questions.question: CALL instruction:
questions.correctAnswer: Pushes RIP, jumps
questions.explanation: Pushes return address then jumps

questions.question: LEA does:
questions.correctAnswer: Computes address, no access
questions.explanation: Load Effective Address = arithmetic

questions.question: xor eax,eax is preferred because:
questions.correctAnswer: Both A and C
questions.explanation: 2 bytes vs 5, recognized as zeroing idiom

questions.question: INC does NOT set:
questions.correctAnswer: CF
questions.explanation: INC doesn't modify carry flag

questions.question: CMP instruction:
questions.correctAnswer: Subtracts, discards, sets flags
questions.explanation: CMP A-B, flags set, result discarded

questions.question: Signed less-than jump:
questions.correctAnswer: jl
questions.explanation: jl checks SF!=OF

questions.question: Unsigned below jump:
questions.correctAnswer: jb
questions.explanation: jb checks CF=1

questions.question: Function prologue:
options: push rbp; mov rbp,rsp; sub rsp,N
questions.correctAnswer: push rbp; mov rbp,rsp; sub rsp,N
questions.explanation: Standard stack frame setup

questions.question: Writing to EAX affects RAX:
questions.correctAnswer: Zeroes upper 32 bits
questions.explanation: 32-bit write zero-extends

questions.question: Writing to AX (16-bit):
questions.correctAnswer: Upper 48 bits preserved
questions.explanation: 16-bit write doesn't zero-extend

questions.question: x86-64 GPR count:
questions.explanation: RAX,RBX,RCX,RDX,RSI,RDI,RBP,RSP,R8-R15

questions.question: Scale factor values:
questions.explanation: byte,word,dword,qword

questions.question: MOVZX =
questions.correctAnswer: Zero-extend
questions.explanation: Move with zero-extension

questions.question: Instruction that pushes RIP and jumps:
questions.correctAnswer: CALL
questions.explanation: CALL pushes return addr, jumps

questions.question: RFLAGS contains:
questions.correctAnswer: Status flags
questions.explanation: ZF,CF,SF,OF etc.

questions.question: System V arg1:
questions.correctAnswer: RDI
questions.explanation: RDI=arg1

questions.question: MS x64 arg1:
questions.correctAnswer: RCX
questions.explanation: RCX=arg1

questions.question: Stack grows:
questions.correctAnswer: Downward
questions.explanation: PUSH decrements RSP

questions.question: RET does:
questions.correctAnswer: Pops addr to RIP, RSP+=8
questions.explanation: RIP=[RSP]; RSP+=8

questions.question: Pipeline bubble:
questions.correctAnswer: Wasted cycle for dependency
questions.explanation: Inserted when instruction waits for previous

questions.question: Branch mispredict penalty:
questions.explanation: Flush cost

questions.explanation: TAGE+perceptron

questions.question: Operand forwarding bypasses:
questions.correctAnswer: Register write-back
questions.explanation: Direct ALU output to next instruction

questions.question: XMM register count:
questions.explanation: XMM0-XMM15

questions.question: AVX register width:
questions.correctAnswer: 256-bit
questions.explanation: YMM are 256-bit

questions.question: syscall entry:
questions.correctAnswer: syscall
questions.explanation: Modern x86-64 syscall

questions.question: push rax decrements RSP by:
questions.explanation: 64-bit push

questions.question: pop rax increments RSP by:
questions.explanation: 64-bit pop

questions.question: Which addressing mode is [rax+rcx*4]?
questions.correctAnswer: Base+index*scale
questions.explanation: Array access pattern

questions.question: JE checks which flag?
questions.correctAnswer: ZF
questions.explanation: Jump if Equal: ZF=1

questions.question: JA checks:
questions.correctAnswer: CF=0 and ZF=0
questions.explanation: Jump if Above (unsigned)

questions.question: JL checks:
questions.correctAnswer: SF!=OF
questions.explanation: Jump if Less (signed)

questions.question: RSP is:
questions.correctAnswer: Stack pointer
questions.explanation: Stack pointer, don't use as GPR

questions.question: LEA can be used for fast multiply:
questions.correctAnswer: Yes, e.g., lea rax,[rax+rax*2] = x3
questions.explanation: LEA computes without memory access

questions.question: Calling convention determines:
questions.correctAnswer: All of the above
questions.explanation: ABI defines all these

questions.question: cdqe instruction:
questions.correctAnswer: Converts dword to qword (sign-extend EAX->RAX)
questions.explanation: Sign-extend for 64-bit addressing

questions.question: What are the four stages of the classic CPU instruction cycle?
questions.correctAnswer: Fetch, Decode, Execute, Writeback
questions.explanation: Fetch: load instruction from memory (PC → instruction register). Decode: determine what the instruction does. Execute: ALU performs the operation. Writeback: store result to register.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a branch predictor?
options: To guess which way a conditional branch will go
To manage power consumption with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: To guess which way a conditional branch will go
questions.explanation: The branch predictor guesses whether a conditional jump will be taken BEFORE the condition is evaluated. This keeps the pipeline full. A misprediction costs 15-20 cycles as the pipeline is flushed.

questions.question: What does 'superscalar' mean in CPU architecture?
options: The CPU runs at a higher clock speed
The CPU can execute multiple instructions per clock cycle
The CPU has multiple cores within the current scope in standard con
The CPU uses a larger cache via the core framework across c
questions.correctAnswer: The CPU can execute multiple instructions per clock cycle
questions.explanation: Superscalar CPUs have multiple execution units that can process independent instructions simultaneously. Modern CPUs issue 4-8 instructions per cycle (4-wide to 8-wide).

questions.question: What happens during a pipeline stall?
options: The CPU powers down in most implementations
The CPU switches to a different thread across all supported platforms with additional processing
A later pipeline stage waits for an earlier instruction to complete
The clock speed is reduced by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: A later pipeline stage waits for an earlier instruction to complete
questions.explanation: A pipeline stall occurs when an instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction that hasn't completed yet. The dependent instruction must wait, creating a 'bubble' in the pipeline.

questions.question: What is the typical penalty for a branch misprediction on modern CPUs?
questions.explanation: When the branch predictor guesses wrong, the speculatively executed instructions must be discarded and the pipeline restarted from the correct path. This costs ~15-20 cycles on modern x86.

questions.question: Four stages of classic CPU instruction cycle?
questions.correctAnswer: Fetch, Decode, Execute, Writeback
questions.explanation: Fetch: load instruction. Decode: determine operation. Execute: ALU. Writeback: store result.

questions.question: Branch predictor purpose?
questions.correctAnswer: Guess branch before condition known
questions.explanation: Keeps pipeline full by guessing before condition is evaluated.

questions.question: Superscalar means?
questions.correctAnswer: Multiple instructions per cycle
questions.explanation: Multiple execution units for parallel instruction processing.

questions.question: Pipeline stall?
questions.correctAnswer: Later stage waits for earlier
questions.explanation: When an instruction depends on previous result not yet available.

questions.question: Branch misprediction penalty?
questions.correctAnswer: 15-20 cycles
questions.explanation: Discard speculatively executed instructions, restart pipeline.

questions.question: Clock period at 4GHz?
questions.explanation: 1/(4x10^9) = 0.25ns. Light travels ~7.5cm.

questions.question: Speed of light as CPU bottleneck?
options: At 4GHz signal can't cross die in one cycle
questions.correctAnswer: At 4GHz signal can't cross die in one cycle
questions.explanation: In 0.25ns electrical signals travel ~4cm. Die is 1-2cm.

questions.question: RFLAGS used for?
questions.correctAnswer: Status flags from arithmetic
questions.explanation: RFLAGS: ZF, CF, SF, OF. Conditional jumps check these.

questions.question: 5-stage pipeline throughput when full?
questions.correctAnswer: 1 per cycle
questions.explanation: Each instruction takes 5 cycles latency, but one completes per cycle.

questions.question: Pipeline bubble?
options: Execute stage does nothing waiting for dependency
questions.correctAnswer: Execute stage does nothing waiting for dependency
questions.explanation: When instruction N+1 depends on N's result.

questions.question: Operand forwarding?
options: Route ALU result directly to next instruction, bypassing writeback
questions.correctAnswer: Route ALU result directly to next instruction, bypassing writeback
questions.explanation: Result available internally before register write. Eliminates many bubbles.

questions.question: Modern x86-64 pipeline depth?
questions.explanation: 14-19 stages: Fetch, Decode, Rename, Dispatch, Issue, Execute, Commit.

questions.question: What are micro-operations (uops)?
options: Each x86 instruction cracked into 1-4 uops for OoO engine
questions.correctAnswer: Each x86 instruction cracked into 1-4 uops for OoO engine
questions.explanation: add rax,rbx = 1 uop. add [mem],rax = 3 uops.

questions.question: What is the uop cache (DSB)?
questions.correctAnswer: Cache for decoded uops
questions.explanation: ~80% of hot loop instructions decode from uop cache at ~4/cycle.

questions.question: Register renaming?
options: Maps architectural regs to larger pool of physical regs
questions.correctAnswer: Maps architectural regs to larger pool of physical regs
questions.explanation: Eliminates WAW and WAR hazards. Different 'rax' uses may map to different physical regs.

questions.question: Out-of-order execution?
questions.correctAnswer: Instructions dispatched when operands ready
questions.explanation: If instruction 5 waits for RAM but 7 is ready, 7 executes first.

questions.question: IPC measures what?
questions.correctAnswer: Instructions Per Clock
questions.explanation: Modern CPUs achieve IPC of 2-4.

questions.question: Why clock speeds plateaued at ~5GHz?
questions.correctAnswer: More voltage -> exponentially more heat
questions.explanation: Since ~2005, gains come from IPC increases (power wall).

questions.question: What is SIMD?
questions.correctAnswer: Single Instruction Multiple Data
questions.explanation: SSE: 4xfloat. AVX: 8xfloat. AVX-512: 16xfloat.

questions.question: AVX register width?
questions.explanation: SSE: XMM 128-bit. AVX: YMM 256-bit. AVX-512: ZMM 512-bit.

questions.question: CPU thermal throttling?
options: Reduces clock speed, voltage, then shutdown
questions.correctAnswer: Reduces clock speed, voltage, then shutdown
questions.explanation: 4GHz drops to 3GHz/2GHz. If still overheating, cuts power.

questions.question: Why sort data before processing?
questions.correctAnswer: Sorted -> branch predictable -> <1% mispredict
questions.explanation: Random: 50% mispredict. Each costs 10-20 cycles.

questions.question: ROB size on modern CPU?
questions.correctAnswer: 320-512 entries
questions.explanation: Zen4: 320. Raptor Lake: 512.

questions.question: L1 instruction cache?
questions.correctAnswer: Caches frequent instructions
questions.explanation: Fetch stage reads from L1i. Typically 32KB per core.

questions.question: Typical IPC for modern CPU?
questions.explanation: Through pipelining, superscalar, and OoO execution.

questions.question: What is the CPU's brain for computations?
questions.correctAnswer: ALU
questions.explanation: The ALU performs arithmetic and logic operations.

questions.question: 1 cycle at 4GHz:
questions.explanation: 1/4GHz

questions.question: What is the typical size of an L1 data cache on a modern CPU core?
questions.explanation: L1 data cache is typically 32KB (Zen 4) or 48KB (Raptor Lake). It's the smallest and fastest cache, with ~4-5 cycle latency.

questions.question: How many bytes are in an x86 cache line?
questions.explanation: x86 CPUs use 64-byte cache lines. When any byte in a cache line is accessed, the entire 64-byte line is loaded from the next cache level. This is why sequential access is fast (spatial locality).

questions.question: Why does false sharing hurt performance?
options: It increases memory usage in most implementations
Threads on different cores invalidate each other's cache lines
It causes the CPU to use incorrect data with additional processing
It disables the cache entirely across all supported platforms
questions.correctAnswer: Threads on different cores invalidate each other's cache lines
questions.explanation: False sharing: two threads modify different variables that happen to share a cache line. Each write invalidates the other core's cache line, causing constant cache misses despite no actual data sharing.

questions.question: What is the approximate latency difference between L1 cache and main memory access?
questions.explanation: L1 cache: ~1ns (4 cycles at 4GHz). DDR5 DRAM: ~80-100ns. That's roughly 100x difference. In CPU cycles: L1 = 4 cycles, DRAM = ~200-400 cycles.

questions.question: What does the MESI protocol ensure?
questions.correctAnswer: Cache coherence
questions.explanation: MESI (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid) is a cache coherence protocol that ensures all CPU cores see a consistent view of memory. It tracks the state of each cache line across all caches.

questions.question: What is the difference between write-through and write-back cache policies?
options: Write-through writes to main memory immediately; write-back only writes when the cache line is evicted
Write-through writes all data in a single pass; write-back writes one byte at a time
Write-through uses L1; write-back uses L2
questions.correctAnswer: Write-through writes to main memory immediately; write-back only writes when the cache line is evicted
questions.explanation: Write-through: every store updates cache AND main memory — simple but high traffic. Write-back: stores only update cache, marking line 'dirty.' Main memory updated only on eviction, reducing traffic dramatically.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the dirty bit in a write-back cache?
questions.correctAnswer: Marks cache lines modified but not yet written to main memory — on eviction, dirty lines must be written back
acceptableAnswers: tracks modified cache lines needing writeback
flags lines changed in cache but not in memory
questions.explanation: When CPU writes to a cached address in write-back mode, the line is updated and dirty bit set. On eviction: if dirty, write to memory; if clean, discard. Avoids unnecessary writes for read-only data.

questions.question: What does write-allocate mean?
options: Cache allocates a new line for every write
On a write miss, the cache loads the line from memory first, then performs the write — pairs naturally with write-back
On a write miss, data is written directly to memory without caching
Cache pre-allocates space for future writes
questions.correctAnswer: On a write miss, the cache loads the line from memory first, then performs the write — pairs naturally with write-back
questions.explanation: Write-allocate: fetch line on write miss, then modify in cache. Pairs with write-back — subsequent writes hit in cache. No-write-allocate: write directly to memory, don't cache. Pairs with write-through.

questions.question: What problem do write-back caches create for multi-core consistency?
questions.correctAnswer: A dirty line in Core A's L1 contains data not yet in main memory — if Core B reads the same address, it gets stale data
acceptableAnswers: dirty lines in one core's cache are invisible to other cores
questions.explanation: Modified data sits in a core's private cache, invisible to others. Cache coherence protocols (MESI) solve this: cores snoop bus traffic and invalidate/update copies. Without coherence, multi-threaded programs see inconsistent memory.

questions.question: Typical L1 data cache size?
questions.explanation: L1d: ~32KB (Zen4) or 48KB (Raptor Lake). 4-5 cycle latency.

questions.question: x86 cache line size?
questions.correctAnswer: 64 bytes
questions.explanation: When any byte accessed, entire 64-byte line is loaded.

questions.question: Why does false sharing hurt?
options: Different cores invalidate each other's cache lines
questions.correctAnswer: Different cores invalidate each other's cache lines
questions.explanation: Two threads modify different variables on same cache line.

questions.question: L1 vs main memory latency difference?
questions.explanation: L1 ~1ns (4 cycles). DRAM ~80-100ns.

questions.question: What does MESI ensure?
questions.correctAnswer: Cache coherence
questions.explanation: MESI keeps all cores' cached views of memory consistent.

questions.question: Write-through vs write-back?
options: W-T writes memory immediately; W-B writes only on eviction
W-T uses L1, W-B uses L2
questions.correctAnswer: W-T writes memory immediately; W-B writes only on eviction
questions.explanation: Write-back stores only to cache, marking dirty. Memory updated on eviction.

questions.question: Dirty bit purpose in write-back cache?
questions.correctAnswer: Marks cache lines modified but not yet written to memory. On eviction, dirty lines must be written back.
acceptableAnswers: tracks modified cache lines needing writeback
questions.explanation: On eviction: dirty -> write to memory. Clean -> discard.

questions.question: Write-allocate means?
options: On write miss, loads line first then writes
questions.correctAnswer: On write miss, loads line first then writes
questions.explanation: Pairs with write-back: subsequent writes hit in cache.

questions.question: What problem do write-back caches create for multi-core?
questions.correctAnswer: Dirty line in one core's L1 contains data not in memory. Other core reading gets stale data.
acceptableAnswers: dirty lines in one cache are invisible to other cores
questions.explanation: Modified data sits in private cache. MESI solves through snooping.

questions.question: Why is RAM slow relative to CPU?
questions.correctAnswer: RAM 5-10cm away; 50-100ns round trip = 200-400 cycles
questions.explanation: CPU executes hundreds of instructions while waiting for one RAM access.

questions.question: Fastest to slowest hierarchy?
options: Reg > L1 > L2 > L3 > RAM > SSD
questions.correctAnswer: Reg > L1 > L2 > L3 > RAM > SSD

questions.question: Typical L2 cache size per core?
questions.correctAnswer: 256KB-2MB
questions.explanation: L2 private per core. Latency ~12-14 cycles.

questions.question: Typical L3 cache size?
questions.correctAnswer: 8-32MB shared
questions.explanation: L3 shared across cores. Zen4: 32MB per CCD. Latency ~70-80c.

questions.question: Why are cache lines 64 bytes?
options: DRAM row activation expensive; reading additional columns nearly free
questions.correctAnswer: DRAM row activation expensive; reading additional columns nearly free
questions.explanation: Once DRAM row activated, 8x64-bit words costs nearly same as 1 byte.

questions.question: Spatial locality?
options: Nearby addresses loaded in same cache line
questions.correctAnswer: Nearby addresses loaded in same cache line
questions.explanation: Access array[i]; array[i+1] likely already in cache line.

questions.question: Temporal locality?
options: Same memory repeatedly accessed, stays in cache
questions.correctAnswer: Same memory repeatedly accessed, stays in cache
questions.explanation: Data accessed once, likely accessed again soon.

questions.question: Set-associative cache?
options: Address maps to set, can go in any N ways
questions.correctAnswer: Address maps to set, can go in any N ways
questions.explanation: 8-way L1d: each address maps to one set of 8 slots.

questions.question: MESI 'Modified' state?
questions.correctAnswer: This core wrote to line, RAM is stale, no other core has copy
questions.explanation: Must write back to memory on eviction.

questions.question: MESI 'Exclusive' state?
questions.correctAnswer: This core has line, RAM matches, no other copy
acceptableAnswers: cache matches RAM, only this core
questions.explanation: Can be written to without notifying other cores.

questions.question: Fix for false sharing?
questions.correctAnswer: alignas(64) to separate cache lines
questions.explanation: struct alignas(64) Counters forces per-thread data on different cache lines.

questions.question: What defeats HW prefetchers?
options: Random access (linked lists, hash tables, trees)
questions.correctAnswer: Random access (linked lists, hash tables, trees)
questions.explanation: Prefetcher only tracks forward and regular strides.

questions.question: AoS vs SoA?
questions.correctAnswer: AoS cache-hostile, SoA cache-optimized
questions.explanation: SoA: positions, healths in separate arrays. Every byte loaded is used. 3-5x faster.

questions.question: Ints per 64-byte cache line?
questions.explanation: 64/4 = 16 ints. Sequential access: 1/16 = 6.25% miss rate.

questions.question: Standard cache write policy?
questions.correctAnswer: Write-back + write-allocate
questions.explanation: All modern CPU caches use write-back + write-allocate.

questions.question: Level shared across all cores?
questions.correctAnswer: L3
questions.explanation: L1 and L2 private per core. L3 shared across all cores on die.

options: DRAM can't keep up with CPU

questions.question: What does `malloc(size)` return when allocation fails?
questions.correctAnswer: NULL
questions.explanation: malloc returns NULL on failure. Always check the return value. In C++, `new` throws std::bad_alloc (unless `nothrow` is used). UE's `FMemory::Malloc` returns nullptr on failure.

questions.question: What problem does the buddy allocator solve?
questions.correctAnswer: External fragmentation
questions.explanation: The buddy allocator divides memory into power-of-two blocks. When a block is freed, it's merged with its 'buddy' (adjacent equal-sized block), reducing external fragmentation. Used in Linux kernel page allocation.

questions.question: What is internal fragmentation?
questions.correctAnswer: Wasted space inside an allocated block
acceptableAnswers: Wasted space inside an allocated block
questions.explanation: Internal fragmentation: allocated block is larger than requested. Example: requesting 10 bytes, allocator gives 16 bytes (minimum block size). The 6 extra bytes are internal fragmentation.

questions.question: What does `sbrk()` do on Linux?
options: Allocates a specific amount of memory with additional processing
Moves the program break to expand/shrink the heap
Frees allocated memory in most implementations
Locks a memory region via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: Moves the program break to expand/shrink the heap
questions.explanation: sbrk(increment) adjusts the program's data segment break. Positive increment → expand heap. Negative → shrink. Modern allocators prefer mmap over sbrk for large allocations.

questions.question: Why is `mmap` preferred over `sbrk` for large allocations?
options: mmap is faster across all supported platforms with additional processing
mmap allows returning memory to the OS immediately
mmap uses less memory by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: mmap allows returning memory to the OS immediately
questions.explanation: sbrk can only grow/shrink at the break boundary. If allocated blocks exist beyond the freed region, sbrk can't shrink. mmap maps independent pages that can be munmap'd immediately regardless of order.

questions.question: What does malloc(size) return on failure?
questions.correctAnswer: NULL
questions.explanation: malloc returns NULL on failure. new throws std::bad_alloc.

questions.question: What does sbrk() do on Linux?
options: Moves program break to expand/shrink heap
questions.correctAnswer: Moves program break to expand/shrink heap
questions.explanation: sbrk(+) expands heap. sbrk(-) shrinks. Modern allocators prefer mmap.

questions.question: Why is mmap preferred over sbrk for large allocations?
options: mmap returns memory to OS immediately
questions.correctAnswer: mmap returns memory to OS immediately
questions.explanation: sbrk can only shrink at break boundary. mmap pages can be munmapped any time.

questions.question: What is malloc's minimum allocation size typically?
questions.correctAnswer: 8-16 bytes (minimum block header)
questions.explanation: malloc must store metadata (chunk size, flags) in each block. Minimum includes header overhead.

questions.question: What is the 'brk' address vs 'mmap' threshold in glibc malloc?
questions.correctAnswer: 1MB (MMAP_THRESHOLD)
questions.explanation: Allocations <128KB come from brk heap. >=128KB use mmap (configurable via mallopt).

questions.question: What is the glibc malloc arena?
options: A pool of memory per thread reducing lock contention
questions.correctAnswer: A pool of memory per thread reducing lock contention
questions.explanation: Each arena manages its own heap. Threads pick arena to reduce contention. Default: 2x CPU cores arenas.

questions.question: What are malloc bins?
options: Free lists sorted by chunk size
questions.correctAnswer: Free lists sorted by chunk size
questions.explanation: Fast bins (small), unsorted bin, small bins, large bins. Each holds freed chunks of similar size.

questions.question: What causes external fragmentation?
options: Allocating/freeing varying sizes leaves non-contiguous free gaps
questions.correctAnswer: Allocating/freeing varying sizes leaves non-contiguous free gaps
questions.explanation: Free blocks exist but not contiguous enough for a large allocation request.

questions.question: What is 'malloc_trim' used for?
options: Returning free heap memory to the OS
questions.correctAnswer: Returning free heap memory to the OS
questions.explanation: Normally, free() marks blocks as free but doesn't return pages to OS. trim() releases them.

questions.question: What memory allocator is commonly used in game engines?
options: Custom allocators like UE's FMemory, jemalloc, tcmalloc
Game engines use custom allocators for predictable performance, fragmentation control, and cache behavior.
questions.correctAnswer: Custom allocators like UE's FMemory, jemalloc, tcmalloc
questions.explanation: Game engines use custom allocators for predictable performance.

questions.question: What is a slab allocator?
options: Allocates fixed-size objects from pre-allocated pages
questions.correctAnswer: Allocates fixed-size objects from pre-allocated pages
questions.explanation: Used in kernel (Linux SLUB) and object pools. Each slab holds same-size objects. No fragmentation.

questions.question: What is a stack allocator (arena/region)?
options: Allocates linearly, resets in bulk — perfect for per-frame transient data
questions.correctAnswer: Allocates linearly, resets in bulk — perfect for per-frame transient data
questions.explanation: Allocate: bump pointer. Free: reset pointer to frame start. Fastest allocation pattern.

questions.question: What is std::pmr in C++17?
options: Polymorphic Memory Resource — standardized custom allocator interface
questions.correctAnswer: Polymorphic Memory Resource — standardized custom allocator interface
questions.explanation: std::pmr::monotonic_buffer_resource for fast linear allocation. Also pool and synchronized resources.

questions.question: What does MEMORY_RESOURCE_DEFAULT in UE5 do?
options: A cascading allocator: tlsf -> malloc -> platform
questions.correctAnswer: A cascading allocator: tlsf -> malloc -> platform
questions.explanation: FAST (tlsf) for most. Malloc if too large. Platform if still too large.

questions.question: What is the Two-Level Segregated Fit (TLSF) allocator?
options: A general-purpose allocator with deterministic O(1) allocation
questions.correctAnswer: A general-purpose allocator with deterministic O(1) allocation
questions.explanation: TLSF uses two-level free lists divided by size class. Worst-case O(1). Used in UE5.

questions.question: What is a free list allocator?
options: A singly-linked list of free blocks; each allocation walks the list for a fit
questions.correctAnswer: A singly-linked list of free blocks; each allocation walks the list for a fit
questions.explanation: First-fit: returns first block big enough. Best-fit: returns smallest sufficient block.

questions.question: What is 'first-fit' vs 'best-fit' in free list allocation?
options: First-fit faster but more fragmentation; best-fit slower but tighter packing
questions.correctAnswer: First-fit faster but more fragmentation; best-fit slower but tighter packing
questions.explanation: First-fit: O(n), high fragmentation. Best-fit: O(n), lower fragmentation. jemalloc/tcmalloc combine.

questions.question: What is a memory pool?
questions.correctAnswer: Pre-allocated block of memory for fixed-size objects
questions.explanation: No malloc/free per object. O(1) allocate/deallocate. Zero fragmentation. Used in game object pools.

questions.question: malloc uses:
questions.correctAnswer: Free list
questions.explanation: Tracks free memory holes

questions.question: First-fit:
questions.correctAnswer: First large enough
questions.explanation: In address order

questions.question: Best-fit:
questions.correctAnswer: Smallest that fits
questions.explanation: Minimizes waste

questions.question: Buddy system block sizes:
questions.correctAnswer: Powers of 2
questions.explanation: 2^n sizes

questions.correctAnswer: addr XOR size
questions.explanation: XOR gives O(1) buddy

questions.question: Buddy alloc time:

questions.explanation: ~18 levels max

questions.question: Slab allocator:
questions.correctAnswer: Fixed-size objects
questions.explanation: Page divided into equal objects

questions.question: tcache latency:

questions.explanation: Pop from linked list

questions.question: Coalescing:
questions.correctAnswer: Adjacent free blocks
questions.explanation: Merge adjacent to prevent fragmentation

questions.question: External fragmentation:
options: Free memory in unusable small pieces
questions.correctAnswer: Free memory in unusable small pieces
questions.explanation: Holes between allocations

questions.explanation: 65B→128B rounds up

questions.explanation: Both for O(1) coalescing

questions.explanation: Zero fragmentation

questions.question: Stack allocator freed by:

questions.explanation: Frame alloc: one pointer move

questions.explanation: Kernel extension

questions.explanation: Same size = no external frag

questions.question: Embedded free list pointers:

questions.explanation: Free objects are list nodes

questions.question: mimalloc known for:
questions.correctAnswer: Lowest latency, lock-free
questions.explanation: Microsoft's mimalloc

questions.question: Buddy coalescing proceeds:
questions.correctAnswer: From small to large
questions.explanation: Merge buddies into larger blocks

questions.question: What is the size of a standard page on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: 4KB
questions.explanation: The standard page size on x86-64 is 4KB (4096 bytes). Huge pages: 2MB (PSE) and 1GB (PDPE). The page size balances TLB coverage vs internal fragmentation.

questions.question: How many levels are in the x86-64 page table hierarchy?
questions.explanation: x86-64 uses 4-level paging: PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT (9 bits per level). Each level has 512 entries. The virtual address is split: [16 unused | 9 PML4 | 9 PDPT | 9 PD | 9 PT | 12 offset].

questions.question: What happens during a TLB miss?
options: The program crashes for typical use cases for common
The CPU performs a page walk to find the translation
The OS swaps the page to disk
The CPU retries the instruction with the default setu
questions.correctAnswer: The CPU performs a page walk to find the translation
questions.explanation: On TLB miss, the MMU walks the page table hierarchy (up to 4 memory accesses for 4-level paging) to find the PTE. The translation is then cached in the TLB for future use.

questions.question: What does the 'dirty' bit in a PTE indicate?
options: The page has been written to
The page is corrupted with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: The page has been written to
questions.explanation: The dirty bit indicates the page has been modified since it was loaded. When evicting a page, if dirty=1, the page must be written back to disk. If dirty=0, the page can simply be discarded.

questions.question: What is the purpose of KPTI (Kernel Page Table Isolation)?
questions.correctAnswer: Mitigate the Meltdown vulnerability
questions.explanation: KPTI unmaps kernel pages from userspace page tables to prevent Meltdown attacks. The kernel has its own separate page table. Every syscall must switch page tables, adding 100-500+ cycles overhead.

questions.question: What does `mmap` with MAP_ANONYMOUS do?
options: Maps a file into memory using standard engine approaches
Allocates anonymous memory not backed by a file
Locks memory in RAM across all supported platforms
questions.correctAnswer: Allocates anonymous memory not backed by a file
questions.explanation: MAP_ANONYMOUS creates a mapping not backed by any file. It's how malloc typically allocates large blocks: mmap(NULL, size, PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE, MAP_PRIVATE|MAP_ANONYMOUS, -1, 0).

questions.question: Standard page size on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: 4KB
questions.explanation: Standard page is 4KB. Huge pages: 2MB and 1GB.

questions.question: How many levels in x86-64 page table?
questions.explanation: 4-level paging: PML4 -> PDPT -> PD -> PT. 9 bits per level.

questions.question: What happens on a TLB miss?
questions.correctAnswer: CPU walks page table hierarchy
questions.explanation: MMU walks up to 4 levels for 4-level paging. Translation cached in TLB.

questions.question: What does the dirty bit in a PTE indicate?
questions.correctAnswer: Page has been written to
questions.explanation: Dirty=1 means page modified. On eviction, dirty pages must be written back to disk.

questions.question: What is KPTI?
questions.correctAnswer: Mitigate Meltdown vulnerability by separating kernel/user page tables
questions.explanation: KPTI unmaps kernel pages from userspace page tables.

questions.question: What does mmap with MAP_ANONYMOUS do?
options: Allocates anonymous memory not backed by a file
questions.correctAnswer: Allocates anonymous memory not backed by a file
questions.explanation: MAP_ANONYMOUS creates mapping not backed by file. How malloc allocates large blocks.

questions.question: What does the Present bit in a PTE indicate?
options: Page is loaded in physical RAM
questions.correctAnswer: Page is loaded in physical RAM
questions.explanation: Present=0 triggers page fault. OS loads page from disk then sets Present=1.

questions.question: What is a page fault?
options: Access to a page not in physical memory
questions.correctAnswer: Access to a page not in physical memory
questions.explanation: Page fault -> OS loads page from swap and retries instruction. Users don't notice.

questions.question: What is demand paging?
options: Pages loaded only when accessed, not at allocation time
questions.correctAnswer: Pages loaded only when accessed, not at allocation time
questions.explanation: malloc reserves VA space but doesn't allocate physical pages until first access.

questions.question: What does the Access bit in a PTE track?
options: Page has been accessed (read or write)
questions.correctAnswer: Page has been accessed (read or write)
questions.explanation: Set by MMU on first access. Used by OS for page replacement (NRU algorithm).

questions.question: What is swapping/paging?
options: Moving pages between RAM and disk to virtualize memory
questions.correctAnswer: Moving pages between RAM and disk to virtualize memory
questions.explanation: When RAM is full, OS writes pages to swap file and reclaims physical memory.

questions.question: What is the PTE bit that makes Execute Never (NX) work?
options: The NX bit — marks page as data (not executable), blocking shellcode injection
questions.correctAnswer: The NX bit — marks page as data (not executable), blocking shellcode injection
questions.explanation: NX bit (bit 63) prevents code execution in data pages. Standard on all modern OS.

questions.question: What is ASLR?
options: Address Space Layout Randomization — randomizes memory layout to prevent exploits
questions.correctAnswer: Address Space Layout Randomization — randomizes memory layout to prevent exploits
questions.explanation: Randomizes base addresses of stack, heap, libraries. Makes buffer overflow harder.

questions.question: What does vmstat tell you about virtual memory?
options: Swap in/out rates, memory usage, page faults
questions.correctAnswer: Swap in/out rates, memory usage, page faults
questions.explanation: si (swap in) and so (swap out) > 0 means system is running out of RAM.

questions.question: What is hugepages advantage?
options: Reduce TLB misses — map large regions with fewer entries
questions.correctAnswer: Reduce TLB misses — map large regions with fewer entries
questions.explanation: 2MB page covers 512x more memory per TLB entry. Critical for games and databases.

questions.question: What is copy-on-write (CoW)?
options: Pages are shared until one writes, then copied
Pages are always copied on read
questions.correctAnswer: Pages are shared until one writes, then copied
questions.explanation: Used by fork(): parent/child share pages until modification. PTE marked read-only.

questions.question: What TLB size does a typical modern x86 CPU have?
options: 64-100 entries for L1 TLB + 1000+ for L2 TLB

questions.explanation: Zen4: L1 dTLB 64 entries, L1 iTLB 64. L2 unified TLB ~3072 entries.

questions.question: What is a TLB?
questions.correctAnswer: Translation Lookaside Buffer — cache of recent page table lookups
questions.explanation: The TLB caches virtual-to-physical address translations to avoid page walks.

questions.question: What happens when you access a swapped-out page?

questions.question: What does RAID 0 provide?
questions.correctAnswer: Striping for performance
questions.explanation: RAID 0 (striping) splits data across multiple disks for parallel read/write, improving throughput. No redundancy — a single disk failure loses all data. Capacity = N × disk_size.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of disks required for RAID 5?
questions.explanation: RAID 5 requires at least 3 disks: data distributed across N-1 disks plus one disk's worth of parity. It can survive one disk failure. Capacity = (N-1) × disk_size.

questions.question: What is the 'write hole' problem in RAID 5?
options: A physical defect in the disk platter in most implementations
Data and parity becoming inconsistent after a crash during a write
A security vulnerability as defined in the specification
A performance bottleneck for typical use cases with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: Data and parity becoming inconsistent after a crash during a write
questions.explanation: RAID 5 write requires updating data AND parity on separate disks. If a crash occurs between these two writes, data and parity are inconsistent. ZFS and some hardware RAID controllers use copy-on-write to avoid this.

questions.question: Which RAID level provides mirroring?
questions.correctAnswer: RAID 1
questions.explanation: RAID 1 mirrors all data across two or more disks. Every write goes to all mirrors. Capacity = disk_size (for 2-disk mirror). Can survive N-1 disk failures. Simplest redundancy.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of disks for RAID 6?
options: 2 through the built-in API with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
4 — need at least 2 data disks + 2 parity disks for dual-parity protection
1 under normal conditions with additional processing with typical engine sett
questions.correctAnswer: 4 — need at least 2 data disks + 2 parity disks for dual-parity protection
questions.explanation: RAID 6 uses dual parity (P+Q). Can survive 2 simultaneous disk failures. Minimum 4 disks. Capacity = (N-2) × disk_size. Write penalty: must update both parity blocks.

questions.question: What is the RAID write hole?
options: A physical defect as defined in the specification
When a crash occurs between data write and parity update — inconsistent state after reboot. ZFS and some hardware RAID use copy-on-write to avoid this.
A performance bug using standard engine approaches
A security vulnerability for typical use cases with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: When a crash occurs between data write and parity update — inconsistent state after reboot. ZFS and some hardware RAID use copy-on-write to avoid this.
questions.explanation: RAID 5 write = write data to one disk + update parity on another. If power fails between these, data and parity are inconsistent. Battery-backed cache or CoW prevents this.

questions.question: Minimum disks for RAID 5?
questions.explanation: RAID 5 requires at least 3 disks: N-1 data + 1 parity.

questions.question: What is the RAID 5 write hole?
options: Data/parity inconsistency after crash during write
questions.correctAnswer: Data/parity inconsistency after crash during write
questions.explanation: RAID 5 write updates data AND parity on separate disks. Crash between them = inconsistency.

questions.question: Minimum disks for RAID 6?
questions.explanation: RAID 6 uses dual parity. Min 4 disks. Capacity = (N-2) x disk_size.

questions.question: What is RAID 10 (1+0)?
options: Mirrored stripes — combine mirroring and striping
questions.correctAnswer: Mirrored stripes — combine mirroring and striping
questions.explanation: RAID 10: stripe across mirrored pairs. Good performance + redundancy. Min 4 disks.

questions.question: What is RAID 5 capacity with 4x1TB disks?
questions.correctAnswer: 3TB
questions.explanation: RAID 5: (N-1) x disk = 3 x 1TB = 3TB. One disk worth of parity.

questions.question: How does hardware RAID differ from software RAID?
options: HW RAID has dedicated controller with cache/battery backup; SW RAID uses CPU
questions.correctAnswer: HW RAID has dedicated controller with cache/battery backup; SW RAID uses CPU
questions.explanation: HW RAID offloads parity computation, has battery-backed cache for safe writes.

questions.question: What is ZFS's answer to the RAID 5 write hole?
options: Copy-on-write (CoW) — never overwrite in place, always write new block
questions.correctAnswer: Copy-on-write (CoW) — never overwrite in place, always write new block
questions.explanation: ZFS writes new data + new parity to free space. Old block remains until transaction committed.

questions.question: What is the write penalty for RAID 5?
options: 2x (1 data + 1 parity read, 1 data + 1 parity write = 4 I/Os for 1 data write)
questions.correctAnswer: 2x (1 data + 1 parity read, 1 data + 1 parity write = 4 I/Os for 1 data write)
questions.explanation: RAID 5 write: read old data, read old parity, compute new parity, write data, write parity.

questions.question: What is the write penalty for RAID 10?
questions.correctAnswer: 2x (write to both mirrors)
questions.explanation: RAID 10: each write goes to both mirrors. No parity computation needed.

questions.question: What does RAID 0 capacity equal?
questions.correctAnswer: Total of all disks
questions.explanation: RAID 0 has no redundancy. All disk space is usable.

questions.question: What is a hot spare in RAID?
options: A spare disk that automatically replaces a failed disk in the array
questions.correctAnswer: A spare disk that automatically replaces a failed disk in the array
questions.explanation: Hot spare sits idle until a disk fails, then auto-rebuilds the array.

questions.question: What is RAID rebuild and why is it risky?
options: Reconstructing data on a replacement disk — high stress on remaining disks may cause second failure
questions.correctAnswer: Reconstructing data on a replacement disk — high stress on remaining disks may cause second failure
questions.explanation: During rebuild, all remaining disks are read continuously. Another failure = data loss.

questions.question: What does non-RAID (JBOD) mean?
options: Just a Bunch Of Disks — each disk independent, no redundancy or striping
questions.correctAnswer: Just a Bunch Of Disks — each disk independent, no redundancy or striping
questions.explanation: JBOD presents each disk separately. No array benefits.

questions.question: RAID combines disks for:
questions.correctAnswer: Speed and/or reliability
questions.explanation: Tradeoff of 3 properties

questions.correctAnswer: Striping only, no redundancy
questions.explanation: Data split across disks

questions.question: RAID 0 failure:

questions.explanation: No redundancy
