questions.explanation: Identical copies

questions.question: RAID 5 capacity:
questions.correctAnswer: (N-1)×disk
questions.explanation: 1 parity disk equivalent

questions.question: RAID 5 write penalty due to:
questions.correctAnswer: Read-modify-write cycle
questions.explanation: Must read old data+parity

questions.question: RAID 5 write hole:
questions.correctAnswer: Inconsistent data+parity after crash
questions.explanation: Fixed by NVRAM or COW

questions.question: RAID 6 survives:
questions.correctAnswer: 2 failures
questions.explanation: Dual parity (P+Q)

questions.question: RAID 10:
questions.correctAnswer: Mirrored stripes
questions.explanation: RAID 0 over RAID 1 pairs

questions.question: RAID 10 vs RAID 5:
questions.correctAnswer: RAID 10 faster writes
questions.explanation: No read-modify-write

questions.question: Hardware RAID vs Software RAID:
questions.correctAnswer: All of the above
questions.explanation: Tradeoffs between them

questions.question: mdadm reshape:
questions.correctAnswer: Add disk to live array
questions.explanation: Online expansion

questions.question: ZFS RAID-Z advantage:
questions.correctAnswer: Variable stripe width, COW, checksums
questions.explanation: Software RAID revolution

questions.question: RAID is NOT:
questions.correctAnswer: A backup
questions.explanation: RAID ≠ backup

questions.question: Stripe size tuning:
questions.correctAnswer: All of the above
questions.explanation: Tradeoff based on workload

questions.question: Parity uses which operation?
questions.correctAnswer: XOR
questions.explanation: XOR recovers lost data

questions.question: RAID 4 vs RAID 5:
options: RAID 4 has dedicated parity disk bottleneck
questions.correctAnswer: All of the above
questions.explanation: RAID 5 fixed the bottleneck

questions.question: Chunk size impacts:
questions.correctAnswer: Both speed and write amp
questions.explanation: Affects parallelism

questions.question: RAID rebuild copies:
questions.correctAnswer: Every block (even free space)
questions.explanation: Traditional RAID reads all blocks

questions.question: RAID-Z rebuild reads:
questions.correctAnswer: Only allocated blocks
questions.explanation: It's a filesystem, knows allocations

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of DMA over programmed I/O?
options: Higher security within the current scope
Frees the CPU during data transfers
Lower latency using standard engine approaches
questions.correctAnswer: Frees the CPU during data transfers
questions.explanation: DMA allows peripherals to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU involvement per byte. CPU sets up the transfer and goes back to useful work. The DMA controller handles the actual data movement.

questions.question: What does the DMA controller need from the CPU before starting a transfer?
options: The data to transfer as defined in the specification
Source address, destination address, and transfer size
A list of free memory pages within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: Source address, destination address, and transfer size
questions.explanation: CPU programs the DMA controller with: source address, destination address, transfer count, and direction (read/write). After setup, the DMA controller operates independently.

questions.question: What is bus mastering?
questions.correctAnswer: A device taking control of the memory bus to perform transfers independently
acceptableAnswers: A device controlling the bus for DMA
When a device takes control of the bus
device controls the bus for direct memory access
questions.explanation: Bus mastering: a peripheral device becomes the bus master, driving address and control lines to read/write memory directly. The CPU is temporarily disconnected from the bus during the transfer.

questions.question: What happens when DMA and CPU try to access memory simultaneously?
options: The system crashes across all supported platforms
Cycle stealing — DMA uses cycles the CPU doesn't need
They share the bus equally as defined in the specification
questions.correctAnswer: Cycle stealing — DMA uses cycles the CPU doesn't need
questions.explanation: DMA uses 'cycle stealing': it transfers one word at a time during cycles when the CPU doesn't need the memory bus. The CPU is only stalled if both need memory simultaneously.

questions.question: What does an IOMMU do?
options: Encrypts DMA transfers under normal conditions with additional processing
Translates device virtual addresses to physical addresses
Speeds up DMA transfers for typical use cases
Compresses data during transfer using standard engine approaches
questions.correctAnswer: Translates device virtual addresses to physical addresses
questions.explanation: IOMMU (I/O Memory Management Unit) provides address translation for DMA, similar to the MMU for CPU. Enables: device isolation (VM passthrough), scatter-gather, and protection against malicious DMA.

questions.question: Primary benefit of DMA over PIO?
questions.correctAnswer: Frees CPU during data transfers
questions.explanation: DMA transfers data directly without CPU involvement per byte.

questions.question: What does DMA controller need from CPU?
options: Source address, dest address, transfer size
questions.correctAnswer: Source address, dest address, transfer size
questions.explanation: CPU programs DMA with source, destination, count. Then DMA runs independently.

questions.question: DMA and CPU simultaneous memory access?
options: Cycle stealing — DMA uses cycles CPU doesn't need
questions.correctAnswer: Cycle stealing — DMA uses cycles CPU doesn't need
questions.explanation: DMA transfers one word at a time when CPU doesn't need the bus.

questions.question: What is scatter-gather DMA?
options: DMA from fragmented memory regions into contiguous device buffer using a descriptor list
questions.correctAnswer: DMA from fragmented memory regions into contiguous device buffer using a descriptor list
questions.explanation: Instead of one contiguous buffer, DMA reads a list of {addr, len} pairs.

questions.question: How does programmed I/O (PIO) differ from DMA?
options: CPU reads/writes each register/data word directly — slow but simple
questions.correctAnswer: CPU reads/writes each register/data word directly — slow but simple
questions.explanation: PIO: CPU loops loading bytes from device register to memory. Wastes CPU.

questions.question: What is a DMA descriptor ring?
options: Circular buffer of transfer descriptors in memory — NIC/disk controller reads them
questions.correctAnswer: Circular buffer of transfer descriptors in memory — NIC/disk controller reads them
questions.explanation: CPU writes commands to ring. Device processes them via DMA. Efficient batch processing.

questions.question: What is the bottleneck for PCIe DMA bandwidth?
questions.correctAnswer: PCIe lane count and generation
questions.explanation: PCIe 4.0 x16 = ~32 GB/s. Each generation doubles bandwidth. Lane count: x1, x4, x8, x16.

questions.question: What is SWIOTLB (software bounce buffer)?
options: A fixed pool of contiguous physical memory for DMA when hardware can't address all RAM
questions.correctAnswer: A fixed pool of contiguous physical memory for DMA when hardware can't address all RAM
questions.explanation: Old 32-bit devices can't address >4GB. Kernel copies to bounce buffer first, then DMA.

questions.question: What does descriptor-based DMA avoid?
options: CPU copying data between user and kernel buffers
questions.correctAnswer: CPU copying data between user and kernel buffers
questions.explanation: Descriptors point directly to user-space buffers (with IOMMU translation). Zero-copy.

questions.question: DMA solves:
questions.correctAnswer: CPU copying every byte
questions.explanation: Without DMA CPU copies every byte

questions.question: DMA without:
questions.correctAnswer: CPU involved per byte
questions.explanation: CPU just sets up, DMA transfers

questions.question: Bus mastering means:
questions.correctAnswer: Device has own DMA engine
questions.explanation: PCIe device initiates transfers

questions.question: IOMMU provides:
questions.correctAnswer: DMA address translation and isolation
questions.explanation: Like MMU for devices

questions.question: Scatter-gather DMA:
questions.correctAnswer: Non-contiguous pages list
questions.explanation: SGL handles non-contiguous phys mem

questions.question: DirectStorage:
questions.correctAnswer: SSD→GPU via DMA, CPU bypassed
questions.explanation: GPU reads directly from NVMe

questions.question: DMA attack prevention:
options: IOMMU restricts device to mapped regions
questions.correctAnswer: IOMMU restricts device to mapped regions
questions.explanation: Device isolation

questions.question: Audio DMA uses:
questions.correctAnswer: Circular buffer
questions.explanation: Ring buffer for streaming

questions.question: Cache coherency for DMA:
questions.correctAnswer: DMA can snoop CPU caches
questions.explanation: Or requires explicit clflush

questions.question: DMA transfer starts with:
questions.correctAnswer: CPU programs DMA descriptor
questions.explanation: CPU sets source, dest, count

questions.question: What is the key difference between TCP and UDP?
options: TCP is faster through the built-in API with regular usag
TCP is connection-oriented with guaranteed delivery
TCP uses less memory by default behavior in most practi
questions.correctAnswer: TCP is connection-oriented with guaranteed delivery
questions.explanation: TCP: connection-oriented, guaranteed in-order delivery, flow control, congestion control. UDP: connectionless, no delivery guarantees, no ordering. TCP for reliability (web, file transfer), UDP for speed (games, VoIP).

questions.question: What is the three-way handshake in TCP?
questions.correctAnswer: SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK
questions.explanation: TCP connection setup: Client sends SYN with random sequence number. Server responds with SYN-ACK (acknowledging client's SYN, sending its own SYN). Client sends ACK. Connection established. Adds 1 RTT latency.

questions.question: What port range is available for user applications?
questions.explanation: Ports 0-1023: well-known (HTTP:80, SSH:22). 1024-49151: registered ports (can be used by applications). 49152-65535: dynamic/private ports (ephemeral, used for client connections).

questions.question: What does the TCP Nagle algorithm do?
options: Delays small packets to combine them
Encrypts the TCP stream in most practical
Increases the window size within the stand
questions.correctAnswer: Delays small packets to combine them
questions.explanation: Nagle's algorithm delays sending small segments until previous data is acknowledged OR enough data accumulates to fill a segment. Reduces overhead for many small writes. Can be disabled with TCP_NODELAY for low-latency apps (games).

questions.question: What socket call prepares a server to accept connections?
questions.correctAnswer: listen()
questions.explanation: Sequence: socket() → bind() → listen() → accept(). listen() marks the socket as passive and creates the backlog queue for incoming connections. accept() blocks until a client connects.

questions.question: Key TCP vs UDP difference?
questions.correctAnswer: TCP connection-oriented with guaranteed delivery
questions.explanation: TCP: reliable, in-order, flow/congestion control. UDP: fast, no guarantees.

questions.question: Three-way handshake?
questions.correctAnswer: SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK
questions.explanation: Client SYN. Server SYN-ACK. Client ACK. Adds 1 RTT.

questions.question: User application port range?
questions.explanation: 0-1023: well-known. 1024-49151: registered. 49152-65535: ephemeral.

questions.question: Nagle's algorithm does what?
options: Delays small packets to combine them
questions.correctAnswer: Delays small packets to combine them
questions.explanation: Delays sending small segments until previous data is ACK'd.

questions.question: Server socket call to accept connections?
questions.correctAnswer: listen()
questions.explanation: socket()->bind()->listen()->accept(). listen() creates backlog queue.

questions.question: Why do games prefer UDP over TCP?
options: TCP's reliability causes head-of-line blocking and latency — old data is useless
questions.correctAnswer: TCP's reliability causes head-of-line blocking and latency — old data is useless
questions.explanation: Games need current state, not reliable delivery of stale data.

questions.question: What is head-of-line blocking in TCP?
options: Lost segment blocks all later segments until retransmitted
questions.correctAnswer: Lost segment blocks all later segments until retransmitted
questions.explanation: TCP delivers in-order. Segment 5 lost; 6-10 wait for retransmit.

questions.question: UDP header size?
questions.correctAnswer: 8 bytes
questions.explanation: UDP: source port, dest port, length, checksum. Minimal overhead.

questions.question: How does TCP congestion control work?
options: Sender maintains cwnd — max unacknowledged bytes in flight
questions.correctAnswer: Sender maintains cwnd — max unacknowledged bytes in flight
questions.explanation: Slow start (exponential), congestion avoidance (linear), fast recovery on loss.

questions.question: What is TCP_CORK?
options: Alternative to Nagle — don't send partial segments until explicitly flushed
questions.correctAnswer: Alternative to Nagle — don't send partial segments until explicitly flushed
questions.explanation: Like Nagle but more aggressive. Used with sendfile() for efficient web serving.

questions.question: What does setsockopt SO_REUSEADDR do?
options: Allows binding to port in TIME_WAIT state
questions.correctAnswer: Allows binding to port in TIME_WAIT state
questions.explanation: TIME_WAIT lasts 2xMSL (60-120s). SO_REUSEADDR skips this for server restarts.

questions.question: What is TCP Fast Open (TFO)?
options: Allows data in SYN packet, saving 1 RTT on repeat connections
questions.correctAnswer: Allows data in SYN packet, saving 1 RTT on repeat connections
questions.explanation: Client sends data in SYN. Server processes and responds. Authentication via cookie.

questions.question: What is QUIC?
options: HTTP/3 transport over UDP with multiplexed streams, 0-RTT, built-in TLS
questions.correctAnswer: HTTP/3 transport over UDP with multiplexed streams, 0-RTT, built-in TLS
questions.explanation: QUIC solves TCP's head-of-line blocking by multiplexing independent streams over UDP.

questions.question: How to disable Nagle's algorithm?
questions.correctAnswer: TCP_NODELAY
questions.explanation: setsockopt(fd, IPPROTO_TCP, TCP_NODELAY, &one, sizeof(one)). Sends every byte immediately.

questions.question: TCP guarantees:
questions.correctAnswer: Reliable ordered stream
questions.explanation: Everything arrives, in order

questions.question: UDP advantages for games:
questions.correctAnswer: All of the above
questions.explanation: Games build own reliability

questions.question: TCP three-way handshake:
questions.correctAnswer: SYN,SYN-ACK,ACK
questions.explanation: Connection establishment

questions.question: Head-of-line blocking:
questions.correctAnswer: Lost packet blocks later packets
questions.explanation: Deadly for games

questions.question: MSS =
questions.correctAnswer: Maximum segment size (MTU - headers)
questions.explanation: ~1460 bytes on Ethernet

questions.question: Nagle's algorithm:
questions.correctAnswer: Batches small sends
questions.explanation: Game engines disable with TCP_NODELAY

questions.question: UDP header size:
questions.correctAnswer: 8 bytes
questions.explanation: Source/dest port + length + checksum

questions.question: TCP header size:
questions.correctAnswer: 20 bytes
questions.explanation: Minimum TCP header

questions.question: HTONS converts:
questions.correctAnswer: Host to network short (16-bit)
questions.explanation: For port numbers

questions.question: Berkeley sockets: SOCK_STREAM =
questions.correctAnswer: TCP
questions.explanation: SOCK_STREAM = TCP; SOCK_DGRAM = UDP

questions.question: UE networking uses:
questions.correctAnswer: UDP
questions.explanation: UE builds own reliability on UDP

questions.question: NAT breaks:
questions.correctAnswer: Peer-to-peer connections
questions.explanation: Router shares one IP

questions.question: epoll advantage:
questions.correctAnswer: O(1) vs select's O(n)
questions.explanation: Scalability for many sockets

questions.question: Flow control (TCP):
questions.correctAnswer: rwnd advertised by receiver
questions.explanation: Prevents overwhelming receiver

questions.question: Congestion control:
questions.correctAnswer: AIMD: additive inc, multiplicative dec
questions.explanation: TCP Reno/CUBIC

questions.question: What are Coffman's four necessary conditions for deadlock?
questions.correctAnswer: Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
acceptableAnswers: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
mutual exclusion hold and wait no preemption circular wait
questions.explanation: All four must hold for deadlock to occur. Break any one to prevent/recover from deadlock. Lock ordering breaks circular wait; try_lock breaks hold-and-wait.

questions.question: How does consistent lock ordering prevent deadlock?
options: It makes locks faster for typical use cases
If every thread acquires locks in the same global order (always A before B), circular wait is impossible
It uses fewer locks under normal conditions with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
It's a debugging tool for typical use cases in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: If every thread acquires locks in the same global order (always A before B), circular wait is impossible
questions.explanation: Lock ordering eliminates the circular wait condition. Thread 1: lock(A), lock(B). Thread 2: lock(A), lock(B) — same order, no cycle possible.

questions.question: What is the hold-and-wait condition?
options: A thread holds no locks through the built-in API
A thread holds at least one resource while waiting to acquire additional resources
A thread waits without holding under normal conditions in most practical scenariosxxxxxxx
A thread releases all locks before waiting in most implementations in standard config
questions.correctAnswer: A thread holds at least one resource while waiting to acquire additional resources
questions.explanation: Thread holds lock A while waiting for lock B. If Thread B holds lock B while waiting for lock A — deadlock. Break this by acquiring all locks atomically or releasing held locks before waiting.

questions.question: What is the difference between deadlock and livelock?
options: They're the same as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Deadlock: threads are blocked (sleeping). Livelock: threads are actively changing state but making zero net progress — like two people trying to pass in a corridor
Livelock is faster as defined in the specification in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Deadlock is recoverable for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Deadlock: threads are blocked (sleeping). Livelock: threads are actively changing state but making zero net progress — like two people trying to pass in a corridor
questions.explanation: Deadlock = blocked in FUTEX_WAIT, consuming no CPU. Livelock = threads are RUNNABLE, executing CAS loops, but yield to each other endlessly — burning CPU, zero progress.

questions.question: What is starvation in the context of locking?
options: A deadlock variant through the built-in API
A thread is ready to run but is perpetually denied the resource because higher-priority threads keep acquiring it
A performance problem using standard engine approaches for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A type of livelock through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A thread is ready to run but is perpetually denied the resource because higher-priority threads keep acquiring it
questions.explanation: Starvation: a low-priority thread never gets the lock. Fix with fair queuing (ticket lock) or priority inheritance.

questions.question: Coffman's four necessary conditions for deadlock?
questions.correctAnswer: Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
acceptableAnswers: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
questions.explanation: Break any one to prevent deadlock. Lock ordering breaks circular wait.

questions.question: Hold-and-wait condition?
options: Thread holds resource while waiting for additional resources
questions.correctAnswer: Thread holds resource while waiting for additional resources
questions.explanation: Holds A, waits for B. If B holds B, waits for A -> deadlock.

questions.question: Deadlock vs livelock?
options: Deadlock: blocked, no CPU. Livelock: actively running but zero progress
questions.correctAnswer: Deadlock: blocked, no CPU. Livelock: actively running but zero progress
questions.explanation: Deadlock = FUTEX_WAIT. Livelock = RUNNABLE, CPU burning, yielding endlessly.

questions.question: Starvation?
options: Thread ready but perpetually denied resource due to higher-priority threads
questions.correctAnswer: Thread ready but perpetually denied resource due to higher-priority threads
questions.explanation: Fix with fair queuing (ticket lock) or priority inheritance.

questions.question: What is the Banker's Algorithm?
options: Deadlock avoidance — before granting resource, check if it could lead to deadlock
questions.correctAnswer: Deadlock avoidance — before granting resource, check if it could lead to deadlock
questions.explanation: Requires a priori max resource needs. Determines safe state. Impractical for dynamic systems.

questions.question: What is priority inversion?
options: High-priority thread blocked by low-priority thread preempted by medium-priority thread
questions.correctAnswer: High-priority thread blocked by low-priority thread preempted by medium-priority thread
questions.explanation: Mars Pathfinder disaster (1997). Fix: priority inheritance (FUTEX_LOCK_PI).

questions.question: What is std::scoped_lock?
options: C++17 RAII mutex that acquires multiple mutexes atomically using deadlock-avoidance algorithm
questions.correctAnswer: C++17 RAII mutex that acquires multiple mutexes atomically using deadlock-avoidance algorithm
questions.explanation: scoped_lock(A,B,C) uses try_lock algorithm to avoid deadlock. Both or nothing.

questions.question: What is try_lock() approach to breaking deadlock?
options: Break no-preemption: if can't get all locks, release what you hold and retry
questions.correctAnswer: Break no-preemption: if can't get all locks, release what you hold and retry
questions.explanation: Retry with backoff. Risk of livelock (both keep trying and failing).

questions.question: What is a lock-free data structure?
options: Uses atomic operations instead of mutexes — no blocking, no deadlock possible
questions.correctAnswer: Uses atomic operations instead of mutexes — no blocking, no deadlock possible
questions.explanation: Eliminates mutual exclusion condition (condition 1). CAS loops for synchronization.

questions.question: What is a wait-for graph?
options: Graph: nodes=threads, edges=waiting relation. Cycle = deadlock
questions.correctAnswer: Graph: nodes=threads, edges=waiting relation. Cycle = deadlock
questions.explanation: DFS cycle detection O(V+E). Database servers use this for runtime deadlock detection.

questions.question: What is Two-Phase Locking (2PL)?
options: Growing phase: acquire locks. Shrinking phase: release. Prevents cascading aborts in strict mode.
questions.correctAnswer: Growing phase: acquire locks. Shrinking phase: release. Prevents cascading aborts in strict mode.
questions.explanation: 2PL guarantees serializability. Strict 2PL: release all locks at commit.

questions.question: What is the Mars Pathfinder deadlock?
options: Priority inversion: high-priority bus thread blocked by low-priority meteorology thread
questions.correctAnswer: Priority inversion: high-priority bus thread blocked by low-priority meteorology thread
questions.explanation: Fix: enable priority inheritance. Remote patch from Earth.

questions.question: What thread should be killed in a deadlock cycle?
options: Newest (least work), lowest priority, fewest resources held, or most clean rollback
questions.correctAnswer: Newest (least work), lowest priority, fewest resources held, or most clean rollback
questions.explanation: Policy decision. Databases roll back the transaction with least work done.

questions.question: What are the four Coffman conditions?
options: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
questions.correctAnswer: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
questions.explanation: The four necessary conditions for deadlock.

questions.question: What does 'Mutual Exclusion' mean for deadlock?
options: Resources cannot be shared — only one thread can use a resource at a time
questions.correctAnswer: Resources cannot be shared — only one thread can use a resource at a time
questions.explanation: Mutual exclusion: non-shareable resources.

questions.question: What does 'Hold and Wait' mean?
options: A thread holds already allocated resources while waiting for additional resources
questions.correctAnswer: A thread holds already allocated resources while waiting for additional resources
questions.explanation: Hold-and-wait: holding while requesting more.

questions.question: What does 'No Preemption' mean?
options: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a thread — only the holder can voluntarily release them
questions.correctAnswer: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a thread — only the holder can voluntarily release them
questions.explanation: No preemption: only voluntary release.

questions.question: What does 'Circular Wait' mean?
options: Threads wait in a physical circle
There exists a set of threads {T1,T2,...,Tn} where each Ti waits for a resource held by T(i+1), and Tn waits for T1
Threads wait for the same resource
Threads wait for a fixed time
questions.correctAnswer: There exists a set of threads {T1,T2,...,Tn} where each Ti waits for a resource held by T(i+1), and Tn waits for T1
questions.explanation: Circular wait: cycle in resource allocation.

questions.question: Which condition can be broken by using a global lock ordering?
questions.correctAnswer: Circular Wait
questions.explanation: Lock ordering prevents cycles.

questions.question: Which condition can be broken by std::scoped_lock?
questions.correctAnswer: Hold and Wait (acquires all atomically)
questions.explanation: Scoped_lock: atomic acquisition prevents hold-and-wait.

questions.question: Which condition can be broken by making resources preemptible?
questions.correctAnswer: No Preemption
questions.explanation: If resources can be preempted (try_lock/release), no preemption is removed.

questions.question: Which condition can be broken by using shared resources (read-write locks)?
questions.correctAnswer: Mutual Exclusion
questions.explanation: Sharing resources (read-only) removes mutual exclusion.

questions.question: What is a 'resource allocation graph'?
options: A directed graph where nodes are threads and resources; edges show 'holds' and 'waits'
questions.correctAnswer: A directed graph where nodes are threads and resources; edges show 'holds' and 'waits'
questions.explanation: RAG: threads → resources (holds), resources → threads (is held by).

questions.question: How do you detect deadlock using the resource allocation graph?
options: Check if the graph has a cycle
Check if the graph is empty
questions.correctAnswer: Check if the graph has a cycle
questions.explanation: A cycle in the RAG indicates deadlock (if each resource is single-instance).

questions.question: What is a 'safe state' in the Banker's algorithm?
options: A state where there exists at least one sequence of thread executions ensuring all threads can complete
A state where all resources are free
A state with no pending requests
questions.correctAnswer: A state where there exists at least one sequence of thread executions ensuring all threads can complete
questions.explanation: Safe state: deadlock-free execution sequence exists.

questions.question: What is an 'unsafe state'?
options: A state that may lead to deadlock (not necessarily deadlocked yet) but no guaranteed safe execution sequence exists
questions.correctAnswer: A state that may lead to deadlock (not necessarily deadlocked yet) but no guaranteed safe execution sequence exists
questions.explanation: Unsafe ≠ deadlocked, but could become deadlocked.

questions.question: What are the data structures needed for Banker's Algorithm?
questions.correctAnswer: Available, Max, Allocation, Need matrices
questions.explanation: Banker's: Available, Max, Allocation, Need.

questions.question: What does 'deadlock avoidance' require?
options: Advance knowledge of each thread's maximum resource needs (the Max matrix)
questions.correctAnswer: Advance knowledge of each thread's maximum resource needs (the Max matrix)
questions.explanation: Banker's algorithm needs Max declarations.

questions.question: What is the 'wait-for graph'?
options: A graph showing which threads wait for which other threads
A resource allocation graph with resources removed
questions.correctAnswer: A graph showing which threads wait for which other threads
questions.explanation: Wait-for graph: derived from RAG (remove resources).

questions.question: How does wait-for graph simplify deadlock detection?
options: By removing resource nodes, cycles directly show thread deadlocks without intermediate resource nodes
questions.correctAnswer: By removing resource nodes, cycles directly show thread deadlocks without intermediate resource nodes
questions.explanation: Wait-for: threads → threads (waiting for).

questions.question: What is 'deadlock prevention'?
options: Ensuring at least one Coffman condition can never hold
questions.correctAnswer: Ensuring at least one Coffman condition can never hold
questions.explanation: Prevention: break at least one condition.

questions.question: What is 'deadlock avoidance'?
options: Dynamically checking resource allocations to prevent unsafe states
questions.correctAnswer: Dynamically checking resource allocations to prevent unsafe states
questions.explanation: Avoidance: dynamic checking (Banker's).

questions.question: What is 'deadlock detection and recovery'?
options: Allowing deadlocks to occur, detecting them (cycle detection), and recovering by killing threads or preempting resources
questions.correctAnswer: Allowing deadlocks to occur, detecting them (cycle detection), and recovering by killing threads or preempting resources
questions.explanation: Detection + recovery: deadlock happens, then resolve.

questions.question: What is the 'Ostrich Algorithm' for deadlocks?
options: Burying your head in the sand — ignoring deadlocks entirely
questions.correctAnswer: Burying your head in the sand — ignoring deadlocks entirely
questions.explanation: Ostrich: pretend deadlocks don't happen.

questions.question: When is the Ostrich Algorithm acceptable?
options: When deadlocks are extremely rare and the cost of prevention/avoidance/detection exceeds the cost of occasional system restart
questions.correctAnswer: When deadlocks are extremely rare and the cost of prevention/avoidance/detection exceeds the cost of occasional system restart
questions.explanation: Tradeoff: overhead vs risk.

questions.question: How does the Banker's Algorithm determine if a request is safe?
options: Grant the request, then simulate to see if all threads can finish
Deny the request if it might cause deadlock
questions.correctAnswer: Grant the request, then simulate to see if all threads can finish
questions.explanation: Safety check: simulate granting and see if all can complete.

questions.question: What does the Need matrix in Banker's represent?
options: Max - Allocation — resources each thread may still request
questions.correctAnswer: Max - Allocation — resources each thread may still request
questions.explanation: Need = Max - Allocation.

questions.question: What is the main limitation of the Banker's Algorithm?
options: It requires knowing the maximum resource needs in advance, which is often impractical
questions.correctAnswer: It requires knowing the maximum resource needs in advance, which is often impractical
questions.explanation: Banker's impracticality: a priori max claims.

questions.question: What is 'deadlock detection' vs 'deadlock prevention'?
options: Detection: allow deadlock, find it, recover. Prevention: break a Coffman condition
questions.correctAnswer: Detection: allow deadlock, find it, recover. Prevention: break a Coffman condition
questions.explanation: Detection: laissez-faire + recovery. Prevention: never deadlock.

questions.question: What is a 'wait-die' deadlock prevention scheme?
options: Older transactions wait for younger ones; younger transactions die (abort) if they conflict with older ones
questions.correctAnswer: Older transactions wait for younger ones; younger transactions die (abort) if they conflict with older ones
questions.explanation: Wait-die: younger aborts on conflict.

questions.question: What is a 'wound-wait' deadlock prevention scheme?
options: Older preempts (wounds) younger; younger waits for older
questions.correctAnswer: Older preempts (wounds) younger; younger waits for older
questions.explanation: Wound-wait: older preempts younger.

questions.question: What is 'timeout-based deadlock detection'?
options: If a thread waits longer than a timeout, assume deadlock and abort
questions.correctAnswer: If a thread waits longer than a timeout, assume deadlock and abort
questions.explanation: Timeouts: practical heuristic detection.

questions.question: What is the limitation of timeout-based detection?
options: False positives (long wait due to contention, not deadlock)
questions.correctAnswer: False positives (long wait due to contention, not deadlock)
questions.explanation: Timeout: may abort non-deadlocked threads.

questions.question: Four Coffman conditions:
options: Mutual Excl, Hold+Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
questions.correctAnswer: Mutual Excl, Hold+Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
questions.explanation: Break any one → no deadlock

questions.question: Break mutual exclusion:
questions.correctAnswer: Use shared_mutex or lock-free
questions.explanation: If resource can be shared, it can't deadlock

questions.question: Break hold-and-wait:
questions.correctAnswer: std::scoped_lock acquires all atomically
questions.explanation: Both-or-nothing acquisition

questions.question: Break no preemption:
questions.correctAnswer: try_lock with backoff
questions.explanation: Release held resources on failure

questions.question: Break circular wait:
questions.correctAnswer: Total lock ordering
questions.explanation: Every thread acquires in same order

questions.question: std::scoped_lock(A,B):
questions.correctAnswer: Internal deadlock-avoidance algorithm
questions.explanation: Similar to std::lock()

questions.question: Banker's Algorithm:
options: Avoidance: check if grant could deadlock
questions.correctAnswer: Avoidance: check if grant could deadlock
questions.explanation: Requires maximum need knowledge

questions.question: Priority inversion:
options: High-pri blocked by low-pri, which is preempted by med-pri
questions.correctAnswer: High-pri blocked by low-pri, which is preempted by med-pri
questions.explanation: Mars Pathfinder bug

questions.question: Priority inversion fix:
questions.correctAnswer: Priority inheritance
questions.explanation: Low-pri inherits high priority

questions.question: Strict 2PL:
questions.correctAnswer: Hold all locks until commit
questions.explanation: Prevents cascading aborts

questions.question: Livelock difference from deadlock:
questions.correctAnswer: Threads active, yielding, no progress
questions.explanation: Burning CPU doing nothing

questions.question: Mars Pathfinder fix:
questions.correctAnswer: Enable priority inheritance on mutex
questions.explanation: Famous real-time bug

questions.question: What is the Multi-Level Feedback Queue (MLFQ)?
options: A single queue in most implementations with additional processing with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A scheduler with multiple priority queues — jobs move between queues based on observed behavior (CPU-bound drop, I/O-bound rise)
A hardware queue across all supported platforms with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A networking scheduler by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: A scheduler with multiple priority queues — jobs move between queues based on observed behavior (CPU-bound drop, I/O-bound rise)
questions.explanation: MLFQ learns: short/I/O jobs get high priority. Long CPU jobs get demoted to lower priority with longer time slices. Balances responsiveness with throughput.

questions.question: How does MLFQ prevent starvation of low-priority jobs?
options: It doesn't via the core framework
Priority boost — periodically move all jobs to the top queue, giving everyone a chance
It kills low-priority jobs through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
It uses more cores by default behavior within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Priority boost — periodically move all jobs to the top queue, giving everyone a chance
questions.explanation: Periodic priority boost prevents indefinite starvation. All jobs temporarily get highest priority, ensuring long-running CPU jobs eventually get CPU time.

questions.question: What is proportional share scheduling?
options: Equal shares for all under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Each process gets a guaranteed fraction of CPU time — e.g., Process A gets 2x more than B via lottery or stride scheduling
First-come-first-serve within the current scope across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Round-robin only through the built-in API
questions.correctAnswer: Each process gets a guaranteed fraction of CPU time — e.g., Process A gets 2x more than B via lottery or stride scheduling
questions.explanation: Lottery: each process holds tickets. Scheduler randomly draws — more tickets = more wins. Stride: deterministic, each process has a stride value — smallest stride runs next.

questions.question: What is the Linux CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler)?
options: A lottery scheduler using standard engine approaches in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A proportional share scheduler using virtual runtime (vruntime) — the thread with the lowest vruntime runs next
A MLFQ variant as defined in the specification
A real-time scheduler as defined in the specification under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A proportional share scheduler using virtual runtime (vruntime) — the thread with the lowest vruntime runs next
questions.explanation: CFS tracks vruntime per thread (actual runtime weighted by priority). Red-black tree ordered by vruntime. Pick leftmost (minimum vruntime) to run. Perfect fairness over time.

questions.question: What scheduling class do real-time threads use on Linux?
options: CFS within the current scope with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxx
SCHED_FIFO or SCHED_RR — always preempts CFS threads, provides deterministic scheduling
SCHED_IDLE for typical use cases with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: SCHED_FIFO or SCHED_RR — always preempts CFS threads, provides deterministic scheduling
questions.explanation: Real-time classes have static priority above CFS (nice -20 to +19). SCHED_FIFO: runs until it yields. SCHED_RR: round-robin with time slice. Always chosen before CFS threads.

questions.question: What is MLFQ?
options: Multiple priority queues — jobs move based on observed behavior
questions.correctAnswer: Multiple priority queues — jobs move based on observed behavior
questions.explanation: MLFQ learns: short I/O jobs get high priority. Long CPU jobs demoted.

questions.question: How does MLFQ prevent starvation?
options: Periodic priority boost — all jobs temporarily get highest priority
questions.correctAnswer: Periodic priority boost — all jobs temporarily get highest priority
questions.explanation: Ensures long-running CPU jobs eventually get CPU time.

questions.question: Proportional share scheduling?
options: Each process gets guaranteed fraction of CPU via lottery or stride scheduling
questions.correctAnswer: Each process gets guaranteed fraction of CPU via lottery or stride scheduling
questions.explanation: Lottery: tickets. Stride: deterministic virtual time accounting.

questions.question: Linux CFS?
options: Proportional share using vruntime — thread with lowest vruntime runs next
questions.correctAnswer: Proportional share using vruntime — thread with lowest vruntime runs next
questions.explanation: CFS tracks vruntime (runtime weighted by priority). Red-black tree ordered by vruntime.

questions.question: Real-time scheduling classes on Linux?
options: SCHED_FIFO or SCHED_RR — always preempts CFS threads
questions.correctAnswer: SCHED_FIFO or SCHED_RR — always preempts CFS threads
questions.explanation: Real-time priorities 1-99 above CFS nice -20 to +19.

questions.question: What is lottery scheduling?
options: Random ticket draw — more tickets = higher probability of winning CPU
questions.correctAnswer: Random ticket draw — more tickets = higher probability of winning CPU
questions.explanation: Each thread has tickets. Random draw picks winner. Statistical fairness over time.

questions.question: What is stride scheduling?
options: Deterministic proportional share — thread with lowest pass value runs next
questions.correctAnswer: Deterministic proportional share — thread with lowest pass value runs next
questions.explanation: Stride = constant/tickets. Pass += stride after each quantum. No variance.

questions.question: What is vruntime in CFS?
options: Virtual runtime — CPU time weighted by priority. Thread with lowest vruntime runs next.
questions.correctAnswer: Virtual runtime — CPU time weighted by priority. Thread with lowest vruntime runs next.
questions.explanation: Nice=0: 1ns CPU = 1ns vruntime. Nice=5: 1ns CPU = ~3ns vruntime.

questions.question: What is EEVDF?
options: Earliest Eligible Virtual Deadline First — Linux 6.6+ scheduler replacing CFS
questions.correctAnswer: Earliest Eligible Virtual Deadline First — Linux 6.6+ scheduler replacing CFS
questions.explanation: Each thread has eligible flag (deserves to run) and vdeadline (must run by). Fixes CFS latency issues.

questions.question: What is work stealing?
options: Each thread has task deque. Steal from remote end of idle thread. Local LIFO, remote FIFO.
questions.correctAnswer: Each thread has task deque. Steal from remote end of idle thread. Local LIFO, remote FIFO.
questions.explanation: No kernel involvement. Purely userspace atomics. Scales better than OS balancing.

questions.question: How does Linux CFS handle I/O-bound threads?
options: I/O-bound threads accumulate less vruntime because they sleep and don't consume CPU. Low vruntime -> run immediately when woken.
questions.correctAnswer: I/O-bound threads accumulate less vruntime because they sleep and don't consume CPU. Low vruntime -> run immediately when woken.
questions.explanation: I/O thread blips for 50us, blocks for 10ms. vruntime barely increases. Gets CPU ahead of CPU-bound threads.

questions.question: What is SCHED_DEADLINE on Linux?
options: Earliest Deadline First scheduling — guarantees bandwidth reservation
questions.correctAnswer: Earliest Deadline First scheduling — guarantees bandwidth reservation
questions.explanation: Each thread specifies runtime, period, deadline. Guarantees up to 100% utilization (vs RMS ~69%).

questions.question: What is the rate-monotonic scheduling (RMS) utilization bound for N tasks?
questions.explanation: For 2 tasks: 0.828. For large N: ~ln2 = 69%. EDF can schedule up to 100%.

questions.question: What is MLFQ (Multi-Level Feedback Queue)?
options: A scheduling algorithm using multiple queues with different priorities, where threads move between queues based on behavior
questions.correctAnswer: A scheduling algorithm using multiple queues with different priorities, where threads move between queues based on behavior
questions.explanation: MLFQ: learn thread behavior and adjust priority.

questions.question: How does MLFQ classify threads?
options: Short CPU bursts (interactive) → high priority; long bursts (CPU-bound) → low priority
questions.correctAnswer: Short CPU bursts (interactive) → high priority; long bursts (CPU-bound) → low priority
questions.explanation: MLFQ: interactive = short burst = high priority.

questions.question: What is 'priority boost' in MLFQ?
options: Periodically moving all threads to the topmost queue to prevent starvation
questions.correctAnswer: Periodically moving all threads to the topmost queue to prevent starvation
questions.explanation: Priority boost prevents starvation in MLFQ.

questions.question: What is 'accounting' in MLFQ?
options: Tracking how much of its quantum a thread has used at each level
questions.correctAnswer: Tracking how much of its quantum a thread has used at each level
questions.explanation: Accounting: track quantum usage at each level.

questions.question: What happens when a thread uses its entire time slice in MLFQ?
options: It stays at the same priority
It is moved down one queue (lower priority, longer quantum next time)
questions.correctAnswer: It is moved down one queue (lower priority, longer quantum next time)
questions.explanation: Full quantum usage → CPU-bound → lower priority.

questions.question: What happens when a thread yields the CPU before its quantum expires in MLFQ?
options: It stays at the same priority (interactive pattern — short burst)
questions.correctAnswer: It stays at the same priority (interactive pattern — short burst)
questions.explanation: Yielding early → interactive → same/higher priority.

questions.question: What is 'lottery scheduling'?
options: Each thread gets lottery tickets proportional to its desired share; the scheduler draws a random ticket to choose the next thread
questions.correctAnswer: Each thread gets lottery tickets proportional to its desired share; the scheduler draws a random ticket to choose the next thread
questions.explanation: Lottery scheduling: probabilistically fair.

questions.question: How does lottery scheduling achieve approximate fairness?
options: Over time, threads win the CPU in proportion to their ticket count — the law of large numbers
questions.correctAnswer: Over time, threads win the CPU in proportion to their ticket count — the law of large numbers
questions.explanation: Lottery: long-term proportionally fair.

questions.question: What is 'ticket transfer' in lottery scheduling?
options: A thread can temporarily give its tickets to another thread (e.g., to avoid priority inversion)
questions.correctAnswer: A thread can temporarily give its tickets to another thread (e.g., to avoid priority inversion)
questions.explanation: Ticket transfer: pass tickets to another thread.

questions.question: What is 'ticket inflation'?
options: A thread can temporarily increase its own tickets (if cooperative) — used in user-level schedulers
questions.correctAnswer: A thread can temporarily increase its own tickets (if cooperative) — used in user-level schedulers
questions.explanation: Ticket inflation: cooperative priority adjustment.

questions.question: What is 'stride scheduling'?
options: A deterministic fair scheduler using stride counters — each thread advances its pass by a fixed stride per time slice
questions.correctAnswer: A deterministic fair scheduler using stride counters — each thread advances its pass by a fixed stride per time slice
questions.explanation: Stride: deterministic proportional share.

questions.question: How does stride scheduling work?
options: Each thread has a stride = total_tickets / my_tickets. The thread with the smallest pass value runs next
questions.correctAnswer: Each thread has a stride = total_tickets / my_tickets. The thread with the smallest pass value runs next
questions.explanation: Stride: smallest pass runs next.

questions.question: What is the advantage of stride over lottery scheduling?
options: Stride provides deterministic, exact proportional fairness — no randomness
questions.correctAnswer: Stride provides deterministic, exact proportional fairness — no randomness
questions.explanation: Stride: deterministic vs lottery's probabilistic.

questions.question: What is CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler)?
options: The Linux default scheduler from 2007-2023 using a red-black tree keyed by vruntime
questions.correctAnswer: The Linux default scheduler from 2007-2023 using a red-black tree keyed by vruntime
questions.explanation: CFS: Linux fair scheduler.

questions.question: How does CFS achieve fairness?
options: By always running the thread with the lowest vruntime (CPU time weighted by priority)
questions.correctAnswer: By always running the thread with the lowest vruntime (CPU time weighted by priority)
questions.explanation: CFS: smallest vruntime runs next.

questions.question: What did EEVDF replace CFS in Linux 6.6?
options: Earliest Eligible Virtual Deadline First — a scheduling policy with better latency bounds
questions.correctAnswer: Earliest Eligible Virtual Deadline First — a scheduling policy with better latency bounds
questions.explanation: EEVDF: Linux's modern O(1) scheduling with deadline tracking.

questions.question: What is 'weighting' in CFS?
options: CFS nice values adjust weight: nice=-20 gets ~10× the CPU share of nice=+19
questions.correctAnswer: CFS nice values adjust weight: nice=-20 gets ~10× the CPU share of nice=+19
questions.explanation: CFS weights: nice -20 = heavy, nice 19 = light.

questions.question: What is the default CFS target latency on desktop Linux?
questions.correctAnswer: ~6 ms
questions.explanation: Target latency: all runnable threads get a turn every ~6 ms.

questions.question: If 10 threads are runnable on CFS, what is each thread's approximate time slice?
options: ~0.6 ms (6 ms / 10 threads) with a minimum of 0.75 ms
questions.correctAnswer: ~0.6 ms (6 ms / 10 threads) with a minimum of 0.75 ms
questions.explanation: CFS: time slice = target_latency / N, min = 0.75 ms.

questions.question: What happens if CFS's calculated time slice falls below the minimum granularity?
options: The time slice is set to the minimum granularity (0.75 ms) to prevent thrashing
questions.correctAnswer: The time slice is set to the minimum granularity (0.75 ms) to prevent thrashing
questions.explanation: CFS min granularity: prevent excessive context switching.

questions.question: What is the CFS nice range?

questions.question: What is 'scheduling classes' in the Linux kernel?

questions.correctAnswer: Scheduling on a per-process-group basis — ensures fairness among users not just threads

questions.question: What is 'SCHED_OTHER' on Linux?

questions.question: What is the Windows equivalent of nice values?

questions.question: What is 'SetPriorityClass' on Windows?

questions.correctAnswer: Sets the priority class for a process (IDLE, NORMAL, HIGH, REALTIME, etc.)

questions.correctAnswer: THREAD_PRIORITY_LOWEST through THREAD_PRIORITY_HIGHEST, TIME_CRITICAL, IDLE
questions.explanation: Windows thread priority: -2 to +2 plus special values.

questions.question: MLFQ stands for:
questions.correctAnswer: Multi-Level Feedback Queue
questions.explanation: Multiple priority queues with demotion/promotion

questions.question: MLFQ Rule 1:
questions.correctAnswer: If Prio(A) > Prio(B), A runs
questions.explanation: Strict priority scheduling

questions.question: MLFQ Rule 4:
questions.correctAnswer: Use full quantum → demote
questions.explanation: CPU-bound threads sink

questions.question: Gaming MLFQ:
options: Do I/O right before quantum expires to stay high priority
questions.correctAnswer: Do I/O right before quantum expires to stay high priority
questions.explanation: Fixed by cumulative time tracking

questions.question: Lottery scheduling:
options: Random ticket draw for proportional share
questions.correctAnswer: Random ticket draw for proportional share
questions.explanation: More tickets = more CPU

questions.question: Stride scheduling:
options: Deterministic proportional share via virtual time
questions.correctAnswer: Deterministic proportional share via virtual time
questions.explanation: No short-term variance

questions.question: CFS stands for:
questions.correctAnswer: Completely Fair Scheduler
questions.explanation: Linux's vruntime-based scheduler

questions.question: vruntime:
questions.correctAnswer: Weighted CPU time, pick lowest
questions.explanation: Lowest vruntime = furthest behind

questions.question: EEVDF:
questions.correctAnswer: Earliest Eligible Virtual Deadline First (Linux 6.6+)
questions.explanation: CFS replacement

questions.question: Work stealing:
options: Thread steals tasks from other threads' deques
questions.correctAnswer: Thread steals tasks from other threads' deques
questions.explanation: UE TaskGraph pattern

questions.question: Cache affinity:
options: Thread prefers core it last ran on (warm cache)
questions.correctAnswer: Thread prefers core it last ran on (warm cache)
questions.explanation: Migration causes cold caches

questions.question: RMS:
options: Rate-Monotonic Scheduling: shortest period = highest priority
questions.correctAnswer: Rate-Monotonic Scheduling: shortest period = highest priority
questions.explanation: Static priority real-time

questions.question: EDF:
questions.correctAnswer: Earliest Deadline First: dynamic priority
questions.explanation: Can schedule up to 100% utilization

questions.question: SCHED_FIFO:
questions.correctAnswer: Real-time scheduling, runs until blocked
questions.explanation: Priority 1-99 on Linux

questions.question: What does the perspective projection matrix do?
options: Translates vertices for typical use cases
Maps 3D view-space coordinates to clip space — divides x and y by z (distance), creating perspective foreshortening
Rotates the camera across all supported platforms for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Lighting calculation within the current scope for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Maps 3D view-space coordinates to clip space — divides x and y by z (distance), creating perspective foreshortening
questions.explanation: Perspective matrix stores z in the w component. After vertex shader, the GPU performs the homogeneous divide (x/w, y/w, z/w) — this creates perspective: far objects appear smaller.

questions.question: What is the homogeneous divide (perspective divide)?
options: A vertex shader operation via the core framework within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
GPU hardware step: divides xyz by w after the vertex shader — converts from homogeneous clip space to NDC
A pixel shader under normal conditions
A CPU operation using standard engine approaches within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: GPU hardware step: divides xyz by w after the vertex shader — converts from homogeneous clip space to NDC
questions.explanation: After the vertex shader outputs clip-space coordinates, the GPU's fixed-function hardware performs x/w, y/w, z/w. This is NOT programmable — it always happens.

questions.question: Why does the projection matrix store z in the w component?
options: It doesn't within the current scope
To enable perspective-correct interpolation — w contains the original view-space depth, used to interpolate texture coordinates correctly in screen space
For debugging via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: To enable perspective-correct interpolation — w contains the original view-space depth, used to interpolate texture coordinates correctly in screen space
questions.explanation: Screen-space interpolation of triangle attributes is NOT linear in 3D. Dividing by w (perspective-correct interpolation) fixes this — textures don't warp on triangles viewed at an angle.

questions.question: What is clip space and what happens there?
options: The final screen for typical use cases
A coordinate space where vertices are tested against the view frustum — triangles partially outside the frustum are clipped, generating new vertices at the boundary
Camera space via the core framework with additional processing
World space within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: A coordinate space where vertices are tested against the view frustum — triangles partially outside the frustum are clipped, generating new vertices at the boundary
questions.explanation: In clip space (-w ≤ x,y,z ≤ w), the GPU clips triangles against the 6 frustum planes. Triangles fully inside pass through. Partially visible triangles generate new edge vertices.

questions.question: What is reversed-Z and why is it used?
options: The Z-axis is flipped across all supported platforms with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Far plane mapped to 0, near plane to 1 — floating point precision is denser near 0, giving better depth precision for distant objects
A rendering technique under normal conditions within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A camera setting in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: Far plane mapped to 0, near plane to 1 — floating point precision is denser near 0, giving better depth precision for distant objects
questions.explanation: Standard mapping: near=0, far=1 wastes precision close to camera where it's less needed. Reversed-Z uses FP's natural precision distribution (dense near 0) for far objects where Z-fighting occurs. UE5 uses reversed-Z.

questions.question: What are barycentric coordinates?
options: Pixel coordinates via the core framework across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Three weights (α,β,γ) that express a point inside a triangle as a weighted sum of its vertices — α+β+γ=1, all ≥0 for interior points
UV coordinates through the built-in API
Screen coordinates across all supported platforms across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Three weights (α,β,γ) that express a point inside a triangle as a weighted sum of its vertices — α+β+γ=1, all ≥0 for interior points
questions.explanation: P = α·A + β·B + γ·C. At vertex A: (1,0,0). At centroid: (1/3,1/3,1/3). Used to interpolate any vertex attribute (color, UV, normal) across the triangle surface.

questions.question: How does the GPU rasterizer compute barycentric coordinates?
options: Edge functions — for each pixel, compute signed distances to three edges. Normalize to get (α,β,γ).
Ray tracing using standard engine approaches within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Matrix inversion through the built-in API in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Edge functions — for each pixel, compute signed distances to three edges. Normalize to get (α,β,γ).
questions.explanation: GPU computes E01, E12, E20 edge functions for a pixel. Normalize by triangle area. These ARE the barycentric weights. Extremely efficient — a few MADs per pixel.

questions.question: Why does perspective-correct interpolation need barycentrics divided by w?
options: It doesn't through the built-in API
Screen-space barycentrics are NOT the same as 3D barycentrics due to perspective distortion. Dividing by the interpolated 1/w fixes this.
For performance under normal conditions in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
For anti-aliasing within the current scope in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Screen-space barycentrics are NOT the same as 3D barycentrics due to perspective distortion. Dividing by the interpolated 1/w fixes this.
questions.explanation: Without perspective correction: floor textures warp (affine texture mapping, PS1 style). With it: correct perspective. GPU computes α/z, β/z, γ/z and 1/z, then divides to get correct 3D barycentrics.

questions.question: Where on the triangle does α=1?
questions.correctAnswer: Vertex A
questions.explanation: At vertex A: barycentrics are (1,0,0). At B: (0,1,0). At C: (0,0,1). Each vertex has 1 for its own weight and 0 for others.

questions.question: What is the interior test using barycentrics?
options: Check distance to center within the current scope
All three barycentric coordinates must be ≥ 0 and ≤ 1
Check color value in most implementations with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: All three barycentric coordinates must be ≥ 0 and ≤ 1
questions.explanation: If any barycentric is negative, the point lies outside the triangle. α,β,γ ≥ 0 AND α+β+γ=1 means point is inside or on edge. This is how the GPU determines pixel coverage.

questions.question: What does BRDF stand for and what does it model?
questions.correctAnswer: Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function — models how light reflects off a surface: given incoming and outgoing directions, returns the ratio of reflected radiance
questions.explanation: BRDF(ωi, ωo) = dLo(ωo) / dEi(ωi). Physically based: must be reciprocal f(ωi,ωo)=f(ωo,ωi) and energy conserving ∫f·cosθ dω ≤ 1.

questions.question: What three terms compose the Cook-Torrance microfacet BRDF?
questions.correctAnswer: Normal Distribution Function (D — roughness), Geometric Shadowing (G — self-shadowing), Fresnel (F — angle-dependent reflectance)
questions.explanation: Cook-Torrance = D·G·F / (4·(n·l)·(n·v)). D= GGX/Trowbridge-Reitz. G= Smith. F= Schlick approximation. Physically based — used in UE5, Unity HDRP.

questions.question: What does the GGX normal distribution function model?
options: Microfacet distribution — how many microfacets are aligned with the half-vector at given roughness
Shadow by default behavior with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Specular color as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Microfacet distribution — how many microfacets are aligned with the half-vector at given roughness
questions.explanation: GGX gives the long specular tail seen in real materials. Roughness=0: sharp mirror. Roughness=1: diffuse-like. Used universally in PBR.

questions.question: What is the Fresnel effect?
options: Light absorption in most implementations with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Reflectance increases at grazing angles — water looks reflective at the horizon but transparent looking straight down
Diffuse lighting via the core framework
Shadow mapping within the current scope in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Reflectance increases at grazing angles — water looks reflective at the horizon but transparent looking straight down
questions.explanation: Schlick approximation: F = F0 + (1-F0)·(1-cosθ)⁵. F0 = reflectance at normal incidence (~0.04 for non-metals, tinted for metals). At 90°, ALL materials become fully reflective.

questions.question: What is the difference between metallic and dielectric (non-metal) F0 values?
options: No difference through the built-in API
Dielectrics: F0 ~0.04 (grayscale). Metals: F0 is tinted (gold=orange, copper=reddish) and much higher (0.5-1.0)
Metals are darker within the current scope across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Dielectrics are always white for typical use cases with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Dielectrics: F0 ~0.04 (grayscale). Metals: F0 is tinted (gold=orange, copper=reddish) and much higher (0.5-1.0)
questions.explanation: Metalness workflow: metal=0 → F0=0.04 (dielectric). metal=1 → F0=BaseColor (metal — no diffuse, all color comes from specular). This is the core of PBR texturing.

questions.question: What do spherical harmonics encode?
options: Vertex positions through the built-in API
Lighting information on a sphere — direction-dependent irradiance for diffuse global illumination
Texture coordinates through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Mesh topology for typical use cases with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Lighting information on a sphere — direction-dependent irradiance for diffuse global illumination
questions.explanation: SH encodes functions on a sphere using basis functions (Y_lm). For diffuse GI, 9 coefficients (3 bands, l≤2) capture ~99% of lighting energy.

questions.question: How many SH coefficients encode diffuse indirect lighting?
options: 1 via the core framework with additional processing
questions.explanation: Diffuse lighting is low-frequency — 3 bands (9 coefficients) capture >99% of the signal. Each coefficient is an RGB vector. UE's SkyLight uses 3rd-order SH.

questions.question: What does the l=0 (first band) SH coefficient represent?
options: Directional light through the built-in API within the standard framework
The average (DC) ambient light level — a constant value over the sphere
Shadow information using standard engine approaches in standard configurati
Specular highlights for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: The average (DC) ambient light level — a constant value over the sphere
questions.explanation: Y_00 = constant. The l=0 coefficient is the mean irradiance — the ambient base level. Higher bands add directional variation.

questions.question: How are SH coefficients used in a shader?
options: They're not for typical use cases
Reconstruct irradiance: dot the SH coefficients with the basis functions evaluated at the surface normal direction
For texture sampling through the built-in API with additional processing in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
For vertex animation via the core framework in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Reconstruct irradiance: dot the SH coefficients with the basis functions evaluated at the surface normal direction
questions.explanation: At runtime: evaluate Y_lm(normal) → dot with stored coefficients → get irradiance in that direction. 9 dot products per pixel.

questions.question: What is the convolution property that makes SH efficient for diffuse lighting?
options: Convolution of the light probe with a Lambertian BRDF in SH space is just scaling the coefficients — a dot product at runtime
It's faster to render across all supported platforms with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
It uses GPU hardware across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Convolution of the light probe with a Lambertian BRDF in SH space is just scaling the coefficients — a dot product at runtime
questions.explanation: SH convolution theorem: the SH coefficients of the convolution are the product of the coefficients of each function (times a diagonal matrix). This makes irradiance computation extremely cheap.

questions.question: How are SH light probes baked in Unreal Engine?
options: Manually placed through the built-in API with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Lightmass or GPU Lightmass samples the scene from probe positions, projects the incoming radiance onto SH basis functions, and stores the coefficients
At runtime during gameplay using standard engine approaches for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
They're hand-authored through the built-in API
questions.correctAnswer: Lightmass or GPU Lightmass samples the scene from probe positions, projects the incoming radiance onto SH basis functions, and stores the coefficients
questions.explanation: UE5: Place Lightmass Importance Volumes. Lightmass traces rays from probe positions to sample scene radiance. Projects onto 3rd-order SH (9 coefficients). Stored per-probe. Interpolated at runtime.

questions.question: What is the fundamental principle of Monte Carlo integration?
options: Analytic solutions within the current scope with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxx
Estimate integrals by averaging random samples: ∫f(x)dx ≈ (1/N)·Σ f(x_i)/p(x_i)
Precomputed tables within the current scope with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Estimate integrals by averaging random samples: ∫f(x)dx ≈ (1/N)·Σ f(x_i)/p(x_i)
questions.explanation: MC integration: draw N random samples from distribution p(x), evaluate integrand f(x)/p(x) at each, average. Converges at O(1/√N). Unbiased.

questions.question: What does importance sampling improve?
options: Nothing using standard engine approaches with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Reduces variance by drawing more samples where the integrand is large — matches sampling distribution to the function
Speed only for typical use cases
Memory usage in most implementations for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Reduces variance by drawing more samples where the integrand is large — matches sampling distribution to the function
questions.explanation: Sampling uniformly wastes samples on dark regions. Importance sampling concentrates samples on bright regions (lights, specular lobes).

questions.question: What is Russian roulette in path tracing?
options: A game across all supported platforms
Probabilistically terminating paths: with probability p, continue; with probability 1-p, stop and divide by p. Unbiased, limits infinite recursion.
A Russian game as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A type of noise within the current scope in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Probabilistically terminating paths: with probability p, continue; with probability 1-p, stop and divide by p. Unbiased, limits infinite recursion.
questions.explanation: Without RR, path length is unbounded. RR terminates paths randomly while preserving the expected value (divide surviving paths by p).

questions.question: What is the rendering equation?
questions.correctAnswer: L_o = L_e + ∫ f_r·L_i·(n·ω_i)·dω_i — outgoing light = emitted + reflected (BRDF × incident light × cosine term integrated over hemisphere)
acceptableAnswers: L_o = L_e + integral of BRDF * L_i * cos
questions.explanation: Kajiya 1986. L_o = emitted + reflected. Reflected = integral over hemisphere of BRDF × incoming radiance × cosine of incident angle. Path tracing solves this recursively.

questions.question: What is the difference between path tracing and ray tracing?
options: Ray tracing: single ray → intersection query. Path tracing: recursive ray bounces through the scene, accumulating light at each bounce following the rendering equation.
Path tracing is faster via the core framework with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Ray tracing is deprecated via the core framework with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Ray tracing: single ray → intersection query. Path tracing: recursive ray bounces through the scene, accumulating light at each bounce following the rendering equation.
questions.explanation: Ray trace = TraceRay() → hit result. Path trace = TraceRay() → shade → spawn more rays recursively → accumulate result. Path tracing solves the rendering equation; ray tracing is the building block.

questions.question: What is the typical number of samples per pixel for a clean path-traced image?
options: 1-16 for real-time (denoised), 100-1000+ for offline quality (no denoiser). Denoising (OptiX, DLSS RR) dramatically reduces required samples.
10,000 minimum via the core framework with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Always 1 through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: 1-16 for real-time (denoised), 100-1000+ for offline quality (no denoiser). Denoising (OptiX, DLSS RR) dramatically reduces required samples.
questions.explanation: Without denoising: hundreds to thousands of SPP. With temporal accumulation + DLSS Ray Reconstruction: 1-4 SPP can produce clean images. UE Lumen uses ~4 SPP with denoising.

questions.question: What is the difference between sRGB and linear color space?
options: No difference in most implementations for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
sRGB is gamma-encoded (~γ=2.2) for perceptual uniformity and efficient 8-bit storage; linear is physically correct for light calculations
sRGB is faster for typical use cases
Linear is deprecated by default behavior in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: sRGB is gamma-encoded (~γ=2.2) for perceptual uniformity and efficient 8-bit storage; linear is physically correct for light calculations
questions.explanation: Light is linear (2× photons = 2× brightness). sRGB applies gamma for perceptual encoding (more bits in darks). Shading MUST happen in linear space; convert sRGB→linear before and linear→sRGB after.

questions.question: Why does HDR rendering matter?
options: It's just brighter under normal conditions in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Preserves light values >1.0 for bloom, tone mapping, and eye adaptation — without HDR, bright areas clip to white
It's faster for typical use cases
It uses less memory as defined in the specification within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Preserves light values >1.0 for bloom, tone mapping, and eye adaptation — without HDR, bright areas clip to white
questions.explanation: HDR stores radiance values beyond [0,1]. Tone mapping compresses HDR→LDR for display. Bloom extracts bright regions. Without HDR, fire, sun, and bright lights all clip to pure white.

questions.question: What is ACES tone mapping?
options: A color space only by default behavior with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Academy Color Encoding System — industry-standard HDR→display transform with cinematic color response and desaturation of brights
A file format within the current scope
A GPU feature within the current scope under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Academy Color Encoding System — industry-standard HDR→display transform with cinematic color response and desaturation of brights
questions.explanation: ACES is the film industry standard. Maps HDR scene radiance to display colors with realistic highlight roll-off and color accuracy. Used in UE5 by default.

questions.question: What is the visible color gamut of sRGB vs Rec.2020?
options: sRGB: ~35% of visible colors (narrow). Rec.2020: ~75% (wide gamut). HDR displays use Rec.2020 or DCI-P3 (~50%).
Rec.2020 is smaller for typical use cases in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
sRGB is wider using standard engine approaches with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: sRGB: ~35% of visible colors (narrow). Rec.2020: ~75% (wide gamut). HDR displays use Rec.2020 or DCI-P3 (~50%).
questions.explanation: sRGB is adequate for SDR but clips saturated colors. DCI-P3 captures more reds/greens. Rec.2020 captures most visible colors. UE5 supports HDR output with wide gamut.

questions.question: What does UE's eye adaptation (auto-exposure) do?
options: Nothing under normal conditions across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Simulates human eye adaptation — brightens dark areas and darkens bright areas over time. Uses histogram of scene luminance.
Film grain within the current scope under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Simulates human eye adaptation — brightens dark areas and darkens bright areas over time. Uses histogram of scene luminance.
questions.explanation: Eye adaptation: measure scene average luminance, compute exposure value, interpolate over time (~2-5 second adaptation). Moving from dark cave to bright outdoors → smooth brightness transition.

questions.question: What is a signed distance field?
options: A function f(x) returning the distance to the nearest surface, with negative values inside the object
A rendering technique only by default behavior in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A type of shader under normal conditions with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A function f(x) returning the distance to the nearest surface, with negative values inside the object
questions.explanation: SDF: f(p) < 0 inside, = 0 on surface, > 0 outside. |f(p)| = distance to nearest surface. Enables ray marching, smooth blending, and CSG operations.

questions.question: How does SDF ray marching work?
options: Step by fixed amount within the current scope with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
March along ray by the SDF value at current point — guaranteed not to overshoot, adapts step size
Binary search via the core framework
Random steps for typical use cases with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: March along ray by the SDF value at current point — guaranteed not to overshoot, adapts step size
questions.explanation: SDF ray marching: step distance = f(point). Since f is the minimum distance to any surface, stepping by f guarantees you won't cross a surface. Converges fast near surfaces.

questions.question: What is smooth min (smin) used for in SDF modeling?
options: Nothing as defined in the specification in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Blends two SDFs smoothly — creates organic connection between shapes without sharp edges
Boolean operations by default behavior with additional processing with typical engine set
questions.correctAnswer: Blends two SDFs smoothly — creates organic connection between shapes without sharp edges
