questions.explanation: smin(a, b, k) = -log(exp(-k·a) + exp(-k·b))/k. Blends the minimum of two SDFs. k controls blend radius. Creates metaball-like merging.

questions.question: What is the SDF representation of a sphere?
options: A texture under normal conditions in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
f(p) = distance(p, center) - radius. Negative inside, zero on surface, positive outside.
A mesh for typical use cases
A function call by default behavior with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: f(p) = distance(p, center) - radius. Negative inside, zero on surface, positive outside.
questions.explanation: Sphere SDF: |p - C| - R. Box SDF: max(|p-C| - 0.5*Size). Simple primitives combine via min (union), max (intersection), -f (subtract) to build complex shapes.

questions.question: How does UE use distance fields for GPU particles?
options: They don't as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Distance field collision: particles query the global distance field for collision response — cheaper than per-particle mesh collision
For rendering only under normal conditions with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Distance field collision: particles query the global distance field for collision response — cheaper than per-particle mesh collision
questions.explanation: UE generates a Global Distance Field for static geometry. GPU particles sample the distance field to collide with the world. Much cheaper than mesh-vs-mesh collision for thousands of particles.

questions.question: What is FVector in Unreal Engine?
options: A string type under normal conditions
A 3D vector (float X, Y, Z) — position, direction, velocity. Core math type.
A rotation type through the built-in API under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A color type within the current scope with additional processing across common plat
questions.correctAnswer: A 3D vector (float X, Y, Z) — position, direction, velocity. Core math type.
questions.explanation: FVector is a 12-byte struct (3 floats). Used everywhere: Actor location, velocity, surface normals, direction vectors. Equivalent to glm::vec3 or XMFLOAT3.

questions.question: What does FRotator store?
options: A quaternion via the core framework
Euler angles (Pitch, Yaw, Roll) in degrees — intuitive but suffers from gimbal lock
A matrix across all supported platforms in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A vector using standard engine approaches with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Euler angles (Pitch, Yaw, Roll) in degrees — intuitive but suffers from gimbal lock
questions.explanation: FRotator = (Pitch, Yaw, Roll) in degrees. Used in Blueprints and editor for human readability. Internally converted to FQuat for rotation math. Avoid for interpolation.

questions.question: Why use FQuat instead of FRotator for rotation?
options: FRotator is deprecated by default behavior
FQuat avoids gimbal lock, enables smooth interpolation (SLERP), and composes rotations correctly
FQuat is smaller as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
FQuat is faster to render by default behavior across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: FQuat avoids gimbal lock, enables smooth interpolation (SLERP), and composes rotations correctly
questions.explanation: FQuat = 4 floats (x,y,z,w). No gimbal lock. SLERP provides constant angular velocity. FRotator is for human input; FQuat is for math.

questions.question: What does FVector::DotProduct compute?
options: Cross product through the built-in API across common platformsxxxx
|A||B|cos(θ) — projection of A onto B. Zero when perpendicular.
Distance in most implementations with additional processing
Angle in degrees by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: |A||B|cos(θ) — projection of A onto B. Zero when perpendicular.
questions.explanation: A·B > 0: acute angle. = 0: perpendicular. < 0: obtuse. Used for: view direction test (DotProduct(Forward, ToTarget)), lighting (N·L).

questions.question: What does FVector::Normalize() do?
options: Makes the vector zero as defined in the specification within the standard frameworkx
Scales the vector to unit length (magnitude = 1) while preserving direction
Rotates the vector for typical use cases
Projects onto another vector via the core framework within the standard frameworkx
questions.correctAnswer: Scales the vector to unit length (magnitude = 1) while preserving direction
questions.explanation: Normalize() returns vector/length. FVector::GetSafeNormal() returns unit vector or zero vector if input is degenerate. Normalization is not free — sqrt is expensive.

questions.question: What is the quaternion double-cover problem?
options: Memory overhead in most implementations across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Q and -Q represent the SAME rotation. Before SLERP, check dot product — if negative, negate one to take the shorter arc on the hypersphere.
Speed issue through the built-in API across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Q and -Q represent the SAME rotation. Before SLERP, check dot product — if negative, negate one to take the shorter arc on the hypersphere.
questions.explanation: Quaternions double-cover SO(3). q and -q produce identical 3D rotations. Without correction, SLERP might interpolate the long way around (359° rotation instead of 1°).

questions.question: What does FTransform store?
options: Rotation (FQuat), Translation (FVector), and Scale3D (FVector) — the complete rigid-body transform
Only position within the current scope in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A 4x4 matrix directly in most implementations within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Rotation (FQuat), Translation (FVector), and Scale3D (FVector) — the complete rigid-body transform
questions.explanation: FTransform decomposes transforms: Rotation (quaternion, no gimbal lock), Translation (vector), Scale (vector). More efficient than storing a full 4x4 matrix.

questions.question: How is a 4x4 matrix constructed from an FTransform?
options: Scale matrix · Rotation matrix · Translation matrix — composed in that order (SRT)
Rotation only across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Direct component storage using standard engine approaches within the standard framew
questions.correctAnswer: Scale matrix · Rotation matrix · Translation matrix — composed in that order (SRT)
questions.explanation: SRT order: Scale → Rotate → Translate. The quaternion converts to 3x3 rotation, embedded in 4x4 with translation. ToMatrixWithScale() produces the final transform.

questions.question: What is the relationship between FTransform and FMatrix in UE?
options: FTransform is the decomposed storage; FMatrix is the 4x4 computation-ready form. Convert with ToMatrixWithScale()
They're the same across all supported platforms with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
FTransform is deprecated within the current scope with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: FTransform is the decomposed storage; FMatrix is the 4x4 computation-ready form. Convert with ToMatrixWithScale()
questions.explanation: FTransform for storage (no redundancy, no shear). FMatrix for GPU (shaders need 4x4). Convert at CPU→GPU boundary.

questions.question: What is TransformPosition vs TransformVector?
options: TransformPosition applies translation (for points). TransformVector ignores translation (for directions/normals). Both apply rotation and scale.
Position is faster through the built-in API with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Vector is deprecated as defined in the specification within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: TransformPosition applies translation (for points). TransformVector ignores translation (for directions/normals). Both apply rotation and scale.
questions.explanation: FTransform::TransformPosition(Pos): T * R * S * Pos (includes translation). TransformVector(Vec): R * S * Vec (no translation — vectors can't be translated, they're directions).

questions.question: What does FTransform::GetRelativeTransform do?
options: Nothing under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Converts a world-space transform to be relative to another transform — GetRelativeTransform(Parent, Child) = Parent⁻¹ * Child
Scales within the current scope with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Converts a world-space transform to be relative to another transform — GetRelativeTransform(Parent, Child) = Parent⁻¹ * Child
questions.explanation: Relative transform: transform child into parent's local space. Parent.Inverse() * Child. Used for: getting a weapon's transform relative to the character's hand.

questions.question: Why is FTransform preferred over FMatrix for object transforms?
options: It's faster to render in most implementations with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
FTransform enforces valid decomposition (no shear, uniform scale or separate axes). FMatrix can accumulate numerical error and non-rigid deformations.
FMatrix is deprecated under normal conditions
FTransform uses less memory in most implementations in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: FTransform enforces valid decomposition (no shear, uniform scale or separate axes). FMatrix can accumulate numerical error and non-rigid deformations.
questions.explanation: FTransform stores (Rotation, Translation, Scale) separately. Re-normalize quaternion to prevent drift. FMatrix is general 4×4 — error accumulates in all 16 entries, can introduce skew/shear.

questions.question: What does FMath::Lerp do?
options: Linear interpolation: A + (B-A)·Alpha. Alpha=0 → A; Alpha=1 → B.
Clamps as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxx
Normalizes across all supported platforms for common use casesxx
questions.correctAnswer: Linear interpolation: A + (B-A)·Alpha. Alpha=0 → A; Alpha=1 → B.
questions.explanation: Lerp(A, B, Alpha) blends between A and B. Unclamped: Alpha outside [0,1] extrapolates. Used everywhere: animation blending, camera smoothing, color transitions.

questions.question: What does FMath::Clamp do?
options: Interpolates across all supported platforms in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Constrains a value between Min and Max: if X < Min → Min; if X > Max → Max; else → X
Averages within the current scope with additional processing under normal operationxxxxx
Finds the maximum within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: Constrains a value between Min and Max: if X < Min → Min; if X > Max → Max; else → X
questions.explanation: Clamp(X, Min, Max) ensures X ∈ [Min, Max]. Used for player input bounds, camera pitch limits, health bar percentages.

questions.question: What does FMath::Sin/Cos use — radians or degrees?
options: Radians — all UE math uses radians internally
Both — configurable by default behavior with t
Neither within the current scope with regular us
questions.correctAnswer: Radians — all UE math uses radians internally
questions.explanation: UE's FMath trig functions take radians. FMath::DegreesToRadians() and RadiansToDegrees() convert. FRotator stores degrees for editor display only.

questions.question: What does FMath::FInterpConstantTo do?
options: Constant-speed movement toward target — moves by fixed amount per second, stops at target
Easing under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Rotation using standard engine approaches under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Constant-speed movement toward target — moves by fixed amount per second, stops at target
questions.explanation: FInterpConstantTo(Current, Target, DeltaTime, Speed) moves toward target at constant rate. Unlike FInterpTo (exponential), this moves linearly and stops exactly at target.

questions.question: What does FMath::FindDeltaAngle return?
options: An absolute angle for typical use cases in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
The shortest signed angular distance between two angles in degrees — e.g., -5° vs 355° returns -10°
A quaternion through the built-in API with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: The shortest signed angular distance between two angles in degrees — e.g., -5° vs 355° returns -10°
questions.explanation: FindDeltaAngle(-5, 355) = -10 (degrees). Handles wrap-around at ±180°. Used for: smoothly rotating toward target, camera look-at interpolation.

questions.question: What is the closest point on a line segment to a point?
options: The midpoint via the core framework with additional processing
Project the point onto the line, clamp the parameter t to [0,1]
The endpoint under normal conditions in standard configurationsx
The origin for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Project the point onto the line, clamp the parameter t to [0,1]
questions.explanation: t = dot(P-A, B-A) / dot(B-A, B-A). Clamp t to [0,1]. Closest point = A + t·(B-A). For t<0 → A; for t>1 → B; otherwise the projection.

questions.question: What geometric operation does the dot product enable for collision?
options: Area calculation across all supported platforms in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Projecting one vector onto another — used for closest point, separation axis tests, and distance to plane
Volume calculation under normal conditions within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Projecting one vector onto another — used for closest point, separation axis tests, and distance to plane
questions.explanation: Dot product measures how parallel two vectors are. a·b = |a||b|cos(θ). Essential for: point-to-line projection, plane distance, SAT overlap testing.

questions.question: What is the difference between a ray cast and a sweep in UE?
options: Same by default behavior with additional processing
Ray cast: infinitely thin line query. Sweep: moves a shape (sphere, box, capsule) along a path, detecting overlaps. Sweep is more expensive but catches thin geometry.
Sweep is faster by default behavior
Ray cast is deprecated under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: Ray cast: infinitely thin line query. Sweep: moves a shape (sphere, box, capsule) along a path, detecting overlaps. Sweep is more expensive but catches thin geometry.
questions.explanation: LineTraceSingle = ray cast (thin). SweepSingleByChannel = sweep with shape. Sweep prevents tunneling through small gaps. Use ray for bullets/projectiles; use sweep for character movement.

questions.question: What does FMath::LineBoxIntersection compute?
options: Box volume through the built-in API under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Whether a line segment intersects an axis-aligned box — slab method: compute t_entry/t_exit per axis, check overlap
Box surface area using standard engine approaches in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Whether a line segment intersects an axis-aligned box — slab method: compute t_entry/t_exit per axis, check overlap
questions.explanation: Slab method: for each axis, compute t_near=(min-O)/D, t_far=(max-O)/D. t_enter = max(t_nears), t_exit = min(t_fars). Hit if t_enter ≤ t_exit. Handles parallel rays (D=0).

questions.question: What is a convex hull used for in collision detection?
options: Rendering via the core framework with additional processing in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Approximating complex shapes with a tight-fitting convex polyhedron — GJK/EPA can collide any convex shape efficiently
Texture mapping as defined in the specification for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Approximating complex shapes with a tight-fitting convex polyhedron — GJK/EPA can collide any convex shape efficiently
questions.explanation: Convex hull = shrink-wrap around mesh vertices. Simplifies collision geometry while maintaining tight bounds. GJK algorithm handles any convex shape. Generated offline or at runtime.

questions.question: What is FRichCurve in UE?
options: A single keyframe under normal conditions in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A cubic Bezier curve with key-value pairs and configurable tangents — used for animation, timeline, and material curves
A random number generator across all supported platforms with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A color curve in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: A cubic Bezier curve with key-value pairs and configurable tangents — used for animation, timeline, and material curves
questions.explanation: FRichCurve stores keyframes with Arrive/Leave tangents. Evaluates via cubic Hermite interpolation between keys. Used in UCurveFloat, TimelineComponent, material instances.

questions.question: What does FMath::RandRange do?
options: Returns a random float in [Min, Max]
Generates a random vector in standard confi
questions.correctAnswer: Returns a random float in [Min, Max]
questions.explanation: FMath::RandRange(Min, Max) uses FRandomStream internally. RandRange with ints → integer in [Min, Max]. Seed with FRandomStream for deterministic randomness.

questions.question: What is an FRuntimeFloatCurve?
options: A compile-time curve as defined in the specification under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
An optimized curve structure for runtime evaluation — stores pre-baked lookup from FRichCurve for fast access without keyframe iteration
A Blueprint curve in most implementations
A material curve via the core framework with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: An optimized curve structure for runtime evaluation — stores pre-baked lookup from FRichCurve for fast access without keyframe iteration
questions.explanation: FRichCurve = editor-friendly with keyframes and tangents. FRuntimeFloatCurve = bakes to uniform samples for O(1) runtime evaluation. Used for gameplay curves that need fast evaluation.

questions.question: What does FRandomStream provide that FMath::Rand doesn't?
options: Deterministic, seedable random sequence — same seed always produces the same sequence. Essential for procedural generation that needs to be reproducible.
Crypto-safe random using standard engine approaches
questions.correctAnswer: Deterministic, seedable random sequence — same seed always produces the same sequence. Essential for procedural generation that needs to be reproducible.
questions.explanation: FRandomStream(Seed) creates a reproducible RNG. FMath::Rand() uses a global RNG — non-deterministic between runs. Use FRandomStream for procedural content that must be consistent across sessions.

questions.question: What is FMath::SRand() for?
options: Random floats only as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Seeds the global RNG — sets the seed for subsequent FMath::Rand() calls. Useful for testing with reproducible randomness.
String randomization within the current scope within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Seeds the global RNG — sets the seed for subsequent FMath::Rand() calls. Useful for testing with reproducible randomness.
questions.explanation: SRand(Seed) seeds UE's global RNG. All subsequent FMath::Rand() calls produce deterministic sequence for that seed. Use for: test reproducibility, network-synced random events.

questions.question: How does Cast<T> achieve O(1) without RTTI?
options: UHT generates StaticClass() with TCastFlags<T>. Cast compares flags as integer AND — no vtable walk.
dynamic_cast in most implementations in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
reinterpret_cast by default behavior under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: UHT generates StaticClass() with TCastFlags<T>. Cast compares flags as integer AND — no vtable walk.
questions.explanation: Each UClass has a bitmask of cast flags (EClassCastFlags). Cast<T> checks GetClass()->HasAnyCastFlag(TCastFlags<T>::value). Single integer operation.

questions.question: What is CastChecked?
options: A slower Cast through the built-in API
Asserts/crashes if the cast fails — use when the type MUST be correct
Returns nullptr within the current scope in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxx
A deprecated function using standard engine approaches within the standar
questions.correctAnswer: Asserts/crashes if the cast fails — use when the type MUST be correct
questions.explanation: CastChecked<T>(Obj) = 'I know Obj is T'. Crashes on failure (better than silent nullptr bug). Use in controlled paths where failure = logic error.

questions.question: What does DECLARE_CASTCLASS_FLAGS do?
options: Nothing visible as defined in the specification
Generates a compile-time bitmask for fast Cast<T> checks — one bit per class in the hierarchy
Marks a class as abstract for typical use cases under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Creates a Blueprint class using standard engine approaches with additional processing in most practi
questions.correctAnswer: Generates a compile-time bitmask for fast Cast<T> checks — one bit per class in the hierarchy
questions.explanation: DECLARE_CASTCLASS_FLAGS(T) creates TCastFlags_V<T> for O(1) cast checking. Used by Cast<T>(). Without it, Cast falls back to IsA<T>() which is slower.

questions.question: What does DECLARE_CASTCLASS_FLAGS do?
options: Generates a compile-time bitmask (TCastFlags_V<T>) for O(1) type checking per class
Creates a Blueprint class via the core framework in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxx
Marks a class as abstract by default behavior in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Generates a compile-time bitmask (TCastFlags_V<T>) for O(1) type checking per class
questions.explanation: Each UCLASS gets a unique bit in the cast flags mask. Cast<T> checks if the object's class has that bit set — one integer AND operation.

questions.question: What happens if you use Cast<T> on an object pending kill?
options: Returns nullptr by default behavior with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Still succeeds — pending kill objects retain class flags but IsValid() returns false. Always check IsValid after Cast.
Returns the object across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Still succeeds — pending kill objects retain class flags but IsValid() returns false. Always check IsValid after Cast.
questions.explanation: Pending kill objects pass IsA()/Cast() but are about to be destroyed. Use IsValid(Obj) which checks both nullptr and pending kill state.

questions.question: How does Cast<T> compare to dynamic_cast in terms of performance?
options: Same speed for typical use cases
Cast<T> is ~10x faster — one integer AND vs dynamic_cast's vtable walk + type_info comparison
Cast<T> is slower across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
dynamic_cast doesn't exist in UE under normal conditions under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Cast<T> is ~10x faster — one integer AND vs dynamic_cast's vtable walk + type_info comparison
questions.explanation: dynamic_cast walks the class hierarchy and compares type_info objects. UE's Cast<T> uses static class flags generated at compile time — a single integer bitmask check.

questions.question: When should you use Cast<T> vs IsA<T>?
options: No difference using standard engine approaches
Cast<T>: do something WITH the result (calls methods). IsA<T>: just checking (bool, no dereference).
Cast is always better by default behavior with additional processing for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
IsA is deprecated across all supported platforms in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Cast<T>: do something WITH the result (calls methods). IsA<T>: just checking (bool, no dereference).
questions.explanation: Cast<T> returns T* or nullptr — use when you need to call T methods. IsA<T>() returns bool — use for filtering (e.g., 'if (Actor->IsA<AEnemy>())'). IsA is slightly faster.

questions.question: What is the #1 casting pitfall in UE?
options: Slow performance through the built-in API with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Forgetting to check for nullptr after Cast<T> — accessing null pointer → crash
Memory leak as defined in the specification within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Wrong type cast for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Forgetting to check for nullptr after Cast<T> — accessing null pointer → crash
questions.explanation: Cast<T> returns nullptr on failure. Pattern: 'if (auto* Actor = Cast<AActor>(Obj)) { Actor->DoThing(); }'. Always null-check. Use CastChecked when failure is impossible.

questions.question: Why not use dynamic_cast in UE?
options: It works fine using standard engine approaches with additional processing
UE disables RTTI — dynamic_cast doesn't exist. Use Cast<T> instead.
It's slower only by default behavior
It's deprecated in C++20 for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: UE disables RTTI — dynamic_cast doesn't exist. Use Cast<T> instead.
questions.explanation: UE builds with /GR- (no RTTI). dynamic_cast, typeid don't exist. Cast<T> uses static class flags generated by UHT — faster and works without RTTI.

questions.question: Why should you avoid Cast<T> in performance-critical loops?
options: It crashes in loops by default behavior
Even 5-cycle Cast overhead adds up on thousands of iterations. Cast once and cache the typed pointer.
It leaks memory through the built-in API in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
It's not thread-safe as defined in the specification with additional processing with the default setupx
questions.correctAnswer: Even 5-cycle Cast overhead adds up on thousands of iterations. Cast once and cache the typed pointer.
questions.explanation: Pattern: TArray<AActor*> AllActors; for (auto* A : AllActors) { if (auto* PC = Cast<APlayerController>(A)) Process(PC); }. Better: prefilter into TArray<APlayerController*>.

questions.question: What is the difference between Interface Cast and regular Cast?
options: Same operation within the current scope
Interface Cast uses GetInterfaceAddress() to handle the UObject-to-interface pointer offset. Regular Cast checks class hierarchy.
Interface Cast is slower under normal conditions for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Regular Cast fails for interfaces within the current scope in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Interface Cast uses GetInterfaceAddress() to handle the UObject-to-interface pointer offset. Regular Cast checks class hierarchy.
questions.explanation: Interfaces in UE don't participate in the class hierarchy the same way. Cast<IInterface> uses GetInterfaceAddress() to find the correct vtable offset.

questions.question: When does Cast<T> fail silently vs crash?
options: Never crashes via the core framework
Cast returns nullptr on failure. CastChecked asserts on failure. Use CastChecked when the type MUST be correct and nullptr indicates a logic bug.
Always crashes within the current scope in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Returns a default object via the core framework across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Cast returns nullptr on failure. CastChecked asserts on failure. Use CastChecked when the type MUST be correct and nullptr indicates a logic bug.
questions.explanation: Cast<T> → nullptr (graceful). CastChecked<T> → assert crash (fail-fast). Choose based on whether the cast should ALWAYS succeed.

questions.question: What data structure does TSet use?
options: Red-black tree as defined in the specification
Hash table — same as TMap but keys only, no values. O(1) average lookup.
Sorted array using standard engine approaches with additional processing
Linked list within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: Hash table — same as TMap but keys only, no values. O(1) average lookup.
questions.explanation: TSet = TMap<Key, void> internally. Uses sparse array with chaining. Elements must have GetTypeHash(). No duplicates allowed. Like std::unordered_set.

questions.question: What does TSet::Contains() return?
options: The element within the current scope
true if the element exists in the set
An index across all supported platforms
An iterator via the core framework with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: true if the element exists in the set
questions.explanation: Contains(Key) returns bool. Use Find(Key) to get a pointer to the stored element (useful for custom hash/equality types).

questions.question: How do you iterate over a TSet?
options: Index-based only for typical use cases
Range-for or iterator-based — TSet supports begin()/end(). Order is arbitrary (hash order).
You can't iterate for typical use cases with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Only via TMap within the current scope with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Range-for or iterator-based — TSet supports begin()/end(). Order is arbitrary (hash order).
questions.explanation: TSet is a range: 'for (auto& Item : MySet)'. Order is implementation-defined (hash bucket order). Use TSortedSet or sort separately if order matters.

questions.question: What is the difference between TSet and TArray for duplicate prevention?
options: TSet enforces uniqueness inherently — inserting a duplicate has no effect. TArray requires manual checking. TSet::Add returns index of existing element if duplicate.
TArray is better via the core framework
TSet is deprecated in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: TSet enforces uniqueness inherently — inserting a duplicate has no effect. TArray requires manual checking. TSet::Add returns index of existing element if duplicate.
questions.explanation: TSet::Add returns FSetElementId (index) and bool (whether new element was added). Use TSet for: unique player IDs, processed item tracking, any 'have I seen this before' check.

questions.question: How does TSet handle iteration order?
options: Sorted order for typical use cases
Hash bucket order — elements appear in arbitrary order based on hash values. Use TSortedSet or sort separately for ordered iteration.
Insertion order via the core framework for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Random order via the core framework with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Hash bucket order — elements appear in arbitrary order based on hash values. Use TSortedSet or sort separately for ordered iteration.
questions.explanation: TSet iteration order is implementation-defined (sparse array hash bucket order). NOT insertion order, NOT sorted. If you need predictable order, use TArray or collect and sort.

questions.question: What is UENUM() used for?
options: Exposes a C++ enum to Blueprint, reflection, and the editor — UHT generates metadata
Marking a UCLASS as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Creating a struct in most implementations with additional processing with the default
questions.correctAnswer: Exposes a C++ enum to Blueprint, reflection, and the editor — UHT generates metadata
questions.explanation: UENUM() marks an enum for UE reflection. Enables: Blueprint enum variables, editor dropdowns, replication, serialization. Without it, the enum is C++ only.

questions.question: What is the ENUM_CLASS_FLAGS macro for?
options: A comment in most implementations with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxx
Enables bitwise operations (|, &, ^) on enum class values — creates a strongly-typed bitfield
Marks a UCLASS in most implementations
Creates a Blueprint enum by default behavior in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Enables bitwise operations (|, &, ^) on enum class values — creates a strongly-typed bitfield
questions.explanation: ENUM_CLASS_FLAGS(EMyFlags) overloads | & ^ for the enum class type. Used for: collision channels, gameplay tags, permission flags. Compile-time type-safe.

questions.question: What does UENUM(BlueprintType) do?
options: Makes an enum available for Blueprint variables, function parameters, and return types
Marks a class as abstract across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxx
Creates a C++ enum for typical use cases with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Makes an enum available for Blueprint variables, function parameters, and return types
questions.explanation: BlueprintType specifier on UENUM exposes the enum to the Blueprint editor. Without it, the enum exists in C++ only. With it, designers can use it in Blueprint graphs.

questions.question: What does EnumHasAnyFlags do?
options: Toggles a flag under normal conditions with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Checks if a flag value has ANY of the specified bits set — used for multi-select flags like collision responses
Creates a new flag by default behavior
Converts to string by default behavior under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Checks if a flag value has ANY of the specified bits set — used for multi-select flags like collision responses
questions.explanation: EnumHasAnyFlags(Value, Flag) = (Value & Flag) != 0. EnumHasAllFlags checks ALL bits. Used for: collision channel queries, gameplay tag containers, permission checks.

questions.question: Why use ENUM_CLASS_FLAGS instead of just int?
options: It's faster through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Type safety — the compiler prevents accidentally mixing flag types or passing integers where a specific flag type is expected. Self-documenting.
It's required under normal conditions for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Type safety — the compiler prevents accidentally mixing flag types or passing integers where a specific flag type is expected. Self-documenting.
questions.explanation: ENUM_CLASS_FLAGS generates operator|, &, ^ for the specific enum type. You can't accidentally pass ECollisionChannelFlags to a function expecting EObjectFlags. Compile-time checking.

questions.question: Why does UE use T-prefixed container templates instead of STL?
options: Style only across all supported platforms for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Custom allocator integration, reflection support, GC awareness, and console platform optimizations not available in STL
STL is slower in most implementations
STL doesn't compile on consoles as defined in the specification under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Custom allocator integration, reflection support, GC awareness, and console platform optimizations not available in STL
questions.explanation: TArray, TMap, TSet integrate with UE's allocator system (FMemory), support UPROPERTY serialization, and work with UE's reflection system. STL containers don't.

questions.question: What is the key difference between TArray and std::vector?
options: No difference as defined in the specification
TArray supports UPROPERTY serialization, replication, and Blueprint access. Similar performance, different API.
TArray is faster always by default behavior with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
TArray is on the heap only for typical use cases across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: TArray supports UPROPERTY serialization, replication, and Blueprint access. Similar performance, different API.
questions.explanation: Both are dynamic arrays with exponential growth. TArray adds: UPROPERTY (GC/replication/BP), Slack parameter on growth, and Emplace instead of emplace_back.

questions.question: What is the naming convention for UE template types?
options: T prefix — TArray, TMap, TSet, TSharedPtr, TUniquePtr, TWeakObjectPtr
U prefix across all supported platforms with additional processing
A prefix within the current scope with typical engine settingsxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: T prefix — TArray, TMap, TSet, TSharedPtr, TUniquePtr, TWeakObjectPtr
questions.explanation: UE naming: T = template container. F = regular struct. U = UObject. A = AActor. I = interface. E = enum. S = Slate widget. Consistent naming makes code self-documenting.

questions.question: Can TArray be used with garbage-collected UObject pointers?
options: Yes but raw TArray<UObject*> doesn't prevent GC. Use TArray<TObjectPtr<UObject>> or UPROPERTY on the array.
Only in Blueprints using standard engine approaches with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Only TMap can using standard engine approaches in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Yes but raw TArray<UObject*> doesn't prevent GC. Use TArray<TObjectPtr<UObject>> or UPROPERTY on the array.
questions.explanation: UE GC doesn't traverse raw TArray members. If the array isn't UPROPERTY(), the UObjects inside can be garbage collected → dangling pointers. Use TObjectPtr or UPROPERTY().

questions.question: What is TArrayView?
options: A copy of the array using standard engine approaches with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A non-owning view into a subset of a TArray — pointer + count. Like std::span. Zero allocation, zero copy.
A heap-allocated array through the built-in API with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A sorted array via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: A non-owning view into a subset of a TArray — pointer + count. Like std::span. Zero allocation, zero copy.
questions.explanation: TArrayView<T> = lightweight borrowed reference. Pass array slices to functions without copying. Cannot outlive the source array. Similar to std::span<T> in C++20.

questions.question: What does GAS stand for in Unreal Engine?
options: General Animation System using standard engine approaches across common platformsx
Gameplay Ability System — UE's framework for abilities, attributes, and effects
Global Asset System in most implementations
Game Audio System as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Gameplay Ability System — UE's framework for abilities, attributes, and effects
questions.explanation: GAS provides: UGameplayAbility (abilities with cooldowns/costs), UAttributeSet (replicated stats), and UGameplayEffect (buffs/debuffs). Server-authoritative with client prediction.

questions.question: How does GAS handle client-side prediction of abilities?
options: It doesn't predict via the core framework with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
The client activates the ability locally for instant feedback; the server validates and corrects. Similar to movement prediction.
Always server-only for typical use cases
Via rollback netcode via the core framework with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: The client activates the ability locally for instant feedback; the server validates and corrects. Similar to movement prediction.
questions.explanation: Client predicts ability activation (animation, effects). Server validates (checks cooldowns, costs) and either confirms or corrects. Replication reconciles state on misprediction.

questions.question: What is a GameplayTag used for in GAS?
options: A display name for typical use cases
Hierarchical tags for categorizing abilities, effects, and blocking interactions (e.g., 'Ability.Magic.Fire')
A player name through the built-in API with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A debugging label under normal conditions in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Hierarchical tags for categorizing abilities, effects, and blocking interactions (e.g., 'Ability.Magic.Fire')
questions.explanation: FGameplayTag = hierarchical string identifier. Used for: ability tags (what an ability IS), cancel tags (what cancels it), block tags (what prevents it). O(1) comparison via FName index.

questions.question: What is a GameplayEffect in GAS?
options: A visual effect via the core framework in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A data-driven modifier that changes attributes over time — instant, duration, or infinite. Handles stacking, prediction, and replication.
A sound effect using standard engine approaches within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A data-driven modifier that changes attributes over time — instant, duration, or infinite. Handles stacking, prediction, and replication.
questions.explanation: GameplayEffect: Instant (one-shot damage), Duration (temporary buff), Infinite (permanent until removed). Modifies UAttributeSet values with modifiers, executes GameplayCues for visual feedback.

questions.question: What is an AbilityTask in GAS?
options: A performance monitor as defined in the specification in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
An async task that runs during ability execution — e.g., PlayMontageAndWait, WaitTargetData. Ticks independently and completes asynchronously.
A cooldown timer in most implementations for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A debugger through the built-in API
questions.correctAnswer: An async task that runs during ability execution — e.g., PlayMontageAndWait, WaitTargetData. Ticks independently and completes asynchronously.
questions.explanation: AbilityTask = async operation within an ability. WaitTargetData: wait for player to select target. PlayMontageAndWait: play animation, complete when animation finishes. Tick while ability is active.

questions.question: How does GAS replicate GameplayTags?
options: They don't replicate using standard engine approaches with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
GameplayTags are replicated as FNames (interned string indices) — O(1) comparison and minimal bandwidth.
As strings as defined in the specification
Via RPC by default behavior with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: GameplayTags are replicated as FNames (interned string indices) — O(1) comparison and minimal bandwidth.
questions.explanation: FGameplayTag stores an FName (4-byte index into global name table). Replication sends the index, not the string. Efficient bandwidth usage for tag-heavy GAS networking.

questions.question: What is the Iris replication system in UE5?
options: A rendering system as defined in the specification
Next-gen replication: replaces legacy Actor channel replication with filtered, prioritized, bandwidth-managed data delivery
A networking protocol across all supported platforms across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
An AI system by default behavior with additional processing with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Next-gen replication: replaces legacy Actor channel replication with filtered, prioritized, bandwidth-managed data delivery
questions.explanation: Iris is UE5's new replication system. Features: data stream filtering (only send what's relevant), dynamic prioritization (important objects get more bandwidth), bandwidth management (fair sharing).

questions.question: What does Iris filtering do?
options: Determines which clients receive which replicated data — e.g., only nearby players get enemy position updates
Encrypts data through the built-in API in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Compresses data in most implementations in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Determines which clients receive which replicated data — e.g., only nearby players get enemy position updates
questions.explanation: Iris filters data per connection. Spatial filtering: only replicate nearby objects. Conditional filtering: only replicate when condition holds (e.g., team match). Reduces bandwidth.

questions.question: What does Iris prioritization do?
options: Dynamically assigns replication priority — important objects (player pawn) get more bandwidth than less important ones (ambient props)
Random selection through the built-in API with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Round-robin as defined in the specification with additional processing with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Dynamically assigns replication priority — important objects (player pawn) get more bandwidth than less important ones (ambient props)
questions.explanation: Prioritization: player pawn = highest priority (frequent updates). Distant enemies = lower priority (less frequent). Under bandwidth pressure, low-priority objects are throttled first.

questions.question: What is spatial filtering in Iris?
options: A rendering technique through the built-in API
Only replicating actors that are close enough to a connection — distance-based culling to save bandwidth
An audio filter through the built-in API with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A physics optimization under normal conditions for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Only replicating actors that are close enough to a connection — distance-based culling to save bandwidth
questions.explanation: Iris spatial filter: each connection has a relevant area. Actors outside this area are not replicated to that connection. Configurable cull distance and grid-based spatial queries.

questions.question: How does Iris prioritize which actors get bandwidth first?
options: FIFO order via the core framework with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Dynamic priority: more 'important' actors (player's own pawn, enemies in view) get higher priority and more frequent updates
Alphabetical via the core framework across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Dynamic priority: more 'important' actors (player's own pawn, enemies in view) get higher priority and more frequent updates
questions.explanation: Priority system: local pawn = highest priority, visible enemies = medium, distant ambient actors = low. Under bandwidth pressure, low-priority actors are throttled first.

questions.question: What is the key difference between Iris and legacy UE replication?
options: No difference via the core framework
Legacy: per-Actor channels (one per replicated Actor, O(actors)). Iris: data streams decoupled from Actor count — filters, prioritization, and bandwidth management are stream-level.
Iris uses TCP through the built-in API
Iris is deprecated across all supported platforms
questions.correctAnswer: Legacy: per-Actor channels (one per replicated Actor, O(actors)). Iris: data streams decoupled from Actor count — filters, prioritization, and bandwidth management are stream-level.
questions.explanation: Iris decouples replication from Actor lifecycle. Data streams aggregate replication data; filtering and prioritization happen per-stream, not per-actor. Scales to larger worlds.

questions.question: What does PushModel do in UE5 replication?
options: Nothing new within the current scope with additional processing with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Explicit dirty marking — you call MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY to tell the system a property changed, avoiding per-tick property comparison
A rendering technique via the core framework under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Explicit dirty marking — you call MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY to tell the system a property changed, avoiding per-tick property comparison
questions.explanation: Without PushModel, UE compares every replicated property every tick to detect changes. PushModel: you mark properties dirty explicitly. System only replicates changed properties. Huge bandwidth savings.

questions.question: How do you enable PushModel for a property?
options: It's automatic via the core framework with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Mark the UPROPERTY with Replicated and the class with PushModel; call MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME when the value changes
Add a macro in most implementations
Set a config flag only within the current scope across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Mark the UPROPERTY with Replicated and the class with PushModel; call MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME when the value changes
questions.explanation: Opt-in: set bPushModel = true on the class. Use MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME(ThisClass, PropertyName) when modifying. Only dirty properties are considered for replication.

questions.question: What is the performance benefit of PushModel?
options: No benefit as defined in the specification with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Avoids per-tick property comparison for all replicated properties — only dirty-marked properties are checked
Faster serialization through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
More bandwidth through the built-in API
questions.correctAnswer: Avoids per-tick property comparison for all replicated properties — only dirty-marked properties are checked
questions.explanation: Without PushModel: compare every replicated property every tick. With PushModel: only properties explicitly marked dirty via MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY are considered for replication.

questions.question: How do you mark a property as dirty with PushModel?
options: Call a global function within the current scope across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME(ThisClass, PropertyName) — type-safe macro using compile-time property name
Set a flag on the object in most implementations within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME(ThisClass, PropertyName) — type-safe macro using compile-time property name
questions.explanation: UE5 PushModel macro: MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME(UMyClass, Health). Uses compile-time name lookup for type safety. The property must be marked Replicated and the class must enable PushModel.

questions.question: What does bPushModel enable on a UCLASS?
options: Nothing under normal conditions with additional processing under normal operationxxxx
Opts the class into PushModel replication — required before MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY works
Enables Blueprint replication via the core framework
Makes the class networkable as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Opts the class into PushModel replication — required before MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY works
questions.explanation: Set bPushModel = true in the UCLASS declaration. Without this, even if individual properties have Replicated, PushModel dirty marking is ignored.

questions.question: What is the average time complexity of quicksort?
questions.correctAnswer: O(n log n)
questions.explanation: Quicksort: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst case (already sorted with bad pivot). Introsort (std::sort) uses quicksort with heapsort fallback to guarantee O(n log n) worst case.

questions.question: When is counting sort better than comparison-based sorts?
options: Always as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
When the range of values is small and known — O(n+k) beats O(n log n) for small k
Only for strings within the current scope in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: When the range of values is small and known — O(n+k) beats O(n log n) for small k
questions.explanation: Counting sort: O(n+k) where k = value range. No comparisons. For k << n log n, it's faster. Used for: sorting player scores (0-1000), pixel luminance, character frequencies.

questions.question: What sort does std::sort use internally?
options: Bubble sort within the current scope within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Introsort — begins with quicksort, switches to heapsort if recursion depth too high. Guaranteed O(n log n).
Merge sort for typical use cases
Insertion sort using standard engine approaches with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Introsort — begins with quicksort, switches to heapsort if recursion depth too high. Guaranteed O(n log n).
questions.explanation: Introsort = quicksort with depth limit. If partitioning repeatedly picks bad pivots (depth > 2*log₂n), switches to heapsort for the remaining subarray. Best of both worlds.

questions.question: What algorithm does std::stable_sort use?
options: Merge sort — requires O(n) extra memory but preserves element order for equal keys
Heapsort as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Insertion sort across all supported platforms with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Merge sort — requires O(n) extra memory but preserves element order for equal keys
questions.explanation: std::stable_sort guarantees stability: equal elements maintain relative order. Uses merge sort (O(n log n), O(n) extra memory). Use when secondary sort keys matter.

questions.question: When is insertion sort faster than O(n log n) algorithms?
options: Never as defined in the specification
On very small arrays (n<~16) — the constant factor is so small that it beats quicksort's overhead. std::sort switches to insertion sort for small subarrays.
Only for strings for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: On very small arrays (n<~16) — the constant factor is so small that it beats quicksort's overhead. std::sort switches to insertion sort for small subarrays.
questions.explanation: Insertion sort: O(n²) asymptotic but minimal overhead. For n<16, cache-friendly sequential writes beat quicksort's recursive overhead. Hybrid sorts use this.

questions.question: What problem does Dijkstra's algorithm solve?
options: Sorting as defined in the specification
Shortest path from a single source to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edges
Maximum flow by default behavior with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Minimum spanning tree in most implementations with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Shortest path from a single source to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edges
questions.explanation: Dijkstra: priority queue of unvisited nodes. O(E log V) with min-heap. Finds shortest paths. Greedy — always picks closest unvisited node. Fails with negative edge weights.

questions.question: What does BFS find on an unweighted graph?
options: Maximum flow within the current scope
Shortest path in terms of edge count — level-order traversal
Topological order through the built-in API for common use cases
Articulation points across all supported platforms in standard conf
questions.correctAnswer: Shortest path in terms of edge count — level-order traversal
questions.explanation: BFS = queue-based traversal. Visits nodes in increasing distance from source. Guarantees shortest path by edge count on unweighted graphs. O(V+E).

questions.question: What is a topological sort?
options: A sorting algorithm for numbers using standard engine approaches within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Linear ordering of DAG vertices where every edge goes left-to-right — used for build dependency ordering, animation blending graphs
A shortest path algorithm under normal conditions
A search algorithm under normal conditions with additional processing for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Linear ordering of DAG vertices where every edge goes left-to-right — used for build dependency ordering, animation blending graphs
questions.explanation: Topological sort: output order where all dependencies come before dependents. O(V+E) via DFS or Kahn's algorithm. Fails on cycles. Used for: build systems, animation node graphs.

questions.question: What is the minimum spanning tree problem?
options: Finding the longest path in most implementations with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Connecting all vertices with minimum total edge weight — Prim's (dense graphs) or Kruskal's (sparse graphs)
Finding all cycles under normal conditions
Finding articulation points across all supported platforms in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Connecting all vertices with minimum total edge weight — Prim's (dense graphs) or Kruskal's (sparse graphs)
questions.explanation: MST: connect all nodes with minimum total cost. Prim: O(E log V), good for dense. Kruskal: O(E log E), good for sparse. Applications: network layout, dungeon connectivity.

questions.question: What does Floyd-Warshall compute?
options: Single-source shortest path by default behavior under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
All-pairs shortest paths — O(V³) DP algorithm that works with negative edges (no negative cycles)
Minimum spanning tree through the built-in API
Topological order using standard engine approaches with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: All-pairs shortest paths — O(V³) DP algorithm that works with negative edges (no negative cycles)
questions.explanation: Floyd-Warshall: for each k, relax all (i,j) paths via k. dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]). O(V³) but simpler than V×Dijkstra for dense graphs.

questions.question: What is the core principle of dynamic programming?
options: Recursion without memoization using standard engine approaches with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxx
Break a problem into overlapping subproblems, solve each once, store results (memoization/tabulation), combine for final answer
Divide and conquer for typical use cases
Brute force as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Break a problem into overlapping subproblems, solve each once, store results (memoization/tabulation), combine for final answer
questions.explanation: DP = optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems. Two approaches: top-down (memoization: cache recursive results), bottom-up (tabulation: fill table iteratively). Converts exponential to polynomial.

questions.question: What classic DP problem is used in game AI pathfinding with limited resources?
options: Knapsack problem — maximize value with limited weight capacity. Used for: ability loadout selection, loot optimization with inventory weight limit.
Sorting across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Graph coloring for typical use cases for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Knapsack problem — maximize value with limited weight capacity. Used for: ability loadout selection, loot optimization with inventory weight limit.
questions.explanation: 0/1 Knapsack: each item taken or not. O(n·W) DP. Unbounded: unlimited copies. Used in games for character builds, resource allocation, procedural loot.

questions.question: What is memoization in DP?
options: A logging technique across all supported platforms in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Caching results of expensive function calls — if the same input recurs, return cached result instead of recomputing
A sorting method in most implementations
A graph algorithm in most implementations under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Caching results of expensive function calls — if the same input recurs, return cached result instead of recomputing
questions.explanation: Top-down DP: recursive function + hash map/array cache. Before computing f(n), check if result exists. If so, return cached. Converts exponential recursion to polynomial.

questions.question: What is the difference between memoization and tabulation?
options: No difference through the built-in API
Memoization: top-down (recursive + cache). Tabulation: bottom-up (iteratively fill table). Same complexity, different approach.
Memoization is faster across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Tabulation uses more memory across all supported platforms in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Memoization: top-down (recursive + cache). Tabulation: bottom-up (iteratively fill table). Same complexity, different approach.
questions.explanation: Memoization = lazy, only computes needed states. Tabulation = eager, computes all states in order. Tabulation avoids recursion overhead but may compute unnecessary states.

questions.question: What is the longest common subsequence problem?
options: Finding the longest substring for typical use cases with additional processing for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Finding the longest sequence that appears in both strings, not necessarily contiguous — O(nm) DP. Used for diff algorithms.
Finding all common characters through the built-in API with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A sorting problem for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Finding the longest sequence that appears in both strings, not necessarily contiguous — O(nm) DP. Used for diff algorithms.
questions.explanation: LCS: dp[i][j] = LCS of A[0..i] and B[0..j]. If A[i]==B[j]: 1+dp[i-1][j-1]. Else: max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Foundation of diff, merge, version control.

questions.question: What is amortized analysis?
options: Worst-case analysis via the core framework
Average cost per operation over a sequence — expensive operations are rare, averaging to cheap per-op cost
Best-case analysis across all supported platforms within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Constant analysis under normal conditions with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Average cost per operation over a sequence — expensive operations are rare, averaging to cheap per-op cost
questions.explanation: Example: dynamic array push. Single push may be O(n) (reallocation), but N pushes cost O(N) total → O(1) amortized per push. Used to prove efficiency of data structures.

questions.question: What does profiling on actual hardware reveal that Big O doesn't?
options: Cache misses, branch mispredictions, memory latency — real performance differs from asymptotic analysis. O(n log n) with 10× cache misses can be slower than O(n²) with perfect cache.
Algorithm correctness for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Cache misses, branch mispredictions, memory latency — real performance differs from asymptotic analysis. O(n log n) with 10× cache misses can be slower than O(n²) with perfect cache.
questions.explanation: Big O ignores constants. Reality: cache effects dominate at scale. Linear scan of sorted array (O(n)) often beats binary search (O(log n)) for small N due to prefetching. Profile, don't assume.

questions.question: What is the amortized cost of std::vector::push_back?
options: O(n) as defined in the specification
O(1) amortized — individual pushes may be O(n) (reallocation), but averaged over N pushes it's O(1)
O(log n) using standard engine approaches with additional processing with typical engine settingsxxxxxx
O(n²) as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: O(1) amortized — individual pushes may be O(n) (reallocation), but averaged over N pushes it's O(1)
questions.explanation: N pushes cost O(N) total (geometric series of reallocation costs). Amortized per push = O(1). Reallocation happens rarely (1/N frequency, O(N) cost each → O(1) per operation).

questions.question: Why does cache matter more than Big O in practice?
options: It doesn't through the built-in API
A linear scan of a contiguous array (O(n)) can beat a binary search (O(log n)) for small n because the linear scan streams through cache while binary search jumps randomly
Cache doesn't affect algorithms by default behavior
Big O always wins within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: A linear scan of a contiguous array (O(n)) can beat a binary search (O(log n)) for small n because the linear scan streams through cache while binary search jumps randomly
questions.explanation: Binary search: random memory access, 1 cache miss per probe. Linear scan: sequential access, hardware prefetcher loads ahead. For n<~256, linear scan often wins due to cache effects.

questions.question: What does perf (Linux) or VTune (Intel) measure that Big O analysis misses?
options: Algorithm correctness for typical use cases with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Cache misses, branch mispredictions, CPU cycles, IPC — real hardware counters revealing where time is actually spent
Memory usage via the core framework
Line count through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Cache misses, branch mispredictions, CPU cycles, IPC — real hardware counters revealing where time is actually spent
questions.explanation: Big O counts operations. Reality: an L3 cache miss costs ~200 cycles — enough to execute 400 instructions. A 'cheap' operation with a cache miss is 400× more expensive than Big O suggests.

questions.question: Why does TArray::Contains perform differently than std::find on vector?
options: Same performance using standard engine approaches in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
TArray stores a size counter and uses direct pointer access. std::find uses iterator abstraction. Both O(n) but TArray may have ~10-20% less overhead.
TArray is always faster via the core framework
std::find is always faster by default behavior with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: TArray stores a size counter and uses direct pointer access. std::find uses iterator abstraction. Both O(n) but TArray may have ~10-20% less overhead.
questions.explanation: Both linear scan. But TArray avoids iterator abstraction overhead. The real difference: TArray stores inline allocator pointer — better cache locality than separate std::vector allocator.

questions.question: What is the performance difference between TMap::Find and std::unordered_map::find?
options: Identical across all supported platforms with additional processing
Both O(1) hash lookup. TMap uses sparse array (better iteration/cache) vs std's node-based chaining (pointer chasing). TMap iteration is significantly faster.
TMap is slower across all supported platforms
std is always better for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Both O(1) hash lookup. TMap uses sparse array (better iteration/cache) vs std's node-based chaining (pointer chasing). TMap iteration is significantly faster.
questions.explanation: Both O(1) average. TMap: keys/values in contiguous sparse array → cache-friendly iteration. std::unordered_map: separately allocated nodes → pointer-chasing on iteration. TMap wins on traversal speed.

questions.question: Why is TArray iteration faster than std::vector iteration?
options: It's not for typical use cases
TArray stores elements inline with the allocator pointer, improving cache locality. std::vector stores allocator separately.
TArray uses SIMD through the built-in API with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
TArray is in a different memory region using standard engine approaches across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: TArray stores elements inline with the allocator pointer, improving cache locality. std::vector stores allocator separately.
questions.explanation: TArray layout: [Data*, Count, Max]. std::vector: [Begin*, End*, CapacityEnd*]. The TArray layout groups the control block with the element pointer — fewer indirections during iteration.

questions.question: What is the performance difference between TMap::Find and std::unordered_map::find?
options: Both O(1). TMap uses sparse array (contiguous storage) for cache-friendly iteration. std uses node-based chaining (pointer chasing). TMap iteration is ~2-3x faster.
TMap is slower across all supported platforms
std is always better through the built-in API with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: Both O(1). TMap uses sparse array (contiguous storage) for cache-friendly iteration. std uses node-based chaining (pointer chasing). TMap iteration is ~2-3x faster.
questions.explanation: Same asymptotic complexity. Different memory layout: TMap's sparse array stores hash, key, value contiguously → cache-friendly. std::unordered_map allocates separate nodes → random memory access on iteration.

questions.question: What pattern makes TSet the right choice over TArray for membership tests?
options: No pattern through the built-in API
When you repeatedly check 'is X in this collection' — TSet::Contains is O(1) vs TArray::Contains O(n). For large collections, TSet is dramatically faster.
When you need ordering by default behavior
For small collections only by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: When you repeatedly check 'is X in this collection' — TSet::Contains is O(1) vs TArray::Contains O(n). For large collections, TSet is dramatically faster.
questions.explanation: Use TSet for: unique element requirement, membership testing. TArray for: ordered data, indexed access. Hybrid: use TSet for existence check, TArray for ordered iteration.

questions.question: How does UE implement interfaces without multiple inheritance?
options: It doesn't use interfaces in most implementations
UINTERFACE paired with IInterface class — UHT generates message dispatch tables instead of virtual inheritance
Standard C++ virtual inheritance as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Diamond inheritance under normal conditions with additional processing in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: UINTERFACE paired with IInterface class — UHT generates message dispatch tables instead of virtual inheritance
questions.explanation: UE interfaces: UINTERFACE (metadata, never instantiated) + I prefixed class (actual C++ interface). UHT generates a mapping table for O(1) interface function lookup without virtual inheritance.

questions.question: What does GetInterfaceAddress do in UE casting?
options: Returns a string address under normal conditions in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Returns the C++ IInterface pointer from a UObject — handles the offset between UObject and interface vtable
Returns a network address through the built-in API in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Returns the C++ IInterface pointer from a UObject — handles the offset between UObject and interface vtable
questions.explanation: An object implementing an interface stores the interface vtable at a different offset from the UObject base. GetInterfaceAddress computes the correct pointer for interface method calls.

questions.question: How do you check if a UObject implements an interface?
options: Use dynamic_cast through the built-in API
Use obj->GetClass()->ImplementsInterface(UMyInterface::StaticClass()) or cast to the interface
Use instanceof across all supported platforms with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Try calling and catch using standard engine approaches with additional processing with typical engi
questions.correctAnswer: Use obj->GetClass()->ImplementsInterface(UMyInterface::StaticClass()) or cast to the interface
questions.explanation: ImplementsInterface returns bool (fast check via class flags). Or use IMyInterface* Iface = Cast<IMyInterface>(Obj); if (Iface) { Iface->InterfaceMethod(); }.

questions.question: Why can't you use C++ multiple inheritance for interfaces in UE?
options: Style using standard engine approaches in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
UE's reflection and GC system require a single UObject base — multiple virtual inheritance would break the GUObjectArray layout and GC traversal
Compiler limitations through the built-in API with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: UE's reflection and GC system require a single UObject base — multiple virtual inheritance would break the GUObjectArray layout and GC traversal
questions.explanation: UObject uses a fixed memory layout for GC and reflection. Virtual inheritance adds vtable pointers at unpredictable offsets. UE sidesteps this with message dispatch tables generated by UHT.

questions.question: What does Cast<T> do for interface types?
options: Fails always for typical use cases
Returns nullptr always for typical use cases with additional processing with regular usage patt
Returns the IInterface pointer — uses GetInterfaceAddress internally for UINTERFACE types
Converts to string under normal conditions under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Returns the IInterface pointer — uses GetInterfaceAddress internally for UINTERFACE types
questions.explanation: Cast<IInterfaceType> works differently from Cast<UObjectType>. For interfaces, it calls GetInterfaceAddress() to compute the correct pointer offset. UHT generates the dispatch logic.

questions.question: In the OSI model, which layer is responsible for routing packets between networks using IP addresses?
questions.correctAnswer: Network (Layer 3)
questions.explanation: The Network layer (Layer 3) handles logical addressing and routing. IP (Internet Protocol) operates at this layer, determining how packets travel from source to destination across multiple networks. The Data Link layer (Layer 2) uses MAC addresses for local delivery, not inter-network routing.

questions.question: What is the minimal TCP packet overhead in bytes (Ethernet + IP + TCP headers)?
questions.correctAnswer: 54 bytes
questions.explanation: Minimal overhead: 14 bytes Ethernet header + 20 bytes IP header + 20 bytes TCP header = 54 bytes total. For a 20-byte game update, that's 270% overhead — which is why games batch updates into larger packets.

questions.question: Which TCP/IP model layer combines OSI layers 5-7 (Session, Presentation, Application)?
questions.correctAnswer: Application
questions.explanation: The TCP/IP model's Application layer maps to OSI layers 5-7. It handles protocols like HTTP, DNS, TLS, and SSH — everything above the transport layer. The TCP/IP model has only 4 layers vs OSI's 7.

questions.question: During encapsulation, what does TLS add to a packet traveling down the stack?
options: A record header, MAC, and padding
questions.correctAnswer: A record header, MAC, and padding
questions.explanation: TLS adds a record header (~5 bytes), an authentication tag (MAC), and padding to the application data. This encrypts the payload so intermediate routers can't read the content, though they can still see the TCP/IP headers.

questions.question: Under the kernel's network stack, which subsystem handles firewall rules and NAT?
questions.correctAnswer: Netfilter (iptables/nftables)
questions.explanation: Netfilter sits between the Network and Link layers in the kernel stack, processing firewall rules (iptables/nftables) and NAT. Packets pass through chains like PREROUTING, FORWARD, and POSTROUTING before reaching the device driver.

questions.question: What kernel structure contains a socket's send/receive buffers and protocol state?
questions.correctAnswer: struct sock
questions.explanation: The kernel maintains a `struct sock` for each socket, containing send/receive buffers (sk_buff queues), protocol state (TCP control block with seq/ack numbers, congestion window), and a pointer to the protocol handler.

questions.question: Why does a 100-byte game message become 154 bytes on the wire with TCP?
options: 20 IP + 20 TCP + 14 Ethernet headers are added
TLS encryption doubles the payload size
questions.correctAnswer: 20 IP + 20 TCP + 14 Ethernet headers are added
questions.explanation: Each layer adds its own header during encapsulation: IP adds 20 bytes, TCP adds 20 bytes, and Ethernet adds 14 bytes, totaling 54 bytes of overhead on top of the 100-byte payload.

questions.question: What does `SO_RCVBUF` / `SO_SNDBUF` control in the socket API?
options: The buffer sizes affecting throughput via bandwidth-delay product
The IP version (v4 vs v6)altaltaltalt . — as specified
questions.correctAnswer: The buffer sizes affecting throughput via bandwidth-delay product
