questions.question: What is a reentrant function?
acceptableAnswers: a function that can be safely called while it's already executing, without corrupting state
questions.explanation: A reentrant function can be interrupted and safely called again. Non-reentrant functions (like malloc) use global/locked state that corrupts on re-entry.

questions.question: What does `raise(SIGINT)` do?
acceptableAnswers: sends SIGINT to the calling process itself
questions.explanation: `raise(SIGINT)` is equivalent to `kill(getpid(), SIGINT)`. It sends the specified signal to the calling process.

questions.question: What is the difference between `_exit()` and `exit()` regarding signal handlers?
acceptableAnswers: _exit() is async-signal-safe (immediate kernel exit); exit() is not safe (calls atexit handlers, flushes buffers)
questions.explanation: `_exit()` immediately terminates without cleanup — it's a direct syscall. `exit()` runs atexit handlers and flushes stdio buffers before calling `_exit()`. Only `_exit()` is safe in signal handlers.

questions.question: What are guard pages in the context of signal delivery?
acceptableAnswers: inaccessible memory pages beyond the stack — accessing them triggers a page fault detected as stack overflow -> SIGSEGV
questions.explanation: Guard pages have no page table entries. When RSP crosses into a guard page, the MMU faults. The kernel recognizes this as stack overflow and delivers SIGSEGV.

questions.question: What is a use-after-free bug?
options: Calling free() twice on the same pointer
Reading or writing memory after it has been freed — the memory may have been reallocated to another part of the program
Allocating memory but never freeing it
Freeing memory that was allocated on the stack
questions.correctAnswer: Reading or writing memory after it has been freed — the memory may have been reallocated to another part of the program
questions.explanation: After free(ptr), the memory is returned to the allocator and may be reused. Your dangling pointer now corrupts someone else's data. Works 99% of the time, crashes 1% — when the freed memory happened to be reused.

questions.question: Why does reading uninitialized memory often 'work' in debug but fail in release?
options: MSVC fills stack with 0xCC in debug — reading gives a recognizable pattern. In release, it's whatever garbage was left behind
Release builds add extra bounds checking
questions.correctAnswer: MSVC fills stack with 0xCC in debug — reading gives a recognizable pattern. In release, it's whatever garbage was left behind
questions.explanation: Debug: MSVC fills uninitialized stack with 0xCC, heap with 0xCD. Predictable wrong values. Release: memory contains whatever the previous function left — truly random garbage. Classic 'works in debug, crashes in release.'

questions.question: What tool detects memory bugs by instrumenting every memory access at compile time?
questions.correctAnswer: Address Sanitizer (ASan)
questions.explanation: ASan (-fsanitize=address) instruments every load, store, and allocation. Maintains 'shadow memory' tracking valid bytes. Catches use-after-free, buffer overflow, stack-use-after-scope at the exact instruction with detailed reports.

questions.question: Why can't reference counting collect cycles?
questions.correctAnswer: In a cycle (A→B, B→A), each object holds a reference to the other, so their counts never reach zero even with no external references
acceptableAnswers: cycles keep reference counts above zero
circular references prevent counts from reaching zero
questions.explanation: Two objects with shared_ptr to each other both have refcount ≥1. Even when unreachable, neither releases. weak_ptr breaks cycles — it doesn't increment the reference count.

questions.question: In mark-and-sweep GC, what are 'roots'?
options: The base classes in an inheritance hierarchy
The initial set of objects known to be reachable: global variables, stack variables, CPU registers
The oldest objects in the heap
The memory addresses where GC metadata is stored
questions.correctAnswer: The initial set of objects known to be reachable: global variables, stack variables, CPU registers
questions.explanation: Mark phase starts from roots — everything the program can access without following pointers. Recursively marks all reachable objects. Sweep frees unmarked ones. Correctly handles cycles since unreachable cycles won't be marked.

questions.question: Why does UE5 use mark-and-sweep GC for UObjects rather than reference counting?
options: UObjects form complex webs of cross-references (Materials→Textures, Blueprints→Meshes) that would leak with reference counting due to undetected cycles
The C++ standard requires mark-and-sweep for virtual classes
questions.correctAnswer: UObjects form complex webs of cross-references (Materials→Textures, Blueprints→Meshes) that would leak with reference counting due to undetected cycles
questions.explanation: UE's object graph is highly interconnected. Reference counting would leak cycles. Mark-and-sweep correctly identifies unreachable subgraphs even with internal cycles. UE uses clusters for faster marking.

questions.question: What is the generational hypothesis in garbage collection?
questions.correctAnswer: Most objects die young — collect the nursery frequently and cheaply, promote survivors to older generations collected rarely
acceptableAnswers: most objects die young so collect them separately
young objects collected frequently, old rarely
questions.explanation: Most objects have very short lifetimes. By collecting only the young generation (nursery) most of the time, you avoid scanning the entire heap. Survivors promote to older generations. Dramatically reduces average GC pause time.

questions.question: Why should game engines avoid GC allocations during gameplay?
options: GC pauses cause frame time spikes — even incremental GC adds unpredictable overhead that can drop frames below 60 FPS
Game engines don't support GC at all
questions.correctAnswer: GC pauses cause frame time spikes — even incremental GC adds unpredictable overhead that can drop frames below 60 FPS
questions.explanation: At 60 FPS, budget is 16.7ms/frame. A 5ms GC pause = 30% budget lost. Even incremental collectors add variable overhead. Solution: object pools, pre-allocate, GC only during loading screens.

questions.question: What is a stack buffer overflow and why is it dangerous?
questions.correctAnswer: Writing past the end of a stack array can overwrite the saved return address — enabling attackers to redirect execution to arbitrary code
acceptableAnswers: writing past array bounds on stack corrupting return address
stack smashing that overwrites return address
questions.explanation: Writing past a stack buffer overwrites the saved return address above it. An attacker overwrites it with malicious code address. When the function returns, execution jumps there. Mitigations: stack canaries, ASLR, W^X.

questions.question: What is the main danger of the double free bug?
options: It always causes an immediate crash
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: After the first `free`, the block may be reallocated to another object. The second `free` puts the already-in-use block back on the free list. Now two different allocations can return the same memory, leading to hard-to-debug corruption as both 'owners' write to the same location.

questions.question: What is an off-by-one error?
options: Writing or reading exactly one element past (or before) the valid range — e.g., `for (i=0; i<=10; i++) arr[i]=0;` on a 10-element array
Being off by one in a multiplication
A timer off by one tick
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: An off-by-one error writes or reads exactly one position outside the valid range. Classic examples: using `<=` instead of `<` in a loop bound, or allocating `strlen(input)` instead of `strlen(input)+1` for the null terminator.

questions.question: How does AddressSanitizer (ASan) detect memory bugs?
options: By using shadow memory — maintaining a byte-for-byte shadow map that tracks whether every 8 bytes of application memory is addressable, and instrumenting every memory access to check the shadow map
By adding random checks at compile time
By recording all allocations and frees in a log
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: ASan maintains a shadow memory map: 1 shadow byte for every 8 bytes of application memory. Each shadow byte records whether the 8 bytes are fully addressable, partially addressable, or poisoned (freed/red zone). Every memory load/store is instrumented with a shadow check.

questions.question: What is the difference between AddressSanitizer and Valgrind?
options: ASan is for Windows, Valgrind is for Linux
ASan is faster but less accurate
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: ASan requires recompilation and runs at ~2× slowdown, detecting use-after-free, buffer overflow, double free, and leaks. Valgrind runs on unmodified binaries (~10-20×), additionally detecting uninitialized memory reads and data races. Use ASan in development, Valgrind for final verification.

questions.question: What is a memory leak and why is an accumulating leak dangerous?
options: Writing past the end of a buffer
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: A memory leak loses the reference to allocated memory, making it unfreeable. An accumulating leak (in a loop or per-frame allocation) grows Resident Set Size monotonically. Eventually the process exhausts available RAM and the OOM killer terminates it.

questions.question: What is the 'process-leak' vs 'accumulating leak' distinction?
options: A process-leak is intentionally allocated once and never freed until exit (harmless, OS reclaims on exit). An accumulating leak grows continuously (catastrophic)
questions.correctAnswer: A process-leak is intentionally allocated once and never freed until exit (harmless, OS reclaims on exit). An accumulating leak grows continuously (catastrophic)
questions.explanation: Process-leaks (e.g., a global texture atlas allocated at startup and never freed) have no practical impact — the OS reclaims all process memory on exit. Accumulating leaks (allocations in loops or per-frame that lose their pointers) grow without bound and eventually cause OOM.

questions.question: What is the fundamental problem with reference counting?
options: It's too slow for any practical use
It uses too much memory for the counters
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Reference counting cannot handle cycles. If object A holds a shared_ptr to B and B holds a shared_ptr to A, both have refcount = 1 (from each other). When the external references drop, the refcount goes from 2 to 1 each — a permanent leak. `weak_ptr` breaks the cycle.

questions.question: How does mark-and-sweep garbage collection work?
options: Mark phase: traverse all reachable objects from roots, marking them as live. Sweep phase: walk the entire heap; unmarked objects are garbage and reclaimed
Objects are copied between memory regions
Objects are reference-counted and freed when count hits zero
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Mark-and-sweep has two phases: (1) Mark — starting from roots (globals, stack, registers), recursively traverse and mark every reachable object. (2) Sweep — scan the entire heap; any object NOT marked is garbage and its memory is reclaimed. Cycles are naturally handled since reachability, not counting, determines liveness.

questions.question: What is the advantage of a copying (Cheney's) garbage collector?
options: It never needs to scan the heap
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Copying collectors move live objects to a new region ('to-space'), then reclaim the entire old region ('from-space') at once. This compacts all live objects, eliminating external fragmentation. Allocation after collection is a simple pointer bump — the fastest possible allocation.

questions.question: What is the weak generational hypothesis?
options: Most objects die young — 90%+ of objects die within the first few MB of allocation
Old objects should be collected more frequently
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: The weak generational hypothesis is the empirical observation that most objects die young (90%+ within a few MB of allocation). Generational GC exploits this by splitting the heap into young (nursery) and old (tenured) generations, collecting the young generation frequently (cheap) and the old rarely (expensive).

questions.question: What problem does the write barrier solve in generational garbage collection?
options: An old-generation object may contain a pointer to a young-generation object. Without a write barrier, minor GC (scanning only young objects) would miss this reference and incorrectly collect the young object
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: During minor GC, only young objects are scanned. If an old object points to a young object, that pointer must be treated as a root during minor GC. The write barrier tracks all pointer stores into old objects, maintaining a 'remembered set' of cross-generational pointers.

questions.question: Why does UE5 use mark-and-sweep GC rather than reference counting for UObjects?
options: Reference counting is not implemented in C++
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Game object graphs frequently contain cycles (e.g., a Material references a Texture which is referenced by multiple Materials). Mark-and-sweep handles cycles naturally. It also has no per-reference-count overhead (unlike shared_ptr's atomic increment/decrement) and can be made incremental to avoid frame hitches.

questions.question: What is the 'vtable hijacking' exploit related to memory corruption?
acceptableAnswers: when memory corruption overwrites a C++ object's vtable pointer (vptr), an attacker can redirect virtual function calls to arbitrary code
questions.explanation: C++ objects store a hidden vtable pointer (vptr) at offset 0. When a virtual function is called, the compiler generates: `mov rax, [rdi]; call [rax + offset]`. If a use-after-free or buffer overflow overwrites the vptr, the CPU loads a garbage address — or one carefully chosen by an attacker.

questions.question: What is Valgrind's approach to detecting uninitialized memory reads?
acceptableAnswers: Valgrind translates every x86 instruction to VEX IR, tracking a 'V-bit' (validity bit) for every bit of memory and registers — reads from undefined bits are flagged
questions.explanation: Valgrind runs on a synthesized CPU, translating every instruction to VEX IR. Every bit of memory carries a V-bit: 0 when undefined (freshly allocated or never written), 1 when defined (written to). Memcheck flags any operation where undefined data affects a conditional branch, syscall argument, or pointer dereference.

questions.question: What is the purpose of ASan's quarantine of freed memory?
acceptableAnswers: instead of returning freed memory to the allocator, ASan poisons it and holds it in quarantine — any subsequent access through a dangling pointer hits the poisoned region and is reported
questions.explanation: ASan quarantines freed memory rather than returning it to the OS or general pool. The freed region is marked as poisoned in the shadow map. Any read/write through a dangling pointer triggers an immediate ASan report with the exact allocation, free, and access locations.

questions.question: What is double free and why is it more dangerous than it sounds?
acceptableAnswers: freeing the same pointer twice — the first free returns the memory to the allocator, the second free corrupts free list metadata if the memory was reallocated
questions.explanation: After the first `free`, the memory goes to the allocator's free list. If it's been reallocated to another object, the second `free` puts a block that's still in use back on the free list. This can cause the same memory to be returned by two `malloc` calls, corrupting both users.

questions.question: How does `-fstack-protector-strong` help detect buffer overflows?
acceptableAnswers: the compiler inserts a random 'canary' value between local variables and the saved RBP; the epilogue checks it before ret — corruption means a buffer overflow occurred
questions.explanation: Stack protector inserts a random 8-byte canary on the stack between local buffers and the saved RBP/return address. Before the function returns, the canary is checked. If it was overwritten (by a buffer overflow), the program aborts with a stack smashing detected error.

questions.question: What is shadow memory in the context of ASan?
acceptableAnswers: a dedicated memory region where ASan maintains 1 byte of metadata for every 8 bytes of application memory, encoding addressability information
questions.explanation: ASan's shadow memory is a separate memory region mapping each 8-byte application memory chunk to 1 shadow byte. The shadow byte encodes: 0 = all 8 bytes addressable, negative = all bytes poisoned, positive k = first k bytes addressable. Every memory access is instrumented to check this shadow map.

questions.question: Why does `sizeof(buf)` in a function receiving an array parameter give 8 instead of the array size?
acceptableAnswers: when an array is passed to a function, it decays to a pointer, and sizeof(pointer) = 8 on x86-64, not the array size
questions.explanation: In C/C++, array parameters decay to pointers. `void ClearBuffer(int* buf)` receives a pointer, not an array. `sizeof(buf)` = sizeof(int*) = 8 (on x64), not the array's total byte size. The caller must pass the size explicitly.

questions.question: What happens to the `fd` (forward) and `bk` (back) pointers in a freed malloc chunk?
acceptableAnswers: when freed, the chunk's user data area is overwritten with forward and backward pointers to adjacent free chunks in the free list
questions.explanation: When a chunk is freed, its user data area (which previously held application data) is repurposed for the free list: `fd` points to the next free chunk, `bk` to the previous. This is why use-after-free reads of freed memory return allocator internal data, not the original content.

questions.question: What is a pointer arithmetic confusion bug?
acceptableAnswers: miscalculating pointer offsets, especially when mixing sizeof(pointer) vs sizeof(array) or wrong pointer subtraction types
questions.explanation: Pointer arithmetic confusion bugs include: using `sizeof(pointer)` instead of `sizeof(array)`, forgetting pointer arithmetic divides by element size, and casting pointers to different types for subtraction. Example: `memset(buf, 0, sizeof(buf))` on a pointer clears only 8 bytes, not the full array.

questions.question: What is Cheney's algorithm for copying garbage collection?
acceptableAnswers: a breadth-first copying collector that copies objects from from-space to to-space, using a scan pointer to traverse copied objects and forwarding pointers to avoid duplicates
questions.explanation: Cheney's algorithm: (1) Copy root objects from from-space to to-space, updating pointers. (2) Use a scan pointer to breadth-first traverse each copied object, forwarding its pointer fields. (3) When scan catches up to free pointer, all live objects are in to-space. (4) Swap from/to spaces. Allocation is a simple pointer bump.

questions.question: What is `std::weak_ptr` and what problem does it solve?
acceptableAnswers: a non-owning reference that doesn't increment the refcount, used to break reference cycles by replacing one direction of a cyclic shared_ptr pair
questions.explanation: `std::weak_ptr` is a non-owning smart pointer. It doesn't affect the reference count. To access the object, you must lock it (creating a temporary shared_ptr). It's used to break reference cycles: instead of `shared_ptr<Node> prev`, use `weak_ptr<Node> prev`.

questions.question: How does UE5's `-MemoryValidation` provide lightweight memory debugging?
acceptableAnswers: by adding guard pages around allocations, poisoning freed memory with 0xDEAD, checking allocator metadata integrity, and tracking allocation file/line — at <10% overhead vs ASan's ~100%
questions.explanation: UE5's MemoryValidation provides ASan-like checking with <10% overhead: guard pages catch overflows immediately, 0xDEAD poisoning makes use-after-free reads obvious, integrity checks catch double-free and wild-pointer free, and allocation tracking enables `memreport` dumps.

questions.question: What is the object pool pattern and how does it eliminate GC pressure?
acceptableAnswers: pre-allocates a fixed pool of objects, Acquire returns an idle one (O(1)), Release resets and returns it (O(1)) — no heap allocation or deallocation during gameplay
questions.explanation: Object pools pre-allocate a batch of objects and recycle them. `Acquire()` pops from a free list (no `new`/`malloc`). `Release()` resets the object and pushes back (no `delete`/`free`). This eliminates heap fragmentation and allocation overhead from hot paths.

questions.question: What is a V-bit in Valgrind's Memcheck?
acceptableAnswers: a validity bit tracking whether each bit of memory and each CPU register has been assigned a defined value
questions.explanation: Valgrind's Memcheck associates a V-bit (validity bit) with every bit of program memory and each CPU register. Initially, allocated memory has V=0 (undefined). On writes, V-bits become 1 (defined). Memcheck flags operations where undefined data affects program behavior — conditional branches, pointer dereferences, or syscall arguments.

questions.question: How does `memreport -full` help diagnose memory leaks in UE5?
acceptableAnswers: outputs a full allocation breakdown by category (Texture, Mesh, Audio, etc.), enabling detection of accumulating leaks in specific resource types
questions.explanation: `memreport -full` dumps memory usage by category, class, and asset. Run it at intervals during gameplay — a monotonically increasing counter in a specific category identifies an accumulating leak. Use `obj list` to find which specific objects are leaking.

questions.question: What is a forwarding pointer in copying garbage collection?
acceptableAnswers: a pointer left at an object's original location (in from-space) pointing to its new location (in to-space), so subsequent references to the same object find the correct copy
questions.explanation: During Cheney's algorithm, when an object is first encountered and copied to to-space, a forwarding pointer is written at its original from-space address. Any later reference to the same object finds the forwarding pointer and uses the new copy, preventing duplicate copying.

questions.question: What is the `GC.Sweep` console command in UE5?
acceptableAnswers: forces an immediate garbage collection sweep to run, useful for testing what gets collected and when
questions.explanation: `GC.Sweep` manually triggers a UObject garbage collection cycle. It's used during development to verify that objects are correctly being collected, or to force collection and observe memory usage changes.

questions.question: What is the difference between a minor GC and a major GC in generational collection?
acceptableAnswers: minor GC collects only the young generation (nursery) — fast but frequent; major GC collects the entire heap including old generation — slow but rare
questions.explanation: Minor GC runs when Eden fills up: it scans only young objects (plus the remembered set of old→young pointers). Most dead objects are found quickly with minimal work. Major GC runs when the old generation fills: it traces the entire object graph, marks live objects, and may compact. Major GCs are much more expensive.

questions.question: What is a dangling pointer?
acceptableAnswers: a pointer that points to memory that has been freed (deallocated) — accessing it causes use-after-free
questions.explanation: A dangling pointer is any pointer whose target memory has been freed. The pointer still holds the old address, but the memory may be reused. This is the root cause of use-after-free bugs. Setting pointers to `nullptr` after `delete` prevents accidental dereference.

questions.question: What does `AddToRoot()` do in UE5's GC system?
acceptableAnswers: pins a UObject, preventing it from being garbage collected (removes it from the set of collectible objects)
questions.explanation: `AddToRoot()` adds a UObject to the root set — the set of objects considered permanently reachable by the GC. The object won't be collected until `RemoveFromRoot()` is called. This is used for objects that must persist across frame boundaries or level loads.

questions.question: What makes double free particularly dangerous?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: The first free returns memory to the allocator. If reallocated, the second free puts an in-use block back on the free list. Two different objects can then own the same memory.

questions.question: How does ASan detect memory bugs?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: ASan uses shadow memory: 1 shadow byte per 8 application bytes. It encodes how many bytes are addressable, partially addressable, or poisoned. Every load/store is instrumented.

questions.question: What is the difference between ASan and Valgrind?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: ASan requires recompilation (~2×) detecting use-after-free, overflow, double free. Valgrind runs unmodified binaries (~10-20×) additionally detecting uninitialized reads and data races.

questions.question: What is an accumulating memory leak?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: An accumulating leak (loop or per-frame allocation losing its pointer) grows Resident Set Size monotonically. Eventually, the OOM killer terminates the process.

questions.question: What is the problem with reference counting?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: A→B and B→A forms a cycle. Both have refcount=1 from each other. When external references drop, refcount goes 2→1 each — neither can reach 0. `weak_ptr` breaks the cycle.

questions.question: How does mark-and-sweep GC work?
options: Mark: traverse all reachable objects from roots. Sweep: scan all heap; unmarked = garbage
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Mark phase: start from roots, recursively traverse and mark every reachable object. Sweep phase: scan entire heap; unmarked objects are reclaimed. Handles cycles naturally.

questions.question: What is the advantage of a copying (Cheney's) collector?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Copying collectors move live objects to to-space, then reclaim from-space wholesale. This compacts objects, eliminates fragmentation, and allocation becomes a pointer bump.

questions.question: What problem does the write barrier solve in generational GC?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Minor GC scans only young objects. If an old object points to a young one, that pointer is invisible to minor GC. The write barrier tracks these stores into old objects.

questions.question: Why does UE5 use mark-and-sweep GC for UObjects?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Game object graphs frequently have cycles. Mark-and-sweep handles them naturally, has no per-reference atomic overhead, and can be made incremental to avoid frame hitches.

questions.question: What is vtable hijacking?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: C++ objects store a vptr at offset 0. The compiler generates `mov rax, [rdi]; call [rax + offset]` for virtual calls. Overwriting the vptr lets attackers redirect execution.

questions.question: How does Valgrind detect uninitialized memory reads?
acceptableAnswers: translates to VEX IR, tracks V-bits for every bit of memory and registers — flags reads from undefined bits
questions.explanation: Valgrind runs on a synthesized CPU. Every bit carries a V-bit: 0=undefined (fresh allocation), 1=defined (written). Flagged when undefined data affects branches, syscalls, or pointers.

questions.question: What is ASan's quarantine?
acceptableAnswers: freed memory is poisoned and held aside instead of being returned to the allocator — any dangling pointer access hits it
questions.explanation: ASan quarantines freed memory (poisoned in shadow map). Any access through a dangling pointer immediately hits poisoned memory, triggering a detailed ASan report.

questions.question: What is double free?
acceptableAnswers: calling free/delete on the same pointer twice
questions.explanation: First `free` returns the block to the free list. If reallocated, the second `free` corrupts the free list. Next `malloc` may return the same memory to two callers.

questions.question: What does `-fstack-protector-strong` insert?
acceptableAnswers: a random canary value between locals and saved RBP, checked before ret — corruption causes abort
questions.explanation: The compiler inserts a random 8-byte canary. If a buffer overflow overwrites it, the epilogue detects the mismatch and aborts with 'stack smashing detected'.

questions.question: How does the allocator use fd/bk pointers in a freed chunk?
acceptableAnswers: freed chunks reuse user data area for free list forward/back pointers — use-after-free reads return allocator metadata
questions.explanation: When freed, the chunk's user data stores `fd` (next free chunk) and `bk` (previous). Reading freed memory returns these allocator pointers, not original data.

questions.question: What is Cheney's algorithm?
acceptableAnswers: breadth-first copying collector: copy roots to to-space, scan copied objects forwarding their fields, swap spaces
questions.explanation: Cheney's algorithm: (1) Copy roots from from-space to to-space. (2) Breadth-first scan copied objects, forwarding pointer fields. (3) When scan = free, all live objects moved. (4) Swap spaces.

questions.question: What does `std::weak_ptr` solve?
acceptableAnswers: breaks reference cycles by providing a non-owning reference that doesn't increment refcount
questions.explanation: `weak_ptr` is a non-owning smart pointer. Use it for back-references in cycles: instead of `shared_ptr<Node> prev`, use `weak_ptr<Node> prev`.

questions.question: What does UE5's `-MemoryValidation` check?
acceptableAnswers: guard pages, freed memory poisoning (0xDEAD), allocator metadata integrity, allocation tracking at <10% overhead
questions.explanation: UE5's MemoryValidation provides guard pages (immediate overflow detection), 0xDEAD poisoning (obvious use-after-free reads), integrity checks, and tracking all at <10% overhead.

questions.question: What is an object pool and how does it help?
acceptableAnswers: pre-allocates objects and recycles them — Acquire/Release are O(1) with no heap allocation
questions.explanation: Object pools pre-allocate and recycle objects. `Acquire()` pops from free list (no malloc). `Release()` resets and pushes back (no free). Eliminates allocation overhead on hot paths.

questions.question: What is a forwarding pointer in copying GC?
acceptableAnswers: a pointer left at the original location pointing to the new copy, preventing duplicate copies of the same object
questions.explanation: When an object is copied to to-space during Cheney's algorithm, a forwarding pointer is written at the old address. Any later reference to the same object finds the forwarding address.

questions.question: What is minor GC vs major GC?
acceptableAnswers: minor = collects young generation only (fast); major = collects entire heap including old generation (slow)
questions.explanation: Minor GC runs when Eden fills: scans only young objects plus remembered set. Most dead objects found quickly. Major GC traces the entire object graph — expensive.

questions.question: What does `AddToRoot()` do in UE5 GC?
acceptableAnswers: pins a UObject so it won't be garbage collected
questions.explanation: `AddToRoot()` adds to the GC root set. The object won't be collected until `RemoveFromRoot()` is called. Used for objects that must persist across frames.

questions.question: What is a process-leak vs accumulating leak?
acceptableAnswers: process-leak = one-time allocation never freed (harmless, OS reclaims); accumulating = continuous growth (catastrophic OOM)
questions.explanation: Process-leaks (e.g., global texture atlas) have no practical impact. Accumulating leaks (per-frame allocation without freeing) grow until OOM.

questions.question: What does Valgrind's `--leak-check=full` do?
acceptableAnswers: reports all definitely lost, indirectly lost, possibly lost, and still reachable memory blocks at exit
questions.explanation: `--leak-check=full` shows each leaked block with its allocation stack trace. Reports 'definitely lost' (no pointer to the block) and 'indirectly lost' (structures pointing to other lost blocks).

questions.question: What are the 8 canonical memory bugs?
acceptableAnswers: bad pointer deref, uninitialized read, buffer overflow, use-after-free, double free, memory leak, off-by-one, pointer arithmetic confusion
questions.explanation: The eight bugs: (1) bad pointer, (2) uninitialized read, (3) buffer overflow, (4) dangling pointer/use-after-free, (5) double free, (6) memory leak, (7) off-by-one, (8) pointer arithmetic confusion.

questions.question: What does the `typename` keyword disambiguate in template code when it appears BEFORE a qualified name like `T::value_type`?
options: It tells the compiler that `value_type` is a TYPE, not a static member — without `typename`, dependent qualified names default to value interpretation.
It declares a new template parameter `value_type` that shadows the enclosing class, enabling local type deduction within the template body.
It prevents name mangling on the qualified name, ensuring the linker can resolve `T::value_type` using C linkage conventions.
It enables RTTI for the dependent type, allowing `typeid(T::value_type)` to correctly identify the type at runtime.
questions.correctAnswer: It tells the compiler that `value_type` is a TYPE, not a static member — without `typename`, dependent qualified names default to value interpretation.
questions.explanation: In template code, `T::value_type` is a dependent name — the compiler doesn't know if it's a type, value, or template. Adding `typename` (e.g., `typename T::value_type`) tells the compiler 'this is a type.' Without it, the compiler assumes it's a value and produces confusing errors. This is part of two-phase lookup: phase 1 checks non-dependent names; phase 2 checks dependent names at instantiation time.

options: `process()` is a dependent name — since `Base<T>` depends on `T`, the compiler defers lookup. Fix with `this->process()` or `Base<T>::process()`.
`process()` is private in `Base<T>` for all specializations except `T=void`, so derived classes cannot access it at any parse stage. This additional
Template member functions can only call other template functions due to ODR constraints, and `process()` is a non-template member.
The base class hasn't been instantiated yet because the compiler defers all template instantiation to link time.
questions.correctAnswer: `process()` is a dependent name — since `Base<T>` depends on `T`, the compiler defers lookup. Fix with `this->process()` or `Base<T>::process()`.
questions.explanation: This is the classic dependent base class problem. Because `Base<T>` depends on the template parameter `T`, the compiler cannot know what `Base<T>` looks like during phase 1 (before instantiation). If `T` is later specialized to a type where `Base<T>` has no `process()`, the non-dependent lookup would be wrong. The fix — `this->process()` or `Base<T>::process()` — makes the name dependent, deferring lookup to phase 2 when `T` is known.

questions.question: What does `.template` (dot-template) syntax disambiguate?
options: It tells the compiler that `<int>` starts template arguments, not a less-than comparison — used when calling member templates on dependent objects.
It accesses a template class's static member variable that was defined with the `static template` storage specifier introduced in C++14.
It declares a template member function inside a non-template class where the compiler requires explicit syntactic disambiguation.
It specializes a member function template outside the class body where `template<>` alone would be ambiguous with partial specialization. This addition
questions.correctAnswer: It tells the compiler that `<int>` starts template arguments, not a less-than comparison — used when calling member templates on dependent objects.
questions.explanation: Like `typename` for types, `.template` (and `->template`, `::template`) tells the compiler that what follows `<` is a template argument list, not a less-than comparison. Without it, `obj.foo<int>()` could parse as `(obj.foo) < (int) > ()` — a comparison chain — rather than a template function call.

questions.question: A template template parameter is declared as `template<typename> typename Container`. What does this allow?
options: It accepts a class template (like `std::vector`) as an argument — the outer class uses `Container<T>` without pre-specifying the element type.
It creates a template that generates new class templates through recursive instantiation, similar to how metaprogramming produces types from types.
It inherits from a template base with unbound parameters, enabling the derived class to defer parameterization to its own instantiation.
It deduces function template arguments from the return type alone, bypassing the normal rule that template parameters must appear in the function parameter list.
questions.correctAnswer: It accepts a class template (like `std::vector`) as an argument — the outer class uses `Container<T>` without pre-specifying the element type.
questions.explanation: Template template parameters let you write `Stack<int, std::vector>` instead of `Stack<int, std::vector<int>>`. The outer template accepts a template as a parameter: `template<typename T, template<typename> typename Container> class Stack { Container<T> data; };`. The trap: `std::vector` actually takes TWO template parameters (the second is the default allocator), so you need `template<typename, typename> typename Container` or use a template alias.

questions.question: What is SFINAE an acronym for, and what does it allow?
options: Substitution Failure Is Not An Error
Static Function Interface Name Assembly Extractor — a mechanism for generating function names from template parameters
Safe Function Inlining Needs Additional Encoding — a compiler optimization for template functions
Standard Framework for Interface Name Aliasing Exceptions — the C++ standard's naming convention rules
questions.correctAnswer: Substitution Failure Is Not An Error
questions.explanation: SFINAE is the mechanism that makes `std::enable_if` work. When the compiler tries to substitute template arguments into an overload, and that substitution produces invalid code (e.g., accessing a type that doesn't exist), it doesn't emit an error — it simply removes that overload from consideration. This allows you to conditionally enable or disable function templates based on type properties. — when substituting template arguments into a function declaration fails, the compiler discards that overload instead of emitting an error

questions.question: In C++17, what is the pre-C++17 equivalent of `if constexpr (std::is_integral_v<T>)` for selecting different code paths at compile time?
options: SFINAE with `std::enable_if` on separate overloads, or tag dispatch — both select the correct function at compile time before `if constexpr` existed.
A runtime `if` combined with `constexpr` variables — the optimizer would recognize compile-time constants and eliminate dead branches during code generation.
The `#if` preprocessor directive combined with compiler built-ins like `__is_integral` — this was the only portable way to branch before C++17.
Explicit template specialization where a primary template handles integral types and partial specializations cover all remaining categories.
questions.correctAnswer: SFINAE with `std::enable_if` on separate overloads, or tag dispatch — both select the correct function at compile time before `if constexpr` existed.
questions.explanation: Before C++17's `if constexpr`, compile-time branching required SFINAE: `template<typename T, std::enable_if_t<std::is_integral_v<T>, int> = 0>`. `if constexpr` makes this dramatically cleaner — the discarded branch is never instantiated, so it can contain code that would be invalid for the given type, which wasn't possible with plain `if`.

questions.question: What does `std::enable_if_t<std::is_floating_point_v<T>, int> = 0` do in a template parameter list?
options: If T is a floating-point type, `enable_if_t` resolves to `int`, making the...
It sets a default template argument of 0 for all types, with an additional constraint check
It creates a static assertion that fails at compile time if T is not a floating-point type
It initializes a member variable of the template class to 0 if T is a floating-point type
questions.correctAnswer: If T is a floating-point type, `enable_if_t` resolves to `int`, making the...
questions.explanation: This is the classic enable_if pattern for function templates. `enable_if_t<Condition, Type>` resolves to `Type` if `Condition` is true, otherwise substitution fails. By placing it as a defaulted template parameter (`int = 0`), the overload simply vanishes from the candidate set when the condition is false, and the compiler picks another overload (or emits a 'no matching function' error if none remain). If T is NOT floating-point, substitution fails, and SFINAE removes this overload.  template parameter `int = 0` valid.

questions.question: Explain why `template<typename T> void foo(T&)` and `template<typename T> void foo(T&&)` CAN coexist as overloads, but `template<typename T> void foo(T)` and `template<typename T> void foo(T&)` can be ambiguous. What rule governs this?
questions.correctAnswer: `T&` and `T&&` (universal reference) are different deduction contexts — lvalues deduce `T&` while rvalues deduce `T`. But `T` (by-value) and `T&` (by-ref) both accept lvalues, creating ambiguity. The compiler uses partial ordering rules: more specialized templates are preferred. `T&` is more specialized than `T` for lvalue arguments because any call that matches `T&` also matches `T`, but not vice versa. However, the rules are complex and can still produce ambiguity in edge cases.
questions.explanation: Template overload resolution uses partial ordering. The compiler synthesizes argument types and tries to deduce each overload's parameters from the other's. The one that succeeds in both directions (the more general one) loses. `T&` is more specialized than `T` because if you can pass an argument to `T&`, you can also pass it to `T`, but not vice versa (rvalues can't bind to `T&`). When both are viable, the more specialized one wins. But `T` and `T&` can be ambiguous when both are equally viable for the same argument category.
acceptableAnswers: `T&` and `T&&` (universal reference) are different deduction contexts — lvalues deduce `T&` while rvalues deduce `T`. But `T` (by-value) and `T&` (by-ref) both accept lvalues, creating ambiguity. The compiler uses partial ordering rules: more specialized templates are preferred. `T&` is more specialized than `T` for lvalue arguments because any call that matches `T&` also matches `T`, but not vice versa. However, the rules are complex and can still produce ambiguity in edge cases.

questions.question: According to Meyers' framework, how does `template<typename T> void f(T param)` deduce `T` when called with `int x = 5; f(x);`?
options: Case 2 (universal reference): `T = int&`, `param = int&` — forwarding reference deduction
Template argument deduction fails because `param` is not a reference
questions.correctAnswer: Case 3 (pass-by-value): `T = int`, `param = int`.
questions.explanation: Meyers' Case 3 covers pass-by-value parameters. The parameter type is `T`, not `T&` or `T&&`. When the argument is an lvalue (`x`), `T` deduces to `int` (the reference-ness and const-ness of `x` are ignored). If `x` were `const int`, `T` would STILL deduce to `int` — top-level const is stripped in pass-by-value deduction. This is why pass-by-value gives you a copy regardless. Top-level const and references are stripped.

questions.question: What is the critical difference between `decltype(x)` and `decltype((x))` when `x` is an `int` variable?
options: `decltype(x)` = `int` (declared type of the name). `decltype((x))` = `int&` — parenthesized names become lvalue expressions, and decltype adds a reference for lvalues.
Both give `int` — the outer parentheses in `decltype((x))` are a redundant grouping operator with no effect on the deduced type, contrary to common misconception. This addi
`decltype(x)` gives `int&` and `decltype((x))` gives `int` — the parentheses suppress the reference that would normally be added to a variable name.
`decltype(x)` gives `int&&` for block-scope variables while `decltype((x))` gives `int` by stripping both reference layers during deduction.
questions.correctAnswer: `decltype(x)` = `int` (declared type of the name). `decltype((x))` = `int&` — parenthesized names become lvalue expressions, and decltype adds a reference for lvalues.
questions.explanation: This is the 'parenthesized names trap' from Meyers Item 3. `decltype` has two modes: for unparenthesized names, it returns the declared type. For expressions (including parenthesized names), it returns `T&` for lvalue expressions and `T&&` for xvalue expressions. `(x)` is an lvalue expression, so `decltype((x))` = `int&`. This matters immensely for `decltype(auto)` return types: `decltype(auto) f() { int x = 5; return (x); }` returns `int&` — a dangling reference! `decltype((x))` gives `int&` — the extra parentheses make it an lvalue EXPRESSION, and decltype adds a reference for lvalue expressions.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of `decltype(auto)`?
options: To deduce a return type that perfectly preserves references
To automatically apply `auto` type deduction to `decltype` expressions
To enable automatic return type deduction in C++11
To declare variables whose type matches the initializer's type exactly, including cv-qualifiers
questions.correctAnswer: To deduce a return type that perfectly preserves references
questions.explanation: `auto` return type deduction strips references (like template Case 3). If a function should return a reference, `auto` loses it. `decltype(auto)` uses `decltype` rules, preserving reference-ness: if the return expression is an lvalue, the return type is an lvalue reference. The trap: `decltype(auto) f() { int x = 5; return (x); }` returns `int&` (dangling!) while `decltype(auto) f() { int x = 5; return x; }` returns `int` (safe). — it uses `decltype` deduction rules instead of `auto` (template) deduction rules

questions.question: Why does `template<typename T> class Widget { Widget(T&& x); };` act as a 'perfect forwarding constructor' that can hijack copy construction?
options: For non-const lvalues, the template instantiates as `Widget(Widget&)` — a PERFECT match. The copy constructor takes `const Widget&`, requiring a conversion.
The template constructor takes priority because user-declared constructors always outrank implicitly-declared ones in overload resolution per the standard.
The forwarding reference constructor deletes the copy constructor through a hidden SFINAE constraint defined by the standard library implementation. This additi
Since `T&&` in a constructor is an rvalue reference, and copy construction uses rvalue semantics, the template matches the copy constructor perfectly.
questions.correctAnswer: For non-const lvalues, the template instantiates as `Widget(Widget&)` — a PERFECT match. The copy constructor takes `const Widget&`, requiring a conversion.
questions.explanation: This is Meyers Item 26. When you write `Widget w2(w1)` where `w1` is non-const, the compiler considers: (1) the implicit copy constructor `Widget(const Widget&)`, and (2) the template `Widget(Widget&)` (instantiated with `T=Widget&`). The template version is a PERFECT match (no const-qualification conversion needed), so it wins. The copy constructor is bypassed. This is the universal-reference-overloading trap — the solution is tag dispatch, `if constexpr`, or SFINAE to disable the template for Widget types.

questions.question: In `template<typename T> void f(T&& x)`, why is `T&&` called a 'universal reference' (or 'forwarding reference') and NOT just an rvalue reference?
options: `T` is being DEDUCED — `T&&` binds to lvalues (T→T&, collapsing to T&) and rvalues (T→T, giving T&&). Without deduction, `&&` is always rvalue reference.
The `&&` syntax is overloaded by the standard — any `&&` in a parameter declaration accepts both lvalues and rvalues regardless of deduction context.
The standard requires all `T&&` parameters to accept both value categories, enforced by a mandatory implicit conversion in the compiler frontend. This additi
`std::forward` converts any `&&` parameter into a universal reference by injecting reference collapsing rules at the call site at compile time.
questions.correctAnswer: `T` is being DEDUCED — `T&&` binds to lvalues (T→T&, collapsing to T&) and rvalues (T→T, giving T&&). Without deduction, `&&` is always rvalue reference.
questions.explanation: `T&&` is a forwarding reference ONLY when `T` is being deduced (in a function template, or `auto&&`). The key is template argument deduction + reference collapsing: an lvalue argument deduces `T=SomeType&`, and `SomeType& &&` collapses to `SomeType&`. An rvalue argument deduces `T=SomeType`, giving `SomeType&&`. This is why `std::vector<T>&&` is always an rvalue reference — `T` is not being deduced in that context.

questions.question: How can you view the actual type deduced by the compiler for a template parameter?
options: Use an incomplete class template `struct TD;` and write `TD<T> td;` — the error message reveals T. Or `boost::typeindex::type_id<T>().pretty_name()`.
Use `std::cout << type_of(T)` — a C++17 utility that prints the fully qualified type name of any template parameter to standard output. This additional c
Inspect the object file with `objdump -t` to find the mangled symbol, then decode with `c++filt` to extract the deduced type name.
Use `static_assert(sizeof(T) == 0)` which forces a compiler error that prints the type of T in the diagnostic along with the assertion text.
questions.correctAnswer: Use an incomplete class template `struct TD;` and write `TD<T> td;` — the error message reveals T. Or `boost::typeindex::type_id<T>().pretty_name()`.
questions.explanation: The 'Type Display' (TD) trick is the classic method: `template<typename T> struct TD;` (declared but never defined). Writing `TD<T> td;` causes a compiler error like 'error: aggregate TD<int> td has incomplete type' — the type is right there in the error. `typeid(T).name()` works at runtime (with typeinfo) but returns mangled names on some compilers. `boost::typeindex::type_id<T>().pretty_name()` gives human-readable names.

questions.question: Explain what happens when `decltype(auto)` is used as a return type and the return statement is `return (local_variable)`. What's the bug?
questions.correctAnswer: `decltype(auto)` uses `decltype` rules. `(local_variable)` is an lvalue expression, so `decltype` deduces `Type&` — a reference to the local variable. The function returns a DANGLING REFERENCE to a stack variable that is destroyed when the function returns. The fix: return `local_variable` (no parentheses) which deduces `Type` (by value), or use `auto` instead which always strips references. This is one of the most subtle UB traps in modern C++.
questions.explanation: This is the classic `decltype(auto)` pitfall from Meyers Item 3. The parentheses transform a name (`x`) into an expression (`(x)`). `decltype` treats expressions differently from names: lvalue expressions get a reference appended. Since `(local_variable)` is an lvalue expression, the deduced return type is a reference to the local — which is destroyed at function exit. The solution: never parenthesize the return expression with `decltype(auto)`, or use `auto` when you want value semantics.
acceptableAnswers: `decltype(auto)` uses `decltype` rules. `(local_variable)` is an lvalue expression, so `decltype` deduces `Type&` — a reference to the local variable. The function returns a DANGLING REFERENCE to a stack variable that is destroyed when the function returns. The fix: return `local_variable` (no parentheses) which deduces `Type` (by value), or use `auto` instead which always strips references. This is one of the most subtle UB traps in modern C++.

questions.question: What happens to a `std::thread` object if its destructor runs while the thread is still joinable (neither joined nor detached)?
options: The thread is automatically joined (waited for completion) This approach was ...
The thread is automatically detached and continues running independently
The thread continues running and the `std::thread` object silently releases its handle
questions.correctAnswer: `std::terminate()` is called — the program crashes.
questions.explanation: This is the most dangerous `std::thread` trap. The `std::thread` destructor calls `std::terminate()` if the thread is still joinable — it does NOT automatically join or detach. This is by design: auto-join would risk deadlocking the destructor, and auto-detach would risk the thread accessing destroyed stack variables. The solution: RAII thread wrapper (like `std::jthread` in C++20, which auto-joins in its destructor). You MUST either `join()` or `detach()` every thread before its `std::thread` object is destroyed.

questions.question: What is the difference between `std::lock_guard` and `std::unique_lock`?
options: `lock_guard` always owns the lock until destruction — it cannot be unlocked early or transferred.
`lock_guard` is for shared mutexes and `unique_lock` is for exclusive mutexes
`lock_guard` provides better performance because it uses atomic operations
`lock_guard` can be copied but `unique_lock` cannot This behavior is specifie... This additional conte
questions.correctAnswer: `lock_guard` always owns the lock until destruction — it cannot be unlocked early or transferred.
questions.explanation: `lock_guard` is minimalist: lock on construction, unlock on destruction. Zero overhead beyond the mutex itself. `unique_lock` is more flexible: you can defer locking (`std::defer_lock`), unlock early (`.unlock()`), re-lock (`.lock()`), and transfer ownership via move. `unique_lock` is required for `std::condition_variable::wait()` because the wait may need to unlock and re-lock internally. `unique_lock` supports deferred locking, early unlocking, and move semantics.

questions.question: In `std::condition_variable::wait(lock, predicate)`, why is the predicate (the lambda) necessary instead of just checking `queue.empty()` before waiting?
options: Because of spurious wakeups — the condition variable may wake up even when no thread signaled it.
Because the condition variable's internal queue requires a predicate for thread scheduling This additi
Because the mutex must be unlocked before the predicate runs Some legacy code...
Because the predicate converts the wait from a blocking call to a non-blocking one
questions.correctAnswer: Because of spurious wakeups — the condition variable may wake up even when no thread signaled it.
questions.explanation: The predicate form `cv.wait(lock, [] { return !queue.empty(); })` is equivalent to `while (queue.empty()) { cv.wait(lock); }`. This handles: (1) spurious wakeups — the OS may wake the thread for no reason, (2) TOCTOU — another thread may have consumed the data between wakeup and lock re-acquisition. Always use the predicate form of wait — it's the correct pattern. The predicate loop handles this and the TOCTOU race between checking and waiting.

questions.question: What is the difference between `std::launch::async` and `std::launch::deferred` when passed to `std::async`?
options: `async` guarantees the function runs on a new thread.
`async` runs the function immediately and `deferred` runs it after a timeout
`async` uses thread pools and `deferred` creates dedicated threads This appro...
`async` is for CPU-bound tasks and `deferred` is for IO-bound tasks This diff...
questions.correctAnswer: `async` guarantees the function runs on a new thread.
questions.explanation: `std::launch::async` creates a new thread (or uses a thread pool — implementation-defined) to run the function concurrently. `std::launch::deferred` does NOT run the function until someone calls `get()` or `wait()` on the future — and it runs on the thread that makes that call. The default (`std::launch::async | std::launch::deferred`) lets the implementation choose — which is why you should explicitly specify `std::launch::async` if you need concurrent execution. `deferred` guarantees the function runs lazily on the CALLING thread when `get()` or `wait()` is called on the future.

questions.question: What is the dangerous trap with `std::async`'s returned future if you don't store it?
options: The future's destructor BLOCKS until the async task completes — making `std::async` effectively synchronous if the returned.
The async task is immediately cancelled and never executes This differs from ...
The async task runs but its results are lost, causing a memory leak This beha...
The future is automatically converted to `std::shared_future` which has different semantics
questions.correctAnswer: The future's destructor BLOCKS until the async task completes — making `std::async` effectively synchronous if the returned.
questions.explanation: `std::async` returns a `std::future`. The destructor of a `std::future` obtained from `std::async` (with `std::launch::async` policy) BLOCKS until the task completes. If you write `std::async(std::launch::async, foo);` without storing the future, the temporary future is destroyed immediately, blocking until `foo()` finishes — making the call synchronous. Always store the returned future in a variable. future is a temporary

questions.question: How does `std::scoped_lock` differ from calling `std::lock()` on multiple mutexes manually?
options: `std::scoped_lock` uses the same deadlock-avoidance algorithm as `std::lock()` internally, but wraps it in RAII — it locks ALL.
`std::scoped_lock` locks mutexes one at a time, unlike `std::lock()` which locks them simultaneously
`std::scoped_lock` uses spinlocks internally for better performance This inte...
`std::scoped_lock` can only lock a single mutex, while `std::lock()` can lock multiple
questions.correctAnswer: `std::scoped_lock` uses the same deadlock-avoidance algorithm as `std::lock()` internally, but wraps it in RAII — it locks ALL.
questions.explanation: `std::scoped_lock` (C++17) is the successor to `std::lock_guard` for multi-mutex locking. It uses `std::lock()`'s deadlock-avoidance algorithm (try-lock-and-back-off with ordered locking) to acquire multiple mutexes, then releases them all in its destructor. It's the preferred way to lock multiple mutexes in modern C++. mutexes in one deadlock-free operation and auto-unlocks on scope exit

questions.question: Explain the relationship between `std::promise`, `std::future`, and `std::packaged_task`. How do they connect?
questions.correctAnswer: `std::promise<T>` is the WRITE end: you call `promise.set_value(x)` to set the result. `std::future<T>` is the READ end: you call `future.get()` to retrieve the result. They're connected via `promise.get_future()`. `std::packaged_task<R(Args...)>` wraps a callable and provides its own promise-future pair automatically: calling the task executes the function and sets the promise; the caller gets the future via `task.get_future()`. `std::async` internally uses promise-future, providing the simplest interface: one call returns a future directly.
questions.explanation: The promise-future pair is the one-shot channel pattern: promise is the 'transmitter,' future is the 'receiver.' `promise.set_value()` can only be called once; `future.get()` can only be called once (call `future.share()` to get a `shared_future` for multiple reads). `packaged_task` combines a callable with a promise — calling the task like a function sets the promise automatically. `std::async` is the highest-level abstraction: it handles thread creation and promise-future pairing in one call.
acceptableAnswers: `std::promise<T>` is the WRITE end: you call `promise.set_value(x)` to set the result. `std::future<T>` is the READ end: you call `future.get()` to retrieve the result. They're connected via `promise.get_future()`. `std::packaged_task<R(Args...)>` wraps a callable and provides its own promise-future pair automatically: calling the task executes the function and sets the promise; the caller gets the future via `task.get_future()`. `std::async` internally uses promise-future, providing the simplest interface: one call returns a future directly.

If a class handles both data persistence AND business logic, a change to the database schema could break business rules. By separating concerns, changes propagate minimally. This directly reduces regression bugs and makes the codebase easier to reason about. unrelated functionality

questions.question: The Open-Closed Principle (OCP) says classes should be 'open for extension, closed for modification.' How do C++ templates achieve this?

questions.correctAnswer: Templates enable static polymorphism — new types can be passed to a template function without modifying the function's source code.

questions.question: The Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) states that subtypes must be substitutable for their base types. Which of the following is an LSP violation?

A `Cat` class inheriting from `Animal` and overriding `speak()` to return 'meow'
A `DieselCar` class inheriting from `Car` and adding a `refuelDiesel()` method

questions.correctAnswer: A `Square` class inheriting from `Rectangle` where `Square::setWidth()` also changes height — code expecting a `Rectangle` breaks.
questions.explanation: The Rectangle-Square problem is the canonical LSP violation. The subtype (Square) strengthens the base type's invariants (width == height). Code that works correctly with Rectangle (calling setWidth then setHeight independently) breaks with Square. The fix: don't use inheritance for Square-from-Rectangle — they don't have an 'is-a' relationship in terms of mutable behavior. when `setWidth` and `setHeight` interact unexpectedly

questions.question: The Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) says clients should not depend on interfaces they don't use. What's the C++ consequence of violating ISP?
options: Unnecessary recompilation — changing a method a client doesn't use still forces recompilation of all dependent translation units.
The compiler emits larger binaries because unused virtual functions are still included
Memory usage increases because the vtable grows with unused functions This be...
Run-time performance degrades because the CPU must check which methods are actually used
questions.correctAnswer: Unnecessary recompilation — changing a method a client doesn't use still forces recompilation of all dependent translation units.
questions.explanation: ISP is about minimizing interface surface. In C++, a fat interface means: (1) when ANY method's signature changes, ALL clients recompile even if they don't use that method, (2) implementing classes must stub out methods they don't support. The fix: split large interfaces into smaller, role-specific ones. A `Renderable` interface should not include `Serialize()`. Also, clients must provide implementations for pure virtual methods they don't need.

questions.question: The Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) says high-level modules should not depend on low-level modules — both should depend on abstractions. How does dependency injection implement this in C++?
options: The high-level class receives a pointer/reference to an abstract interface in its constructor.
The high-level class uses `#include` to bring in the low-level module's header

questions.correctAnswer: The high-level class receives a pointer/reference to an abstract interface in its constructor.
questions.explanation: DIP inverts the traditional dependency direction: instead of `GameEngine` → `OpenGLRenderer` (high depends on low), both depend on `IRenderer` (an abstraction). Dependency injection is the mechanism: the concrete renderer is created externally and injected via constructor, setter, or template parameter. This enables: testing (mock renderer), swapping implementations (OpenGL → Vulkan), and independent development. The concrete implementation is passed from outside — the high-level class only knows about the abstraction, not the implementation.

questions.question: Which SOLID principle does the Strategy pattern most directly implement?
options: Open-Closed Principle — you add new strategies without modifying the context class that uses them, keeping existing code untouched.
Single Responsibility Principle — each strategy class handles exactly one algorithm, keeping responsibilities cleanly separated and independently testable.
Liskov Substitution Principle — all strategy implementations conform to the same interface, allowing any strategy to substitute for any other safely.
Dependency Inversion Principle — the context depends on the abstract strategy interface rather than concrete implementations, inverting the dependency direction.
questions.correctAnswer: Open-Closed Principle — you add new strategies without modifying the context class that uses them, keeping existing code untouched.
questions.explanation: While Strategy touches multiple SOLID principles, its PRIMARY motivation is OCP: the context class is closed for modification (you don't change its code) but open for extension (you add new strategy subclasses). The context only knows about the `IStrategy` interface — new concrete strategies are plug-and-play.

questions.question: How would you explain the relationship between SRP and testability to a junior developer?
questions.correctAnswer: A class with one responsibility has one reason to fail, so tests are focused and diagnostic. When a class with 5 responsibilities fails a test, you don't know WHICH responsibility broke — you have to debug. With SRP, each class has its own test suite, and a failing test tells you exactly what went wrong. Additionally, classes with single responsibilities are easier to mock and isolate in unit tests because they have fewer dependencies.
questions.explanation: SRP and testability are deeply connected. Fat classes are hard to test because: (1) they need many mocks, (2) test failures are ambiguous, (3) setup code is complex. SRP naturally produces testable units: small, focused classes with clear contracts. This is why TDD practitioners often discover SRP violations — the test is hard to write because the class does too much.
acceptableAnswers: A class with one responsibility has one reason to fail, so tests are focused and diagnostic. When a class with 5 responsibilities fails a test, you don't know WHICH responsibility broke — you have to debug. With SRP, each class has its own test suite, and a failing test tells you exactly what went wrong. Additionally, classes with single responsibilities are easier to mock and isolate in unit tests because they have fewer dependencies.

questions.question: In the Observer pattern, what is the most common memory management trap?

questions.correctAnswer: The dangling observer problem: if an observer is destroyed without unsubscribing, the subject holds a dangling pointer.

questions.question: What problem does the Command pattern solve that a simple function call does not?

questions.question: What is the Meyers Singleton and why is it preferred over the classic 'static pointer + mutex' approach?

Since C++11, the compiler guarantees thread-safe initialization of local statics — only one thread runs the constructor. It's lazy (constructed on first `get()` call), and destroyed during static destruction (reverse order). This eliminated the need for the Double-Checked Locking Pattern (which was broken before C++11's memory model). automatically destroyed at program exit — no explicit `new`, no mutex, no memory leak

questions.question: When should you NOT use the Singleton pattern? Provide a CONCRETE scenario where it would be the wrong choice.
questions.correctAnswer: A logging system that different subsystems need to configure differently. If the physics system wants log level DEBUG and the rendering system wants log level ERROR, a Singleton Logger forces all subsystems to share the same configuration. The Singleton conflates 'only one instance exists' with 'only one type exists' — the second may be false. Solution: dependency injection — each subsystem gets its own Logger instance configured independently. Singleton is appropriate when the constraint is truly global (e.g., hardware device manager, memory allocator root).
questions.explanation: Singleton's main drawback is that it's global mutable state. It makes testing harder (can't inject a mock), creates hidden coupling (any code can access it), and prevents having multiple instances even when needed. The rule of thumb: use Singleton only when having more than one instance would be a LOGICAL ERROR (not just inconvenient), such as a hardware abstraction layer for a physical device.

The Singleton conflates 'only one instance exists' with 'only one type exists' — the second may be false. Singleton is appropriate when the constraint is truly global (e.g., hardware device manager, memory allocator root).

questions.question: Which design pattern would you use for a game's undo/redo system, and what additional concern does the "command state drift" trap address?
questions.correctAnswer: The Command pattern, with each action stored as a Command object containing both `execute()` and `undo()`. The 'command state drift' trap: if `undo()` tries to REVERSE the operation (e.g., 'move entity by -dx') instead of RESTORING the previous state, accumulated floating-point precision errors cause drift over many undo/redo cycles. The fix: store the BEFORE state as a snapshot in the command, and `undo()` restores it exactly.
questions.explanation: Undo/redo is the textbook Command pattern use case. Each user action creates a Command (e.g., `MoveEntityCommand(entity, oldPos, newPos)`) pushed onto a stack. Undo calls `command.undo()` which restores `oldPos`. The drift trap is subtle but important in games where tiny floating-point errors accumulate. Snapshot-based undo (store `oldPos`, restore it exactly) is correct; delta-based undo (move by `-dx`) drifts.
acceptableAnswers: The Command pattern, with each action stored as a Command object containing both `execute()` and `undo()`. The 'command state drift' trap: if `undo()` tries to REVERSE the operation (e.g., 'move entity by -dx') instead of RESTORING the previous state, accumulated floating-point precision errors cause drift over many undo/redo cycles. The fix: store the BEFORE state as a snapshot in the command, and `undo()` restores it exactly.

questions.question: The Visitor pattern's classic implementation uses 'double dispatch.' What does double dispatch mean in this context?
options: The correct `visit()` overload is chosen based on the runtime types of TWO objects: the visitor AND the element being visited.

questions.correctAnswer: The correct `visit()` overload is chosen based on the runtime types of TWO objects: the visitor AND the element being visited.
questions.explanation: Single dispatch (virtual functions): the function called depends on ONE runtime type (the object). Double dispatch: depends on TWO runtime types. Visitor achieves this via `element.accept(visitor)` → `visitor.visitConcreteElement(*this)` — the first call dispatches on the element type, the second on the visitor type. This is the mechanism that makes Visitor work without knowing all element types at compile time. The element's `accept()` dispatches to the visitor's `visit()` for the concrete element type.

questions.question: How does `std::variant` with `std::visit` solve the same problem as the Visitor pattern, and what advantage does it have?

`std::variant` is always faster than the Visitor pattern because it uses direct dispatch
`std::variant` allows new element types to be added without recompiling existing code
`std::variant` eliminates the need for the 'accept' method on each element
questions.correctAnswer: `std::variant` holds one of N types; `std::visit` applies a visitor (callable) to whichever type is currently active.
questions.explanation: `std::variant` + `std::visit` is the modern C++ replacement for many Visitor use cases. With the `overloaded` pattern (`template<class... Ts> struct overloaded : Ts... { using Ts::operator()...; };`), each type gets its own lambda. If you forget a type, the compiler errors. Trade-off: variant types are a closed set (known at compile time); classic Visitor allows adding element types at runtime. Unlike classic Visitor, the set of types is known at COMPILE TIME — you get exhaustive handling guarantees and no inheritance hierarchy is needed.

questions.question: CRTP (Curiously Recurring Template Pattern) enables static polymorphism. How does it eliminate the cost of virtual function calls?

questions.correctAnswer: The base class `Base<Derived>` calls `static_cast<Derived*>(this)->method()` — the call is resolved at COMPILE TIME via.
questions.explanation: CRTP: `class Derived : public Base<Derived>`. `Base<Derived>` knows the exact derived type at compile time, so `static_cast<Derived*>(this)->doWork()` is a direct function call — the compiler can even inline it. No vtable, no indirection, no runtime dispatch overhead. The cost: you can't store different derived types in a heterogeneous container (they're different types, unlike virtual inheritance where `Base*` can point to any `Derived`). templates, producing a direct function call with no vtable lookup

questions.question: The Bridge pattern's purpose is to 'decouple an abstraction from its implementation.' How does this differ from simply using an abstract interface?

questions.explanation: The classic bridge example: `N` Shape types (Circle, Square, Triangle) × `M` Renderers (OpenGL, Vulkan, DirectX) = `N × M` subclasses with inheritance alone. With Bridge: N Shape subclasses hold an `IRenderer*` — total `N + M` classes. Both hierarchies can evolve independently. This is the N×M → N+M transformation: composition over inheritance at the architectural level. An abstract interface only decouples ONE hierarchy from its client. Bridge enables the N×M → N+M class reduction.

questions.question: Type Erasure allows storing objects of different types in a single container without a common base class. What is the key mechanism that makes this work?

questions.correctAnswer: An internal 'concept' (abstract interface) and a templated 'model' (concrete wrapper).
questions.explanation: Type Erasure = external polymorphism without inheritance. The pattern: `class AnyShape { struct Concept { virtual void draw() = 0; }; template<typename T> struct Model : Concept { T obj; void draw() override { obj.draw(); } }; std::unique_ptr<Concept> p; };`. The constructor `template<typename T> AnyShape(T x)` creates a `Model<T>`. The outer class is NOT a template — it's a concrete type. This is how `std::function` and `std::any` work. The outer class holds a pointer to the concept; the model wraps any type that satisfies the interface. The template is hidden INSIDE the constructor, not on the class itself.

questions.question: What problem does External Polymorphism solve that regular inheritance cannot?

External Polymorphism creates a separate adapter hierarchy that wraps these types and provides the virtual interface. This is the ultimate 'open for extension' pattern — you extend types you don't own without modifying them. the polymorphism is external to the type, not baked into its class hierarchy

questions.question: Compare CRTP and virtual functions: in what scenario would CRTP be the BAD choice despite its performance advantage?
questions.correctAnswer: When you need to store different derived types in a single heterogeneous container. With virtual functions, `std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Base>>` can hold any `Derived`. With CRTP, each `Base<Derived1>` and `Base<Derived2>` are DIFFERENT types — you cannot store them in the same vector without type erasure. CRTP is for when you know the exact type at compile time and want zero-overhead dispatch; virtual functions are for when you need runtime polymorphism across unknown types.
questions.explanation: CRTP trades flexibility for performance. The `Base<Derived>` is a different type for each `Derived` — `Base<Circle>` and `Base<Square>` are completely unrelated types in the type system. You lose the ability to write generic code that operates on 'any shape' via a base pointer. This is the fundamental trade-off: compile-time polymorphism (CRTP, templates) vs runtime polymorphism (virtual functions).
acceptableAnswers: When you need to store different derived types in a single heterogeneous container. With virtual functions, `std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Base>>` can hold any `Derived`. With CRTP, each `Base<Derived1>` and `Base<Derived2>` are DIFFERENT types — you cannot store them in the same vector without type erasure. CRTP is for when you know the exact type at compile time and want zero-overhead dispatch; virtual functions are for when you need runtime polymorphism across unknown types.

questions.question: What is the Pimpl (Pointer to Implementation) idiom and what specific problem does it solve?
options: Pimpl hides implementation details in a source file behind an opaque pointer in the header.
