questions.question: At what physical address does the x86 CPU begin executing after power-on reset?
options: 0x00000000 (the start of physical memory) (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
0xFFFFFFF0 (16 bytes below the 4GB address space limit)
0xFFFFFFF0 (16 bytes below the 4GB address space limit)
questions.correctAnswer: 0xFFFFFFF0 (16 bytes below the 4GB address space limit)
questions.explanation: The reset vector on x86 is hardwired to 0xFFFFFFF0. The first instruction is a JMP to the firmware entry point. At this stage, RAM is NOT initialized — the CPU executes directly from the firmware ROM (BIOS flash chip or UEFI SPI flash).

questions.question: In the legacy BIOS boot sequence, where is the first sector of the boot device (MBR) loaded into RAM?
questions.explanation: BIOS loads the first 512-byte sector (Master Boot Record) from the boot device into physical RAM at address 0x00007C00, then jumps to it. This address was chosen for compatibility with the original IBM PC (1981) — 0x7C00 = 32KB - 1KB, leaving 32KB for the BIOS data area below it.

questions.question: What is a key limitation of legacy BIOS that UEFI overcomes?
options: BIOS cannot initialize PCIe devices, which is a documented behavior in the spec
BIOS runs in 16-bit real mode, limiting addressable memory to 1MB during boot
BIOS requires a GPU to be present before booting, which is a documented behavior in the spec
BIOS runs in 16-bit real mode, limiting addressable memory to 1MB during boot
questions.correctAnswer: BIOS runs in 16-bit real mode, limiting addressable memory to 1MB during boot
questions.explanation: Legacy BIOS operates in 16-bit real mode, which can only address 1MB of memory (20-bit address bus). The bootloader must transition the CPU to 32-bit protected mode or 64-bit long mode. UEFI boots directly into 32-bit or 64-bit mode, accessing the full address space from the start.

questions.question: What is the role of DXE (Driver Execution Environment) in the UEFI boot process?
options: It runs the Power-On Self-Test to check hardware integrity — this is explicitly stated in the specification
It loads UEFI drivers in parallel — CPU driver, PCIe bus driver, USB driver, filesystem driver
It loads the OS kernel directly without a bootloader — this is explicitly stated in the specification
It loads UEFI drivers in parallel — CPU driver, PCIe bus driver, USB driver, filesystem driver — to initialize the hardware platform
questions.correctAnswer: It loads UEFI drivers in parallel — CPU driver, PCIe bus driver, USB driver, filesystem driver
questions.explanation: DXE is the main UEFI initialization phase after PEI (Pre-EFI Initialization). It loads modular drivers that initialize CPU features (protected/long mode), enumerate the PCIe bus, bring up storage controllers (SATA/NVMe), and mount filesystems (FAT32 for the EFI System Partition). Drivers load in parallel for faster boot times.

questions.question: What is the EFI System Partition (ESP) and why is it required?
options: A special partition holding the OS kernel image, which is a documented behavior in the spec
A FAT32 partition containing UEFI bootloaders — the firmware reads boot entries from here
A recovery partition that stores factory system images, which is a documented behavior in the spec
A FAT32 partition containing UEFI bootloaders — the firmware reads boot entries from here
questions.correctAnswer: A FAT32 partition containing UEFI bootloaders — the firmware reads boot entries from here
questions.explanation: The ESP is a FAT32-formatted partition (typically 100-500MB) that holds UEFI bootloader files (e.g., ESP/EFI/BOOT/BOOTX64.EFI). UEFI firmware has a built-in FAT32 driver — it can read files from the ESP without any OS loaded. Boot entries are stored in NVRAM and reference paths within the ESP.

questions.question: What happens during the GRUB (GRand Unified Bootloader) stage in the boot process?
options: GRUB directly executes the OS kernel with no intermediate steps — this is explicitly stated in the specification (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
GRUB presents a boot menu, loads the selected kernel image and initrd into memory, sets up kernel parameters, and jumps to the kernel entry point
GRUB performs a full memory test before loading the OS — this is explicitly stated in the specification
GRUB presents a boot menu, loads the selected kernel image and initrd into memory, sets up kernel parameters, and jumps to the kernel entry point
questions.correctAnswer: GRUB presents a boot menu, loads the selected kernel image and initrd into memory, sets up kernel parameters, and jumps to the kernel entry point
questions.explanation: GRUB (or systemd-boot, or Windows Boot Manager) is the intermediate stage between firmware and kernel. It reads the kernel image (vmlinuz) and initial RAM disk (initrd) from the boot partition into memory, sets up the kernel command line, and transfers control. The initrd contains essential drivers and scripts needed to mount the real root filesystem.

questions.question: What is Secure Boot and what does it verify?
options: It encrypts the entire boot partition to prevent tampering — this is explicitly stated in the specification
It cryptographically verifies the signature of each boot component (firmware → bootloader → kernel) using a chain of trust rooted in platform keys
It verifies that the RAM is functioning correctly before booting — this is explicitly stated in the specification (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
It cryptographically verifies the signature of each boot component (firmware → bootloader → kernel) using a chain of trust rooted in platform keys
questions.correctAnswer: It cryptographically verifies the signature of each boot component (firmware → bootloader → kernel) using a chain of trust rooted in platform keys
questions.explanation: Secure Boot (UEFI feature) verifies that each stage of the boot chain is signed by a trusted key. The platform key (PK) is the root of trust; the Key Exchange Key (KEK) signs allowed bootloaders; the signature database (db) lists allowed hashes. If any component's signature fails verification, the boot is halted — preventing rootkits and bootkits.

questions.question: x86 power-on reset address?
questions.correctAnswer: 0xFFFFFFF0 (16 bytes below 4GB limit)
questions.explanation: Reset vector hardwired. First instruction is JMP to firmware entry.

questions.question: Legacy BIOS loads MBR to what address?
questions.explanation: BIOS loads first 512 bytes of boot device to 0x7C00 and jumps to it.

questions.question: Key BIOS limitation overcome by UEFI?
options: 16-bit real mode limits memory to 1MB during boot
questions.correctAnswer: 16-bit real mode limits memory to 1MB during boot
questions.explanation: UEFI boots directly into 32/64-bit mode, accessing full address space.

questions.question: Role of DXE in UEFI?
options: Loads UEFI drivers in parallel (CPU, PCIe, USB, FS)
questions.correctAnswer: Loads UEFI drivers in parallel (CPU, PCIe, USB, FS)
questions.explanation: DXE is main UEFI init phase after PEI. Modular drivers for hardware initialization.

questions.question: EFI System Partition (ESP) file system?
questions.correctAnswer: FAT32
questions.explanation: ESP is FAT32-formatted. UEFI firmware has built-in FAT32 driver.

questions.question: What does GRUB do?
options: Presents menu, loads kernel+initrd, sets params, jumps to kernel
questions.correctAnswer: Presents menu, loads kernel+initrd, sets params, jumps to kernel
questions.explanation: GRUB is the bootloader between firmware and kernel.

questions.question: What does Secure Boot verify?
options: Cryptographic signatures of each boot component (FW->bootloader->kernel)
questions.correctAnswer: Cryptographic signatures of each boot component (FW->bootloader->kernel)
questions.explanation: Chain of trust rooted in platform keys. Halt boot if signature fails.

questions.question: What is the difference between BIOS and UEFI boot modes?
options: BIOS=16-bit real mode, MBR; UEFI=32/64-bit mode, GPT, EFI apps
questions.correctAnswer: BIOS=16-bit real mode, MBR; UEFI=32/64-bit mode, GPT, EFI apps
questions.explanation: UEFI recognizes GPT partition tables, loads bootloader .efi files from ESP.

questions.question: What is the UEFI boot manager?
options: Firmware component that reads NVRAM boot entries and launches the bootloader
questions.correctAnswer: Firmware component that reads NVRAM boot entries and launches the bootloader
questions.explanation: Boot entries stored in NVRAM. User can select from menu. Falls back to ESP/EFI/BOOT/BOOTX64.EFI.

questions.question: What is the role of PEI (Pre-EFI Initialization)?
options: Minimal CPU/memory init to set up DRAM
questions.correctAnswer: Minimal CPU/memory init to set up DRAM
questions.explanation: PEI is first UEFI phase. Only has cache-as-RAM (CAR). Initializes DRAM, then jumps to DXE.

questions.question: What is UEFI capsule update?
questions.correctAnswer: Secure firmware update mechanism
questions.explanation: UEFI can update firmware by copying a capsule to the ESP and resetting. No OS needed.

questions.question: What does the UEFI Boot Services provide?
options: ExitBootServices() — memory map, time, protocols available before kernel takes over
questions.correctAnswer: ExitBootServices() — memory map, time, protocols available before kernel takes over
questions.explanation: After ExitBootServices, UEFI runtime services (variable store, reset) remain.

questions.question: What is Coreboot?
questions.correctAnswer: Open-source firmware replacement for BIOS/UEFI
questions.explanation: Coreboot initializes hardware minimally then jumps to payload (SeaBIOS, UEFI, etc.).

questions.question: What does SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads) mean in GPU architecture?
options: All GPU cores run different instructions but on the same data — this is explicitly stated in the specification
Threads are grouped into warps (NVIDIA: 32 threads, AMD: 64 threads) that all execute the same instruction simultaneously on different data
GPU threads execute in lockstep only when accessing shared memory — this is explicitly stated in the specification (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
Threads are grouped into warps (NVIDIA: 32 threads, AMD: 64 threads) that all execute the same instruction simultaneously on different data
questions.correctAnswer: Threads are grouped into warps (NVIDIA: 32 threads, AMD: 64 threads) that all execute the same instruction simultaneously on different data
questions.explanation: SIMT is the GPU's fundamental execution model. A warp (NVIDIA, 32 threads) or wavefront (AMD, 64 threads) shares one instruction fetch and decode unit. All 32 threads execute the same instruction on their own data lanes — enormous power and area savings compared to 32 independent cores each fetching their own instructions.

questions.question: What is warp divergence and why is it a performance killer?
options: When warps from different SMs try to access the same memory bank simultaneously, which is a documented behavior in the spec
When threads in the same warp take different branches of an if/else — both paths execute serially, halving throughput
When warps complete at different times, causing synchronization overhead, which is a documented behavior in the spec
When threads in the same warp take different branches of an if/else — both paths execute serially, halving throughput
questions.correctAnswer: When threads in the same warp take different branches of an if/else — both paths execute serially, halving throughput
questions.explanation: Because all threads in a warp share one program counter, when they diverge at a branch, the GPU serializes both paths using an active mask. Threads taking the 'if' path execute while 'else' threads are masked off, then vice versa. Worst case: 32-way divergence = 1/32 throughput. This is why GPU code should avoid data-dependent branching within a warp.

questions.question: How does the GPU hide memory latency without out-of-order execution or branch prediction?
options: It uses a much faster type of VRAM that has lower latency than system RAM, which is a documented behavior in the spec (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
It maintains many active warps per SM — when one warp stalls waiting for memory, the scheduler instantly switches to another
It caches all global memory accesses in L1 cache — this is explicitly stated in the specification
It maintains many active warps per SM — when one warp stalls waiting for memory, the scheduler instantly switches to another
questions.correctAnswer: It maintains many active warps per SM — when one warp stalls waiting for memory, the scheduler instantly switches to another
questions.explanation: GPU memory latency is ~400 cycles. The GPU doesn't try to reduce it — it hides it with parallelism. An SM can have 64 active warps (2048 threads). Warp scheduling is zero-cost: the scheduler picks a ready warp from the pool every cycle. As long as there's always a ready warp, the execution units stay busy while other warps wait for memory.

questions.question: What is occupancy in GPU computing?
options: The percentage of GPU memory currently in use, which is a documented behavior in the spec
The ratio of active warps on an SM to the maximum theoretical warps the SM can support
The number of SMs actually powered on during execution, which is a documented behavior in the spec
The ratio of active warps on an SM to the maximum theoretical warps the SM can support
questions.correctAnswer: The ratio of active warps on an SM to the maximum theoretical warps the SM can support
questions.explanation: Occupancy = active_warps / max_warps_per_SM. Higher occupancy means more warps available to hide latency. But occupancy is limited by resource usage: registers per thread (more registers = fewer threads fit) and shared memory per block. The optimal occupancy isn't always 100% — sometimes fewer threads with more registers each runs faster.

questions.question: What is shared memory in GPU architecture and why is it critical for performance?
options: Memory shared between the GPU and CPU over PCIe — this is explicitly stated in the specification
On-chip SRAM (~128KB per SM) shared among threads in the same block
System RAM that the GPU can access via DMA — this is explicitly stated in the specification
On-chip SRAM (~128KB per SM) shared among threads in the same block — ~100x lower latency than global memory
questions.correctAnswer: On-chip SRAM (~128KB per SM) shared among threads in the same block
questions.explanation: Shared memory is a programmer-managed scratchpad on each SM. Latency: ~20 cycles vs ~400 for global memory (GDDR6X/HBM). Threads in a block can cooperatively load data from global memory into shared memory, synchronize with __syncthreads(), and then all threads access the data at near-register speed. Critical for matrix multiplication, convolution, and any algorithm with data reuse.

questions.question: How many CUDA cores does an RTX 4090 have and how are they organized?
options: 16,384 CUDA cores organized as 128 SMs × 128 cores each
8,192 CUDA cores organized as 64 SMs × 128 cores each
24,576 CUDA cores organized as 96 SMs × 256 cores each (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
10,240 CUDA cores organized as 80 SMs × 128 cores each
questions.correctAnswer: 16,384 CUDA cores organized as 128 SMs × 128 cores each
questions.explanation: The RTX 4090 (AD102) has 128 Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs), each with 128 CUDA cores = 16,384 total. Each SM also contains 4 tensor cores, 1 RT core, 128KB L1/shared memory, and a warp scheduler that can manage 64 warps (2048 threads). Total transistor budget per SM: ~2-3 million vs ~50-100 million per CPU core.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a CPU core and a GPU SM's approach to parallelism?
options: CPU cores have more ALUs per core than GPU SMs — this is explicitly stated in the specification
CPU cores use out-of-order execution and branch prediction for single-thread performance
CPU cores use SIMT; GPU SMs use SMT — this is explicitly stated in the specification
CPU cores use out-of-order execution and branch prediction for single-thread performance; GPU SMs use massive thread-level parallelism to hide latency
questions.correctAnswer: CPU cores use out-of-order execution and branch prediction for single-thread performance
questions.explanation: A CPU core (~50-100M transistors) is optimized for single-thread latency: out-of-order execution, sophisticated branch predictors, large private caches. A GPU SM (~2-3M transistors) is optimized for throughput: simple in-order execution, zero-cost warp switching, tiny caches per thread but massive aggregate bandwidth. The same transistor budget buys 128 simple processors instead of 1 complex one.

questions.question: What is SIMT?
options: All cores different instructions same data
Threads grouped into warps (32 NVIDIA, 64 AMD) executing same instruction simultaneously
GPU threads lockstep only for shared memory
questions.correctAnswer: Threads grouped into warps (32 NVIDIA, 64 AMD) executing same instruction simultaneously
questions.explanation: One instruction fetch/decode shared across 32 threads. Enormous area savings.

questions.question: Warp divergence?
options: Threads in same warp take different branches — both paths execute serially
questions.correctAnswer: Threads in same warp take different branches — both paths execute serially
questions.explanation: Shared program counter means if/else both execute. Worst case: 1/32 throughput.

questions.question: How does GPU hide memory latency?
options: Many active warps per SM — instant switch when one stalls
questions.correctAnswer: Many active warps per SM — instant switch when one stalls
questions.explanation: An SM can have 64 active warps. Zero-cost warp scheduling hides 400-cycle latency.

questions.question: What is shared memory in GPU?
options: On-chip SRAM (~128KB per SM) shared by threads in same block
questions.correctAnswer: On-chip SRAM (~128KB per SM) shared by threads in same block
questions.explanation: ~20 cycle latency vs ~400 for global memory. Critical for data reuse.

questions.question: RTX 4090 CUDA cores and organization?
questions.correctAnswer: 16,384 cores, 128 SMs x 128 cores each
questions.explanation: AD102 has 128 SMs, each with 128 CUDA cores, 4 tensor cores, 1 RT core.

questions.question: CPU core vs GPU SM approach to parallelism?
options: CPU: out-of-order + branch prediction for single-thread perf. GPU: massive thread-level parallelism
questions.correctAnswer: CPU: out-of-order + branch prediction for single-thread perf. GPU: massive thread-level parallelism
questions.explanation: CPU core ~50-100M transistors. GPU SM ~2-3M. Same budget buys 128 simple processors.

questions.question: What is a Streaming Multiprocessor (SM)?
options: NVIDIA's name for a GPU compute unit containing CUDA cores, warp scheduler, shared memory
A special CPU core for graphics
questions.correctAnswer: NVIDIA's name for a GPU compute unit containing CUDA cores, warp scheduler, shared memory
questions.explanation: Each SM has CUDA cores, tensor cores, RT cores, scheduler, and register file.

questions.question: What is __syncthreads() in CUDA?
options: A barrier — all threads in block must reach it before any proceed
A thread sync across all blocks
questions.correctAnswer: A barrier — all threads in block must reach it before any proceed
questions.explanation: Ensures all shared memory writes are visible to all threads before continuing.

questions.question: What is L1 cache on a GPU used for?
options: Shared memory + L1 data cache combined (configurable partition)
questions.correctAnswer: Shared memory + L1 data cache combined (configurable partition)
questions.explanation: Can be split 50/50 or fully as shared memory for compute workloads.

questions.question: What is the GPU GigaThread Engine?
questions.correctAnswer: Distributes thread blocks to SMs
questions.explanation: GigaThread Engine assigns CTAs to SMs for load balancing across the GPU.

questions.question: What is Unified Memory in CUDA?
options: Single address space shared between CPU and GPU
questions.correctAnswer: Single address space shared between CPU and GPU
questions.explanation: CUDA 6+: CPU and GPU pointers can be accessed directly. Pages migrate automatically.

questions.question: What is wavefront on AMD GPUs?
options: 64 threads executing in lockstep (AMD's term for warp)
questions.correctAnswer: 64 threads executing in lockstep (AMD's term for warp)
questions.explanation: AMD uses 64-thread wavefronts. Larger than NVIDIA's 32-thread warps.

questions.question: What is the RT core on NVIDIA GPUs?
options: Hardware unit for BVH traversal and ray-triangle intersection
questions.correctAnswer: Hardware unit for BVH traversal and ray-triangle intersection
questions.explanation: Dedicated RT cores perform ray-intersection tests in hardware, accelerating ray tracing.

questions.question: What are tensor cores used for?
options: Matrix multiply-accumulate — used for DLSS, AI, and FP16/INT8 compute
questions.correctAnswer: Matrix multiply-accumulate — used for DLSS, AI, and FP16/INT8 compute
questions.explanation: Tensor cores perform D = A*B + C in one operation. Critical for denoising and neural rendering.

questions.question: What is the fundamental advantage of interrupts over polling for I/O?
options: Interrupts are faster than polling in all cases — this is explicitly stated in the specification
The CPU doesn't waste cycles constantly checking device status — it's notified only when attention is needed
Interrupts can handle multiple devices simultaneously while polling cannot, which is a documented behavior in the spec
The CPU doesn't waste cycles constantly checking device status — it's notified only when attention is needed
questions.correctAnswer: The CPU doesn't waste cycles constantly checking device status — it's notified only when attention is needed
questions.explanation: Polling: the CPU repeatedly reads a device status register in a loop, burning cycles whether or not data is ready. Interrupts: the device signals the CPU via the interrupt controller (APIC) when data is ready. The CPU handles it and returns to other work. For high-throughput scenarios (10G+ networking), polling can actually be faster because interrupt overhead exceeds the cost of busy-waiting.

questions.question: What is the difference between an interrupt and an exception?
options: Interrupts can be masked; exceptions cannot — that's the only difference
Interrupts are handled in user space; exceptions are handled in kernel space
There is no difference — the terms are interchangeable
questions.correctAnswer: Interrupts come from hardware devices (asynchronous)
questions.explanation: Interrupts are asynchronous — a network card signals 'packet arrived' at any time, unrelated to the current instruction. Exceptions are synchronous — a page fault or division by zero happens as a direct result of the instruction being executed. Faults (like page fault) can be fixed and the instruction retried; traps (like breakpoint) execute after the instruction; aborts (like machine check) are unrecoverable.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) being split into top half and bottom half?
options: The top half runs on one core and the bottom half on another for load balancing
The top half runs with interrupts disabled, doing minimal work
The split is architectural only — both halves run identically
The top half runs with interrupts disabled, doing minimal work; the bottom half runs with interrupts enabled, doing the heavy processing
questions.correctAnswer: The top half runs with interrupts disabled, doing minimal work
questions.explanation: Top half (hard IRQ): runs immediately with interrupts disabled on that core — must be fast (microseconds). It acknowledges the interrupt, copies data from device registers, and schedules the bottom half. Bottom half (softirq/tasklet/workqueue): runs later with interrupts enabled, does the heavy processing (network stack, block I/O completion). This prevents long-running ISRs from blocking all other interrupts.

questions.question: What is MSI/MSI-X (Message-Signaled Interrupts) and why is it superior to legacy pin-based interrupts?
options: MSI uses a dedicated interrupt wire per device for lower latency — this is explicitly stated in the specification (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
MSI sends interrupts as PCIe memory writes — each device gets its own interrupt vector, eliminating shared IRQ conflicts
MSI interrupts are encrypted for security — this is explicitly stated in the specification
MSI sends interrupts as PCIe memory writes — each device gets its own interrupt vector, eliminating shared IRQ conflicts
questions.correctAnswer: MSI sends interrupts as PCIe memory writes — each device gets its own interrupt vector, eliminating shared IRQ conflicts
questions.explanation: Legacy INTx interrupts use physical wires shared by multiple devices — the ISR must poll all devices on the shared line to find who interrupted. MSI uses PCIe Memory Write TLPs to deliver interrupts: the device writes to a configured address, and the APIC routes it to the target CPU. MSI-X extends this with up to 2048 vectors per device, enabling per-queue interrupts for NVMe and high-end NICs.

questions.question: What is interrupt coalescing and when would you use it?
options: Combining multiple interrupt handlers into one for code deduplication, which is a documented behavior in the spec (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
Delaying interrupt delivery to batch multiple events into one interrupt — reduces CPU overhead at the cost of added latency
Converting level-triggered interrupts to edge-triggered — this is explicitly stated in the specification
Delaying interrupt delivery to batch multiple events into one interrupt — reduces CPU overhead at the cost of added latency
questions.correctAnswer: Delaying interrupt delivery to batch multiple events into one interrupt — reduces CPU overhead at the cost of added latency
questions.explanation: At high packet rates (10G+ networking), one interrupt per packet would saturate the CPU with ISR overhead. Interrupt coalescing waits until N packets arrive or T microseconds pass, then fires one interrupt to process them all. Trade-off: lower CPU usage but higher latency. Gaming NICs disable coalescing for minimum latency; server NICs tune it for throughput.

questions.question: How does UE5's tick system relate to hardware interrupts?
options: UE5 replaces hardware interrupts with its own software interrupt system — this is explicitly stated in the specification (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
UE's tick system is software polling at a fixed interval (~16ms at 60fps) — it's the OPPOSITE of interrupt-driven design, chosen for determinism
Every UE tick is triggered by a hardware timer interrupt — this is explicitly stated in the specification
UE's tick system is software polling at a fixed interval (~16ms at 60fps) — it's the OPPOSITE of interrupt-driven design, chosen for determinism
questions.correctAnswer: UE's tick system is software polling at a fixed interval (~16ms at 60fps) — it's the OPPOSITE of interrupt-driven design, chosen for determinism
questions.explanation: Game engines use polling (tick-based) rather than interrupt-driven design because games need deterministic, predictable update ordering. An interrupt-driven architecture would mean game logic executes at unpredictable times when input/network packets arrive — debugging nightmare. The OS handles hardware interrupts; the engine polls input state once per frame.

questions.question: What is an NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) and when is it used?
options: An interrupt that the CPU cannot ignore — used for critical hardware errors like memory parity errors and watchdog timeouts
A software interrupt that bypasses the interrupt vector table — this is explicitly stated in the specification
An interrupt generated by user-space programs for real-time signaling, which is a documented behavior in the spec (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
A specially optimized interrupt with zero latency overhead — this is explicitly stated in the specification
questions.correctAnswer: An interrupt that the CPU cannot ignore — used for critical hardware errors like memory parity errors and watchdog timeouts
questions.explanation: NMI cannot be masked by the CPU's interrupt flag (IF in RFLAGS). It's reserved for catastrophic hardware events: ECC memory uncorrectable errors, hardware watchdog timer expiry, PCIe SERR (system error). The NMI handler runs immediately regardless of what the CPU is doing — used for crash dumps, kernel debugging, and hardware failure logging.

questions.question: Fundamental advantage of interrupts over polling?
options: CPU doesn't waste cycles checking device status — notified when needed
questions.correctAnswer: CPU doesn't waste cycles checking device status — notified when needed
questions.explanation: Polling: CPU loops reading status register. Interrupts: device signals when ready.

questions.question: Interrupt vs Exception difference?
options: Interrupts from hardware (async); exceptions from instructions (sync)
questions.correctAnswer: Interrupts from hardware (async); exceptions from instructions (sync)
questions.explanation: Interrupts: network card. Exceptions: page fault (synchronous to instruction).

questions.question: Why split ISR into top/bottom half?
options: Top half runs with interrupts disabled, minimal work; bottom half does heavy processing
questions.correctAnswer: Top half runs with interrupts disabled, minimal work; bottom half does heavy processing
questions.explanation: Top half: acknowledge device, copy registers, schedule bottom. Bottom: heavy work with interrupts enabled.

questions.question: MSI/MSI-X advantage over legacy pin-based interrupts?
options: MSI sends interrupts as PCIe memory writes — each device gets own vector
questions.correctAnswer: MSI sends interrupts as PCIe memory writes — each device gets own vector
questions.explanation: Legacy INTx shared by multiple devices. MSI-X supports up to 2048 vectors per device.

questions.question: Interrupt coalescing?
options: Delay interrupt to batch events — lower CPU at cost of latency
questions.correctAnswer: Delay interrupt to batch events — lower CPU at cost of latency
questions.explanation: At high packet rates, coalesce N packets into one interrupt. Gaming: disable. Server: tune.

questions.question: NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) used for?
options: Critical hardware errors (ECC errors, watchdog timeout)
questions.correctAnswer: Critical hardware errors (ECC errors, watchdog timeout)
questions.explanation: Cannot be masked by IF flag. Used for crash dumps and hardware failure logging.

questions.question: What is an APIC?
options: Advanced Programmable Interrupt Controller — routes interrupts to specific CPU cores
questions.correctAnswer: Advanced Programmable Interrupt Controller — routes interrupts to specific CPU cores
questions.explanation: Local APIC per core. I/O APIC receives device interrupts and routes to target core.

questions.question: What is the Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT)?
options: Table mapping interrupt vectors to handler addresses
questions.correctAnswer: Table mapping interrupt vectors to handler addresses
questions.explanation: On x86, IDT has 256 entries. Each maps vector number to ISR code segment+offset.

questions.question: What is an IRQ?
options: Interrupt ReQuest — numbered line a device uses to signal
questions.correctAnswer: Interrupt ReQuest — numbered line a device uses to signal
questions.explanation: Each device gets an IRQ number (0-15 legacy, more with MSI). OS maps IRQ to handler.

questions.question: Why might polling be faster than interrupts?
options: At very high throughput, ISR overhead exceeds cost of busy-waiting
questions.correctAnswer: At very high throughput, ISR overhead exceeds cost of busy-waiting
questions.explanation: 10G+ networking: interrupt per packet saturates CPU. Polling in batches is more efficient.

questions.question: What is a softirq in Linux?
options: Deferred interrupt processing — runs at softirq context with interrupts enabled
questions.correctAnswer: Deferred interrupt processing — runs at softirq context with interrupts enabled
questions.explanation: After top half schedules, softirq runs later. Used for networking RX/TX and block I/O.

questions.question: What is the interrupt vector table on x86?
options: IDT — 256 entries, each pointing to ISR
questions.correctAnswer: IDT — 256 entries, each pointing to ISR
questions.explanation: Vectors 0-31: CPU exceptions. 32-255: hardware interrupts and system calls.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a Type 1 (bare-metal) and Type 2 (hosted) hypervisor?
options: Type 1 runs directly on hardware; Type 2 runs as a program inside a host OS
Type 1 supports more VMs than Type 2
Type 1 uses hardware virtualization; Type 2 uses software emulation
Type 1 is for servers; Type 2 is for desktops — there's no technical difference
questions.correctAnswer: Type 1 runs directly on hardware; Type 2 runs as a program inside a host OS
questions.explanation: Type 1 hypervisors (VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V, Xen) run directly on the physical hardware with no underlying OS — the hypervisor IS the operating system. Type 2 hypervisors (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation) run as an application inside a host OS. Type 1 has lower overhead (no host OS to go through); Type 2 is easier to install and use for development.

questions.question: What do Intel VT-x and AMD-V provide that makes efficient virtualization possible?
options: They provide software APIs for creating virtual machines — this is explicitly stated in the specification
They add a new CPU mode (VMX root/non-root) where the CPU traps privileged instructions so the hypervisor can emulate them
They provide hardware encryption for VM memory isolation — this is explicitly stated in the specification
They add a new CPU mode (VMX root/non-root) where the CPU traps privileged instructions so the hypervisor can emulate them — eliminating the need for binary translation
questions.correctAnswer: They add a new CPU mode (VMX root/non-root) where the CPU traps privileged instructions so the hypervisor can emulate them
questions.explanation: Before VT-x/AMD-V, hypervisors used binary translation (rewriting guest kernel code on the fly) or paravirtualization (modifying the guest OS). Hardware virtualization adds VMX root (hypervisor) and non-root (guest) modes. When a guest executes a privileged instruction (like MOV CR3 to change page tables), the CPU does a VM exit to the hypervisor, which emulates the instruction, then does a VM entry to resume the guest. This is fast enough for near-native performance.

questions.question: What are Extended Page Tables (EPT/NPT) and why are they critical for memory virtualization?
options: They extend the guest's page tables to support more RAM — this is explicitly stated in the specification
They provide a second layer of address translation: guest virtual → guest physical → host physical
They encrypt page table entries to prevent VM escape attacks — this is explicitly stated in the specification
They provide a second layer of address translation: guest virtual → guest physical → host physical — eliminating the hypervisor's shadow page table overhead
questions.correctAnswer: They provide a second layer of address translation: guest virtual → guest physical → host physical
questions.explanation: Without EPT, the hypervisor maintains shadow page tables — copies of the guest's page tables remapped to host physical addresses. Every guest page table modification causes a VM exit. With EPT, the CPU's MMU walks TWO page tables: the guest's page table (GVA→GPA) then the EPT (GPA→HPA). The guest can modify its page tables freely (no VM exits); only EPT violations trap to the hypervisor. This revolutionized VM performance.

questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between Docker containers and virtual machines?
options: Docker uses a different filesystem format than VMs
Containers share the host OS kernel; VMs each run their own complete OS kernel
Containers are slower than VMs because of the shared kernel overhead (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
Containers share the host OS kernel; VMs each run their own complete OS kernel
questions.correctAnswer: Containers share the host OS kernel; VMs each run their own complete OS kernel
questions.explanation: Containers use OS-level virtualization: all containers on a host share the same Linux kernel. Isolation comes from namespaces (pid, net, mnt, uts, ipc, user, cgroup) — each container sees its own process tree, network stack, filesystem, etc. VMs run separate kernels with hardware-enforced isolation via VT-x/EPT. Containers: lightweight, fast startup, no hardware emulation. VMs: stronger isolation, can run different OSes.

questions.question: What is the 'noisy neighbor' problem in virtualization?
options: When a VM's console output interferes with neighboring VMs — this is explicitly stated in the specification (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
When one VM consumes disproportionate shared resources (CPU cache, memory bandwidth, disk I/O) and degrades performance of other VMs on the same physical host
When VMs with similar IP addresses cause routing conflicts — this is explicitly stated in the specification
When one VM consumes disproportionate shared resources (CPU cache, memory bandwidth, disk I/O) and degrades performance of other VMs on the same physical host
questions.correctAnswer: When one VM consumes disproportionate shared resources (CPU cache, memory bandwidth, disk I/O) and degrades performance of other VMs on the same physical host
questions.explanation: While CPU cores and RAM can be dedicated to VMs, shared resources like last-level cache, memory bandwidth, and storage I/O can't be perfectly isolated. A 'noisy' VM running memory-bandwidth-intensive workloads (like video transcoding) pollutes the L3 cache and saturates memory channels, slowing down co-located VMs. Cloud providers mitigate this with resource reservation and live migration.

questions.question: What is SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization) and why is it important for GPU virtualization?
options: It allows VMs to share a single GPU via software scheduling — this is explicitly stated in the specification
A single physical PCIe device exposes multiple 'virtual functions' — each VM gets direct hardware access to its own virtual device, achieving near-native I/O performance
It provides a virtual GPU driver that translates API calls to the physical GPU — this is explicitly stated in the specification (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
A single physical PCIe device exposes multiple 'virtual functions' — each VM gets direct hardware access to its own virtual device, achieving near-native I/O performance
questions.correctAnswer: A single physical PCIe device exposes multiple 'virtual functions' — each VM gets direct hardware access to its own virtual device, achieving near-native I/O performance
questions.explanation: SR-IOV splits a physical PCIe device into one Physical Function (PF, managed by the hypervisor) and multiple Virtual Functions (VFs, assigned directly to VMs). Each VF gets its own PCIe config space, BARs, and MSI-X vectors. The guest VM's driver talks directly to the VF hardware via DMA with IOMMU-enforced isolation. Used for GPU virtualization (NVIDIA vGPU, AMD MxGPU) and high-performance networking.

questions.question: Type 1 vs Type 2 hypervisor?
options: Type 1 runs on hardware; Type 2 runs inside host OS
Type 1 uses HW virt, Type 2 uses SW emulation
questions.correctAnswer: Type 1 runs on hardware; Type 2 runs inside host OS
questions.explanation: Type 1: ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V. Lower overhead. Type 2: VirtualBox, Workstation.

questions.question: Intel VT-x / AMD-V provide what?
options: New CPU mode (VMX root/non-root) — privileged instructions VM-exit to hypervisor
questions.correctAnswer: New CPU mode (VMX root/non-root) — privileged instructions VM-exit to hypervisor
questions.explanation: Before VT-x: binary translation. Now: HW trap on privileged instructions.

questions.question: Extended Page Tables (EPT/NPT)?
options: Second layer: guest virtual -> guest physical -> host physical
questions.correctAnswer: Second layer: guest virtual -> guest physical -> host physical
questions.explanation: Without EPT: shadow page tables + VM exit on every guest page table modification.

questions.question: Docker containers vs VMs?
options: Containers share host kernel; VMs run separate kernel
questions.correctAnswer: Containers share host kernel; VMs run separate kernel
questions.explanation: Containers: OS-level virtualization via namespaces/cgroups. Fast startup, light.

questions.question: Noisy neighbor problem?
options: One VM consumes disproportionate shared resources (cache, mem bandwidth, I/O), degrading others
questions.correctAnswer: One VM consumes disproportionate shared resources (cache, mem bandwidth, I/O), degrading others
questions.explanation: Shared L3 cache and memory bandwidth can't be perfectly isolated.

questions.question: What is SR-IOV?
options: Single PCIe device exposes multiple VFs — each VM gets direct hardware access
questions.correctAnswer: Single PCIe device exposes multiple VFs — each VM gets direct hardware access
questions.explanation: PF (Physical Function) managed by hypervisor. VFs assigned directly to VMs.

questions.question: What is PCI passthrough?
options: Assigning entire PCIe device to a VM, bypassing hypervisor
questions.correctAnswer: Assigning entire PCIe device to a VM, bypassing hypervisor
questions.explanation: VM talks to device directly. Requires IOMMU for DMA isolation.

questions.question: What is a VM-Exit?
options: Transition from guest mode to hypervisor on privileged instruction or event
questions.correctAnswer: Transition from guest mode to hypervisor on privileged instruction or event
questions.explanation: Hypervisor emulates the operation, then VM-Entry resumes guest. Each VM-Exit costs ~1000 cycles.

questions.question: What is paravirtualization?
options: Guest OS is modified to use hypervisor calls instead of privileged instructions
questions.correctAnswer: Guest OS is modified to use hypervisor calls instead of privileged instructions
questions.explanation: Xen paravirtual: guest knows it's virtualized and calls hypervisor directly. No trap-and-emulate.

questions.question: What is KSM (Kernel Same-page Merging)?
options: Deduplicates identical memory pages across VMs to save RAM
questions.correctAnswer: Deduplicates identical memory pages across VMs to save RAM
questions.explanation: Multiple VMs running same OS: many identical pages. KSM scans, merges, marks CoW.

questions.question: What is a cgroup in Linux?
options: Control group — limits, accounts for, and isolates resource usage (CPU, memory, I/O)
questions.correctAnswer: Control group — limits, accounts for, and isolates resource usage (CPU, memory, I/O)
questions.explanation: cgroups control how much CPU, memory, and I/O a process group can use.

questions.question: What is a Linux namespace?
options: Isolates and virtualizes system resources — each container gets its own view
questions.correctAnswer: Isolates and virtualizes system resources — each container gets its own view
questions.explanation: Namespaces: pid, net, mnt, uts, ipc, user, cgroup. Each container sees its own process tree.

questions.question: Hypervisor:
questions.correctAnswer: Creates and manages VMs
questions.explanation: Type 1 (bare metal) or Type 2 (hosted)

questions.question: Full virtualization:
options: Unmodified guest OS, full hardware emulation
questions.correctAnswer: Unmodified guest OS, full hardware emulation
questions.explanation: VM thinks it's on real hardware

questions.question: Container vs VM:
questions.correctAnswer: Container shares host kernel
questions.explanation: Namespaces + cgroups

questions.question: Docker uses:
questions.correctAnswer: Linux namespaces and cgroups
questions.explanation: Lightweight isolation

questions.question: KVM:
questions.correctAnswer: Kernel-based Virtual Machine (type 1)
questions.explanation: Linux kernel module

questions.question: VT-x/AMD-V provide:
questions.correctAnswer: Hardware virtualization support
questions.explanation: CPU extensions for VMs

questions.question: cgroups limit:
questions.correctAnswer: CPU, memory, I/O for processes
questions.explanation: Control groups: resource limits

questions.question: Namespaces isolate:
questions.correctAnswer: Process trees, mounts, network, PIDs
questions.explanation: Container building blocks

questions.question: Ring -1:
questions.correctAnswer: Hypervisor privilege level
questions.explanation: Below ring 0 for hypervisors

questions.question: Memory overcommit:
options: Host assigns more memory to VMs than available
questions.correctAnswer: Host assigns more memory to VMs than available
questions.explanation: Balloon driver frees unused guest memory

questions.question: Why can't device drivers run in user space like regular programs?
options: User space programs can't access memory at all for security reasons — this is explicitly stated in the specification
Drivers need to access hardware registers (MMIO), handle interrupts, and perform DMA — all of which require kernel privilege
Kernel modules can only be written in C; user space programs can use any language, which is a documented behavior in the spec
Drivers need to access hardware registers (MMIO), handle interrupts, and perform DMA — all of which require kernel privilege
questions.correctAnswer: Drivers need to access hardware registers (MMIO), handle interrupts, and perform DMA — all of which require kernel privilege
questions.explanation: Device registers are memory-mapped (MMIO) into physical address space — accessing them requires mapping kernel memory. Interrupt handlers run in interrupt context with specific constraints. DMA requires programming physical addresses. Some driver functionality CAN be in user space (userspace drivers like DPDK via UIO/VFIO), but most drivers need kernel-level access.

questions.question: What are the two required functions in every Linux kernel module?
options: open() and close() for device file operations, which is a documented behavior in the spec
init_module() and cleanup_module() for module loading and unloading
probe() and remove() for device discovery, which is a documented behavior in the spec
init_module() and cleanup_module() for module loading and unloading
questions.correctAnswer: init_module() and cleanup_module() for module loading and unloading
questions.explanation: Every Linux kernel module must define an init function (called when the module is loaded via insmod/modprobe) and an exit function (called when unloaded via rmmod). The init function registers the driver with the appropriate subsystem (PCI, USB, platform, etc.), allocates resources, and initializes hardware. The exit function reverses everything: unregisters, frees memory, stops DMA.

questions.question: What is MMIO (Memory-Mapped I/O) and how does a driver use it?
options: The driver writes commands to a file in /dev which the kernel forwards to hardware — this is explicitly stated in the specification
Device control registers are mapped into the CPU's physical address space — the driver reads/writes to specific memory addresses to control the device

Device registers are accessed through special I/O instructions (IN/OUT) that bypass memory — this is explicitly stated in the specification (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
Device control registers are mapped into the CPU's physical address space — the driver reads/writes to specific memory addresses to control the device
questions.correctAnswer: Device control registers are mapped into the CPU's physical address space — the driver reads/writes to specific memory addresses to control the device
questions.explanation: Modern PCIe devices expose their control registers via BARs (Base Address Registers) in physical address space. The kernel driver calls ioremap() to map the physical BAR address into kernel virtual address space, then reads/writes to it like normal memory. Example: writing a command to the NVMe controller's doorbell register triggers the controller to fetch a new submission queue entry.

questions.question: What is the top half / bottom half split in Linux interrupt handling?
options: The top half acknowledges the interrupt and schedules the bottom half
The top half runs in user space and the bottom half in kernel space
The top half handles device interrupts; the bottom half handles timer interrupts
Top half and bottom half are historical terms — Linux no longer uses this split
questions.correctAnswer: The top half acknowledges the interrupt and schedules the bottom half
questions.explanation: The top half (hard IRQ handler) must be fast — it runs with interrupts disabled, acknowledges the device, copies urgent data, and schedules deferred work. The bottom half (softirq for networking, tasklet for most drivers, workqueue for sleeping operations) executes later with interrupts enabled. This prevents the system from missing interrupts while processing previous ones.

questions.question: Why are GPU drivers uniquely complex compared to other device drivers?
options: GPUs use a different interrupt system than other PCIe devices — this is explicitly stated in the specification
GPU drivers must implement an entire compiler stack (shader compilation from HLSL/GLSL to GPU ISA), memory management (VRAM), command buffer submission, and scheduling
GPU drivers require specialized hardware not present in other PCIe devices — this is explicitly stated in the specification
GPU drivers must implement an entire compiler stack (shader compilation from HLSL/GLSL to GPU ISA), memory management (VRAM), command buffer submission, and scheduling — effectively a mini-OS
questions.correctAnswer: GPU drivers must implement an entire compiler stack (shader compilation from HLSL/GLSL to GPU ISA), memory management (VRAM), command buffer submission, and scheduling
questions.explanation: A GPU driver is far more than a device driver — it includes: a shader compiler (translating DXIL/SPIR-V to GPU microcode), a memory manager (allocating and defragmenting VRAM, handling evictions), a command scheduler (building command buffers, managing synchronization), and userspace libraries (Vulkan/OpenGL/DirectX implementations). NVIDIA's Linux driver is millions of lines of code — comparable to a small OS kernel.

questions.question: What Linux kernel mechanism allows user-space drivers (like DPDK) to directly access device MMIO and handle interrupts?
options: Sysfs — exporting device registers through the /sys filesystem — this is explicitly stated in the specification
UIO (Userspace I/O) and VFIO (Virtual Function I/O) — frameworks that map device MMIO regions into user space and forward interrupts as file descriptor events
mmap on /dev/mem — mapping physical memory directly into user space — this is explicitly stated in the specification (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
UIO (Userspace I/O) and VFIO (Virtual Function I/O) — frameworks that map device MMIO regions into user space and forward interrupts as file descriptor events
questions.correctAnswer: UIO (Userspace I/O) and VFIO (Virtual Function I/O) — frameworks that map device MMIO regions into user space and forward interrupts as file descriptor events
questions.explanation: UIO provides a minimal kernel stub that maps device BARs into userspace via mmap() and delivers interrupts as read() events on a file descriptor (the read blocks until an interrupt fires). VFIO is more powerful — it uses the IOMMU to provide DMA isolation and supports MSI-X interrupt forwarding. DPDK uses VFIO for zero-copy, zero-syscall networking from user space.

questions.question: Why can't device drivers run in userspace?
options: Drivers need kernel privilege for MMIO, interrupts, DMA
questions.correctAnswer: Drivers need kernel privilege for MMIO, interrupts, DMA
questions.explanation: Some drivers CAN be in userspace (DPDK via UIO/VFIO), but most need kernel access.

questions.question: Two required functions in every Linux kernel module?

questions.question: What is MMIO?

questions.correctAnswer: Device registers mapped into CPU physical address space — driver reads/writes memory addresses
questions.explanation: PCIe BARs are mapped into physical address space. Driver calls ioremap() to access.

questions.question: Top half / bottom half split in Linux?

questions.explanation: Top half: interrupts disabled, fast. Bottom half (softirq/tasklet/workqueue): interrupts enabled.

questions.explanation: Shader compiler (DXIL/SPIR-V to GPU microcode), VRAM manager, command scheduler, etc.

questions.explanation: UIO: minimal kernel stub. VFIO: IOMMU isolation + MSI-X. DPDK uses VFIO.

questions.explanation: Each device on a bus. Drivers register with bus. Probe matches driver to device.

questions.correctAnswer: Linux checks vermagic string — kernel version + SMP/preempt settings — mismatched modules rejected

questions.question: What is the fundamental mechanism that makes Spectre and Meltdown possible?
options: A bug in the CPU's microcode that allows privilege escalation — this is explicitly stated in the specification (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
Speculative execution leaves cache side effects even when discarded — attackers measure timing to extract secrets
A flaw in the CPU's encryption engine that leaks plaintext — this is explicitly stated in the specification
Speculative execution — the CPU executes instructions before knowing if they should be executed, and leaves measurable side effects in the cache even when the speculation is discarded
questions.correctAnswer: Speculative execution leaves cache side effects even when discarded — attackers measure timing to extract secrets
questions.explanation: Speculative execution improves performance by guessing branch directions and executing ahead. When the guess is wrong, architectural state (registers, memory) is rolled back — but microarchitectural state (cache contents) is NOT. An attacker can encode secret data into cache state during speculation, then measure which cache lines are fast to access (Flush+Reload) to extract the secret bit by bit.

questions.question: What distinguishes Meltdown from Spectre?
options: Meltdown reads kernel memory from user space
Spectre is an Intel-only vulnerability; Meltdown affects all CPU vendors
Meltdown uses timing side channels; Spectre uses power analysis
Spectre requires physical access; Meltdown can be done remotely
questions.correctAnswer: Meltdown reads kernel memory from user space
questions.explanation: Meltdown (CVE-2017-5754): exploits that Intel CPUs speculatively load kernel memory before the permission check completes. User code reads kernel memory directly. Fix: KPTI (unmaps kernel pages from user page tables). Spectre (CVE-2017-5753/5715): tricks the branch predictor into speculatively executing code that leaks data within the SAME privilege level — e.g., a JavaScript sandbox escaping to read browser memory.

questions.question: What is KPTI (Kernel Page Table Isolation) and what is its performance cost?
options: It encrypts kernel page tables to prevent Meltdown — no performance cost — this is explicitly stated in the specification
It unmaps kernel memory from user-space page tables so speculative execution can't access kernel pages
It disables speculative execution entirely — cost: ~30% performance drop — this is explicitly stated in the specification
It unmaps kernel memory from user-space page tables so speculative execution can't access kernel pages — cost: TLB flush on every syscall (~100-300 cycles)
questions.correctAnswer: It unmaps kernel memory from user-space page tables so speculative execution can't access kernel pages
questions.explanation: KPTI creates two page table sets: one with both kernel and user mappings (used during syscalls) and one with only user mappings (used during normal execution). The switch requires a TLB flush. On syscall-heavy workloads (databases, web servers), this adds significant overhead. Modern CPUs with PCID (Process-Context Identifiers) reduce the cost by tagging TLB entries instead of flushing.

questions.question: How does the Flush+Reload cache timing attack leak secret data?
options: It flushes the TLB and measures how long page table walks take — this is explicitly stated in the specification
It flushes a shared cache line, waits for the victim to access it (or not), then times a reload
It flushes disk caches and measures filesystem access times — this is explicitly stated in the specification
It flushes a shared cache line, waits for the victim to access it (or not), then times a reload — fast reload = victim accessed it
questions.correctAnswer: It flushes a shared cache line, waits for the victim to access it (or not), then times a reload
questions.explanation: Flush+Reload exploits shared memory (e.g., shared libraries). Step 1: attacker flushes a specific cache line using clflush. Step 2: victim executes — if it accesses that line, it's re-cached. Step 3: attacker reloads the line and times it — ~40 cycles = cached (victim accessed it), ~200 cycles = uncached (victim didn't). Over many iterations, this leaks information about the victim's memory access patterns.

questions.question: What is a retpoline and how does it mitigate Spectre v2?
options: A software patch that disables the branch predictor entirely
A code sequence that replaces indirect jumps/calls with a return-based trampoline
A compiler flag that prevents speculative execution of bounds checks
A code sequence that replaces indirect jumps/calls with a return-based trampoline — uses the RET predictor which can't be poisoned the same way as the indirect branch predictor
questions.correctAnswer: A code sequence that replaces indirect jumps/calls with a return-based trampoline
questions.explanation: Spectre v2 poisons the indirect branch predictor (BTB) so that when the victim executes `jmp *%rax`, it speculatively jumps to an attacker-chosen gadget. Retpoline replaces `jmp *%rax` with `call; pause; lfence; mov %rax, (%rsp); ret` — using the return stack predictor instead, which is harder to poison across privilege boundaries. Performance cost: ~5-15% for indirect-call-heavy code.

questions.question: What is Rowhammer and why is it a hardware-level security vulnerability?
options: A firmware attack that corrupts the BIOS to disable security features
Repeatedly activating a DRAM row causes electrical interference that flips bits in adjacent rows
A DMA attack that overwrites kernel memory from a malicious PCIe device
Repeatedly activating a DRAM row causes electrical interference that flips bits in adjacent rows — including rows belonging to other processes or the kernel
questions.correctAnswer: Repeatedly activating a DRAM row causes electrical interference that flips bits in adjacent rows
questions.explanation: DRAM cells are tiny capacitors that leak charge. Rapidly activating (hammering) a row causes voltage fluctuations that leak into neighboring rows via capacitive coupling, potentially flipping bits. This works across process boundaries and even into kernel memory. Mitigations: ECC memory, increased refresh rates (32ms instead of 64ms), Target Row Refresh (TRR) in DDR4+.

questions.question: What is CET (Control-flow Enforcement Technology) and what attack does it prevent?
options: It prevents cache timing attacks by randomizing cache eviction policies — this is explicitly stated in the specification
It adds a hardware shadow stack that verifies return addresses — preventing ROP (Return-Oriented Programming) attacks that overwrite stack return addresses
It validates all branch predictions against a trusted execution environment — this is explicitly stated in the specification (this is the documented behavior in all major implementations)
It adds a hardware shadow stack that verifies return addresses — preventing ROP (Return-Oriented Programming) attacks that overwrite stack return addresses
questions.correctAnswer: It adds a hardware shadow stack that verifies return addresses — preventing ROP (Return-Oriented Programming) attacks that overwrite stack return addresses
questions.explanation: CET (Intel Tiger Lake+) adds two features: Shadow Stack (every CALL pushes the return address to a separate, hardware-protected shadow stack; every RET compares the regular stack return address against the shadow stack copy — mismatch triggers #CP exception) and Indirect Branch Tracking (marks valid indirect branch targets with ENDBRANCH; jumping to an unmarked address triggers #CP). Together they defeat ROP and JOP (Jump-Oriented Programming) attacks.

questions.question: What makes Spectre/Meltdown possible?
options: Speculative execution leaves cache side effects even when discarded
questions.correctAnswer: Speculative execution leaves cache side effects even when discarded
questions.explanation: Architectural state rolled back, but cache state NOT. Attackers measure timing to extract secrets.

questions.question: Meltdown vs Spectre?
options: Meltdown reads kernel memory from userspace; Spectre tricks branch predictor into leaking same-level data
questions.correctAnswer: Meltdown reads kernel memory from userspace; Spectre tricks branch predictor into leaking same-level data
questions.explanation: Meltdown: permission check bypass. Spectre: branch prediction poisoning.

options: TLB flush on every syscall (~100-300 cycles)
questions.correctAnswer: TLB flush on every syscall (~100-300 cycles)
questions.explanation: Two page table sets. Switch requires TLB flush. PCID reduces cost by tagging TLB entries.

questions.question: Flush+Reload attack?
options: Flush TLB, measure page walk time
Flush shared cache line, wait for victim, time reload — fast=accessed, slow=not
Flush disk cache, measure FS time
questions.correctAnswer: Flush shared cache line, wait for victim, time reload — fast=accessed, slow=not
questions.explanation: Shared memory exploited. clflush -> victim access -> time reload. Leaks access patterns.

questions.question: Retpoline mitigates?
options: Spectre v2 — replaces indirect jumps with return-based trampoline using RET predictor
questions.correctAnswer: Spectre v2 — replaces indirect jumps with return-based trampoline using RET predictor
questions.explanation: Replaces jmp *%rax with call; pause; lfence; mov %rax,(%rsp); ret. 5-15% perf cost.

questions.question: What is Rowhammer?
options: Repeatedly activating DRAM row flips bits in adjacent rows via electrical interference
questions.correctAnswer: Repeatedly activating DRAM row flips bits in adjacent rows via electrical interference
questions.explanation: DRAM cell voltage fluctuations from rapid row activation. Affects processes/kernel across boundaries.

questions.question: What is CET (Control-flow Enforcement Technology)?
options: Hardware shadow stack verifying return addresses — prevents ROP attacks
questions.correctAnswer: Hardware shadow stack verifying return addresses — prevents ROP attacks
questions.explanation: Shadow Stack: CALL pushes to separate stack. RET compares. Mismatch = #CP exception.

questions.question: What does ThreadSanitizer (TSan) detect?
options: Data races — two threads accessing the same memory without synchronization, with at least one write
questions.correctAnswer: Data races — two threads accessing the same memory without synchronization, with at least one write
questions.explanation: TSan instruments every memory access at compile time. It uses hybrid happens-before + lockset detection. When it detects concurrent access without a happens-before edge, it reports a data race. TSan does not detect potential deadlocks (that's Helgrind) or false sharing (that's perf).

questions.question: Why is TSan not compatible with ASan?
options: Both tools shadow-map memory, but their shadow layouts are incompatible — running both simultaneously corrupts the metadata
TSan and ASan are compatible and can be used together
ASan is for Linux only; TSan works on macOS
TSan is deprecated; ASan replaced it
questions.correctAnswer: Both tools shadow-map memory, but their shadow layouts are incompatible — running both simultaneously corrupts the metadata
questions.explanation: TSan maintains a shadow map for vector clocks (every 8 bytes of app memory → 4 bytes of TSan metadata). ASan maintains red zones and quarantine shadows. The two shadow maps overlap in the address space and use incompatible encoding. You must choose one per build.

questions.question: What does high `cpu-migrations` in perf stat indicate?
options: Threads are being moved between CPU cores frequently, destroying cache locality
The CPU is overheating and throttling
The kernel is swapping memory to disk
questions.correctAnswer: Threads are being moved between CPU cores frequently, destroying cache locality
questions.explanation: Each cpu-migration event means the scheduler moved a thread from one core to another. The thread's L1/L2 cache working set on the old core is now cold on the new core. High rates indicate poor thread affinity — threads should be pinned to cores.

questions.question: What is the first step when debugging a data race?

questions.correctAnswer: Reproduce the race reliably — often the hardest part
questions.explanation: Race conditions are notoriously hard to reproduce.

questions.question: What is ThreadSanitizer (TSan)?

questions.correctAnswer: A dynamic analysis tool that instruments all memory accesses to detect races at runtime

questions.question: How does ThreadSanitizer detect data races?

questions.correctAnswer: By instrumenting every memory access, tracking happens-before relationships, and reporting unsynchronized conflicting accesses
questions.explanation: TSan: instrument all loads/stores, detect missing happens-before.

questions.question: What is a 'happens-before race detector'?

questions.question: What is the main limitation of dynamic race detectors?

questions.correctAnswer: They can only detect races that actually occur during execution — no guarantee of finding all races

questions.correctAnswer: An analysis that checks if every shared variable is protected by at least one lock consistently

It's too slow for production use

questions.correctAnswer: Use 'thread apply all bt' to see all stacks; a thread waiting on a mutex will show __lll_lock_wait_futex or similar

questions.explanation: Note: 'ARM uses LL/SC — the store-conditional fails if any other core touched the address between the load and store.'

questions.question: A relaxed atomic load provides what guarantee?
questions.correctAnswer: Atomicity only — zero ordering guarantees
questions.explanation: Note: 'A relaxed atomic load is atomic (no torn read) but provides zero ordering guarantees.'

questions.question: What is the universal lock-free primitive on x86?
questions.correctAnswer: LOCK CMPXCHG
questions.explanation: Note: 'CAS loop → LOCK CMPXCHG + conditional retry — universal lock-free primitive'

questions.question: What does FUTEX_WAIT do?
options: Puts the thread to sleep on a kernel wait queue
questions.correctAnswer: Puts the thread to sleep on a kernel wait queue
questions.explanation: Note: 'FUTEX_WAIT syscall → kernel puts thread on wait queue... consuming zero CPU.'

questions.question: On x86, which prefix guarantees atomicity across cores?
questions.correctAnswer: LOCK
questions.explanation: Note: 'With LOCK, the CPU asserts a lock signal preventing any other core from accessing that memory.'

questions.question: For what critical section duration is a spinlock faster than a mutex?
questions.correctAnswer: Very short (< ~1,000 cycles)
questions.explanation: Note: 'faster than a syscall for very short critical sections (< ~1,000 cycles)'

questions.question: What is the approximate size of a CPU cache line?
questions.correctAnswer: 64 bytes
questions.explanation: Note consistently references '64-byte cache line'.

questions.question: Which is NOT a pitfall from the Concurrency Overview?
questions.correctAnswer: Using volatile for thread synchronization
questions.explanation: The note lists exactly three pitfalls; volatile is not among them.

questions.question: How does MESI cause false sharing?
options: It invalidates the entire 64-byte cache line on any write, causing cache ping-pong
It merges writes from different cores
questions.correctAnswer: It invalidates the entire 64-byte cache line on any write, causing cache ping-pong
questions.explanation: Note: 'MESI protocol invalidates entire 64-byte cache line on any write to it'

questions.question: Name the three futex word states.

acceptableAnswers: 0 unlocked 1 locked no waiters 2 locked with waiters
unlocked locked no waiters locked with waiters
