questions.question: What does lock-free programming use instead of mutexes?
options: CAS loops with helping and dummy nodes for ABA
questions.correctAnswer: CAS loops with helping and dummy nodes for ABA
questions.explanation: Note: 'Lock-free structure → CAS loops everywhere; helping; dummy nodes for ABA'

questions.question: Which is faster: mutex fast path or atomic increment?
options: About the same cost (~15-30 cycles each)
questions.correctAnswer: About the same cost (~15-30 cycles each)
questions.explanation: Mutex fast path ~20 cycles, atomic increment ~15-30 cycles.

questions.question: What does a data race require per the C++ standard?
options: Two+ threads accessing same memory concurrently, at least one write, no synchronization, no happens-before
Two threads writing the same variable
questions.correctAnswer: Two+ threads accessing same memory concurrently, at least one write, no synchronization, no happens-before
questions.explanation: Note defines four conditions for a data race.

questions.question: What is the real cost of mutex contention?
options: Cache-line bouncing and context switching, not the lock itself
questions.correctAnswer: Cache-line bouncing and context switching, not the lock itself
questions.explanation: Note: 'The real cost of contention is cache-line bouncing and context switching, not the lock itself.'

questions.question: How does the C++ standard treat data races?
questions.correctAnswer: As undefined behavior
questions.explanation: Note: 'The result is undefined behavior per the C++ standard'

questions.question: What does a condition variable use at the kernel level?
questions.correctAnswer: FUTEX_WAIT/FUTEX_WAKE on internal futex
questions.explanation: Note: 'Condition variable → FUTEX_WAIT on internal futex, FUTEX_WAKE on notify'

questions.question: What three mechanisms affect how the CPU reorders memory operations?
questions.correctAnswer: Store buffer, cache coherence, instruction reordering
acceptableAnswers: store buffer cache coherence instruction reordering
store buffers cache coherence instruction reordering
questions.explanation: Note: 'Memory ordering controls how the CPU's store buffer and cache coherence allow reordering.'

questions.question: What CAS instruction return code indicates success?
questions.correctAnswer: ZF = 1 (Zero Flag set)
questions.explanation: Note: 'ZF = 1 (success), ZF = 0 (failure)'

questions.question: What does 'helping' mean in lock-free programming?
options: One thread assists another to complete its operation, then proceeds
questions.correctAnswer: One thread assists another to complete its operation, then proceeds
questions.explanation: Mentioned as a lock-free technique.

questions.question: What is the cycle cost of a modern cache-locked atomic operation on L1 hit?
questions.correctAnswer: ~20-40 cycles
questions.explanation: Note: 'Cache lock: ~20-40 cycles for L1 hit'

questions.question: Name the memory ordering levels available in C++ atomics.
questions.correctAnswer: Relaxed, acquire/release, sequentially consistent
questions.explanation: Note: 'Memory ordering (relaxed, acquire, release, seq_cst)'

questions.question: What is a CAS loop?
options: LOCK CMPXCHG + conditional retry until success
questions.correctAnswer: LOCK CMPXCHG + conditional retry until success
questions.explanation: Note: 'CAS loop → LOCK CMPXCHG + conditional retry'

questions.question: On what architecture is LL/SC the primary atomic mechanism?
questions.correctAnswer: ARM/AArch64
questions.explanation: Note: 'LL/SC (ARM) → Hardware monitors address between load-linked and store-conditional'

questions.question: How does a spinlock differ from a futex at the kernel level?
options: Spinlock never calls the kernel; futex calls FUTEX_WAIT on contention
questions.correctAnswer: Spinlock never calls the kernel; futex calls FUTEX_WAIT on contention
questions.explanation: Spinlock = LOCK XCHG in a loop; futex = CAS fast path + FUTEX_WAIT slow path.

questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between atomicity and ordering?
options: Atomicity prevents torn reads/writes; ordering controls visibility across cores
questions.correctAnswer: Atomicity prevents torn reads/writes; ordering controls visibility across cores
questions.explanation: The chapter separates these two properties.

questions.question: True or False: a relaxed atomic store on one core is immediately visible on another core.
options: False — relaxed ordering provides no visibility guarantee
True on x86 but false on ARM
questions.correctAnswer: False — relaxed ordering provides no visibility guarantee
questions.explanation: Relaxed ordering means no guarantee of visibility across cores.

questions.question: What does futex stand for?
questions.correctAnswer: Fast user-space mutex
questions.explanation: The note introduces futex as 'fast userspace mutex'.

questions.question: On modern CPUs, what does the LOCK prefix actually lock?
options: Only the target cache line in L1 cache
questions.correctAnswer: Only the target cache line in L1 cache
questions.explanation: Note: 'lock ONLY the target cache line in L1. Other cores can access other cache lines freely.'

questions.question: What distinguishes a data race from a race condition?
options: Data races are unsynchronized memory access; race conditions are broader timing bugs
Data races only happen on ARM
questions.correctAnswer: Data races are unsynchronized memory access; race conditions are broader timing bugs
questions.explanation: The chapter distinguishes these types of concurrency failures by root cause.

questions.question: What is the approximate cycle cost range of an atomic increment?
questions.correctAnswer: 15-30 cycles
questions.explanation: Note: 'Atomic increment → LOCK XADD — ~15–30 cycles'

questions.question: What approach do lock-free structures use for the ABA problem?
options: Dummy nodes, tagged pointers, or double-width CAS
questions.correctAnswer: Dummy nodes, tagged pointers, or double-width CAS
questions.explanation: Note mentions 'dummy nodes for ABA'.

questions.question: What three hardware mechanisms enabled atomic operations across CPU generations?
options: Bus lock (old), cache lock (modern), LOCK CMPXCHG (Skylake+)
questions.correctAnswer: Bus lock (old), cache lock (modern), LOCK CMPXCHG (Skylake+)
questions.explanation: The table describes three eras of atomic hardware support.

questions.question: What does MESI stand for?
questions.correctAnswer: Modified Exclusive Shared Invalid
questions.explanation: MESI: Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid.

questions.question: Name the four MESI cache coherence states.
questions.correctAnswer: Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid
questions.explanation: MESI: Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid.

questions.question: Why does false sharing cause ~100x degradation?
options: The cache line is invalidated on every write across cores
questions.correctAnswer: The cache line is invalidated on every write across cores
questions.explanation: When two cores share a cache line, each write invalidates it on the other core.

questions.question: What is the x86 instruction for compare-and-swap?
questions.correctAnswer: LOCK CMPXCHG
questions.explanation: Note: 'LOCK CMPXCHG' is the compare-and-swap instruction.

questions.question: What instruction does a CAS loop repeatedly execute until success?
questions.correctAnswer: LOCK CMPXCHG
questions.explanation: Note: 'LOCK CMPXCHG + conditional retry'

questions.question: What is the key tradeoff between mutex and spinlock?
options: Spinlock for short critical sections avoids context switch overhead
questions.correctAnswer: Spinlock for short critical sections avoids context switch overhead
questions.explanation: Spinlock avoids ~2000 cycle context switch cost for short waits.

questions.question: How does the 'helping' pattern work in lock-free structures?
options: One thread finishes another's partially-completed operation
questions.correctAnswer: One thread finishes another's partially-completed operation
questions.explanation: Helping is a technique where encountering a partially-completed operation triggers completion.

questions.question: Which C++ type provides atomic operations with memory ordering?
questions.correctAnswer: std::atomic
questions.explanation: std::atomic<T> is the C++ abstraction for atomic operations.

questions.question: What futex word values represent the mutex states in a two-phase lock?
questions.correctAnswer: 0 (unlocked), 1 (locked no waiters), 2 (locked with waiters)
acceptableAnswers: 0 1 2 unlocked locked no waiters locked with waiters
0 unlocked 1 locked no waiters 2 locked with waiters
questions.explanation: Note: '0 Unlocked, 1 Locked no waiters, 2 Locked with waiters'

questions.question: What is the x86 LOCK XADD instruction used for?
questions.correctAnswer: Atomic increment (read, add, write)
questions.explanation: Note: 'Atomic increment → LOCK XADD'

questions.question: What prevents a C++ compiler from reordering atomic operations with seq_cst ordering?
options: Sequentially consistent ordering prevents all compiler and hardware reordering
questions.correctAnswer: Sequentially consistent ordering prevents all compiler and hardware reordering
questions.explanation: Seq_cst is the strongest ordering — prevents both compiler and CPU reordering.

questions.question: On which hardware does relaxed atomics pose the greatest risk?
questions.correctAnswer: ARM (weaker memory model)
questions.explanation: x86's TSO often masks relaxed atomic mistakes that manifest on ARM's weaker model.

questions.question: What does `RSP` point to at all times?
options: The highest address of the stack
The current 'top' of the stack — the lowest address currently in use (the stack grows downward)
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: RSP (Stack Pointer) always points to the current top of the stack, which is the numerically LOWEST address currently in use on x86-64. The stack grows downward toward lower addresses.

questions.question: What does the `call` instruction do atomically?
options: Pushes the current RIP (return address) onto the stack and jumps to the callee address
questions.correctAnswer: Pushes the current RIP (return address) onto the stack and jumps to the callee address
questions.explanation: The `call addr` instruction atomically: (1) pushes RIP (the address of the next instruction after `call`) onto the stack, and (2) jumps to the specified address (the callee). `ret` reverses this: pops the return address from the stack into RIP.

questions.question: Which direction does the stack grow on x86-64?
options: The stack doesn't grow — it's fixed size
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: On x86-64, the stack grows downward — RSP decreases as items are pushed. 'Top of stack' is the numerically smallest address. `push rax` decrements RSP by 8 and writes RAX at [RSP]. `pop rax` reads from [RSP] and increments RSP by 8.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the base pointer (RBP) in a function's stack frame?
options: RBP provides a fixed anchor within the stack frame — local variables are at [RBP - offset] and arguments at [RBP + offset], with offsets that don't change during the function
RBP points to the top of the stack
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: RBP serves as a fixed reference point during the function's execution. Since RSP moves with push/pop operations, local variable offsets would be a moving target relative to RSP. RBP is set once in the prologue and never changed, providing stable offsets for locals and arguments.

questions.question: What does the standard function prologue do?
options: push rbp; mov rbp, rsp; sub rsp, N
questions.correctAnswer: A
questions.explanation: The standard prologue: `push rbp` saves the caller's frame pointer, `mov rbp, rsp` sets the callee's frame base to current stack top, and `sub rsp, N` allocates N bytes for local variables (rounded up to maintain 16-byte alignment).

questions.question: What does the `leave` instruction do?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: `leave` is a single-byte instruction that performs `mov rsp, rbp` (deallocate locals by restoring RSP to frame base) followed by `pop rbp` (restore caller's frame pointer). It's the standard way to reverse the prologue.

questions.question: What does Frame Pointer Omission (FPO) do?
options: Omitted frame pointers are required for security
FPO is only available in MSVC
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: FPO (enabled by `-fomit-frame-pointer` or `-O2`) skips `push rbp; mov rbp, rsp`. RBP becomes a general-purpose register (6% more registers). All local access uses `[RSP + offset]`, where the compiler tracks exact RSP deltas. Debugging is harder without the RBP chain.

questions.question: Where are stack-passed arguments located relative to RBP in the standard frame?
options: Arguments are never on the stack
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: In the standard frame layout: `[RBP + 8]` = return address (pushed by `call`), `[RBP + 16]` and above = stack-passed arguments. `[RBP - 8]` and below = local variables.

questions.question: What is stored at `[RSP]` immediately after a `push rbp` in the callee prologue?
questions.correctAnswer: C
questions.explanation: After the callee executes `push rbp`, the value at `[RSP]` is the caller's RBP. This value is then restored by `pop rbp` in the epilogue, giving the caller back its original frame pointer.

questions.question: Why is stack allocation extremely fast compared to heap allocation?
options: The stack has its own dedicated cache
The OS pre-allocates all stack memory
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Stack allocation is just `sub rsp, N` — a single register operation (~1 cycle). Heap allocation involves function call overhead, free list traversal (`malloc`), potentially a syscall (`sbrk`/`mmap`), and page table manipulation.

questions.question: In the x86-64 System V ABI, which registers hold the first two integer arguments?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Under System V AMD64 ABI (Linux, macOS), the first integer argument goes in RDI and the second in RSI. The System V register argument sequence is: RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9.

questions.question: What is the return register for integer values on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: C
questions.explanation: RAX always holds the integer return value. Functions place their return value in RAX before executing `ret`. For 128-bit values, RAX holds the low 64 bits and RDX holds the high 64 bits.

questions.question: When does RIP get modified outside of `call`, `ret`, and `jmp` instructions?
acceptableAnswers: never — RIP is only modified by control-flow instructions (call, ret, jmp, jcc); it advances automatically through sequential code
questions.explanation: RIP (Instruction Pointer) advances automatically by the length of each decoded instruction. It cannot be read or written directly by most instructions. Only control-flow instructions (`call`, `ret`, `jmp`, `jcc`) explicitly change RIP to a non-sequential address.

questions.question: Why is 'top of stack' the lowest address on x86-64?
acceptableAnswers: the stack grows downward — push decrements RSP and writes at the new lower address, so the active 'top' is the numerically smallest address
questions.explanation: The stack grows from high addresses to low addresses. `push` decrements RSP by 8 (or the operand size) then stores at the new RSP. `pop` loads from RSP then increments it. So the 'top' (most recently pushed item) is always at the lowest active stack address.

questions.question: What are the callee-saved registers in the System V AMD64 ABI?
acceptableAnswers: RBX, RBP, R12, R13, R14, R15
questions.explanation: In System V, the callee-saved registers are RBX, RBP, and R12-R15. If a function uses these, it must save their original values (typically by pushing them in the prologue) and restore them before returning.

questions.question: What happens to the stack when a leaf function without local variables executes?
acceptableAnswers: a leaf function (one that calls no other functions) may have no prologue/epilogue — just its body and a ret instruction
questions.explanation: A leaf function with no locals and no other function calls may skip the prologue entirely. It just executes its body instructions and `ret`. The return address is already at [RSP] from the caller's `call` instruction.

questions.question: What is the purpose of `sub rsp, N` in the function prologue?
acceptableAnswers: allocates N bytes on the stack for local variables by moving RSP down (to a lower address)
questions.explanation: `sub rsp, N` decrements the stack pointer by N bytes, effectively reserving N bytes of stack space for local variables. N is rounded up to maintain 16-byte stack alignment. The locals occupy addresses from RSP (after the sub) to RBP-1.

questions.question: How does a debugger unwind the call stack when frame pointers are omitted?
acceptableAnswers: it uses DWARF unwind tables (.eh_frame) that describe how to find the caller's RSP and RIP at each instruction point
questions.explanation: Without frame pointers, the debugger can't follow the RBP chain. Instead, it reads DWARF `.eh_frame` (or `.debug_frame`) metadata. These tables contain 'Call Frame Information' (CFI) describing at each instruction address how to reconstruct the previous frame's registers.

questions.question: What is the 'red zone' in the System V ABI?
acceptableAnswers: 128 bytes below RSP that the current function owns — signal handlers won't overwrite it, so leaf functions can use it without adjusting RSP
questions.explanation: The System V AMD64 ABI defines a 128-byte red zone below RSP. The kernel's signal handlers guarantee they won't touch this area. Leaf functions can store temporary data in the red zone without executing `sub rsp, N` / `add rsp, N`, saving two instructions.

questions.question: Why is `enter` instruction slower than `push rbp; mov rbp, rsp; sub rsp, N`?
acceptableAnswers: enter does more work (saves multiple frame pointers for nested function support in Pascal) and has microcode-assisted implementation, taking ~10-20 cycles vs 3 instructions taking ~3-4 cycles total
questions.explanation: The `enter` instruction was designed for languages with nested functions (like Pascal). It saves the old frame pointer and optionally creates a display (chain of frame pointers). This complex behavior is microcoded and slow (~10-20 cycles). C/C++ compilers never use it; they emit the individual instructions.

questions.question: What is the instruction pointer (RIP) used for?
acceptableAnswers: holds the memory address of the next instruction to fetch and execute
questions.explanation: RIP is the instruction pointer on x86-64. It holds the address of the next instruction the CPU will fetch from memory. It advances automatically through sequential code and is modified by call/ret/jmp/jcc for non-sequential flow.

questions.question: How does `alloca()` (dynamic stack allocation) work and why is it dangerous?
acceptableAnswers: alloca() allocates by subtracting a runtime-computed value from RSP; it's dangerous because a large or unbounded allocation can overflow the stack past the guard page, causing SIGSEGV
questions.explanation: `alloca(N)` compiles to `sub rsp, N` where N is a runtime value. This is fast — no heap, no free. But the total stack usage depends on the runtime value. If N is controlled by user input or grows unbounded, it can overflow the fixed-size stack, hitting the guard page and crashing.

questions.question: What is the return address in the context of function calls?
acceptableAnswers: the address of the instruction immediately after the call instruction, pushed onto the stack by call and popped by ret
questions.explanation: When `call func` executes, it pushes the address of the next instruction (the one after `call`) onto the stack. This is the 'return address.' When `ret` executes, it pops this address into RIP, resuming execution right after the original call.

questions.question: What is stored in [RBP+8] in the standard stack frame?
acceptableAnswers: the return address (pushed by the caller's call instruction)
questions.explanation: `[RBP+8]` is the slot containing the return address. It was pushed by the caller's `call` instruction before the callee's prologue ran. It's located above the saved RBP (at [RBP+0]) because the stack grows downward.

questions.question: Why do compilers at -O2 often skip RBP entirely?
acceptableAnswers: to free RBP as an additional general-purpose register (6% more registers), enabling more register-level parallelism and fewer spills
questions.explanation: Skipping the frame pointer (FPO) makes RBP available as a general-purpose register. With 15 instead of 16 GPRs available after saving RBP for frame use, FPO recovers one register. This can reduce register spilling in functions with many live variables.

questions.question: What does RSP always point to?
options: The current 'top' of the stack — lowest address in use (stack grows downward)
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: RSP points to the top of the stack, which is the numerically LOWEST address in use. The stack grows downward toward lower addresses.

questions.question: Which way does the stack grow on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: The stack grows downward. `push` decrements RSP then writes. `pop` reads then increments RSP. 'Top of stack' is the smallest address.

questions.question: What is the purpose of RBP in the stack frame?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: RBP provides stable offsets. Since RSP moves with push/pop, RBP is set once in the prologue and remains fixed, giving constant offsets for locals and arguments.

questions.question: Where are stack-passed arguments relative to RBP?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: In the standard frame: `[RBP+8]` = return address, `[RBP+16]+` = stack arguments. `[RBP-8]` and below = local variables.

questions.question: What is at [RSP] after `push rbp` in the prologue?
questions.correctAnswer: C
questions.explanation: After `push rbp`, `[RSP]` holds the caller's RBP value. It's restored by `pop rbp` in the epilogue to give the caller back its frame pointer.

questions.question: Why is stack allocation extremely fast?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Stack allocation = `sub rsp, N` (~1 cycle). Deallocation = `add rsp, N`. No malloc overhead, no free list, no syscall. Just a register operation.

questions.question: In the System V ABI, which registers hold the first two integer arguments?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: System V: RDI (1st), RSI (2nd), RDX (3rd), RCX (4th), R8 (5th), R9 (6th). RAX holds the return value.

questions.question: What register holds the integer return value on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: C
questions.explanation: RAX always holds the integer return value. For 128-bit values, RAX = low 64 bits, RDX = high 64 bits.

questions.question: How does RIP normally advance through code?
acceptableAnswers: automatically by the length of each decoded instruction; only changed by call/ret/jmp
questions.explanation: RIP advances by the instruction length after each decode. Only control-flow instructions (call, ret, jmp, jcc) explicitly redirect RIP to a non-sequential address.

questions.question: What are the callee-saved registers in System V AMD64 ABI?
acceptableAnswers: RBX, RBP, R12, R13, R14, R15
questions.explanation: If a function uses RBX, RBP, or R12-R15, it must save the original values (typically by pushing in the prologue) and restore them before returning.

questions.question: What does a leaf function's prologue look like?
acceptableAnswers: a leaf function that calls nothing and has no locals may have NO prologue — just its body and ret
questions.explanation: A leaf function (no calls, no locals) can skip the prologue entirely. The return address is already at [RSP] from the caller's `call`.

questions.question: Why does sub rsp, N in the prologue round N up?
acceptableAnswers: to maintain 16-byte stack alignment so SSE instructions like movaps can be used safely
questions.explanation: The ABI requires RSP % 16 == 0 before call. After push rbp, RSP % 16 == 0. `sub rsp, N` must keep this alignment, so N is rounded to a 16-byte multiple.

questions.question: How does a debugger unwind without frame pointers?
acceptableAnswers: using DWARF .eh_frame unwind tables describing how to reconstruct the previous frame at each instruction point
questions.explanation: Without RBP, debuggers read `.eh_frame` CFI (Call Frame Information) tables. These describe at each instruction address how to recover the caller's registers.

questions.question: What is the red zone in System V ABI?
acceptableAnswers: 128 bytes below RSP that the current function owns — signal handlers won't touch it
questions.explanation: The red zone is 128 bytes below RSP that the function can use without adjusting RSP. The kernel's signal handlers guarantee they won't overwrite it.

questions.question: Why is `enter` instruction slower than explicit prologue?
acceptableAnswers: enter does complex work (multiple frame pointer chains for Pascal) in microcode, taking ~10-20 cycles vs 3 simple instructions
questions.explanation: `enter` was designed for languages with nested functions (Pascal). Its complex multi-frame saving is microcoded and slow (~10-20 cycles). C/C++ compilers never use it.

questions.question: What is the return address and where is it stored during a call?
acceptableAnswers: the address of the instruction after call, pushed onto the stack by the call instruction
questions.explanation: `call func` pushes the next instruction's address onto the stack. `ret` pops this into RIP, resuming execution after the call.

questions.question: What is at [RBP+8] in a standard stack frame?
acceptableAnswers: the return address (pushed by caller's call instruction)
questions.explanation: `[RBP+8]` = return address, pushed by `call` before the callee prologue runs. It's above saved RBP because the stack grows downward.

questions.question: What is at [RBP+0] in a standard stack frame?
acceptableAnswers: the saved caller's RBP (pushed by push rbp in the prologue)
questions.explanation: `[RBP+0]` = the caller's RBP, saved by `push rbp` in the prologue. Restored by `pop rbp` in the epilogue.

questions.question: What is at [RBP-4] in a standard stack frame?
acceptableAnswers: a local variable (4 bytes below the frame base)
questions.explanation: Local variables occupy addresses below RBP. `[RBP-4]` is a typical 4-byte local (like an int). The compiler assigns each local a fixed negative offset from RBP.

questions.question: Why is stack memory automatically reclaimed when a function returns?
acceptableAnswers: the epilogue's mov rsp, rbp or add rsp, N restores RSP to where it was before locals were allocated — no explicit free needed
questions.explanation: Stack deallocation is just `mov rsp, rbp` or `add rsp, N` — restoring RSP to the pre-allocation value. The 'freed' space is immediately available for the next function's frame.

questions.question: What registers are used to pass the first two integer arguments on System V x86-64?
questions.explanation: Under System V AMD64: RDI = arg1, RSI = arg2, RDX = arg3, RCX = arg4, R8 = arg5, R9 = arg6. Arguments beyond 6 go on the stack.

questions.question: What does `push rax` do to RSP and memory?
acceptableAnswers: decrements RSP by 8, then writes the value of RAX to the new [RSP]
questions.explanation: `push rax`: RSP ← RSP-8; [RSP] ← RAX. The stack grows downward, so push makes room by moving RSP to a lower address.

questions.question: What does `pop rax` do to RSP and memory?
acceptableAnswers: reads the value at [RSP] into RAX, then increments RSP by 8
questions.explanation: `pop rax`: RAX ← [RSP]; RSP ← RSP+8. The value is removed from the stack by moving RSP up to a higher address.

questions.question: What is the difference between RIP-relative and absolute addressing in function calls?
acceptableAnswers: call rel32 = RIP-relative (position-independent); call [address] = absolute (direct memory reference)
questions.explanation: `call rel32` uses a 32-bit offset from the current RIP, making the call position-independent. `call [address]` loads the target from a memory location. PIE executables use RIP-relative.

questions.question: Why must RSP be 16-byte aligned before a `call` instruction?
options: The CPU crashes if RSP isn't aligned
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: SSE instructions like `movaps` (Move Aligned Packed Single-Precision) and `movdqa` fault on misaligned addresses. The ABI mandates RSP % 16 == 0 before call so that functions can use aligned SSE moves for local variables without manual alignment.

questions.question: Under the System V AMD64 ABI, which registers hold integer arguments 1-6?
options: RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9
RCX, RDX, R8, R9 (first four only)
RDI, RSI, RAX, RBX, R8, R9
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: System V AMD64 ABI uses: RDI (1st), RSI (2nd), RDX (3rd), RCX (4th), R8 (5th), R9 (6th). Arguments 7+ are passed on the stack (right-to-left, above the return address).

questions.question: Under the Microsoft x64 ABI, which registers hold integer arguments 1-4?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Microsoft x64 ABI uses: RCX (1st), RDX (2nd), R8 (3rd), R9 (4th). Arguments 5+ go on the stack. This differs from System V which uses RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9.

questions.question: What is the biggest porting trap between System V and Windows calling conventions?
options: RDI and RSI are caller-saved on System V (callee can freely clobber them) but callee-saved on Windows (must save and restore)
Windows uses a different order for float arguments
System V doesn't support float arguments
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: On System V, RDI and RSI are caller-saved argument registers — the callee can freely clobber them. On Windows, they are callee-saved — if a Windows function uses RDI or RSI, it MUST save and restore them. This is the #1 porting trap.

questions.question: What is the 'shadow space' in the Microsoft x64 ABI?
options: 32 bytes of space the caller must allocate on the stack before every call, where the callee can spill its four register arguments (RCX, RDX, R8, R9)
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: On Windows x64, the caller must allocate 32 bytes of shadow space on the stack before every call. The callee can spill its four register arguments (RCX, RDX, R8, R9) into this space. This simplifies variadic functions and gives debuggers a predictable place to find arguments.

questions.question: Why doesn't the red zone exist on Windows?
options: Windows doesn't need it — it's slower with one
Windows has a different stack growth direction
Windows uses a larger red zone instead
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: The Microsoft x64 ABI explicitly states that memory below RSP is volatile. The kernel's interrupt handlers and Windows exception dispatch may overwrite it without warning. Cross-platform code must never assume a red zone. Always `sub rsp, N` on Windows.

questions.question: After `call` pushes the return address (8 bytes), what is RSP's alignment?
options: RSP % 16 == 8 (misaligned — the return address push misaligns it)
questions.correctAnswer: RSP % 16 == 8 (misaligned — the return address push misaligns it)
questions.explanation: Before `call`, RSP % 16 == 0 (caller ensures this). The `call` instruction pushes 8 bytes (return address), making RSP % 16 == 8. The callee's `push rbp` (8 bytes) then re-aligns to RSP % 16 == 0.

questions.question: What does the `thiscall` convention define for C++ member functions?
options: `this` is passed as an implicit first argument — in RCX (Windows) or RDI (System V)
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: C++ member functions receive `this` as an implicit first argument. On Windows x64, `this` goes in RCX. On System V, `this` goes in RDI (the first argument register). The calling convention is otherwise the same as a regular function.

questions.question: How does a virtual function call resolve through the vtable?
options: The compiler emits a direct call to the function
Virtual functions are resolved at link time
The kernel resolves virtual function calls
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: For `obj->vf()`, the compiler generates: load vtable pointer (vptr) from `[this]` (offset 0 in the object), load function pointer from `[vptr + offset]` (the offset is fixed for each virtual function), and call through that function pointer.

questions.question: Under System V, where are floating-point arguments passed?
options: In XMM0 through XMM7 (up to 8 float args)
In a separate float register file
questions.correctAnswer: In XMM0 through XMM7 (up to 8 float args)
questions.explanation: Under System V AMD64, floating-point arguments go in XMM0-XMM7 (up to 8 arguments). Integer and float argument registers are separate — XMM registers don't overlap with integer registers. AL is set to the number of XMM registers used for variadic functions.

questions.question: What is the purpose of setting AL before calling variadic functions like printf on System V?
options: AL contains the first variadic argument
AL is the format string length
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: For variadic functions on System V, the caller sets AL (low byte of RAX) to the number of XMM registers used for floating-point arguments. The callee (e.g., printf) uses this to know how many XMM registers to save in the va_list save area.

questions.question: Under Windows x64, where does va_start point inside printf?
acceptableAnswers: into the 32-byte shadow space, which holds spills of RCX, RDX, R8, R9 at known offsets
questions.explanation: On Windows x64, the caller's shadow space holds the four register arguments. `va_start` points into this shadow space. As `va_arg` iterates, it reads from the shadow space first (4 register slots), then continues past the return address onto the stack for additional arguments.

questions.question: What are the callee-saved registers on Windows x64 that are NOT callee-saved on System V?
questions.explanation: On Windows x64, RDI, RSI, and XMM6-XMM15 are callee-saved. On System V, RDI and RSI are caller-saved (argument registers), and no XMM registers are callee-saved. This means Windows functions must save/restore RDI/RSI if they use them.

questions.question: What is the shadow space requirement when calling a function with 0 arguments on Windows x64?
acceptableAnswers: the caller must still allocate 32 bytes of shadow space, even for zero-argument functions
questions.explanation: The shadow space (32 bytes, 4×8 bytes for RCX/RDX/R8/R9 spills) is required for EVERY call on Windows x64, even functions taking zero arguments. The callee may use this space for register spills regardless of how many arguments the function takes.

questions.question: Under the System V ABI, which registers must a callee save (preserve their values) if it wants to use them?
acceptableAnswers: RBX, RBP, R12, R13, R14, R15
questions.explanation: In System V, the callee-saved registers are RBX, RBP, and R12-R15. If a function modifies any of these, it must save the original value (typically by pushing it in the prologue) and restore it before returning.

questions.question: What is the calling convention for variadic functions on Windows x64?
acceptableAnswers: same as regular (RCX, RDX, R8, R9 for first 4 args, shadow space, stack for 5+); but all register args are spilled to shadow space at function entry
questions.explanation: Windows variadic functions use the same convention as regular functions: first 4 args in RCX/RDX/R8/R9, shadow space allocated, additional args on stack. The key difference: the callee immediately spills all register arguments to the shadow space at entry, so `va_list` walks a linear sequence.

questions.question: Why did Microsoft create its own calling convention for x64 instead of adopting System V?
acceptableAnswers: historical continuity from __fastcall, more callee-saved registers (RDI, RSI, XMM6-XMM15) reducing save/restore churn, shadow space for debuggability, and historical independence from AMD's specification
questions.explanation: Microsoft's x64 convention extends their previous x86 `__fastcall` pattern (ECX/EDX → RCX/RDX/R8/R9). Windows has more callee-saved registers, reducing save/restore across tightly-coupled calls. The shadow space makes debugging easier (arguments always findable at [RSP+0-24]). And AMD published the System V spec; Microsoft chose to create their own.

questions.question: What is the range of red zone memory addresses available to a leaf function on System V?
questions.explanation: The red zone is the 128 bytes below RSP. A leaf function (one that doesn't call other functions) can use this area freely without adjusting RSP. The kernel's signal handlers guarantee they won't overwrite the red zone.

questions.question: When does the red zone become unsafe even on System V?
acceptableAnswers: the moment the function calls another function — `call` pushes the return address at [RSP-8], overwriting data stored in the red zone's first 8 bytes
questions.explanation: A leaf function can safely use the red zone. But the moment it executes `call some_function`, the return address is pushed at [RSP-8], which is inside the red zone. Any data stored in bytes [RSP-8] through [RSP-1] of the red zone is overwritten.

questions.question: How many bytes of shadow space must a Windows x64 caller allocate, and why exactly that amount?
acceptableAnswers: 32 bytes — 4 slots of 8 bytes each for RCX, RDX, R8, R9 register spills
questions.explanation: The shadow space is exactly 32 bytes: 4 argument registers × 8 bytes each. The caller subtracts 32 from RSP before the call, and the callee may write any or all of its register arguments into these slots. The slots are at [RSP+0] (RCX), [RSP+8] (RDX), [RSP+16] (R8), [RSP+24] (R9).

questions.question: What is the Itanium C++ ABI name mangling prefix for a function `foo(int, float)`?
questions.explanation: Under Itanium ABI: `_Z` is the mangling prefix, `3foo` encodes the name (3 characters, 'foo'), `i` = int, `f` = float. The linker sees `_Z3fooif`, not `foo(int, float)`. MSVC uses a different scheme: `?foo@@YAXHM@Z`.

questions.question: Under System V AMD64 ABI, which registers hold integer args 1-6?
options: RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9
RCX, RDX, R8, R9 (first four only)
RDI, RSI, RAX, RBX, R8, R9
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: System V: RDI (1), RSI (2), RDX (3), RCX (4), R8 (5), R9 (6). Arguments 7+ go on the stack (right-to-left above return address).

questions.question: Under Microsoft x64 ABI, which registers hold integer args 1-4?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Microsoft x64: RCX (1), RDX (2), R8 (3), R9 (4). Args 5+ on stack. Differs from System V (which uses RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9).

questions.question: What is the biggest porting trap between System V and Windows conventions?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: On System V, RDI and RSI are caller-saved (callee can clobber). On Windows, they're callee-saved — must save/restore. #1 porting trap for assembly and intrinsics.

questions.question: What is the shadow space in Microsoft x64 ABI?
options: 32 bytes the caller must allocate before every call for register argument spills (RCX,RDX,R8,R9)
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: On Windows x64, the caller allocates 32 bytes shadow space before every call. The callee can spill RCX/RDX/R8/R9 here. Simplifies variadic functions and debugging.

questions.question: Why is there no red zone on Windows?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Windows has no red zone. Memory below RSP is volatile — interrupt handlers, exception dispatch, and debugger breakpoints may overwrite it. Always sub rsp, N on Windows.

questions.question: After `call` pushes 8 bytes (return address), what is RSP's alignment?
questions.correctAnswer: Misaligned (RSP%16==8)
questions.explanation: Before call: RSP % 16 == 0. `call` pushes 8 bytes → RSP % 16 == 8. The callee's `push rbp` (8 bytes) re-aligns to RSP % 16 == 0.

questions.question: How is `this` passed in C++ member functions?
options: As implicit first arg: RCX (Windows) or RDI (System V)
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: `this` is an implicit first argument: RCX on Windows x64, RDI on System V. The convention is otherwise identical to a regular function.

questions.question: How does a virtual function call work at the assembly level?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: `obj->vf()`: load vptr from `[this]` (offset 0), load function address from `[vptr + fixed_offset]`, call through it. Same convention as regular indirect call.

questions.question: Under System V, where do floating-point args go?
questions.correctAnswer: XMM0 through XMM7 (up to 8)
questions.explanation: System V: floats in XMM0-XMM7 (up to 8). AL is set to the number of XMM registers used (important for variadic functions like printf).

questions.question: What does AL contain before calling printf on System V?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: AL = count of XMM registers containing float arguments. printf uses this to know how many XMM registers to save in the va_list area.

questions.question: What are the callee-saved registers on Windows that are NOT callee-saved on System V?
questions.explanation: Windows x64 saves: RDI, RSI, XMM6-XMM15 in addition to System V's RBX, RBP, R12-R15. RDI/RSI are caller-saved (argument) registers on System V.

questions.question: Does a 0-argument function call on Windows need shadow space?
acceptableAnswers: yes — the caller must still allocate 32 bytes of shadow space even for zero-argument functions
questions.explanation: Shadow space (32 bytes) is required for EVERY call on Windows x64, regardless of argument count. The callee may still use the space.

questions.question: On Windows, where does va_start point inside printf?
acceptableAnswers: into the 32-byte shadow space, which holds spills of RCX/RDX/R8/R9 at known offsets
questions.explanation: `va_start` points into the shadow space. `va_arg` walks linearly: shadow space (4 slots), then past the return address onto the stack for additional args.

questions.question: Why did Microsoft create its own x64 calling convention?
acceptableAnswers: historical continuity from __fastcall, more callee-saved registers, shadow space for debuggability, and independence from AMD's System V spec
questions.explanation: Microsoft's convention extends x86 `__fastcall` (ECX/EDX → RCX/RDX/R8/R9). More callee-saved registers reduce save/restore across tight calls. Shadow space simplifies debugging.

questions.question: What is the callee-saved register set in System V?
acceptableAnswers: RBX, RBP, R12, R13, R14, R15
questions.explanation: These 6 registers must be preserved by the callee. To use them, save original values (push in prologue) and restore before return.

questions.question: How many bytes of shadow space on Windows and why?
acceptableAnswers: 32 bytes — 4 slots × 8 bytes each for RCX, RDX, R8, R9 spills
questions.explanation: The shadow space is exactly 32 bytes: 4 argument register slots of 8 bytes. Located at [RSP+0] (RCX), [RSP+8] (RDX), [RSP+16] (R8), [RSP+24] (R9).

questions.question: What is the Itanium C++ ABI mangling for `foo(int, float)`?
questions.explanation: `_Z` = prefix, `3foo` = name with length, `i` = int, `f` = float. MSVC would mangle the same as `?foo@@YAXHM@Z`.

questions.question: What is the `thiscall` convention for C++ on Windows x64?
acceptableAnswers: this is passed in RCX (the first argument register)
questions.explanation: On Windows x64, `this` goes in RCX as an implicit first argument. The remaining explicit arguments follow the normal Windows convention in RDX, R8, R9, and stack.

questions.question: What is the value of RSP modulo 16 after the callee's `push rbp`?
questions.explanation: Before call: RSP%16=0. Call pushes 8 bytes → RSP%16=8. `push rbp` pushes 8 more → RSP%16=0. The prologue re-aligns the stack.

questions.question: What does the shadow space enable for variadic functions on Windows?
acceptableAnswers: simpler argument walking — va_list reads linearly from shadow space (4 register spills) then onto the stack
questions.explanation: On Windows, variadic functions receive register args in RCX/RDX/R8/R9. The callee spills these to the shadow space. va_list walks linearly: shadow space (4 args) + stack (5+).

questions.question: What is the System V AMD64 calling convention's handling of caller-saved registers?
acceptableAnswers: RAX, RCX, RDX, RSI, RDI, R8-R11 are caller-saved — the caller must save them if needed after a call
questions.explanation: These 10 registers are scratch: the callee can freely clobber them. If the caller needs the values after the call, it must save them before calling.

questions.question: On Windows x64, where would a debugger find argument 3 without reading registers?
acceptableAnswers: at [RSP+16] in the caller's shadow space
questions.explanation: Windows convention spills all 4 register args to the 32-byte shadow space at known offsets: arg3 (R8) is at [RSP+16] in the caller's frame.

questions.question: What is a stack buffer overflow?
options: An unbounded write (like `strcpy`) writes past the end of a stack-allocated buffer, overwriting adjacent stack data such as the saved RBP and return address
The call stack exceeds available memory
A buffer overflow in heap memory
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: A stack buffer overflow occurs when an unbounded write (e.g., `strcpy`, `gets`) writes past the end of a stack-allocated buffer. In the standard frame layout, overflowing the buffer corrupts the saved RBP and then the return address — enabling arbitrary code execution via `ret`.

questions.question: In a typical stack buffer overflow, what is corrupted first after the local buffer?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: In the stack layout (growing downward): local variables are at [RBP-N] to [RBP-1], saved RBP at [RBP+0], return address at [RBP+8]. Overflowing from the buffer goes upward: first overwrites saved RBP, then the return address.

questions.question: How does a stack canary protect against buffer overflows?
options: The compiler inserts a random 8-byte value between local variables and the saved RBP. The epilogue checks it before `ret` — if corrupted, the program aborts
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: A stack canary is a random 8-byte value placed between the local buffers and the saved RBP. Any buffer overflow that tries to overwrite the return address must first pass through the canary. The function epilogue checks the canary value; if it was modified, the program aborts with a stack smashing detected error.

questions.question: How can an attacker bypass a stack canary?
options: By leaking the canary value via an information disclosure bug (e.g., format string vulnerability, out-of-bounds read), then including the correct canary value in the overflow payload
By using a different CPU architecture
By disabling the canary at runtime
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: The canary value is random but constant for a given thread. If an attacker can leak the canary value through an information disclosure vulnerability (like a format string bug that reads stack memory), they can include the correct canary in their overflow payload, keeping the check from detecting the corruption.

questions.question: What does the NX (No-Execute) bit do?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: The NX bit (also called DEP, Data Execution Prevention) is a feature in page table entries. When set, the page cannot be executed — the MMU raises a fault on instruction fetch. Stack and heap pages are typically NX, preventing attackers from executing shellcode injected into these regions.

questions.question: How does Return-Oriented Programming (ROP) bypass NX/DEP?
options: ROP chains together short code sequences (gadgets) ending in `ret` that already exist in executable memory (like libc), without injecting new code
ROP uses the kernel to execute shellcode
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: ROP bypasses NX by not injecting new code. Instead, it finds small instruction sequences ('gadgets') in existing executable code (libraries, the executable itself) that end with `ret`. The attacker chains these gadgets by writing their addresses sequentially on the stack, creating arbitrary behavior without executing any injected code.

questions.question: What does ASLR do?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) randomizes the base addresses of the stack, heap, shared libraries, and the executable itself (if PIE). Without knowing the exact address of useful gadgets or shellcode, the attacker's hardcoded return addresses are likely to be wrong, crashing the process instead of hijacking it.

questions.question: What does the Intel CET Shadow Stack protect?
options: Shadow copies of data on the heap
A hardware-maintained, protected copy of return addresses. `ret` compares the stack's return address against the shadow copy — mismatch triggers a #CP fault
Shadow copies of the stack canary
A secondary stack for exception handling
questions.correctAnswer: A hardware-maintained, protected copy of return addresses. `ret` compares the stack's return address against the shadow copy — mismatch triggers a #CP fault
questions.explanation: Intel CET (Control-flow Enforcement Technology) Shadow Stack is a hardware feature. The CPU maintains a dedicated shadow stack (write-protected from normal code) containing copies of return addresses pushed by `call`. On `ret`, the CPU compares the regular stack's return address against the shadow copy. A mismatch indicates control-flow hijacking and raises a #CP (Control Protection) fault.

questions.question: What happens when a recursive function has no base case and reaches the stack limit?
options: RSP crosses into the guard pages → the MMU raises a page fault → the kernel detects stack overflow → delivers SIGSEGV (Linux) or STATUS_STACK_OVERFLOW (Windows)
The kernel allocates more stack space
questions.correctAnswer: RSP crosses into the guard pages → the MMU raises a page fault → the kernel detects stack overflow → delivers SIGSEGV (Linux) or STATUS_STACK_OVERFLOW (Windows)
questions.explanation: The OS places guard pages (inaccessible memory pages) beyond the stack allocation. When RSP enters the guard pages, any memory access triggers a page fault. The kernel's page fault handler detects this as a stack overflow and terminates the process with SIGSEGV.

questions.question: With an 8 MB stack and 48 bytes per frame, what's the maximum recursion depth?
questions.explanation: 8 MB = 8,388,608 bytes. At ~48 bytes per frame (unoptimized), maximum depth ≈ 8,388,608 / 48 ≈ 174,763 calls. However, in practice, stack usage per frame is often higher (local variables, register spills, alignment padding), significantly reducing the actual depth.

questions.question: What is Tail Call Optimization (TCO)?
options: When a function call is the last operation (in tail position), the compiler transforms `call` into `jmp` — reusing the current stack frame, so recursion depth doesn't grow the stack
The compiler adds tail recursion manually
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: TCO converts `call func` into `jmp func` when the call is in tail position (the calling function has no more work after the call returns). This reuses the current stack frame instead of pushing a new one. Recursive functions with TCO use constant stack space regardless of recursion depth.

questions.question: What is a format string vulnerability?
options: When user input is passed as the format string to printf (e.g., `printf(user_input)` instead of `printf("%s", user_input)`), allowing attackers to read/write stack memory via %x, %n, etc.
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: A format string vulnerability occurs when user-controlled data is passed directly as the format string argument to printf-family functions. Attackers can use `%x` to leak stack values, `%s` to read arbitrary memory, and `%n` to write to arbitrary memory addresses — enabling code execution.

questions.question: Why does inlining a function eliminate call/ret overhead?
options: Inlining changes the function to a macro
Inlining uses a different calling convention
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Inlining copies the function body directly at the call site. This eliminates: the `call` instruction (push return address + jump), the prologue/epilogue (push/pop rbp), register save/restore, and the potential branch mispredict penalty from `ret`.

questions.question: In stack overflow exploits, what does `ret` do with the overwritten return address?
acceptableAnswers: pops the attacker-controlled value from [RSP] into RIP, redirecting execution to the attacker's chosen address
questions.explanation: After a buffer overflow overwrites the saved RBP and return address, the function epilogue eventually executes `ret`. This instruction pops the top of stack (the attacker's bytes at what was [RBP+8]) into RIP. The CPU then begins executing from whatever address the attacker specified.

questions.question: What is the default stack size for the main thread on Windows?
questions.explanation: Windows defaults to 1 MB stack for the main thread, set in the PE header. Thread stacks created via `CreateThread` also default to 1 MB (configurable via `dwStackSize`). This is significantly smaller than Linux's 8 MB default.

questions.question: What is a guard page in the context of stack overflow detection?
acceptableAnswers: an inaccessible memory page placed just beyond the stack allocation — accessing it triggers a page fault, which the kernel interprets as stack overflow
questions.explanation: Guard pages are virtual memory pages with no page table entries (neither readable, writable, nor executable). The OS places them at the bottom of the stack region. When the stack grows into a guard page, any memory access triggers a page fault, cleanly detecting stack overflow rather than silently corrupting adjacent memory.

questions.question: What is the 'check()' macro in UE5 and how does it relate to stack unwinding?
acceptableAnswers: a runtime assertion that, when triggered, captures the call stack by unwinding frame pointers for crash reporting
questions.explanation: UE5's `check()` (or `ensure()`) macro performs a runtime condition check. On failure, it captures the call stack. The crash reporter then performs stack unwinding: starting from the current RBP, reading [RBP+8] (return address) and [RBP+0] (saved RBP) to walk the chain of frames.

questions.question: How does stack unwinding in UE5's crash reporter work?
acceptableAnswers: starting from current RBP, reads [RBP+8] for return addresses (symbol lookup via .pdb), then follows [RBP+0] to the caller's RBP, repeating until RBP=0
questions.explanation: Stack unwinding walks the chain of RBP-linked frames: at each frame, `[RBP+8]` gives a return address (looked up in .pdb symbols to get function name), and `[RBP+0]` gives the previous frame's RBP. Development builds preserve frame pointers (`/Oy-`) for this purpose.

questions.question: Why does UE build Development configurations with `/Oy-` (no frame pointer omission)?
acceptableAnswers: to preserve the RBP frame pointer chain for reliable stack unwinding in crash reports and debugging, at a ~2-3% performance cost
questions.explanation: Without frame pointers, stack unwinding relies on DWARF/PDB metadata which is less reliable. Development builds use `/Oy-` to keep the RBP chain for accurate call stacks in crash reports. Shipping builds omit frame pointers for performance and rely on symbol files.

questions.question: What is the `si_addr` field in `siginfo_t` used for during SIGSEGV?
acceptableAnswers: contains the faulting memory address that caused the segfault
questions.explanation: When a SIGSEGV handler is registered with `SA_SIGINFO`, the `siginfo_t` struct's `si_addr` field contains the address of the memory access that caused the fault. This is critical for crash diagnostics — it tells you exactly which address the program tried to access.

questions.question: What is the primary security concern with `gets()`?
acceptableAnswers: gets() has no bounds checking — it reads input into a fixed buffer until EOF or newline, guaranteeing buffer overflow on any line longer than the buffer
questions.explanation: `gets()` reads from stdin into a buffer without any size limit. Any input longer than the buffer causes a guaranteed stack buffer overflow. This function was so dangerous it was removed from the C11 standard. Use `fgets()` with explicit size limit.

questions.question: What is the difference between SMEP and SMAP at the hardware level?
acceptableAnswers: SMEP (Supervisor Mode Execution Prevention) prevents the kernel from executing userspace code; SMAP (Supervisor Mode Access Prevention) prevents the kernel from accessing userspace data
questions.explanation: SMEP prevents kernel code execution of userspace pages (defeats ret2user attacks). SMAP prevents the kernel from reading/writing userspace memory unless explicitly enabled. Both are hardware-level mitigations in page table entries, enforced by the MMU.

questions.question: What is a ROP gadget?
acceptableAnswers: a short sequence of instructions in executable memory ending with `ret` that can be chained together by placing their addresses sequentially on the stack
questions.explanation: A ROP gadget is a sequence of existing machine code instructions (typically 2-5 instructions) followed by a `ret`. By overflowing the stack with gadget addresses, an attacker chains these snippets to perform arbitrary computation without injecting any new code.

questions.question: How does the Itanium C++ ABI name mangling encode `void foo(int x = 10, double y = 3.14)`?
acceptableAnswers: default arguments are not part of the mangled name — they don't affect the function signature; it's mangled as _Z3foid for foo(double) since int has default
questions.explanation: Default arguments are NOT part of the function's type for mangling purposes. `void foo(int x = 10, double y = 3.14)` is mangled as `foo(int, double)` → `_Z3foid`. Defaults are only relevant at the call site.

questions.question: Why does ROP require knowledge of the exact addresses of gadgets?
acceptableAnswers: each ROP gadget address must be written precisely on the stack as the return address; incorrect addresses crash the process — this is why ASLR (randomizing library base addresses) is an effective defense
questions.explanation: ROP chains require exact addresses for each gadget. If ASLR randomizes library base addresses across runs, the attacker doesn't know where any gadget is located. Combined with stack canary and NX, ROP requires at least one information leak to discover the randomization slide.

questions.question: What happens when `longjmp` is called inside a C++ function that uses RAII?
acceptableAnswers: longjmp unwinds the stack without calling destructors for local objects in skipped frames, causing resource leaks (memory, file handles, mutex locks)
questions.explanation: `longjmp` restores the CPU register state (RSP, RIP) to the `setjmp` save point, skipping all intermediate stack frames. No destructors are called for objects in those frames. A `std::vector`'s destructor never runs — its heap buffer is leaked. This is why C++ code should use exceptions, not setjmp/longjmp.

questions.question: What is RELRO (Relocation Read-Only)?
acceptableAnswers: a security feature that makes the GOT and other relocation tables read-only after dynamic linking, preventing GOT overwrite attacks
questions.explanation: RELRO (RELocation Read-Only) has two levels: Partial RELRO makes the GOT read-only (but the PLT GOT remains writable for lazy binding). Full RELRO (with `-z now`) resolves all symbols at startup and makes the ENTIRE GOT read-only, preventing GOT overwrite exploits.

questions.question: In a stack overflow, what is corrupted first after the local buffer?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Stack layout: locals at [RBP-N] to [RBP-1], saved RBP at [RBP+0], return address at [RBP+8]. Overflow goes from buffer upward: first corrupts saved RBP, then return address.

questions.question: How does ROP bypass NX/DEP?
options: Chains together short code sequences (gadgets) ending in ret from existing executable code — no injected shellcode needed
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: ROP finds small 'gadgets' (instructions ending with `ret`) in existing executable code. By placing gadget addresses on the stack, the attacker chains them to perform arbitrary computation.

questions.question: What does Intel CET Shadow Stack protect?
options: Hardware-maintained protected copy of return addresses — ret compares against it; mismatch → #CP fault
questions.correctAnswer: Hardware-maintained protected copy of return addresses — ret compares against it; mismatch → #CP fault
questions.explanation: CET Shadow Stack is a hardware feature: the CPU maintains a write-protected shadow stack of return addresses. `ret` compares stack vs shadow copy. Mismatch = control-flow hijack → #CP fault.

questions.question: What happens when recursion hits the stack limit?
options: RSP enters guard pages → page fault → kernel detects stack overflow → SIGSEGV (Linux) or STATUS_STACK_OVERFLOW (Windows)
questions.correctAnswer: RSP enters guard pages → page fault → kernel detects stack overflow → SIGSEGV (Linux) or STATUS_STACK_OVERFLOW (Windows)
questions.explanation: Guard pages beyond the stack aren't mapped. When RSP enters them, the MMU faults. The kernel recognizes stack overflow and terminates the process with SIGSEGV.

questions.question: What is the maximum recursion depth with 8 MB and 48 bytes per frame?
questions.explanation: 8 MB = 8,388,608 bytes. At ~48 bytes/frame: ~174,763 calls. In practice, stack usage is higher (locals, spills, alignment), reducing actual depth.

questions.question: Why does inlining eliminate call/ret overhead?
questions.correctAnswer: B
questions.explanation: Inlining copies the function body at the call site. Eliminates: `call` (push+ jump), prologue, epilogue, register save/restore, and potential branch mispredict at `ret`.

questions.question: What does `ret` do with an overwritten return address?
acceptableAnswers: pops the attacker-controlled value from [RSP] into RIP, redirecting execution
questions.explanation: After a buffer overflow overwrites [RBP+8] (the return address), `ret` pops this value into RIP. The CPU starts executing at whatever address the attacker specified.

questions.question: What is the default stack size on Windows main thread?
questions.explanation: Windows defaults to 1 MB for the main thread (set in PE header). Thread stacks also default to 1 MB (configurable via CreateThread). Linux uses 8 MB.

questions.question: What is a guard page?
acceptableAnswers: an inaccessible memory page beyond the allocation — accessing it triggers a page fault, cleanly detecting stack overflow
questions.explanation: Guard pages have no page table entries (no r/w/x access). When the stack grows into a guard page, any access triggers a page fault, cleanly detecting overflow.

questions.question: How does stack unwinding work in UE5 crash reports?
acceptableAnswers: starting from current RBP, reads [RBP+8] for return addresses (symbol lookup via .pdb), follows [RBP+0] to caller's RBP, repeats until RBP=0
questions.explanation: The RBP chain is a hardware-linked list. `[RBP+8]` = return address (function + offset). `[RBP+0]` = previous frame's RBP. Walk until RBP = 0 (thread entry).

questions.question: Why does UE5 build Development with `/Oy-`?
acceptableAnswers: to preserve RBP frame pointers for reliable stack unwinding in crash reports, at ~2-3% performance cost
questions.explanation: Without frame pointers, stack unwinding depends on .pdb metadata which is less reliable. Development keeps RBP chain for accurate call stacks. Shipping omits them for performance.

questions.question: What is the `si_addr` field in siginfo_t for SIGSEGV?
acceptableAnswers: contains the memory address of the faulting access
questions.explanation: With `SA_SIGINFO`, `siginfo_t->si_addr` gives the exact address the program tried to access when the SIGSEGV occurred. Critical for diagnosing crashes.

questions.question: Why is `gets()` dangerous?
acceptableAnswers: gets() has no bounds checking — input longer than the buffer causes a guaranteed stack overflow
questions.explanation: `gets()` reads stdin into a buffer with NO size limit. Any input longer than the buffer overflows it. Removed from C11 standard. Use `fgets()` with explicit size.

questions.question: What is the difference between SMEP and SMAP?
acceptableAnswers: SMEP prevents kernel from executing userspace code; SMAP prevents kernel from accessing userspace data
questions.explanation: SMEP (Supervisor Mode Execution Prevention): kernel cannot execute userspace pages. SMAP (Supervisor Mode Access Prevention): kernel cannot read/write userspace memory without explicit enable.

questions.question: Why does ROP require exact gadget addresses?
acceptableAnswers: each gadget address must be written precisely on the stack; wrong addresses crash — ASLR defends by randomizing library bases
questions.explanation: ROP chains need exact addresses. ASLR randomizes library base addresses across runs, so the attacker doesn't know where any gadget is. Information leaks can reveal the randomization slide.

questions.question: What happens when longjmp is called in C++ with RAII objects?
acceptableAnswers: longjmp unwinds the stack without calling destructors, causing resource leaks
questions.explanation: `longjmp` restores the saved register state directly, skipping all intermediate frames without running destructors. A `std::vector`'s heap buffer is leaked. Use exceptions in C++.

questions.question: What is Full RELRO?
acceptableAnswers: makes the GOT read-only after startup by resolving all symbols at load time (with -z now)
questions.explanation: Full RELRO resolves all dynamic symbols at startup and makes the entire GOT read-only. This prevents GOT overwrite attacks. Requires `-z now` at link time.

questions.question: What does `-fstack-protector-strong` protect against that basic `-fstack-protector` doesn't?
acceptableAnswers: functions with local arrays of any size (including char/vla), not just functions with buffers >= 8 bytes
questions.explanation: `-fstack-protector` protects functions with buffers >= 8 bytes. `-fstack-protector-strong` also protects functions with any sized local array, VLA, or struct address-taking.

questions.question: What is a SEH (Structured Exception Handling) chain overwrite attack?
acceptableAnswers: overwriting the exception handler chain pointer on the stack so that when an exception occurs, the attacker's handler runs instead of the program's
questions.explanation: On Windows, the SEH chain links exception handlers in stack frames. A buffer overflow can overwrite the next handler pointer. When an exception fires, the corrupted chain redirects to the attacker's code.

questions.question: What does `__declspec(guard(overflow))` do in MSVC?
acceptableAnswers: enables Control Flow Guard (CFG) checks for indirect calls, preventing ROP/JOP attacks
questions.explanation: MSVC's Guard (CFG) adds checks before indirect calls (`call *[rax]`). If the target address isn't a valid, known function entry point, the process is terminated. This blocks most ROP chains.

questions.question: What is the fastest synchronization primitive?
questions.correctAnswer: std::atomic (relaxed load/store = one instruction)
questions.explanation: Atomic relaxed load/store is a single instruction on most CPUs.

questions.question: Which synchronization method avoids kernel involvement entirely?
questions.correctAnswer: std::atomic (no kernel even under contention)
questions.explanation: Atomics are entirely userspace.

questions.question: What is the fastest lock when contention is very rare?
options: A spinlock that calls sched_yield on contention
questions.correctAnswer: std::mutex (uncontended = ~20 cycle CAS fast path)
questions.explanation: Uncontended mutex: ~20 cycles, no kernel.

questions.question: What is the fastest lock when critical sections are very short (< 1000 cycles) and contention is likely?
questions.correctAnswer: A spinlock (avoids context switch overhead)
questions.explanation: Spinlock: busy-wait avoids context switch cost.

questions.question: What is the best choice for read-mostly data?
questions.correctAnswer: std::shared_mutex (allows parallel readers)
questions.explanation: shared_mutex: concurrent readers, exclusive writers.

questions.question: What is the best choice for N identical resources?
questions.correctAnswer: std::counting_semaphore<N>
questions.explanation: Counting semaphore: manage N resources.

questions.question: What is the best choice for one-time thread rendezvous?
questions.correctAnswer: std::latch (counts down, one-shot)
questions.explanation: std::latch: single-use synchronization.

questions.question: What is the best choice for repeated multi-thread synchronization at phase boundaries?
questions.correctAnswer: std::barrier (reusable, with completion callback)
questions.explanation: std::barrier: phase-based reuse.

questions.question: What is the best choice for blocking until data is available?
questions.correctAnswer: A mutex + condition variable (wait/notify pattern)
questions.explanation: CV: efficient blocking until condition is true.

questions.question: What is the best choice for one-time async result delivery?
questions.correctAnswer: std::promise/std::future (one-shot value channel)
questions.explanation: Promise/future: asynchronous value delivery.

questions.question: What is the best choice for thread pools?
options: A fixed-size thread pool with work queue
questions.correctAnswer: A fixed-size thread pool with work queue
questions.explanation: Thread pool: amortize creation cost.

questions.question: What is the best choice for one-time initialization?
questions.correctAnswer: std::call_once and std::once_flag
questions.explanation: std::call_once: safe one-time init.

questions.question: What is the best choice for atomic counters?
questions.correctAnswer: std::atomic<int> with relaxed or fetch_add
questions.explanation: std::atomic: counter = fetch_add.

questions.question: What is the best choice for a producer-consumer with a bounded buffer?
questions.correctAnswer: std::mutex + std::condition_variable (wait on full/empty)
questions.explanation: CV: producer/consumer synchronization.

questions.question: What tool detects data races at runtime?
questions.correctAnswer: ThreadSanitizer (TSan)
questions.explanation: TSan: dynamic race detection.

questions.question: What tool detects deadlocks in gdb?
questions.correctAnswer: thread apply all bt
questions.explanation: Show all thread backtraces to find blocked threads.

questions.question: What tool detects false sharing?
questions.correctAnswer: perf c2c (Linux) or VTune (cross-platform)
questions.explanation: perf c2c: cache-to-cache false sharing detection.

questions.question: What is the common solution for false sharing?
questions.correctAnswer: Align per-thread data to 64-byte boundaries (alignas(64))
questions.explanation: alignas(64): each variable on its own cache line.

questions.question: What is the simplest fix for deadlock?
options: Use std::scoped_lock (C++17) for all multi-mutex acquisitions
questions.correctAnswer: Use std::scoped_lock (C++17) for all multi-mutex acquisitions
questions.explanation: scoped_lock: atomic multi-lock with deadlock avoidance.

questions.question: What is the simplest fix for livelock?
options: Add randomized backoff to break symmetry between threads
questions.correctAnswer: Add randomized backoff to break symmetry between threads
questions.explanation: Random backoff: break symmetric retry patterns.

questions.question: What is the simplest fix for starvation?
options: Use fair locks (FIFO ordering) or priority inheritance
questions.correctAnswer: Use fair locks (FIFO ordering) or priority inheritance
questions.explanation: Fairness: prevent thread from waiting indefinitely.

questions.question: What is the most expensive concurrent operation?
options: A context switch (1-15 µs + cache warmup)
questions.correctAnswer: A context switch (1-15 µs + cache warmup)
questions.explanation: Context switch: register save + cache/TLB penalty.

questions.question: What is the cheapest concurrent operation?
questions.correctAnswer: A relaxed atomic load (single instruction)
questions.explanation: Relaxed load: MOV instruction.

questions.question: What memory ordering is strongest (most expensive)?
questions.correctAnswer: memory_order_seq_cst
questions.explanation: Seq_cst: full synchronization, most expensive.

questions.question: What memory ordering is weakest (cheapest)?
questions.correctAnswer: memory_order_relaxed
questions.explanation: Relaxed: atomicity only, cheapest.

questions.question: On x86, which memory orderings are free (no extra instructions)?
options: relaxed, acquire, release (all free due to TSO)
questions.correctAnswer: relaxed, acquire, release (all free due to TSO)
questions.explanation: x86 TSO: acquire/release implicit; only seq_cst needs MFENCE.

questions.question: On ARM, which memory ordering is free?
questions.correctAnswer: Only relaxed (no barrier needed)
questions.explanation: ARM: relax = free, acquire/release = dmb, seq_cst = dmb full.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of lock-free data structures?
options: No kernel involvement even under contention — no context switches
questions.correctAnswer: No kernel involvement even under contention — no context switches
questions.explanation: Lock-free: avoid blocking.

questions.question: What is the primary disadvantage of lock-free data structures?
options: Complexity, the ABA problem, and memory reclamation challenges
questions.correctAnswer: Complexity, the ABA problem, and memory reclamation challenges
questions.explanation: Lock-free: hard to implement correctly.

questions.question: What is the best choice for counting semaphore in C++20?
questions.correctAnswer: std::counting_semaphore<N>
questions.explanation: C++20: std::counting_semaphore.

questions.question: What is the best choice for a memory barrier (full fence)?
questions.correctAnswer: std::atomic_thread_fence(std::memory_order_seq_cst)
questions.explanation: std::atomic_thread_fence: portable memory barrier.

questions.question: What is the best choice for high-performance thread-local data?
questions.correctAnswer: thread_local keyword (C++11)
questions.explanation: thread_local: zero-overhead per-thread storage.

questions.question: When should you use std::async vs a thread pool?
options: std::async for fire-and-forget; thread pool for recurring tasks
questions.correctAnswer: std::async for fire-and-forget; thread pool for recurring tasks
questions.explanation: std::async: simple; thread pool: efficient for repeated use.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of threads for a CPU-bound parallel loop?
questions.correctAnswer: Number of physical cores
questions.explanation: CPU-bound: match physical cores to avoid SMT oversubscription.

questions.question: What is the typical performance gain of SMT?
questions.correctAnswer: 15-30% throughput improvement
questions.explanation: SMT: ~15-30% for mixed workloads.

questions.question: How does std::atomic_flag compare to std::atomic<bool>?
options: std::atomic_flag is guaranteed lock-free (always); atomic<bool> may use a mutex
questions.correctAnswer: std::atomic_flag is guaranteed lock-free (always); atomic<bool> may use a mutex
questions.explanation: atomic_flag: simple, guaranteed lock-free.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of std::byte over char for memory operations?
options: std::byte enforces type safety — no accidental arithmetic
questions.correctAnswer: std::byte enforces type safety — no accidental arithmetic
questions.explanation: std::byte: type-safe byte storage.

questions.question: What is the best practice for using volatile in concurrent C++?
options: Use it for hardware registers only — not for thread synchronization
Use it for all shared variables
questions.correctAnswer: Use it for hardware registers only — not for thread synchronization
questions.explanation: volatile: not a threading tool.

questions.question: What is the most portable way to get physical core count?
options: Platform-specific APIs (GetLogicalProcessorInformation on Windows, /proc/cpuinfo on Linux)
questions.correctAnswer: Platform-specific APIs (GetLogicalProcessorInformation on Windows, /proc/cpuinfo on Linux)
questions.explanation: Physical core count: platform-specific queries.

questions.question: What is the break-even point for spinlock vs mutex?
questions.correctAnswer: ~1,000 cycles (critical section length)
questions.explanation: Note: < 1000 cycles → spinlock; > 1000 cycles → mutex.

questions.explanation: Hardwired x86 reset vector

questions.correctAnswer: Sets up env, calls main

questions.explanation: Segment descriptors for x86

questions.explanation: SIMT: Single Instruction Multiple Threads

options: Different threads take different branches → serialization
questions.correctAnswer: Different threads take different branches → serialization
